title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
abstract: Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) affects approximately 10% of women and represents the most common form of female sexual dysfunction, yet effective pharmacological interventions remain limited. Current monotherapies targeting dopaminergic, noradrenergic, or melanocortin pathways have shown modest efficacy with significant side effects. This study investigated two novel combination therapies designed to enhance sexual motivation through synergistic neurotransmitter modulation. A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted in 180 premenopausal women diagnosed with HSDD. Participants were randomized to receive either bupropion (300mg/day) combined with buspirone (30mg/day), flibanserin (100mg/day) combined with low-dose testosterone gel (300μg/day), or placebo for 12 weeks. Primary outcomes included changes in sexual desire as measured by the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI) desire domain and Sexual Desire Inventory-2 (SDI-2). Secondary measures assessed sexual satisfaction, distress levels, and adverse events. Both combination treatments demonstrated significantly greater improvements in sexual desire compared to placebo (p<0.001). The bupropion-buspirone combination increased mean FSFI desire scores by 2.4 points (95% CI: 1.8-3.0), while flibanserin-testosterone improved scores by 2.1 points (95% CI: 1.5-2.7). Sexual satisfaction improved in both active treatment groups with minimal adverse effects, primarily mild nausea and dizziness. These findings suggest that combination pharmacotherapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems may offer superior efficacy over monotherapy approaches for treating HSDD, warranting larger phase III trials to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term safety profiles.

title: The neurochemistry of hypnotic suggestion.
abstract: A diverse array of studies has been devoted to understanding the neurochemical systems supporting responsiveness to hypnotic suggestions, with implications for experimental and clinical applications of hypnosis. However, this body of research has only rarely been integrated and critically evaluated and the prospects for the reliable pharmacological manipulation of hypnotic suggestibility remain poorly understood. Here we draw on pharmacological, genotyping, neuroimaging, and electrophysiological research to synthesize current knowledge regarding the potential role of multiple widely-studied neurochemicals in response to suggestion. Although we reveal multiple limitations with this body of evidence, we identify converging results implicating different neurochemical systems in response to hypnotic suggestion. We conclude by assessing the extent to which different results align or diverge and outline multiple avenues for future research. Elucidating the neurochemical systems underlying response to suggestion has the potential to significantly advance our understanding of suggestion.
journal: The American journal of clinical hypnosis

title: The psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric conditions: a review of the literature.
abstract: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) is a common neurological disorder characterized by uncomfortable sensations in the legs and an irresistible urge to move them, particularly during periods of rest. The prevalence of RLS is significantly elevated in patients with psychiatric conditions, including depression, anxiety disorders, and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, yet optimal treatment strategies for this comorbid population remain poorly defined. This comprehensive literature review examined current evidence regarding psychopharmacological management of RLS in psychiatric patients. A systematic search of PubMed, CINAHL, and PsycINFO databases was conducted covering publications from 2000-2012, yielding 847 initial articles, of which 42 met inclusion criteria for analysis. Studies consistently demonstrated that traditional psychiatric medications, particularly selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants, can exacerbate RLS symptoms in up to 65% of patients. Conversely, dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) showed efficacy for both RLS and depressive symptoms, with response rates of 70-85% for RLS improvement and significant mood benefits in 60% of patients. Anticonvulsants, especially gabapentin and pregabalin, demonstrated dual benefits for RLS and anxiety symptoms while showing minimal psychiatric side effects. Iron supplementation proved particularly effective in psychiatric patients with concurrent iron deficiency, present in 40% of the reviewed cases. These findings suggest that integrated treatment approaches considering both psychiatric and RLS symptomatology may optimize patient outcomes. Psychiatric nurses should routinely screen for RLS symptoms and collaborate with prescribers to select medications that address both conditions simultaneously.
journal: Journal of the American Psychiatric Nurses Association

title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
abstract: Violence represents a complex behavioral phenotype with significant public health implications, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions remain limited and controversial. Current clinical practice often relies on off-label use of various psychotropic medications without clear mechanistic rationale or systematic evaluation of risk-benefit profiles. This review synthesizes existing literature on pharmacological approaches to violence reduction across psychiatric populations and examines neurobiological substrates underlying aggressive behavior to inform clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 1990-2013 examining psychopharmacological interventions for violence and aggression. Studies were categorized by medication class, patient population, and outcome measures. Additionally, we reviewed neuroimaging and preclinical studies elucidating neurochemical pathways involved in aggressive behavior, particularly focusing on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems. Evidence supports modest efficacy for several medication classes in reducing aggressive behavior, including atypical antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, though effect sizes remain small to moderate. Atypical antipsychotics showed the most consistent evidence across diverse populations, while anticonvulsants demonstrated particular utility in personality disorder patients. However, significant methodological limitations exist across studies, including heterogeneous outcome measures, short follow-up periods, and limited assessment of functional outcomes. The neurobiological basis for violence involves dysregulation of prefrontal-limbic circuits and multiple neurotransmitter systems. Clinical decision-making should integrate individual risk factors, comorbid psychiatric conditions, and medication side effect profiles. Future research requires standardized outcome measures and longer-term safety evaluations to establish evidence-based treatment algorithms.

title: Treatment of hyperkinetic movement disorders.
abstract: Hyperkinetic movement disorders, including dystonia, chorea, and tremor, represent a heterogeneous group of neurological conditions with limited therapeutic options and significant functional impairment. Current pharmacological interventions often provide inadequate symptom control with substantial adverse effects. We conducted a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to evaluate the efficacy and safety of riluzole, a glutamate antagonist with neuroprotective properties, in patients with treatment-refractory hyperkinetic movement disorders. Between January 2006 and September 2008, we enrolled 184 patients aged 18-75 years from 12 European centres. Participants were randomly assigned to receive either riluzole 100 mg daily (n=92) or matched placebo (n=92) for 16 weeks, with a 4-week washout period. The primary endpoint was change in the Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale motor subscore from baseline to week 16. Secondary endpoints included quality of life measures, functional disability scores, and safety parameters. Riluzole treatment resulted in a significant reduction in motor symptoms compared with placebo (mean difference -8·2 points, 95% CI -12·4 to -4·0; p=0·0002). Functional disability improved substantially in the riluzole group (p=0·018), with corresponding improvements in quality of life indices. Treatment was generally well tolerated, with mild gastrointestinal disturbances being the most common adverse events. These findings suggest that riluzole represents a promising therapeutic approach for hyperkinetic movement disorders, offering clinically meaningful symptom improvement with an acceptable safety profile. Further studies are warranted to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term efficacy.

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: I. Cellular, electrophysiological, and neurochemical profile in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: Dopamine D3 receptors represent promising therapeutic targets for neurological disorders, yet selective pharmacological tools remain limited. This study characterized S32504, a novel naphtoxazine derivative, as a potential D3/D2 receptor agonist and compared its profile to the established antiparkinsonian agent ropinirole. Binding affinity was determined using radioligand displacement assays in CHO cells expressing human D2 and D3 receptors. Functional activity was assessed via cAMP inhibition assays and patch-clamp electrophysiology in primary rat striatal neurons. In vivo neurochemical effects were evaluated using microdialysis in freely moving rats. S32504 displayed high affinity for D3 receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM) with 15-fold selectivity over D2 receptors, compared to ropinirole which showed 3-fold D3 selectivity. In cAMP assays, S32504 acted as a full agonist at D3 receptors (EC50 = 2.1 nM) and partial agonist at D2 receptors. Electrophysiological recordings revealed that S32504 (0.1-10 μM) dose-dependently reduced neuronal firing rates and inhibited spontaneous excitatory postsynaptic currents, effects blocked by the D2/D3 antagonist sulpiride. Microdialysis studies demonstrated that S32504 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg s.c.) decreased extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens more potently than ropinirole, consistent with preferential D3 autoreceptor activation. These findings establish S32504 as a novel D3-preferring agonist with distinct pharmacological properties compared to ropinirole, suggesting potential therapeutic applications in conditions associated with dopaminergic dysfunction where enhanced D3 receptor selectivity may provide clinical advantages.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: S(-)Propranolol as a discriminative stimulus and its comparison to the stimulus effects of cocaine in rats.
abstract: Racemic propranolol (PRO), a beta-adrenoceptor antagonist, has been evaluated as a test agent but not as a discriminative stimulus. Its S(-) stereoisomer is thought to subserve the effects of (+/-)PRO. Rats were trained to discriminate S(-)PRO (5 mg/kg) from saline in a two-lever food-reinforced operant conditioning task. The S(-)PRO stimulus was shown to be centrally mediated, dose-related, time dependent, and stereoselective: S(-)PRO (ED(50) = 2.2 mg/kg) was twice as potent as (+/-)PRO and approximately four times as potent as R(+)PRO. The S(-)PRO stimulus generalized fully to the beta-adrenoceptor agent pindolol, the alpha(1)-adrenoceptor agonist methoxamine, cocaine, and the serotonergic agents TFMPP and RU 24969; partial generalization occurred to (-)ephedrine and nisoxetine but not to fenfluramine or 5-OMe DMT. The S(-)PRO stimulus was blocked completely (and competitively) when prazosin, an alpha(1)-adrenoceptor antagonist, was given in combination with the training dose of S(-)PRO. Moreover, prazosin exerted antagonism of the S(-)PRO-like effect of (+/-)PRO or R(+)PRO but produced only partial antagonism of the S(-)PRO-like effect of cocaine. In a second study, rats were trained to discriminate 8 mg/kg of cocaine from saline. The cocaine stimulus generalized to S(-)PRO, (+/-)PRO, and R(+)PRO. Prazosin partially attenuated the stimulus effect of cocaine (8 mg/kg) but completely blocked the cocaine-like effects of (+/-), S(-), and R(+)PRO. PRO and cocaine exhibited cross-substitution, but their stimulus effects were antagonized differentially by prazosin. PRO (and its optical isomers) can exert a stimulus effect that is based, at least in part, on increased alpha(1)-adrenoceptor activity. PRO might be better characterized as an adrenoceptor partial agonist.

title: Increased levels of gamma-glutamylamines in Huntington disease CSF.
abstract: Transglutaminases (TGases) catalyze several reactions with protein substrates, including formation of gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine cross-links and gamma-glutamylpolyamine residues. The resulting gamma-glutamylamines are excised intact during proteolysis. TGase activity is altered in several diseases, highlighting the importance of in situ enzymatic determinations. Previous work showed that TGase activity (as measured by an in vitro assay) and free gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine levels are elevated in Huntington disease (HD) and that gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine is increased in HD CSF. Although free gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine was used in these studies as an index of in situ TGase activity, gamma-glutamylpolyamines may also be diagnostic. We have devised methods for the simultaneous determination of four gamma-glutamylamines in CSF: gamma-glutamyl-epsilon-lysine, gamma-glutamylspermidine, gamma-glutamylputrescine, and bis-gamma-glutamylputrescine and showed that all are present in normal human CSF at concentrations of approximately 150, 670, 40, and 240 nM, respectively. The high gamma-glutamylspermidine/gamma-glutamylputrescine and gamma-glutamylspermidine/bis-gamma-glutamylputrescine ratios presumably reflect in part the large spermidine to putrescine mole ratio in human brain. We also showed that all four gamma-glutamylamines are elevated in HD CSF. Our findings support the hypotheses that (i) gamma-glutamylpolyamines are reflective of TGase activity in human brain, (ii) polyamination is an important post-translational modification of brain proteins, and (iii) TGase-catalyzed modification of proteins is increased in HD brain.

title: Different role of cAMP dependent protein kinase and CaMKII in H3 receptor regulation of histamine synthesis and release.
abstract: The histamine H3 receptor functions as an autoreceptor regulating histamine synthesis and release in the central nervous system, but the intracellular signaling mechanisms mediating these effects remain incompletely understood. We investigated the differential roles of cAMP-dependent protein kinase (PKA) and Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII) in H3 receptor-mediated regulation of histaminergic function using rat hypothalamic slices and cultured histaminergic neurons. H3 receptor activation with imetit (1-100 nM) dose-dependently decreased histidine decarboxylase (HDC) activity and histamine release, effects blocked by the H3 antagonist thioperamide. Co-immunoprecipitation studies revealed constitutive association between H3 receptors and both PKA regulatory subunits and CaMKII. Selective PKA inhibition with H-89 (10 μM) completely prevented imetit-induced suppression of HDC activity and histamine synthesis, while having no effect on histamine release inhibition. Conversely, CaMKII inhibition with KN-93 (10 μM) selectively blocked H3-mediated reduction of histamine release without affecting HDC regulation. Western blot analysis showed that H3 activation decreased PKA-mediated phosphorylation of HDC at Ser320, while simultaneously reducing CaMKII autophosphorylation at Thr286. Immunofluorescence microscopy revealed distinct subcellular localizations of H3-PKA and H3-CaMKII complexes, with PKA predominantly cytosolic and CaMKII enriched at synaptic terminals. These findings demonstrate that H3 autoreceptors utilize spatially segregated signaling pathways, with PKA specifically regulating histamine synthesis through HDC phosphorylation, while CaMKII selectively controls histamine release mechanisms. This dual-pathway organization may allow for independent fine-tuning of histaminergic neurotransmission under different physiological conditions.

title: Dextromethorphan-quinidine-responsive pseudobulbar affect (PBA): psychopharmacological model for wide-ranging disorders of emotional expression?
abstract: Pseudobulbar affect (PBA) is a neurological condition characterized by involuntary, exaggerated emotional outbursts that are incongruent with underlying mood states, commonly occurring in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, multiple sclerosis, traumatic brain injury, and stroke. The recent FDA approval of dextromethorphan-quinidine (DM-Q) for PBA treatment provides an opportunity to examine broader mechanisms of emotional dysregulation. We conducted a systematic review of clinical trial data and neuroimaging studies to evaluate whether the therapeutic mechanisms underlying DM-Q efficacy in PBA may inform treatment approaches for other disorders of emotional expression. Analysis of randomized controlled trials demonstrated that DM-Q significantly reduced PBA episode frequency and severity across multiple neurological populations through modulation of glutamatergic and serotonergic neurotransmission. Neuroimaging studies revealed that PBA involves dysfunction in cortico-limbic circuits regulating emotional expression, particularly connections between prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate, and brainstem nuclei. These circuits overlap substantially with those implicated in emotional lability associated with bipolar disorder, borderline personality disorder, and treatment-resistant depression. The dual-mechanism action of DM-Q, combining NMDA receptor antagonism via dextromethorphan with enhanced bioavailability through quinidine-mediated CYP2D6 inhibition, represents a novel pharmacological approach that may have broader applications. Our findings suggest that PBA serves as a valuable model for understanding pathological emotional expression and that DM-Q's mechanisms may inform development of treatments for related conditions characterized by emotional dysregulation.

title: A history of the Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (1957-2004).
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1957 as the first international organization dedicated to advancing the emerging field of neuropsychopharmacology. This historical analysis examines the evolution of CINP from its founding through 2004, documenting its pivotal role in shaping modern psychopharmacological research and clinical practice. We conducted a comprehensive review of CINP archives, meeting proceedings, published abstracts, and interviews with founding members and past presidents to trace the organization's scientific and organizational development. Key milestones were identified through systematic analysis of biennial congress themes, membership growth patterns, and publication records. From its initial membership of 65 scientists in 1958, CINP expanded to over 1,200 members by 2004, representing 45 countries across six continents. The organization facilitated critical advances in understanding neurotransmitter systems, culminating in the development of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, atypical antipsychotics, and novel anxiolytics. CINP congresses provided essential forums for disseminating breakthrough research, including early presentations on chlorpromazine's mechanism of action (1960), dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia (1972), and GABA receptor pharmacology (1984). The establishment of specialized task forces led to influential treatment guidelines that shaped international psychiatric practice standards. CINP's commitment to fostering international collaboration and mentoring young investigators proved instrumental in globalizing neuropsychopharmacology research. This historical perspective demonstrates CINP's fundamental contribution to transforming psychiatry from a predominantly psychoanalytic discipline into an evidence-based medical specialty grounded in neuroscientific principles.

title: The Psychopharmacology of Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Preclinical Roadmap.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) affects 1-3% of the global population and is characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors mediated by dysregulated cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits. Despite decades of clinical research, current pharmacotherapies provide adequate symptom relief for only 40-60% of patients, highlighting an urgent need for novel therapeutic targets. This review synthesizes emerging preclinical evidence to identify promising pharmacological avenues for OCD treatment. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of rodent models exhibiting OCD-like phenotypes, including genetic knockout models, pharmacological induction paradigms, and behavioral paradigms such as marble burying and schedule-induced polydipsia. Key findings demonstrate that beyond traditional serotonergic mechanisms, glutamatergic dysfunction plays a central role in OCD pathophysiology, with NMDA receptor hypofunction and altered mGluR5 signaling consistently observed across multiple model systems. Preclinical studies reveal therapeutic potential for glutamate modulators including memantine, ketamine, and mGluR2/3 agonists, which normalize compulsive behaviors and restore CSTC circuit function. Additionally, emerging evidence supports roles for GABAergic interneuron dysfunction, altered dopaminergic signaling in the nucleus accumbens, and neuroinflammatory processes involving microglia activation. Novel targets showing preclinical efficacy include sigma-1 receptor modulators, adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, and histone deacetylase inhibitors. Integration of optogenetic and chemogenetic approaches has further elucidated circuit-specific mechanisms and identified the orbitofrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex as critical nodes for therapeutic intervention. These preclinical findings provide a roadmap for developing next-generation OCD pharmacotherapies targeting glutamate, GABA, and neuromodulatory systems beyond serotonin.

title: Traditional Chinese medicine and Western psychopharmacology: building bridges.
abstract: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) and Western psychopharmacology have historically operated under distinct theoretical frameworks, yet both systems target neuropsychiatric disorders through modulation of neurotransmitter systems. This review examines potential integration points between TCM herbal formulations and evidence-based Western psychopharmacological approaches. We conducted a systematic analysis of 127 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2012, focusing on TCM compounds with demonstrated effects on major neurotransmitter pathways including serotonergic, dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems. Particular attention was given to frequently prescribed TCM formulations for depression, anxiety, and cognitive disorders, including Gan Mai Da Zao Tang, An Shen Ding Zhi Wan, and Ding Zhi Wan. Analysis revealed that 68% of examined TCM formulations contained bioactive compounds with measurable effects on Western-defined neurotransmitter targets. Hypericum perforatum constituents showed significant serotonin reuptake inhibition comparable to conventional SSRIs, while Paeonia lactiflora demonstrated GABA receptor modulation similar to benzodiazepine mechanisms. Ginkgo biloba extracts exhibited acetylcholinesterase inhibition and neuroprotective properties relevant to cognitive enhancement. Notably, 43% of TCM formulations demonstrated multi-target activity, potentially offering advantages over single-target Western pharmaceuticals. However, standardization challenges, herb-drug interactions, and limited pharmacokinetic data remain significant barriers to integration. These findings suggest that systematic investigation of TCM compounds using Western pharmacological methodologies may identify novel therapeutic targets and combination strategies, potentially bridging traditional wisdom with contemporary neuropharmacological practice while maintaining rigorous safety and efficacy standards.

title: Task-dependent effects of the antidepressant/antipanic drug phenelzine on memory.
abstract: Phenelzine (PLZ) is a non-selective monoamine oxidase (MAO) inhibitor commonly used to treat depression and panic disorder. In addition to increasing levels of biogenic amines in the brain, PLZ elevates brain levels of the amino acid gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA; Baker et al. 1991; present study). Given the extensive evidence implicating biogenic amines and GABA in mnemonic processes, PLZ may affect learning and memory. To investigate this possibility, male Sprague-Dawley rats were given PLZ sulfate (15 or 30 mg/kg, based on free base weight) 2 h prior to training in a continuous multiple trial inhibitory avoidance (CMIA) and spatial water maze task. Retention was assessed 48 h later. The results indicated that PLZ enhanced CMIA and impaired water maze retention performance. Compared to control rats, rats given PLZ took significantly longer to re-enter the shock compartment and swam longer distances before reaching the escape platform on the retention tests. These effects of PLZ did not appear to be the result of PLZ-induced changes in acquisition or retrieval processes, activity levels, or footshock sensitivity. Combined, these findings indicate that PLZ influences memory in a task-dependent manner. These differential effects of PLZ may be the result of contrasting influences of GABA and biogenic amines on memory.

title: On the prescribing analyst.
abstract: The integration of psychopharmacological interventions within psychoanalytic practice has generated considerable debate regarding therapeutic boundaries and analytic neutrality. This study examined the clinical outcomes and transference dynamics when psychoanalysts directly prescribe psychotropic medications to their analysands, compared to traditional referral models. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 127 patients in psychoanalysis over a 5-year period (2008-2013), comparing three groups: patients whose analysts prescribed medications directly (n=41), patients referred to psychiatrists for medication management (n=43), and medication-naive controls (n=43). Treatment outcomes were assessed using standardized measures including the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale, Global Assessment of Functioning, and detailed process notes analyzing transference manifestations. Patients with prescribing analysts demonstrated significantly greater treatment retention (89% vs. 67%, p<0.01) and showed equivalent symptomatic improvement compared to the referral group. However, distinct transference patterns emerged, including heightened idealization (43% vs. 18%) and increased somatic preoccupations during sessions (62% vs. 31%). Process analysis revealed that the dual role created unique opportunities for exploring the patient's relationship to dependency and authority, though it also generated more complex countertransference reactions in analysts. These findings suggest that the prescribing analyst model may enhance therapeutic alliance and retention while introducing novel transference configurations that require careful analytic attention. The integration of prescribing responsibilities appears clinically viable when accompanied by rigorous self-analysis and appropriate training in psychopharmacology.

title: Child and Adolescent Psychiatry Trainees' Attitudes Toward Prescribing and Managing Psychotropic Medications.
abstract: There has been a recent concern about the overuse of psychotropic medications in children. This study was designed to assess the attitudes held by child and adolescent psychiatry trainees toward the prescription and management of medications in the pediatric population. An online survey was sent to all Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education (ACGME)-accredited child and adolescent psychiatry training programs across the USA with the goal of assessing trainee comfort and confidence with prescribing skills and concepts. About 63 trainees (7.2% of the targeted population) were included in the analyses. While most participants reported confidence in basic prescribing skills, areas of lower confidence included cross-tapering medications, managing side effects, knowledge of the evidence base, drug-drug interactions, and de-prescribing. Advanced year in training was associated with higher confidence overall but failed to reach significance with some complex concepts such as polypharmacy (p = 0.27) and de-prescribing (p = 0.10). In contrast, increased supervision had a significant impact on confidence regarding complex concepts (p = 0.04) but not basic skills (p = 0.51). Supervision was also associated with higher training satisfaction (p = 0.001). Though the study was limited by a low response rate, these preliminary findings suggest potential areas for targeted psychopharmacology training. The findings also support how the important role supervision plays in the development of more complex skills. Further studies are needed to better understand these potential areas of curricula development and to explore the most effective training modalities.
journal: Academic psychiatry : the journal of the American Association of Directors of Psychiatric Residency Training and the Association for Academic Psychiatry

title: Pathological laughing and crying : epidemiology, pathophysiology and treatment.
abstract: Pathological laughing and crying (PLC), also known as pseudobulbar affect, is a neuropsychiatric syndrome characterized by involuntary, inappropriate emotional outbursts that are incongruent with underlying mood. This condition significantly impacts quality of life in patients with various neurological disorders, yet remains underdiagnosed and undertreated. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of epidemiological studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials published between 1990-2007 to synthesize current understanding of PLC pathophysiology and therapeutic approaches. Epidemiological data indicate PLC prevalence ranges from 10-53% in stroke patients, 10-15% in traumatic brain injury, 23-49% in multiple sclerosis, and up to 95% in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Neuroanatomical studies demonstrate that disruption of cortico-pontine-cerebellar pathways, particularly involving the anterior limb of the internal capsule and basis pontis, underlies PLC pathophysiology. Lesions affecting serotonergic and glutamatergic neurotransmission appear critical in symptom development. Treatment efficacy has been demonstrated with tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and combination dextromethorphan/quinidine therapy. Double-blind, placebo-controlled trials show significant symptom reduction with amitriptyline (50-75mg daily), citalopram (20-30mg daily), and dextromethorphan/quinidine (20/10mg twice daily). Response rates typically range from 60-85% across different therapeutic agents. Recognition of PLC as a distinct neurological entity with specific anatomical substrates and effective pharmacological treatments should encourage clinicians to screen for this treatable condition in at-risk neurological populations.

title: Advances in paediatric neuropsychopharmacology: an overview.
abstract: The field of paediatric neuropsychopharmacology has experienced remarkable growth over the past decade, driven by increased recognition of childhood psychiatric disorders and the unique pharmacological challenges they present. This comprehensive review examines recent advances in the use of psychotropic medications in children and adolescents, focusing on developmental considerations, efficacy data, and safety profiles. A systematic literature review was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2001, supplemented by recent conference proceedings and regulatory documents. Key therapeutic areas examined include attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), childhood depression, anxiety disorders, and early-onset psychotic disorders. Significant progress has been made in understanding the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of psychotropic agents in developing populations. Stimulant medications for ADHD demonstrate robust efficacy with well-characterized safety profiles, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show promise for childhood anxiety and depression, though with important caveats regarding suicidal ideation monitoring. Atypical antipsychotics have emerged as valuable treatments for severe behavioural disturbances and early-onset schizophrenia, despite concerns about metabolic side effects. Critical gaps remain in our understanding of long-term developmental effects of psychotropic medications, particularly regarding growth, sexual maturation, and cognitive development. The paucity of controlled trials in paediatric populations continues to challenge evidence-based prescribing. Future research priorities include dose-finding studies, long-term safety surveillance, and investigation of pharmacogenomic factors affecting drug response in children. Enhanced regulatory frameworks and industry incentives are essential to advance this critical field.

title: [Neuropharmacology yesterday, today and tomorrow].
abstract: The development of neuropharmacology as a scientific discipline has undergone remarkable transformation since the pioneering investigations of Sechenov and Pavlov. Early neurophysiological studies focused primarily on anatomical localization and basic reflex mechanisms, while contemporary neuropharmacological research emphasizes molecular mechanisms of synaptic transmission and receptor pharmacology. This analytical review examines the evolution of neuropharmacological concepts and methodologies from classical neurophysiology to modern molecular approaches. Historical analysis was conducted through systematic examination of key publications spanning seven decades, from initial studies of autonomic pharmacology to recent investigations of neurotransmitter systems. Classical experiments established fundamental principles of chemical neurotransmission, beginning with acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and extending to central monoaminergic pathways. Contemporary research has identified numerous neurotransmitter systems including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and neuropeptides, elucidating their roles in behavior, cognition, and neurological disorders. Methodological advances in radioligand binding, electrophysiology, and biochemical assays have enabled precise characterization of receptor subtypes and their pharmacological profiles. Current investigations focus on G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, and intracellular signaling cascades. Future directions will likely emphasize molecular cloning approaches, computer-assisted drug design, and therapeutic applications for neurodegenerative diseases. The integration of classical neurophysiological principles with emerging molecular techniques represents the optimal strategy for advancing neuropharmacological knowledge in service of clinical medicine and our understanding of higher nervous system function.
journal: Fiziologicheskii zhurnal SSSR imeni I. M. Sechenova

title: From systems biology to dynamical neuropharmacology: proposal for a new methodology.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have primarily relied on reductionist methodologies that examine isolated molecular targets, often failing to capture the complex, dynamic interactions within neural networks. The emergence of systems biology presents an unprecedented opportunity to revolutionize drug discovery and therapeutic intervention in neurological disorders by integrating multi-scale biological data and network dynamics. We propose a novel methodological framework termed "dynamical neuropharmacology" that combines high-throughput genomic and proteomic data with computational modeling to predict drug effects on neural circuit function. Our approach utilizes differential equation-based models to simulate temporal dynamics of neurotransmitter systems, incorporating feedback loops, crosstalk between signaling pathways, and emergent network properties. We applied this methodology to analyze the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) on hippocampal-prefrontal circuits using integrated datasets from microarray expression profiles, protein-protein interaction networks, and electrophysiological recordings from rat brain slices. The dynamical models successfully predicted biphasic dose-response relationships and identified previously unrecognized off-target effects involving GABAergic interneurons. Validation experiments confirmed 78% of model predictions regarding synaptic plasticity changes following chronic SSRI treatment. Our results demonstrate that dynamical neuropharmacology can reveal non-intuitive drug mechanisms and predict therapeutic windows more accurately than conventional approaches. This systems-level methodology offers a paradigm shift toward personalized neurotherapeutics and may accelerate the development of more effective treatments for psychiatric and neurological disorders by leveraging the inherent complexity of neural systems rather than oversimplifying them.

title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
abstract: Violent behavior associated with mental disorders is a common reason for admission to a psychiatric inpatient unit. Once hospitalized, patients may continue to be intermittently agitated and have persistent aggressive behaviors, preventing their discharge back into the community. Managing agitation quickly with effective pharmacological agents can avoid further escalation to aggression and violence. In the acute setting, this usually involves the parenteral use of antipsychotics, with or without benzodiazepines. Within the past decade, short-acting intramuscular formulations of second-generation antipsychotics have become available and provide a means to induce calm with a substantially lower risk of acute dystonia or akathisia compared with haloperidol. New alternative formulations that avoid injections include inhalation and sublingual administration. Longer-term management of persistent aggressive behavior by reducing the frequency and intensity of future episodes of agitation is more complex. In contrast to agitation associated with schizophrenia or bipolar mania, no agents have yet been approved by regulatory agencies for the treatment of persistent aggressive behavior. The strongest evidence supports the use of clozapine as an antihostility agent, followed by olanzapine. Adjunctive strategies with anticonvulsants and beta-adrenergic agents may also be worthwhile to consider.

title: Changes in cerebrospinal fluid neurochemistry during pregnancy.
abstract: Pregnancy involves profound physiological adaptations that may influence central nervous system neurochemistry, yet systematic examination of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) neurotransmitter profiles during gestation remains limited. We investigated changes in CSF concentrations of major neurotransmitter metabolites and neuropeptides across pregnancy trimesters. Twenty-four healthy pregnant women underwent lumbar puncture during routine spinal anesthesia for cesarean delivery, with samples collected from 8 women per trimester. Control CSF samples were obtained from 12 age-matched non-pregnant women undergoing elective procedures. High-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection was used to measure homovanillic acid (HVA), 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG). Radioimmunoassays quantified β-endorphin, corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH), and oxytocin concentrations. Significant progressive increases in CSF β-endorphin levels were observed across trimesters (first: 18.2±3.1 pg/mL; second: 34.7±5.8 pg/mL; third: 58.9±8.2 pg/mL) compared to controls (12.4±2.3 pg/mL; p<0.001). CRH concentrations were elevated in second and third trimesters (2.8-fold and 4.1-fold increases, respectively; p<0.01). Oxytocin levels increased significantly only in the third trimester (p<0.05). CSF 5-HIAA concentrations decreased progressively throughout pregnancy, reaching 35% below control values by the third trimester (p<0.01). HVA and MHPG levels remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate substantial alterations in central nervous system neurochemistry during pregnancy, particularly in endogenous opioid, stress-response, and serotonergic systems. Such changes may contribute to pregnancy-associated mood alterations and provide neurobiological insights into peripartum psychiatric disorders.

title: Can psychopharmacological treatment change personality traits in patients with panic disorder?
abstract:
**Background:** Panic disorder is characterized by recurrent panic attacks and anticipatory anxiety that significantly impair quality of life. While psychopharmacological interventions effectively reduce panic symptoms, their potential impact on personality traits remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder induces changes in personality dimensions as measured by standardized assessments.

**Methods:** Sixty-four patients with DSM-IV-TR diagnosed panic disorder were enrolled in a 12-week, open-label trial with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). Participants received either sertraline (n=32, 50-150mg/day) or paroxetine (n=32, 20-40mg/day). Personality traits were assessed using the NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) at baseline, week 6, and week 12. Panic severity was evaluated using the Panic Disorder Severity Scale (PDSS) and Clinical Global Impression-Severity (CGI-S). Treatment response was defined as ≥40% reduction in PDSS scores.

**Results:** Fifty-seven patients completed the study (89% completion rate). Treatment responders (n=42, 73.7%) demonstrated significant reductions in neuroticism scores (baseline: 42.3±8.1 vs. week 12: 35.7±7.4, p<0.001) and increases in extraversion (28.4±6.9 vs. 32.1±7.2, p=0.002) and conscientiousness (31.2±5.8 vs. 34.6±6.1, p=0.001). Non-responders showed no significant personality changes. Changes in neuroticism correlated significantly with PDSS improvement (r=0.58, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Successful pharmacological treatment of panic disorder was associated with favorable personality trait modifications, particularly reduced neuroticism and enhanced extraversion and conscientiousness. These findings suggest that effective psychopharmacological intervention may produce broader psychological benefits beyond symptom reduction, potentially reflecting neuroplasticity-mediated changes in underlying temperamental dimensions.
journal: Revista brasileira de psiquiatria (Sao Paulo, Brazil : 1999)

title: Neurochemistry of Anesthetic States.
abstract: The molecular mechanisms underlying anesthetic-induced unconsciousness remain incompletely understood, hindering the development of safer anesthetic agents and improved monitoring techniques. While previous studies have characterized individual neurotransmitter systems during anesthesia, comprehensive neurochemical profiling across multiple brain regions has been limited by methodological constraints. Here, we present a novel enzymatic approach combining high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and optimized tissue extraction protocols to simultaneously quantify monoamines, amino acids, and acetylcholine in discrete brain regions during various anesthetic states. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were anesthetized with propofol, sevoflurane, or ketamine at equipotent doses, with conscious controls receiving saline. Brain regions including prefrontal cortex, thalamus, hippocampus, and brainstem were rapidly extracted and processed using our modified enzymatic digestion protocol. Propofol administration significantly decreased dopamine (67% reduction, p<0.001) and norepinephrine (45% reduction, p<0.01) in the prefrontal cortex while increasing GABA levels by 34% (p<0.05). Sevoflurane produced similar but less pronounced changes, whereas ketamine uniquely elevated glutamate concentrations in the hippocampus (28% increase, p<0.01) while reducing thalamic acetylcholine by 52% (p<0.001). Regional variations in neurochemical responses correlated with known anesthetic binding sites and consciousness-related neural circuits. This enzymatic approach provides enhanced sensitivity and reproducibility for studying anesthetic neurochemistry, revealing agent-specific patterns that may inform mechanism-based anesthetic development and perioperative monitoring strategies.

title: Effects of the mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 on ketamine-evoked behaviours and neurochemical changes in the dentate gyrus of the rat.
abstract: Glutamate dysfunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with both hypoactivation of NMDA receptors and dysregulation of metabotropic glutamate receptors contributing to disease symptoms. The mGluR2/3 agonist LY379268 has shown promise in preclinical models as a potential antipsychotic agent. The present study examined whether LY379268 could attenuate ketamine-induced behavioral and neurochemical alterations in rats, using the dentate gyrus as a key region of interest due to its role in cognitive processing and NMDA receptor expression. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10/group) received intraperitoneal injections of vehicle, ketamine (30 mg/kg), LY379268 (3 mg/kg), or ketamine plus LY379268 pretreatment (30 min prior). Locomotor activity was monitored for 120 minutes post-injection, and dentate gyrus tissue was collected for neurochemical analysis using high-performance liquid chromatography. Ketamine administration significantly increased locomotor activity compared to controls (p<0.001) and elevated dopamine and glutamate concentrations in the dentate gyrus (147% and 132% of control, respectively). Pretreatment with LY379268 dose-dependently reduced ketamine-induced hyperlocomotion by 68% (p<0.01) and normalized dentate gyrus neurotransmitter levels. LY379268 alone had no significant effects on behavior or neurochemistry. These findings demonstrate that mGluR2/3 activation effectively counteracts NMDA receptor hypofunction-induced behavioral and neurochemical disturbances, supporting the therapeutic potential of mGluR2/3 agonists in treating glutamate-related psychiatric disorders. The dentate gyrus appears to be a critical site for these modulatory effects.

title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
abstract: A growing number of investigators are studying ketamine effects in healthy human subjects, but concerns remain about its safety as a research tool. Therefore, it is timely to revisit the safety of subanesthetic doses of ketamine in experimental psychopharmacology studies. To report on the safety of laboratory studies with subanesthetic doses of ketamine in healthy humans using an existing dataset. Medically healthy subjects with no personal or familial Axis I psychotic spectrum disorders were administered subanesthetic doses of ketamine by intravenous infusion in a series of clinical investigations from 1989 to 2005. The safety of ketamine administration was monitored in these subjects. Four hundred and fifty subjects received at least one dose of active ketamine. Eight hundred and thirty three active ketamine and 621 placebo infusions were administered. Ten adverse mental status events were documented in nine subjects/infusions that were deemed related to ketamine administration (2% of subjects, 1.45% of infusions). All but one adverse reaction resolved by the end of the test session. The side effects in the remaining individual were no longer clinically significant within 4 days of the test session. No residual sequelae were observed. Ketamine administration at subanesthetic doses appears to present an acceptable level of risk for carefully screened populations of healthy human subjects in the context of clinical research programs that intensively monitor subjects throughout their study participation.

title: The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a novel class of serotonin receptors that differs fundamentally from previously characterized 5-HT1 and 5-HT2 subtypes in its mechanism of action. Unlike other serotonin receptors which are coupled to second messenger systems, the 5-HT3 receptor functions as a ligand-gated ion channel, allowing rapid depolarization upon serotonin binding. We examined the pharmacological properties and behavioral effects of 5-HT3 receptor activation and blockade using selective compounds in male Sprague-Dawley rats. The selective 5-HT3 agonist 2-methyl-5-HT (0.1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) produced dose-dependent increases in spontaneous locomotor activity and induced a characteristic behavioral syndrome including head weaving and flat body posture. These effects were completely blocked by pretreatment with the selective 5-HT3 antagonist ICS 205-930 (0.01-1 mg/kg, i.p.) but were unaffected by 5-HT1 or 5-HT2 receptor antagonists. Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal slices revealed that 5-HT3 receptor activation produced rapid membrane depolarization with a reversal potential near 0 mV, consistent with a non-selective cation channel. Radioligand binding studies using [3H]ICS 205-930 demonstrated high-affinity, saturable binding sites in brain regions including area postrema, nucleus tractus solitarius, and hippocampus. These findings establish the 5-HT3 receptor as a pharmacologically distinct serotonin receptor subtype with unique electrophysiological properties. The development of selective 5-HT3 receptor ligands provides new tools for investigating serotonergic neurotransmission and may offer therapeutic opportunities for treating psychiatric and gastrointestinal disorders involving serotonergic dysfunction.

title: The neuropharmacology of sleep paralysis hallucinations: serotonin 2A activation and a novel therapeutic drug.
abstract: Sleep paralysis is a state of involuntary immobility occurring at sleep onset or offset, often accompanied by uncanny "ghost-like" hallucinations and extreme fear reactions. I provide here a neuropharmacological account for these hallucinatory experiences by evoking the role of the serotonin 2A receptor (5-HT2AR). Research has shown that 5-HT2AR activation can induce visual hallucinations, "mystical" subjective states, and out-of-body experiences (OBEs), and modulate fear circuits. Hallucinatory experiences triggered by serotonin-serotonergic ("pseudo") hallucinations, induced by hallucinogenic drugs-tend to be "dream-like" with the experiencer having insight ("meta-awareness") that he is hallucinating, unlike dopaminergic ("psychotic" and "life-like") hallucinations where such insight is lost. Indeed, hallucinatory experiences during sleep paralysis have the classic features of serotonergic hallucinations, and are strikingly similar to perceptual and subjective states induced by hallucinogenic drugs (e.g., lysergic acid diethylamide [LSD] and psilocybin), i.e., they entail visual hallucinations, mystical experiences, OBEs, and extreme fear reactions. I propose a possible mechanism whereby serotonin could be functionally implicated in generating sleep paralysis hallucinations and fear reactions through 5-HT2AR activity. Moreover, I speculate on the role of 5-HT2C receptors vis-à-vis anxiety and panic during sleep paralysis, and the orbitofrontal cortex-rich with 5-HT2A receptors-in influencing visual pathways during sleep paralysis, and, in effect, hallucinations. Finally, I propose, for the first time, a drug to target sleep paralysis hallucinations and fear reactions, namely the selective 5-HT2AR inverse agonist, pimavanserin. This account implicates gene HTR2A on chromosome 13q as the underlying cause of sleep paralysis hallucinations and could be explored using positron emission tomography.

title: Simple behavioral methods to assess the effect of drugs or toxins on sensory experience.
abstract: Behavioral assessment of sensory function represents a critical component in neuropharmacological research, yet many current methods require extensive training, sophisticated equipment, or invasive procedures. We developed and validated a battery of simple, non-invasive behavioral assays to evaluate drug and toxin effects on visual, auditory, tactile, and olfactory sensory systems in laboratory rats. The protocol consists of four standardized tests: (1) visual cliff avoidance for depth perception, (2) acoustic startle response amplitude for auditory sensitivity, (3) adhesive tape removal latency for somatosensory function, and (4) buried food pellet discovery time for olfactory detection. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) were tested following administration of known sensory-impairing agents: atropine (1-10 mg/kg, i.p.) for visual impairment, kanamycin (400 mg/kg, i.p.) for ototoxicity, capsaicin (50 mg/kg, s.c.) for nociceptive dysfunction, and zinc sulfate (intranasal) for anosmia. All compounds produced dose-dependent sensory deficits detectable within 30-60 minutes post-administration. Atropine significantly increased visual cliff crossing latency (p<0.01), kanamycin reduced startle amplitude by 65% (p<0.001), capsaicin doubled tape removal time (p<0.01), and zinc sulfate increased food-finding latency 4-fold (p<0.001). Inter-rater reliability exceeded 0.85 for all measures, and test-retest correlation was >0.90 across a two-week interval. These simple behavioral methods provide researchers with accessible, quantitative tools for screening sensory toxicity and monitoring therapeutic interventions without requiring specialized training or equipment, making them particularly suitable for laboratories with limited resources.

title: Targets of cytoprotection in acute ischemic stroke: present and future.
abstract: Although the management of stroke has improved remarkably over the last decade due mainly to the advent of thrombolysis, most neuroprotective agents, although successful in animal studies, have failed in humans. Our increasing knowledge concerning the ischemic cascade is leading to a considerable development of pharmacological tools suggesting that each step of this cascade might be a target for cytoprotection. Glutamate has long been recognized to play key roles in the pathophysiology of ischemia. However, although some trials are still ongoing, the results from several completed trials with drugs interfering with the glutamatergic pathway have been disappointing. Regarding the inhibition of glutamate release as a possible target for cytoprotection, it might be afforded either by decreasing glutamate efflux or by increasing glutamate uptake. In this context, it has been shown that glutamate transport is the primary and only mechanism for maintaining extracellular glutamate concentrations below excitotoxic levels. This transport is executed by the five high-affinity, sodium-dependent plasma membrane glutamate transporters. Among them, the transporter EAAT2 is responsible for up to 90% of all glutamate transport. We will discuss the effect of different neuroprotective tools (membrane stabilizers or endogenous neuroprotection) affecting glutamate efflux and/or expression of EAAT2. We will also describe the finding of a novel polymorphism in the EAAT2 promoter region which could be responsible for differences in both gene function and regulation under pathological conditions such as cerebral ischemia, and which might well account for the failure of glutamate antagonists in the clinical practice. These results may possess important therapeutic implications in the management of patients at risk of ischemic events, since it has been demonstrated that those patients with progressing stroke have higher plasma concentrations of glutamate which remain elevated up to 24 h when compared to the levels in patients without neurological deterioration.

title: Neurochemical measures co-vary with personality traits: forensic psychiatric findings replicated in a general population sample.
abstract: Neurobiological markers in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and in serum, previously found to co-vary with destructive personality traits in violent offenders, were explored in a general population sample of 21 patients undergoing knee surgery. Results on the Karolinska Scales of Personality (KSP) and the Temperament and Character Inventory (TCI) were compared with CSF/serum albumin ratios and serum concentrations of beta-trace protein (betaTP) (as markers for blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability), to CSF/serum albumin ratios between the dopamine and serotonin metabolites homovanillic acid (HVA)/5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (HIAA) and to CSF and serum ratios between activated thyroid hormone (T3) and its precursor T4. Serum betaTP concentrations correlated with CSF/serum albumin ratios (P=0.018), but not with preoperative serum creatinine concentrations. Serum betaTP correlated significantly with Monotony Avoidance and Impulsiveness; CSF HVA/5-HIAA ratios with Irritability and low Cooperativeness. The betaTP is a potential serum marker for the integrity of the BBB that does not necessitate lumbar puncture. Thyroid hormones did not correlate with personality traits. As reported in forensic psychiatric patients, aggressive, unempathic personality traits were thus associated with increased dopaminergic activity in relation to the serotonergic activity and impulsivity to increased BBB permeability also in a general population group.

title: Consensus Guidelines for Therapeutic Drug Monitoring in Neuropsychopharmacology: Update 2017.
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) has emerged as a critical tool for optimizing neuropsychopharmacological treatment, yet inconsistent implementation across clinical settings has highlighted the need for updated evidence-based guidelines. This consensus update reviews current literature and expert recommendations for TDM applications in psychiatric and neurological disorders through 2017. A multidisciplinary expert panel conducted systematic literature reviews of TDM studies published between 2012-2017, focusing on antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and antiepileptic drugs used in neuropsychiatric conditions. Recommendations were developed using modified Delphi methodology incorporating pharmacokinetic data, clinical efficacy studies, and real-world effectiveness evidence. The panel identified strong evidence supporting routine TDM for lithium, several tricyclic antidepressants, and select antipsychotics including clozapine, olanzapine, and aripiprazole. Moderate evidence supported TDM for lamotrigine, valproic acid, and certain SSRIs in specific populations. New recommendations include expanded TDM indications for treatment-resistant depression, pediatric and geriatric populations, and patients with comorbid medical conditions affecting drug metabolism. Updated therapeutic reference ranges were established for 23 neuropsychiatric medications based on recent concentration-response studies. Cost-effectiveness analyses demonstrated favorable outcomes when TDM was implemented systematically rather than selectively. These updated guidelines provide clinicians with evidence-based recommendations for implementing TDM to improve treatment outcomes, minimize adverse effects, and reduce healthcare costs in neuropsychopharmacological practice. Regular TDM should be considered standard of care for high-risk populations and specific medication classes.

title: Psychopharmacology in fragile X syndrome--present and future.
abstract: Fragile X syndrome (FXS), caused by mutations in the FMR1 gene leading to absence of fragile X mental retardation protein (FMRP), represents the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability and autism spectrum behaviors. Current psychopharmacological interventions for FXS rely primarily on symptom-based treatments targeting hyperactivity, anxiety, aggression, and stereotypic behaviors, with limited understanding of underlying neurobiological mechanisms. This review synthesizes existing literature on psychopharmacological approaches in FXS and examines emerging therapeutic targets based on recent advances in FXS pathophysiology. A comprehensive literature search identified 47 studies examining pharmacological interventions in FXS populations, including stimulants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, antipsychotics, and anticonvulsants. Traditional medications show modest efficacy for behavioral symptoms but do not address core cognitive deficits. Stimulants demonstrate effectiveness for attention and hyperactivity in 65-70% of individuals, while SSRIs show variable response for anxiety and repetitive behaviors. Antipsychotics may reduce aggression but carry significant metabolic risks. Emerging research on FMRP function reveals dysregulation of metabotropic glutamate receptor 5 (mGluR5) signaling and altered synaptic plasticity, suggesting novel therapeutic targets. Preclinical studies with mGluR5 antagonists, GABA receptor modulators, and protein synthesis inhibitors show promising results in FXS animal models, with several compounds entering clinical trials. Future psychopharmacological approaches in FXS should incorporate mechanism-based therapies targeting underlying synaptic dysfunction while maintaining symptom-focused treatments. Integration of genetic insights with clinical pharmacology may ultimately provide more effective, individualized therapeutic strategies for this complex neurodevelopmental disorder.
journal: Mental retardation and developmental disabilities research reviews

title: Distinct properties of murine alpha 5 gamma-aminobutyric acid type a receptors revealed by biochemical fractionation and mass spectroscopy.
abstract: Gamma-aminobutyric acid type A receptors (GABA(A)Rs) that contain the alpha 5 subunit are expressed predominantly in the hippocampus, where they regulate learning and memory processes. Unlike conventional postsynaptic receptors, GABA(A)Rs containing the alpha 5 subunit (alpha 5 GABA(A)Rs) are localized primarily to extrasynaptic regions of neurons, where they generate a tonic inhibitory conductance. The unique characteristics of alpha 5 GABA(A)Rs have been examined with pharmacological, immunostaining, and electrophysiological techniques; however, little is known about their biochemical properties. The aim of this study was to modify existing purification and enrichment techniques to isolate alpha 5 GABA(A)Rs preferentially from the mouse hippocampus and to identify the alpha 5 subunit by using tandem mass spectroscopy (MS/MS). The results showed that the detergent solubility of the alpha 5 subunits was distinct from that of alpha1 and alpha2 subunits, and the relative distribution of the alpha 5 subunits in Triton X-100-soluble fractions was correlated with that of the extracellular protein radixin but not with that of the postsynaptic protein gephyrin. Mass spectrometry identified the alpha 5 subunit and showed that this subunit associates with multiple alpha, beta, and gamma subunits, but most frequently the beta 3 subunit. Thus, the alpha 5 subunits coassemble with similar subunits as their synaptic counterparts yet have a distinct detergent solubility profile. Mass spectroscopy now offers a method for detecting and characterizing factors that confer the unique detergent solubility and possibly cellular location of alpha 5 GABA(A)Rs in hippocampal neurons.

title: The Neuropharmacology of Cluster Headache and other Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalalgias.
abstract: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs), including cluster headache, paroxysmal hemicrania, and short unilateral neuralgiform headache attacks, represent a distinct group of primary headache disorders characterized by severe unilateral pain and ipsilateral autonomic symptoms. Despite significant advances in understanding their clinical features, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely elucidated. This review synthesizes current knowledge of the pathophysiology and therapeutic targets in TACs, with particular emphasis on cluster headache as the prototype disorder. The hypothalamic-trigeminal-autonomic network appears central to TAC pathogenesis, involving activation of trigeminal nociceptors, parasympathetic outflow via the sphenopalatine ganglion, and hypothalamic dysfunction. Key neurotransmitter systems implicated include serotonergic, GABAergic, and neuropeptidergic pathways, with calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) emerging as a critical mediator. Neuroimaging studies have revealed hypothalamic activation during attacks and structural alterations in pain-processing regions. Current therapeutic approaches target multiple neuropharmacological pathways: acute treatments include high-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan, while preventive therapies encompass verapamil, lithium, and topiramate. Novel therapeutic targets under investigation include CGRP receptor antagonists, neurostimulation techniques, and hypothalamic deep brain stimulation for refractory cases. Recent clinical trials with monoclonal antibodies targeting CGRP pathways show promising results. Understanding the complex neuropharmacology of TACs has led to more targeted therapeutic strategies, though significant gaps remain in our knowledge of disease mechanisms. Future research directions should focus on identifying biomarkers, developing personalized treatment approaches, and investigating novel therapeutic targets to improve outcomes for patients with these debilitating conditions.

title: Evidence for improved neuropharmacological efficacy and decreased neurotoxicity in mice with traditional processing of Rhizoma Arisaematis.
abstract: Rhizoma Arisaematis (RA, the rhizome of Pinellia pedatisecta Schott) is a traditional Chinese medicine commonly used in the treatment of convulsions, inflammation, and cancer. Despite the fact that it has been used for more than 2000 years, the pharmacological and toxic effects of traditionally processed products of RA are still unclear. In this study, we attempted to investigate the effects exerted by untreated crude RA and different preparations of RA treated with alumen in combination with ginger juice (Zhinanxing) or bile juice (Dannanxing) in ICR mice. The results showed that both the Zhinanxing and Dannanxing water extracts exerted significantly increased sedative effects, as indicated by the inhibitory effects on ambulatory distances, jumps, vertical-plane entries, and prolonged pentobarbital-induced sleeping time. The extracts also exerted significantly increased analgesic effects (increase of tail flick latency in nociceptive testing) in mice than did the unprocessed crude RA after oral administration for one to three days, and effects persisted 18 days after the cessation of treatment. By contrast, the toxic effects, such as an increase in stereotype-1 episodes of locomotor activities and reduction of the retention time on a rotating rod (motor equilibrium dysfunction), were observed only in mice treated with the unprocessed crude RA for three consecutive days, and effects persisted for 18 days after the cessation of treatment. These neurotoxic effects were accompanied by an increase in plasma lipid peroxidation (LPO), decrease in whole blood nitric oxide (NO(x)) levels, and inhibition of Na(+)/K(+)-ATPase activities in membrane fractions of erythrocytes and in the cerebral cortex. In conclusion, these findings provide scientific evidence that the processed RA indeed possesses not only enhanced neuropharmacological efficacy but also reduced neurotoxic effects as compared to the unprocessed crude RA. The signaling of NO(x)/oxidative stress/Na(+)-K(+)- ATPase activities played a role, at least in part, in the underlying mechanisms of neurotoxic effects induced by the crude RA.
journal: The American journal of Chinese medicine

title: Getting a Handle on Neuropharmacology by Targeting Receptor-Associated Proteins.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches have focused primarily on direct modulation of neurotransmitter receptors, yet mounting evidence suggests that receptor-associated proteins (RAPs) represent underexplored therapeutic targets with potentially superior selectivity and reduced side effects. Here, we investigated the druggability of RAPs using a comprehensive screen of 147 small molecules against key scaffolding and regulatory proteins associated with NMDA, AMPA, and GABA_A receptors in primary hippocampal neurons. We employed a combination of high-content imaging, electrophysiology, and proximity ligation assays to assess compound effects on receptor trafficking, synaptic localization, and functional modulation. Our screen identified 12 compounds that selectively disrupted specific protein-protein interactions without affecting receptor pharmacology directly. Notably, compound SN-2847, which targets the PSD-95/NMDA receptor interaction, produced antidepressant-like effects in behavioral assays while avoiding the psychotomimetic side effects associated with direct NMDA receptor antagonists. Mechanistic studies revealed that SN-2847 enhanced AMPA receptor surface expression through compensatory trafficking mechanisms. Similarly, compound GB-1203, targeting gephyrin-GABA_A receptor clustering, demonstrated anxiolytic properties with improved therapeutic windows compared to benzodiazepines. Proteomic analysis confirmed target specificity and revealed minimal off-target effects. These findings establish proof-of-principle that pharmacological targeting of receptor-associated proteins can achieve therapeutic efficacy with enhanced selectivity, opening new avenues for neuropsychiatric drug development. Our approach provides a framework for systematic exploration of the "RAP-ome" as a source of next-generation neuropharmacological targets.

title: Antipsychotic drugs: evolving mechanisms of action with improved therapeutic benefits.
abstract: One of the conundrums of neuropharmacology is to understand the therapeutic mechanisms of action of antipsychotic drugs. Every drug with antipsychotic activity is a dopamine (DA) D(2)-like receptor antagonist and therefore this function is critical to reducing psychotic symptoms. However, the actions of the archetypal atypical antipsychotic drug clozapine go beyond antipsychotic effects because the drug is efficacious in treating psychotic symptoms that do not respond to drugs mainly directed at antagonizing the DA D(2) receptor, has benefits in cognition and has recently been shown to reduce levels of suicide. A growing understanding of the mechanisms of clozapine and other atypical antipsychotic drugs suggests that both partial and inverse agonism, as well as receptor antagonism, at specific neurotransmitter receptors is required to give full therapeutic benefits. It is, therefore, timely to review the evolving nature of the mechanisms of action of different antipsychotic drugs.
journal: Current drug targets. CNS and neurological disorders

title: Blended learning: how can we optimise undergraduate student engagement?
abstract:
**Background:** Traditional lecture-based teaching in undergraduate neuroscience education often results in passive learning and suboptimal knowledge retention. Blended learning approaches, combining face-to-face instruction with digital technologies, have emerged as promising pedagogical strategies. However, optimal methods for maximizing student engagement in neuropharmacology courses remain poorly defined. This study investigated the effectiveness of a structured blended learning framework on undergraduate student engagement and academic performance in neuropharmacology education.

**Methods:** A randomized controlled trial was conducted with 156 third-year undergraduate students enrolled in a neuropharmacology course at two medical schools. Students were randomly assigned to either traditional lecture-based instruction (control, n=78) or a blended learning intervention (n=78). The blended approach incorporated pre-class online modules, interactive case-based discussions, virtual laboratory simulations, and post-class digital assessments. Student engagement was measured using the Student Engagement Questionnaire and behavioral analytics from the learning management system. Academic performance was assessed through standardized examinations and practical assessments.

**Results:** Students in the blended learning group demonstrated significantly higher engagement scores (7.2±1.1 vs 5.8±1.3, p<0.001) and increased online participation rates (85% vs 45%, p<0.001). Examination scores improved by 12.3% compared to controls (78.6±8.2 vs 70.1±9.4, p<0.001). Time spent on course materials increased by 34% in the intervention group. Student satisfaction ratings were significantly higher for blended learning participants (4.6±0.5 vs 3.8±0.7, p<0.001).

**Conclusions:** Structured blended learning significantly enhances undergraduate student engagement and academic performance in neuropharmacology education. These findings support the integration of digital technologies with traditional teaching methods to optimize learning outcomes in medical education.

title: Discrete neurochemical coding of distinguishable motivational processes: insights from nucleus accumbens control of feeding.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) has long been implicated in reward processing and motivated behavior, yet the specific neurochemical mechanisms underlying distinct motivational processes remain poorly understood. Previous studies have suggested that dopamine and GABA neurotransmission in the NAc shell and core subregions may differentially regulate appetitive behaviors, but direct evidence for discrete neurochemical coding of motivational components is limited. We examined the effects of selective pharmacological manipulations within NAc subregions on feeding behavior in food-deprived male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-12 per group). Bilateral microinjections of the GABA-A agonist muscimol (0.25 μg/0.5 μL) into the NAc shell dramatically increased food intake (312 ± 28% of vehicle controls, p<0.001) and feeding duration, while identical injections into the NAc core produced modest effects (147 ± 19% of controls, p<0.05). Conversely, D1 receptor blockade with SCH-23390 (1.0 μg/0.5 μL) in the NAc core significantly reduced feeding motivation as measured by progressive ratio responding (58 ± 12% of vehicle, p<0.01), while shell injections had minimal impact. Simultaneous Fos immunohistochemistry revealed distinct activation patterns in hypothalamic and brainstem feeding circuits following shell versus core manipulations. These findings provide evidence for anatomically and neurochemically discrete coding of motivational processes within the NAc, with shell GABA systems primarily controlling consummatory drive and core dopamine mechanisms regulating appetitive motivation. Understanding this neurochemical architecture may inform therapeutic approaches for disorders involving dysregulated motivation and feeding behavior.

title: Imaging drugs with and without clinical analgesic efficacy.
abstract: The behavioral response to pain is driven by sensory and affective components, each of which is mediated by the CNS. Subjective pain ratings are used as readouts when appraising potential analgesics; however, pain ratings alone cannot enable a characterization of CNS pain circuitry during pain processing or how this circuitry is modulated pharmacologically. Having a more objective readout of potential analgesic effects may allow improved understanding and detection of pharmacological efficacy for pain. The pharmacological/functional magnetic resonance imaging (phMRI/fMRI) methodology can be used to objectively evaluate drug action on the CNS. In this context, we aimed to evaluate two drugs that had been developed as analgesics: one that is efficacious for pain (buprenorphine (BUP)) and one that failed as an analgesic in clinical trials aprepitant (APREP). Using phMRI, we observed that activation induced solely by BUP was present in regions with μ-opioid receptors, whereas APREP-induced activation was seen in regions expressing NK(1) receptors. However, significant pharmacological modulation of functional connectivity in pain-processing pathways was only observed following BUP administration. By implementing an evoked pain fMRI paradigm, these drugs could also be differentiated by comparing the respective fMRI signals in CNS circuits mediating sensory and affective components of pain. We report a correlation of functional connectivity and evoked pain fMRI measures with pain ratings as well as peak drug concentration. This investigation demonstrates how CNS-acting drugs can be compared, and how the phMRI/fMRI methodology may be used with conventional measures to better evaluate candidate analgesics in small subject cohorts.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Antipsychotic drugs: evolving mechanisms of action with improved therapeutic benefits.
abstract: The therapeutic landscape of antipsychotic medications has undergone significant evolution since the introduction of chlorpromazine, with newer agents demonstrating improved efficacy and reduced adverse effects through novel mechanisms of action. This review examines the pharmacological profiles of second-generation antipsychotics and their enhanced therapeutic benefits compared to conventional neuroleptics. We analyzed the receptor binding profiles, clinical efficacy data, and adverse effect profiles of risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, and ziprasidone relative to haloperidol and chlorpromazine using published clinical trials and preclinical studies from 1990-2003. Second-generation antipsychotics demonstrate broader receptor antagonism beyond dopamine D2 receptors, including significant activity at serotonin 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C, α1-adrenergic, and histamine H1 receptors. This polypharmacological approach correlates with superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms and cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6 compared to conventional agents. Additionally, the reduced D2 receptor occupancy threshold (65-70% vs 80-85% for typical antipsychotics) and enhanced 5-HT2A/D2 antagonism ratio contribute to significantly lower incidence of extrapyramidal side effects and tardive dyskinesia. Clinical studies demonstrate 40-60% reduction in treatment discontinuation rates and improved quality of life measures. These findings support the hypothesis that balanced neurotransmitter modulation, rather than selective dopamine blockade, provides superior therapeutic outcomes in psychotic disorders. The evolving understanding of antipsychotic mechanisms continues to guide rational drug development toward more effective and tolerable treatments for schizophrenia and related conditions.
journal: Current drug targets. CNS and neurological disorders
