title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
abstract: Classical fear conditioning paradigms have demonstrated that degraded contingency between conditioned stimulus (CS) and unconditioned stimulus (US) presentations results in impaired associative learning, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this phenomenon remain poorly understood. Given the established role of cholinergic neurotransmission in attention and associative learning, we investigated whether muscarinic cholinergic signaling modulates the degraded contingency effect in auditory fear conditioning. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received systemic injections of either atropine (50 mg/kg), physostigmine (0.25 mg/kg), or saline vehicle 30 minutes prior to fear conditioning sessions. Animals underwent either standard fear conditioning (CS-US contingency = 1.0) or degraded contingency training (CS-US contingency = 0.33) using tone-footshock pairings. Freezing behavior was assessed during CS presentations in both acquisition and extinction test sessions 24 hours later. Under vehicle conditions, degraded contingency training produced significantly reduced freezing responses compared to standard conditioning (p<0.001). Atropine administration eliminated this degraded contingency effect, with both training groups showing equivalently low freezing levels. Conversely, physostigmine treatment restored the degraded contingency effect, reinstating the behavioral distinction between standard and degraded training conditions. These findings suggest that muscarinic cholinergic activity is necessary for the normal processing of CS-US contingency information during fear learning. Cholinergic enhancement appears to sharpen contingency detection mechanisms, while cholinergic blockade disrupts the ability to discriminate between high and low contingency learning situations. These results have important implications for understanding attention-based learning disorders and cholinergic therapeutic interventions.

title: Photochemical and pharmacological evaluation of 7-nitroindolinyl-and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl-amino acids as novel, fast caged neurotransmitters.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have emerged as powerful tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neural circuit function with high temporal and spatial resolution. However, existing caged compounds often suffer from slow photolysis kinetics and limited tissue penetration, restricting their utility in physiological studies. We synthesized and characterized two novel classes of caged amino acid neurotransmitters based on 7-nitroindolinyl and 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl protecting groups conjugated to glutamate, GABA, and glycine. Photochemical properties were assessed using flash photolysis with UV laser pulses (355 nm), while biological activity was evaluated in acute hippocampal slices from adult rats using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from CA1 pyramidal neurons. The 7-nitroindolinyl-caged compounds exhibited photolysis time constants of 45-65 μs, representing a 3-4 fold improvement over conventional o-nitrobenzyl derivatives. The 4-methoxy-7-nitroindolinyl analogs demonstrated even faster kinetics (τ = 25-40 μs) with enhanced two-photon cross-sections at 740 nm. Caged glutamate and GABA showed minimal biological activity prior to photolysis (<2% of uncaged response) and complete restoration of native receptor responses following UV illumination. Quantum yields ranged from 0.15-0.28, comparable to existing caged compounds. The improved photolysis kinetics and maintained biological fidelity of these 7-nitroindolinyl-based caged neurotransmitters make them promising tools for high-resolution studies of fast synaptic transmission and enable more precise temporal control in neuropharmacological investigations of receptor function and neural network dynamics.

title: Single-fiber recordings of unmyelinated afferents in pig.
abstract: The functional properties of unmyelinated C-fiber afferents remain incompletely characterized due to technical limitations in recording from these small-diameter axons. Previous studies in rodents have provided important insights, but translational relevance to larger mammals requires investigation in more clinically relevant animal models. Here we describe a novel preparation for obtaining stable single-fiber recordings from unmyelinated cutaneous afferents in anesthetized domestic pigs. The saphenous nerve was carefully dissected and placed on a recording electrode array, allowing isolation of individual C-fiber units through microdissection techniques. Mechanical and thermal stimuli were applied to defined receptive fields on the hindlimb skin while monitoring action potential propagation. We successfully recorded from 47 individual unmyelinated afferents over 23 experiments, with conduction velocities ranging from 0.4-2.1 m/s. Mechanical thresholds varied widely, with 34% classified as high-threshold mechanoreceptors responding only to noxious stimuli, while 28% showed polymodal responses to both mechanical and heat stimuli (>43°C). Cold-sensitive fibers comprised 15% of the sample, responding to cooling below 25°C. The remaining units showed irregular discharge patterns with unclear stimulus relationships. Action potential durations averaged 2.8±0.6 ms, consistent with unmyelinated fiber characteristics. These findings demonstrate that porcine C-fiber afferents exhibit similar functional diversity to those described in smaller mammals, validating the pig as an appropriate large animal model for investigating unmyelinated afferent physiology. This preparation will facilitate future studies examining C-fiber modulation by pharmacological agents in a clinically relevant species.

title: Should we or shouldn't we?: Preface to the Special Issue "Neurochemistry of Reward-Seeking".
abstract: The neurochemical basis of reward-seeking behavior remains one of the most critical areas in contemporary neuroscience, with profound implications for understanding addiction, motivation, and psychiatric disorders. While classical dopaminergic pathways have dominated reward research for decades, emerging evidence suggests a far more complex neurochemical landscape involving glutamatergic, GABAergic, cholinergic, and neuropeptide systems. This special issue addresses fundamental questions about whether current therapeutic approaches targeting reward circuitry are optimally designed and whether novel pharmacological interventions should focus on single-target or multi-system approaches. The collection presents cutting-edge research examining the intricate balance between approach and avoidance behaviors, the role of stress-reward interactions, and the temporal dynamics of neurochemical signaling during reward anticipation and consumption. Key findings highlight the bidirectional nature of reward-seeking, where identical neurochemical substrates can promote both adaptive and maladaptive behaviors depending on environmental context and individual vulnerability factors. Emerging data demonstrate that traditional dopamine-centric models inadequately explain the heterogeneity observed in reward-related disorders and treatment responses. The evidence collectively suggests that future therapeutic strategies should embrace the complexity of reward neurocircuitry rather than pursuing reductionist approaches. These insights have immediate implications for developing more effective treatments for substance use disorders, behavioral addictions, and anhedonia-related conditions. The research presented challenges current paradigms and provides a roadmap for next-generation neurochemical interventions that acknowledge the multifaceted nature of reward-seeking behavior.

title: Roles of transmembrane domain 2 and the first intracellular loop in human noradrenaline transporter function: pharmacological and SCAM analysis.
abstract: The human noradrenaline transporter (hNET) mediates reuptake of synaptic noradrenaline and is a primary target for antidepressants and psychostimulants. While transmembrane domain 2 (TM2) and the first intracellular loop (ICL1) are predicted to be critical for transporter function based on homology models, their specific roles in substrate recognition and transport remain poorly characterized. To investigate the functional importance of these regions, we employed site-directed mutagenesis combined with substituted cysteine accessibility method (SCAM) analysis in HEK293 cells stably expressing hNET constructs. Individual residues within TM2 (positions 118-142) and ICL1 (positions 143-156) were systematically mutated to cysteine and analyzed for noradrenaline uptake activity, ligand binding affinity, and accessibility to membrane-impermeant sulfhydryl reagents. Cysteine substitution at positions Ile119, Phe124, and Ser128 within TM2 resulted in 60-85% reduction in noradrenaline uptake without affecting surface expression levels. SCAM analysis revealed that residues Ile119 and Ser128 are accessible to MTSEA modification from the extracellular side, suggesting their location near the substrate permeation pathway. In ICL1, mutation of Arg148 and Glu151 significantly impaired both uptake function and desipramine binding affinity (Kd increased 4-8 fold). Methanethiosulfonate accessibility patterns indicated that ICL1 undergoes conformational changes during the transport cycle. These findings demonstrate that TM2 contributes directly to the substrate translocation pathway while ICL1 is essential for proper transporter conformational dynamics, providing new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying noradrenaline transport and antidepressant action.

title: In vivo monitoring of extracellular glutamate in the brain with a microsensor.
abstract: Dysregulation of glutamate neurotransmission has been implicated in numerous neurological disorders including stroke, epilepsy, and neurodegenerative diseases. Current methods for measuring extracellular glutamate in vivo, such as microdialysis, suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage. We developed a novel glutamate-selective microsensor for real-time monitoring of extracellular glutamate concentrations in the living brain. The microsensor consists of a platinum microelectrode (25 μm diameter) coated with glutamate oxidase and poly(o-phenylenediamine) to confer selectivity and minimize interferents. The sensor was calibrated in vitro and demonstrated linear response to glutamate (10 μM to 1 mM, r² = 0.98) with minimal interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and GABA. Response time was < 2 seconds with detection limit of 5 μM. In anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8), microsensors were stereotaxically implanted into the striatum and hippocampus. Baseline glutamate levels were 12.3 ± 2.1 μM in striatum and 8.7 ± 1.8 μM in hippocampus. Local application of potassium chloride (100 mM) produced rapid glutamate increases of 340 ± 45% in striatum and 280 ± 38% in hippocampus, returning to baseline within 5 minutes. Focal ischemia induced by middle cerebral artery occlusion resulted in sustained glutamate elevation (peak: 850 ± 120% of baseline) in the ischemic penumbra. This microsensor technology provides unprecedented temporal resolution for monitoring glutamate dynamics and offers valuable insights into excitotoxic mechanisms underlying neurological pathology.

title: The endocannabinoid system: a new molecular target for the treatment of tobacco addiction.
abstract: Tobacco addiction remains a major public health concern with limited therapeutic options and high relapse rates. Recent advances in understanding the endocannabinoid system (ECS) have revealed its crucial role in reward processing and addiction pathways, suggesting potential therapeutic applications for smoking cessation. The present study investigated the effects of cannabinoid CB1 receptor modulation on nicotine self-administration and withdrawal behaviors in rodent models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained to self-administer nicotine (0.03 mg/kg/infusion) and subsequently treated with the CB1 receptor antagonist rimonabant (0.3-3.0 mg/kg, i.p.) or the fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) inhibitor URB597 (0.1-1.0 mg/kg, i.p.). Nicotine self-administration, progressive ratio performance, cue-induced reinstatement, and somatic withdrawal signs were assessed. Rimonabant dose-dependently reduced nicotine self-administration by 45-78% and significantly attenuated cue-induced reinstatement without affecting food-maintained responding. Conversely, URB597 enhanced endogenous anandamide levels and reduced somatic withdrawal symptoms including weight loss, teeth chattering, and anxiety-like behaviors. Neurochemical analysis revealed that both treatments modulated dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens, with rimonabant reducing nicotine-evoked dopamine increases by 60%. These findings demonstrate that pharmacological manipulation of the ECS can effectively reduce nicotine-seeking behaviors and withdrawal symptoms through distinct but complementary mechanisms. The data support the therapeutic potential of targeting cannabinoid signaling pathways for tobacco addiction treatment, warranting further clinical investigation of ECS modulators as novel smoking cessation aids.

title: Neurochemical phenotypes of cardiorespiratory neurons.
abstract: Interactions between the cardiovascular and respiratory systems have been known for many years but the functional significance of the interactions is still widely debated. Here I discuss the possible role of metabotropic receptors in regulating cardiorespiratory neurons in the brainstem and spinal cord. It is clear that, although much has been discovered, cardiorespiratory regulation is certainly one area that still has a long way to go before its secrets are fully divulged and their function in controlling circulatory and respiratory function is revealed.

title: Victor P. Whittaker (1919-2016).
abstract: This Obituary honors Victor P. Whittaker, one of the pioneers in the field of neurochemistry. Victor Whittaker died on 5th July 2016 aged 97 in Cambridge (UK) after a short illness. Victor is best known for his landmark advances in the subcellular fractionation of brain tissue which led to the isolation of synaptosomes and subsequently synaptic vesicles at the beginning of the 1960s and for the cellular and molecular analysis of the cholinergic synapse.

title: Translational neuropharmacology and the appropriate and effective use of animal models.
abstract: This issue of the British Journal of Pharmacology is dedicated to reviews of the major animal models used in neuropharmacology to examine drugs for both neurological and psychiatric conditions. Almost all major conditions are reviewed. In general, regulatory authorities require evidence for the efficacy of novel compounds in appropriate animal models. However, the failure of many compounds in clinical trials following clear demonstration of efficacy in animal models has called into question both the value of the models and the discovery process in general. These matters are expertly reviewed in this issue and proposals for better models outlined. In this editorial, we further suggest that more attention be made to incorporate pharmacokinetic knowledge into the studies (quantitative pharmacology). We also suggest that more attention be made to ensure that full methodological details are published and recommend that journals should be more amenable to publishing negative data. Finally, we propose that new approaches must be used in drug discovery so that preclinical studies become more reflective of the clinical situation, and studies using animal models mimic the anticipated design of studies to be performed in humans, as closely as possible.

title: Open Science Badges in the Journal of Neurochemistry.
abstract: The Open Science Framework (OSF) has the mission to increase openness, integrity, and reproducibility in research. The Journal of Neurochemistry became a signatory of their Transparency and Openness guidelines in 2016, which provides eight modular standards (Citation standards, Data Transparency, Analytic Methods/Code Transparency, Research Materials Transparency, Design and Analysis Transparency, Study Pre-registration, Analysis Plan Transparency, Replication) with increasing levels of stringency. Furthermore, OSF recommends and offers a collection of practices intended to make scientific processes and results more transparent and available in a standardized way for reuse to people outside the research team. It includes making research materials, data, and laboratory procedures freely accessible online to anyone. This editorial announces the decision of the Journal of Neurochemistry to introduce Open Science Badges, maintained by the Open Science Badges Committee and by the Center for Open Science (COS). The Open Science Badges, visual icons placed on publications, certify that an open practice was followed and signal to readers that an author has shared the corresponding research evidence, thus, allowing an independent researcher to understand how to reproduce the procedure.

title: Curcumin ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad: behavioural, biochemical, neurochemical and molecular evidences.
abstract: Pain and depression frequently co-occur in clinical practice, sharing common neurobiological pathways involving monoaminergic dysfunction and neuroinflammation. Reserpine, a monoamine-depleting agent, has been shown to induce both nociceptive hypersensitivity and depressive-like behaviors in rodents, providing a valuable model for studying pain-depression comorbidity. Curcumin, the active constituent of turmeric, possesses anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and monoamine-modulating properties. The present study investigated the therapeutic potential of curcumin in ameliorating reserpine-induced pain-depression dyad in male Wistar rats. Animals received reserpine (1 mg/kg, s.c.) for three consecutive days, followed by curcumin treatment (25, 50, or 100 mg/kg, p.o.) for 14 days. Behavioral assessments included thermal hyperalgesia (hot plate test), mechanical allodynia (von Frey filaments), and depressive-like behaviors (forced swim test, sucrose preference). Biochemical analyses measured oxidative stress markers, pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), and monoamine levels (5-HT, DA, NE) in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Molecular studies examined BDNF, p-CREB, and NF-κB expression. Reserpine administration induced significant thermal hyperalgesia, mechanical allodynia, increased immobility time, and anhedonia. Curcumin dose-dependently reversed these behavioral alterations. Biochemically, curcumin restored depleted monoamine levels, reduced oxidative stress markers and pro-inflammatory cytokines, while upregulating BDNF and p-CREB expression and suppressing NF-κB activation. These findings suggest that curcumin effectively ameliorates reserpine-induced pain-depression comorbidity through multiple mechanisms involving monoaminergic restoration, anti-inflammatory actions, and neuroprotective effects, supporting its potential as a therapeutic intervention for pain-depression dyad.

title: Neuropsychiatric aspects of Parkinson disease psychopharmacology: Insights from circuit dynamics.
abstract: Neuropsychiatric manifestations in Parkinson disease (PD) represent a complex therapeutic challenge, with current pharmacological interventions often producing paradoxical effects on motor and non-motor symptoms. Recent advances in circuit-based neuroimaging and electrophysiological techniques have revealed distinct patterns of dysfunction within cortico-basal ganglia-thalamic loops that underlie these psychiatric comorbidities. This review synthesizes emerging evidence linking dopaminergic medication effects to specific neural circuit alterations in PD patients with depression, anxiety, psychosis, and impulse control disorders. We analyzed data from 847 PD patients across 23 neuroimaging studies published between 2015-2018, examining correlations between pharmacological interventions and circuit-specific functional connectivity patterns. Dopamine replacement therapy demonstrated differential effects on limbic versus associative circuits, with levodopa preferentially normalizing motor circuit dysfunction while potentially exacerbating ventral striatal hyperdopaminergia associated with impulse control disorders. Dopamine agonists showed stronger correlations with altered activity in orbitofrontal-striatal circuits (r=0.73, p<0.001) compared to levodopa (r=0.42, p<0.05). Antipsychotic medications, while effectively treating PD psychosis, produced significant reductions in striatal dopamine D2 receptor availability and compensatory increases in prefrontal-parietal network connectivity. Deep brain stimulation of the subthalamic nucleus demonstrated complex effects on emotional processing circuits, with high-frequency stimulation modulating amygdala-prefrontal connectivity patterns. These findings suggest that circuit-specific approaches to PD neuropsychiatric symptoms may optimize therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects, supporting the development of personalized pharmacological strategies based on individual circuit dysfunction profiles.

title: Plate reader-based assays for measuring cell viability, neuroprotection and calcium in primary neuronal cultures.
abstract: Primary neuronal cultures serve as essential models for investigating neurodegeneration and neuroprotective mechanisms, yet standardized high-throughput methods for assessing neuronal health remain limited. We developed and validated a suite of plate reader-based assays optimized for primary cortical and hippocampal neurons to measure cell viability, neuroprotection, and intracellular calcium dynamics. Primary neurons were isolated from embryonic day 18 rat cortices and hippocampi and cultured in 96-well plates. Cell viability was assessed using MTT, LDH release, and calcein-AM/propidium iodide fluorescence assays. Neuroprotection was evaluated by pre-treating cultures with candidate compounds followed by glutamate or staurosporine-induced excitotoxicity. Intracellular calcium measurements utilized Fluo-4 AM loading with real-time fluorescence detection. All assays demonstrated excellent reproducibility (CV <15%) and Z-factors >0.5, indicating robust assay performance. The MTT assay showed superior sensitivity to neuronal death compared to LDH release, while the dual fluorescence viability assay provided rapid kinetic measurements. Calcium assays successfully detected both NMDA receptor-mediated and voltage-gated calcium channel responses with EC50 values consistent with patch-clamp data. Known neuroprotectants including BDNF and MK-801 showed dose-dependent protective effects against excitotoxic insults. These validated protocols provide researchers with reliable, cost-effective methods for medium-throughput screening of neuroprotective compounds and mechanistic studies in primary neuronal cultures. The standardized approaches facilitate inter-laboratory comparisons and accelerate discovery of novel therapeutic targets for neurodegenerative diseases.

title: The neurophysiological and neurochemical effects of alcohol on the brain are inconsistent with current evidence based models of sleepwalking.
abstract: The DSM-5 and ICSD-3 have removed alcohol from the list of potential triggers for sleepwalking due to the lack of empirical evidence. Recent imaging and EEG based studies of sleepwalking and confusional arousals have provided a more data-based method of examining if alcohol is compatible with what is known about the neurophysiology and neurochemistry of sleepwalking. These studies have demonstrated a deactivation of the frontal areas of the brain, while the cingulate or motor cortex remains active and characterized activation in the form of beta EEG. This increase in activation is attributed to a decrease in the inhibitory activity the neurotransmitter GABAA. This cerebral excitability of the cingulate cortex of sleepwalkers is also present in the brains of sleepwalkers during wakefulness compared to normal controls. Alcohol is well established to have an inhibitory effect on the brain and specifically on the motor areas via the inhibitory effects of increased GABAA activity. Thus, the empirical data show sleepwalking is characterized by a decrease in the inhibitory activity of GABAA - permitting or facilitating motor activity while alcohol has the opposite effect of increasing GABAA and inhibiting motor activity. This is inconsistent with theories that alcohol is somehow a trigger or facilitator for sleepwalking.

title: The psychopharmacology of Huntington disease.
abstract: Huntington disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor dysfunction, cognitive decline, and psychiatric manifestations that significantly impact patient quality of life. While no disease-modifying treatments exist, psychopharmacological interventions remain crucial for managing the complex neuropsychiatric symptoms. This comprehensive review examines current evidence for pharmacological management of psychiatric comorbidities in HD, including depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioral disturbances. We analyzed randomized controlled trials, observational studies, and case series published between 2000-2019, focusing on efficacy and safety profiles of psychotropic medications in HD populations. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and citalopram, demonstrate modest efficacy for depression with acceptable tolerability profiles. Atypical antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and quetiapine, show benefit for psychotic symptoms and agitation, though careful monitoring for extrapyramidal side effects is essential given underlying motor pathology. Benzodiazepines provide short-term anxiety relief but carry risks of cognitive impairment and falls. Novel approaches including lithium and antiepileptic drugs show preliminary promise for mood stabilization and neuroprotection, though larger controlled trials are needed. The unique pathophysiology of HD, involving progressive striatal degeneration and altered neurotransmitter systems, necessitates modified prescribing approaches compared to primary psychiatric disorders. Drug-drug interactions, altered pharmacokinetics in advanced disease stages, and heightened sensitivity to adverse effects require careful consideration. Future research should prioritize developing HD-specific treatment guidelines and investigating neuroprotective agents that may address both psychiatric symptoms and underlying disease progression.

title: Love and attachment: the psychobiology of social bonding.
abstract: Social bonding represents a fundamental aspect of human behavior with significant implications for mental health and neuropsychiatric disorders. While the neurobiological mechanisms underlying attachment have been extensively studied in animal models, translational research examining the psychobiology of human social bonding remains limited. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neurochemical and neuroanatomical substrates of love and attachment, integrating findings from preclinical studies with emerging human neuroimaging data. We examined peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2008 focusing on the roles of oxytocin, vasopressin, dopamine, and endogenous opioids in pair bonding and attachment behaviors. Prairie vole studies demonstrate that oxytocin and vasopressin receptor expression in the nucleus accumbens and ventral pallidum critically regulate pair bond formation, while dopaminergic signaling in mesolimbic reward circuits mediates the reinforcing aspects of social contact. Human neuroimaging studies reveal activation of similar reward pathways during romantic attachment, including the ventral tegmental area, caudate nucleus, and anterior cingulate cortex. Oxytocin administration enhances trust, empathy, and social recognition in healthy volunteers, supporting its role in human bonding. Dysregulation of these systems may contribute to attachment disorders, depression, and autism spectrum disorders. The neurochemistry of love involves complex interactions between neuropeptide, monoamine, and opioid systems that evolved to promote selective social bonds essential for reproductive success and offspring care. Understanding these mechanisms provides insight into the biological basis of human relationships and offers potential therapeutic targets for disorders characterized by impaired social functioning.

title: The endocannabinoid system: a new molecular target for the treatment of tobacco addiction.
abstract: Tobacco addiction is one of the leading preventable causes of mortality in the world and nicotine appears to be the main critical psychoactive component in establishing and maintaining tobacco dependence. Several lines of evidence suggest that the rewarding effects of nicotine, which underlie its abuse potential, can be modulated by manipulating the endocannabinoid system. For example, pharmacological blockade or genetic deletion of cannabinoid CB(1) receptors reduces or eliminates many behavioral and neurochemical effects of nicotine that are related to its addictive potential. This review will focus on the recently published literature about the role of the endocannabinoid system in nicotine addiction and on the endocannabinoid system as a novel molecular target for the discovery of medications for tobacco dependence.

title: Chemogenetics drives paradigm change in the investigation of behavioral circuits and neural mechanisms underlying drug action.
abstract: The advent of chemogenetic tools has revolutionized the field of neuropharmacology by enabling precise temporal and spatial control over neuronal activity in behaving animals. Traditional approaches to studying drug action and behavioral circuits have relied heavily on pharmacological interventions and lesion studies, which often lack specificity and reversibility. Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs (DREADDs) represent a transformative methodology that allows researchers to selectively modulate genetically-defined neuronal populations with high temporal precision. This review examines how chemogenetic approaches have advanced our understanding of neural circuits underlying addiction, reward processing, and drug-seeking behaviors over the past decade. We analyze key studies demonstrating the application of both excitatory (hM3Dq) and inhibitory (hM4Di) DREADDs in dissecting the functional roles of specific brain regions and cell types in behavioral responses to drugs of abuse. Chemogenetic manipulation of dopaminergic neurons in the ventral tegmental area, GABAergic interneurons in the nucleus accumbens, and glutamatergic projections from the prefrontal cortex has provided unprecedented insights into the circuit-level mechanisms of addiction and relapse. Furthermore, the development of next-generation chemogenetic tools, including light-sensitive variants and improved selectivity profiles, continues to expand experimental possibilities. The integration of chemogenetics with advanced behavioral paradigms, electrophysiology, and neuroimaging techniques has established new standards for mechanistic studies in behavioral neuroscience. These technological advances promise to accelerate the translation of basic research findings into targeted therapeutic interventions for substance use disorders and other neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: Dopamine and oxytocin interactions underlying behaviors: potential contributions to behavioral disorders.
abstract: Dopamine is an important neuromodulator that exerts widespread effects on the central nervous system (CNS) function. Disruption in dopaminergic neurotransmission can have profound effects on mood and behavior and as such is known to be implicated in various neuropsychiatric behavioral disorders including autism and depression. The subsequent effects on other neurocircuitries due to dysregulated dopamine function have yet to be fully explored. Due to the marked social deficits observed in psychiatric patients, the neuropeptide, oxytocin is emerging as one particular neural substrate that may be influenced by the altered dopamine levels subserving neuropathologic-related behavioral diseases. Oxytocin has a substantial role in social attachment, affiliation and sexual behavior. More recently, it has emerged that disturbances in peripheral and central oxytocin levels have been detected in some patients with dopamine-dependent disorders. Thus, oxytocin is proposed to be a key neural substrate that interacts with central dopamine systems. In addition to psychosocial improvement, oxytocin has recently been implicated in mediating mesolimbic dopamine pathways during drug addiction and withdrawal. This bi-directional role of dopamine has also been implicated during some components of sexual behavior. This review will discuss evidence for the existence dopamine/oxytocin positive interaction in social behavioral paradigms and associated disorders such as sexual dysfunction, autism, addiction, anorexia/bulimia, and depression. Preliminary findings suggest that whilst further rigorous testing has to be conducted to establish a dopamine/oxytocin link in human disorders, animal models seem to indicate the existence of broad and integrated brain circuits where dopamine and oxytocin interactions at least in part mediate socio-affiliative behaviors. A profound disruption to these pathways is likely to underpin associated behavioral disorders. Central oxytocin pathways may serve as a potential therapeutic target to improve mood and socio-affiliative behaviors in patients with profound social deficits and/or drug addiction.

title: Measurement of plasmalemmal dopamine transport, vesicular dopamine transport, and K(+)-stimulated dopamine release in frozen rat brain tissue.
abstract: Dopamine neurotransmission plays a critical role in motor function, reward processing, and cognition, with dysfunction implicated in Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and addiction. Traditional methods for measuring dopaminergic function require fresh tissue preparations, limiting experimental flexibility and multicenter studies. We developed and validated a novel protocol for simultaneous assessment of three key dopaminergic parameters—plasmalemmal dopamine transporter (DAT) activity, vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) function, and potassium-stimulated dopamine release—using frozen rat brain tissue. Striatal tissue from adult Sprague-Dawley rats was sectioned at 10 μm thickness and stored at -80°C for up to 12 weeks. DAT activity was measured using [³H]dopamine uptake assays in the presence and absence of GBR12909. VMAT2 function was assessed via [³H]dopamine accumulation into synaptic vesicles isolated from thawed tissue homogenates, with reserpine as a selective inhibitor. K⁺-stimulated dopamine release was evaluated using high-performance liquid chromatography following depolarization with 30 mM KCl. Frozen tissue retained 82±5% of DAT activity, 76±7% of VMAT2 function, and 71±9% of K⁺-stimulated release capacity compared to fresh preparations (n=8-12 per group). All three measures remained stable for at least 8 weeks of storage and showed excellent correlation with fresh tissue values (r>0.85, p<0.001). This methodology provides a practical approach for comprehensive dopaminergic assessment in archived samples, facilitating retrospective studies and enabling standardized protocols across multiple laboratories investigating dopamine-related neurological and psychiatric disorders.

title: The future of acute care and prevention in headache.
abstract: Headache disorders represent a significant burden on healthcare systems worldwide, with current therapeutic approaches often providing suboptimal outcomes in acute management and prevention. This review examines emerging pharmacological strategies and technological innovations that may reshape headache treatment paradigms. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent clinical trials, experimental studies, and technological developments in headache medicine published between 2002-2007. Novel acute interventions include selective 5-HT1F receptor agonists showing efficacy without vasoconstriction, inhaled dihydroergotamine formulations for rapid onset, and targeted CGRP antagonists demonstrating promising results in migraine termination. Preventive strategies have expanded beyond traditional approaches to include botulinum toxin type A injections for chronic migraine, topiramate for episodic prevention, and emerging anti-epileptic drugs with dual mechanisms. Technological advances encompass transcranial magnetic stimulation devices, implantable occipital nerve stimulators for refractory cases, and telemedicine platforms enabling remote monitoring and early intervention. Pharmacogenomic approaches are beginning to personalize treatment selection based on individual metabolic profiles and drug responsiveness patterns. Early clinical data suggest that CGRP pathway modulation may provide breakthrough efficacy with improved tolerability profiles compared to current triptans and ergot derivatives. The integration of biomarker identification, personalized medicine approaches, and novel drug delivery systems represents a paradigm shift toward precision headache medicine. These developments promise to transform acute care through more effective, rapidly acting interventions and prevention through targeted, individualized prophylactic strategies, potentially reducing both attack frequency and healthcare utilization in headache populations.
journal: Neurological sciences : official journal of the Italian Neurological Society and of the Italian Society of Clinical Neurophysiology

title: An improved method for analyzing coenzyme Q homologues and multiple detection of rare biological samples.
abstract: Coenzyme Q homologues play critical roles in mitochondrial electron transport and neuroprotection, yet their analysis in rare biological samples such as cerebrospinal fluid and small brain tissue specimens remains technically challenging. Traditional HPLC methods suffer from poor sensitivity and require large sample volumes, limiting their utility in clinical neuroscience research. We developed an improved analytical method combining reversed-phase HPLC with electrochemical and UV detection systems to simultaneously quantify CoQ9, CoQ10, and their reduced forms (ubiquinols) in samples as small as 50 μL. The method utilizes a C18 column with methanol-ethanol gradient elution and post-column reduction with sodium borohydride to enhance detection sensitivity. Sample preparation involves protein precipitation with methanol followed by lipid extraction with hexane-isopropanol. We validated this approach using rat brain homogenates, human cerebrospinal fluid, and neuronal cell cultures. The detection limits were 0.5 pmol for CoQ10 and 0.8 pmol for CoQ9, representing a 10-fold improvement over conventional methods. Intra-assay and inter-assay coefficients of variation were below 8% and 12%, respectively. Recovery rates ranged from 87-94% across different sample types. The method successfully detected age-related changes in CoQ levels in mouse brain regions and demonstrated altered CoQ10/CoQ9 ratios in cerebrospinal fluid from patients with neurodegenerative disorders. This enhanced analytical approach provides researchers with a sensitive, reproducible tool for investigating coenzyme Q metabolism in limited clinical samples, facilitating studies of mitochondrial dysfunction in neurological diseases.

title: Acquiring local field potential information from amperometric neurochemical recordings.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and constant potential amperometry are widely used electrochemical techniques for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter release in behaving animals. However, these methods typically focus solely on neurochemical signals while discarding potentially valuable electrophysiological information present in the raw current recordings. Here we describe a novel signal processing approach to extract local field potential (LFP) information from amperometric neurochemical recordings using carbon fiber microelectrodes. We developed filtering algorithms to isolate low-frequency components (1-100 Hz) from high-frequency electrochemical noise in amperometric recordings obtained from the nucleus accumbens and dorsal striatum of anesthetized rats during electrical stimulation of dopaminergic pathways. Simultaneous recordings were performed using conventional tungsten microelectrodes for LFP validation. Our approach successfully recovered LFP-like signals that correlated strongly with conventional electrophysiological recordings (r = 0.78 ± 0.12, n = 8 animals). The extracted signals exhibited characteristic LFP features including evoked responses to electrical stimulation and spontaneous oscillatory activity in the theta (4-8 Hz) and gamma (30-80 Hz) frequency bands. Signal-to-noise ratio of recovered LFPs was optimal when amperometric recordings were performed at +0.7 V vs Ag/AgCl reference electrode. This method provides a cost-effective means to simultaneously monitor neurochemical and electrophysiological activity from a single electrode, potentially reducing tissue damage while increasing information yield from individual recording sites. The technique may prove particularly valuable for chronic recording applications where electrode count is limited by surgical constraints or for studying neurochemical-electrophysiological coupling in freely moving animals.

title: Listening to the brain: microelectrode biosensors for neurochemicals.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the living brain remains a critical challenge in neuroscience research. Traditional analytical techniques lack the spatial and temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations at synaptic and circuit levels. Here we review recent advances in microelectrode biosensor technology that enable direct, continuous measurement of neurochemicals in vivo with unprecedented precision. Carbon fiber microelectrodes modified with selective enzymes or aptamers have emerged as powerful tools for detecting dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and glutamate with sub-second temporal resolution and nanomolar sensitivity. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry coupled with these biosensors allows discrimination between structurally similar compounds while minimizing tissue damage. We examined validation studies demonstrating sensor selectivity, stability, and biocompatibility across multiple species and brain regions. Recent applications include real-time monitoring of dopamine release in reward circuits during behavioral tasks, tracking glutamate dynamics during synaptic plasticity, and measuring neurotransmitter perturbations in disease models. Emerging multiplexed sensor arrays enable simultaneous detection of multiple analytes, providing unprecedented insight into neurochemical network interactions. However, challenges remain in sensor fouling, drift compensation, and standardization across laboratories. Despite these limitations, microelectrode biosensors represent a transformative technology for understanding brain chemistry in health and disease. Future developments integrating wireless telemetry and advanced nanomaterials promise to further revolutionize our ability to decode the brain's chemical language in real-time.

title: The footfault test as a screening tool in the 6-hydroxydopamine rat model of Parkinson's disease.
abstract: The 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model is widely used to study Parkinson's disease (PD), but current behavioral assessments often lack sensitivity to detect mild-to-moderate dopaminergic deficits. The footfault test, which measures limb placement errors during grid walking, may provide a more sensitive screening tool for motor impairments in this model. We evaluated the footfault test as a behavioral screening method in unilaterally 6-OHDA-lesioned rats and compared its sensitivity to established tests including cylinder test and amphetamine-induced rotations. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) received unilateral 6-OHDA injections into the medial forebrain bundle or sham surgery. Behavioral testing was performed at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-lesion using the footfault test (errors per 50 steps on wire grid), cylinder test (forelimb asymmetry), and amphetamine-induced rotations. Tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry confirmed lesion extent in the substantia nigra and striatum. The footfault test detected significant contralateral forelimb deficits as early as 2 weeks post-lesion in 6-OHDA rats compared to controls (p<0.01), with deficits correlating strongly with striatal dopamine depletion (r=0.78, p<0.001). Footfault sensitivity exceeded that of the cylinder test, detecting impairments in rats with 40-60% striatal dopamine loss, while cylinder test required >70% depletion. Amphetamine rotations showed comparable sensitivity but greater variability. The footfault test provides a simple, sensitive, and quantitative behavioral screening tool for detecting motor deficits in the 6-OHDA rat model, particularly useful for assessing mild-to-moderate dopaminergic lesions that may be missed by conventional tests.

title: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy detection of neurotransmitters in dorsomedial medulla correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function.
abstract: The dorsomedial medulla (DMM) contains critical cardiovascular regulatory neurons that modulate baroreceptor reflex sensitivity, but the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this control remain poorly understood. We hypothesized that GABA and glutamate concentrations in the DMM would correlate with spontaneous baroreceptor reflex function in conscious rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were instrumented with arterial catheters for continuous blood pressure monitoring. Spontaneous baroreceptor sensitivity (BRS) was calculated using sequence analysis of systolic pressure and pulse interval over 2-hour recording periods. Animals were then anesthetized and subjected to localized proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) using a 7.0 Tesla scanner with a 2×2×2 mm³ voxel positioned in the DMM. GABA and glutamate concentrations were quantified relative to creatine using LCModel analysis. BRS ranged from 0.8 to 3.2 ms/mmHg across animals. DMM GABA concentrations showed a strong positive correlation with BRS (r=0.71, P<0.001), while glutamate levels were inversely correlated with BRS (r=-0.58, P=0.003). The GABA/glutamate ratio demonstrated the strongest relationship with baroreceptor sensitivity (r=0.79, P<0.001). Multiple regression analysis revealed that DMM neurotransmitter balance accounted for 65% of the variance in spontaneous BRS. These findings provide the first direct neurochemical evidence linking GABAergic-glutamatergic balance in the DMM to baroreceptor reflex function in vivo, suggesting that altered neurotransmitter homeostasis in this region may contribute to impaired cardiovascular regulation in hypertensive states.

title: Interaction Between the Trace Amine-Associated Receptor 1 and the Dopamine D2 Receptor Controls Cocaine's Neurochemical Actions.
abstract: The trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) has emerged as a potential modulator of dopaminergic neurotransmission, but its precise interaction with dopamine D2 receptors (D2R) in mediating cocaine's effects remains unclear. We investigated the functional relationship between TAAR1 and D2R in controlling cocaine-induced neurochemical responses using pharmacological and molecular approaches in rodent models. Wild-type and TAAR1 knockout mice received acute cocaine administration (15 mg/kg, i.p.) with or without selective TAAR1 agonist RO5263397 (10 mg/kg) or D2R antagonist raclopride (1 mg/kg). Extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens were measured using in vivo microdialysis, while D2R binding and cAMP signaling were assessed using radioligand binding and biochemical assays. Cocaine increased extracellular dopamine by 340% in wild-type mice, but this response was potentiated to 485% in TAAR1 knockout animals. TAAR1 activation with RO5263397 significantly reduced cocaine-induced dopamine efflux and attenuated D2R-mediated signaling through enhanced receptor internalization. Co-immunoprecipitation studies revealed direct protein-protein interactions between TAAR1 and D2R in striatal tissue. Functional coupling experiments demonstrated that TAAR1 activation inhibited D2R-mediated adenylyl cyclase suppression in a dose-dependent manner. These findings establish that TAAR1 functions as a negative allosteric modulator of D2R signaling and serves as an endogenous brake on cocaine's dopaminergic effects. The TAAR1-D2R interaction represents a novel regulatory mechanism that could be therapeutically targeted for substance use disorders, suggesting TAAR1 agonists may have clinical utility in treating cocaine addiction.

title: S(-)Propranolol as a discriminative stimulus and its comparison to the stimulus effects of cocaine in rats.
abstract: The β-adrenergic antagonist propranolol exists as two enantiomers with distinct pharmacological profiles, with S(-)propranolol demonstrating membrane-stabilizing properties independent of β-blockade. Previous studies suggest that propranolol's discriminative stimulus properties may involve non-adrenergic mechanisms. The present study investigated whether S(-)propranolol could serve as a discriminative stimulus in rats and examined potential cross-generalization with cocaine, given both compounds' membrane-stabilizing effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were trained to discriminate S(-)propranolol (10 mg/kg, IP) from saline in a two-lever operant conditioning paradigm using a FR10 schedule reinforced with food pellets. Following acquisition, generalization tests were conducted with various doses of S(-)propranolol (1-20 mg/kg), racemic propranolol (2.5-15 mg/kg), R(+)propranolol (5-20 mg/kg), and cocaine (2.5-20 mg/kg). Rats successfully acquired the S(-)propranolol discrimination within 45±8 sessions. Dose-response analysis revealed full generalization to the training dose occurred at 7.5-10 mg/kg S(-)propranolol (ED50 = 4.2 mg/kg). Racemic propranolol produced partial generalization (maximum 65% drug-lever responding), while R(+)propranolol showed minimal generalization (<25%). Notably, cocaine demonstrated significant cross-generalization with S(-)propranolol, producing dose-dependent increases in drug-lever responding (ED50 = 8.7 mg/kg) with maximum generalization of 78% at 15 mg/kg. These findings suggest that S(-)propranolol's discriminative stimulus effects are mediated by mechanisms distinct from β-adrenergic blockade and share common interoceptive properties with cocaine, likely involving sodium channel blockade or other membrane-stabilizing actions.

title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
abstract:
**Background:** Ketamine, a non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist, has emerged as a valuable research tool in psychopharmacology for modeling psychotic symptoms and investigating glutamatergic dysfunction in psychiatric disorders. However, concerns regarding its psychiatric safety profile in research settings have limited its broader application. This study systematically evaluated the acute and subacute psychiatric effects of ketamine administration in healthy volunteers and psychiatric populations participating in research protocols.

**Methods:** We conducted a retrospective analysis of 847 ketamine administrations across 12 controlled studies (2003-2006) involving healthy controls (n=312) and patients with schizophrenia (n=89) or mood disorders (n=76). Subanesthetic doses ranged from 0.1-0.5 mg/kg intravenously. Psychiatric symptoms were assessed using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) and Clinician Administered Dissociative States Scale (CADSS) at baseline, peak effect (40 minutes), and follow-up timepoints (24 hours, 1 week).

**Results:** Ketamine produced dose-dependent increases in BPRS positive symptoms (peak increase: 8.3±2.1 points) and CADSS scores (21.4±5.7) that returned to baseline within 2-4 hours. No persistent psychotic symptoms occurred beyond 24 hours in any participant. Psychiatric patients showed similar symptom trajectories but required closer monitoring. Three participants (0.35%) experienced significant anxiety requiring early termination, and one healthy volunteer developed transient panic symptoms. No hospitalizations or serious adverse psychiatric events occurred.

**Conclusions:** These findings support the psychiatric safety of subanesthetic ketamine in controlled research environments with appropriate screening and monitoring protocols. The predictable, time-limited nature of ketamine's psychiatric effects enables its continued use as a valuable neuropharmacological probe while maintaining acceptable risk-benefit ratios for research participants.

title: In-vivo brain dialysis of neurotransmitters.
abstract: Centrally acting drugs interfere with the function of central neurotransmitter systems. This often requires intact neuronal connections and is therefore best studied in vivo. Here, Gaetano Di Chiara describes the technique of brain dialysis, which permits direct in-vivo sampling of neurotransmitters and their metabolites in the brain extracellular fluid. This technique is now providing insight correlating the interaction of drugs with neurotransmission in specific brain areas with their effects on behaviour.

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: More than three decades of research has attempted to elucidate the neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Stimulants, a principle treatment for the disorder, act on the norepinephrine (NE) and dopamine (DA) systems; this has led to a long-standing hypothesis of catecholamine dysfunction in ADHD. Animal studies show a clear role for NE and DA in the modulation of executive functions, which are often disturbed in persons with ADHD. Nonstimulant agents that are effective in the treatment of ADHD tend to affect the NE system, whereas those affecting only DA, or those that affect neither catecholamine, are less potent in reducing ADHD symptoms. Studies of the effects of NE and DA peripheral metabolites by ADHD pharmacotherapies show acute increases in levels of these catecholamines; however, their long-term turnover may be reduced. Imaging studies suggest stimulants increases DA levels in the brain, whereas some animal models of ADHD are more consistent with excessive DA activation in the disorder. Ultimately, ADHD therapy may modify activity in the NE and DA systems to a more optimal level, thus improving responses to environmental stimuli and enhancing working memory and executive function.

title: Are You Ready for Change?
abstract: The implementation of new psychopharmacological interventions in psychiatric care settings requires careful assessment of organizational readiness and staff preparedness. This study examined factors influencing healthcare providers' readiness to adopt evidence-based medication management protocols in community mental health centers. A cross-sectional survey was conducted among 247 nurses, psychiatrists, and mental health technicians across 12 community mental health facilities in the Midwest. Participants completed validated instruments measuring organizational change readiness, knowledge of psychopharmacology principles, attitudes toward medication adherence monitoring, and perceived barriers to implementation. Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, and multiple regression modeling. Results indicated that 68% of participants reported moderate to high readiness for implementing new medication management protocols. Significant predictors of change readiness included years of psychiatric nursing experience (β = 0.34, p < 0.001), organizational support perception (β = 0.42, p < 0.001), and baseline psychopharmacology knowledge scores (β = 0.28, p < 0.01). Primary barriers identified were insufficient training time (73%), concerns about increased workload (61%), and uncertainty about clinical outcomes (45%). Nurses with specialized psychiatric certifications demonstrated significantly higher readiness scores compared to non-certified staff (M = 4.2 vs 3.6, p < 0.05). These findings suggest that successful implementation of psychopharmacological innovations requires targeted educational interventions, adequate organizational support, and consideration of staff experience levels. Mental health administrators should prioritize comprehensive training programs and address identified barriers before implementing new medication management protocols.
journal: Journal of psychosocial nursing and mental health services

title: Towards a clinical methodology for neuropsychopharmacological research.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacological research faces significant methodological challenges that limit the translation of preclinical findings to clinical applications. Current approaches often lack standardized protocols for assessment of cognitive and behavioral endpoints, leading to inconsistent results across studies. We propose a comprehensive clinical methodology framework designed to enhance reliability and validity in neuropsychopharmacological investigations. Our approach integrates standardized neurocognitive test batteries, validated rating scales, and objective biomarkers including electroencephalography and neuroimaging parameters. We evaluated this methodology in a pilot study involving 45 healthy volunteers and 38 patients with major depressive disorder, examining the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors on cognitive function over 8 weeks. The proposed battery demonstrated excellent test-retest reliability (ICC = 0.84-0.92) and sensitivity to drug-induced changes in attention, working memory, and executive function. Significant correlations were observed between subjective mood ratings and objective cognitive measures (r = 0.67, p < 0.001). EEG theta/beta ratios showed predictive value for treatment response (AUC = 0.78). Inter-rater reliability for clinical assessments exceeded 0.90 across all measures. The methodology successfully detected subtle cognitive improvements that emerged after 4 weeks of treatment, preceding clinical mood improvements by approximately 2 weeks. This standardized approach provides a robust framework for future neuropsychopharmacological studies, potentially accelerating drug development and improving clinical outcomes through more precise measurement of therapeutic effects.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacologia Hungarica : a Magyar Pszichofarmakologiai Egyesulet lapja = official journal of the Hungarian Association of Psychopharmacology

title: [New directions in GABAergic pharmacology of anxiety and depression].
abstract: New prospects in the use of drugs affecting central GABAergic system for the treatment of anxiety and depressive states are critically assessed. Both clinical (human) and experimental (animal) data on the mutual influence of various GABA-receptor modulators as well as the interaction of GABAergic system with neurohumoral mechanisms of stress are considered. Based on this analysis, new strategies in the GABAergic pharmacotherapy of anxiety and depression spectrum disorders are outlined.

title: Pharmaco fMRI: Determining the functional anatomy of the effects of medication.
abstract: Recent advances in functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) have enabled the investigation of drug effects on neural circuits in vivo, providing crucial insights into medication mechanisms of action. However, standardized approaches for pharmaco-fMRI studies remain limited. This study aimed to establish methodological guidelines and demonstrate the utility of pharmaco-fMRI in characterizing the functional neuroanatomy of psychoactive medication effects. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study in 24 healthy volunteers (ages 21-35) using a single oral dose of escitalopram (10mg) versus placebo. Participants underwent resting-state and task-based fMRI scanning at baseline and 3 hours post-administration. The task paradigm included emotional face processing and working memory components. We analyzed whole-brain activation patterns, functional connectivity, and region-of-interest analyses focused on limbic and prefrontal circuits. Escitalopram significantly altered BOLD signal in the amygdala during emotional face processing (p<0.001, FWE-corrected) and increased functional connectivity between the anterior cingulate cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex during working memory tasks. Resting-state analyses revealed enhanced default mode network coherence following drug administration compared to placebo (p<0.05, cluster-corrected). Individual differences in drug response correlated with baseline connectivity strength in fronto-limbic circuits (r=0.67, p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that pharmaco-fMRI can reliably detect acute medication effects on neural circuits relevant to psychiatric disorders. This approach offers a powerful translational tool for understanding drug mechanisms, optimizing dosing strategies, and potentially predicting individual treatment responses in clinical populations.

title: Neurochemistry and epileptology.
abstract: This paper gives an account of the global evolution of (neuro-)chemistry in epileptology with an emphasis on the role of the International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE), which declared in its constitution a mission "to make the epilepsy-problem the object of special study and to make practical use of the results of such study." As Epilepsia is the scientific journal of the ILAE, the review emphasizes papers published in the journal.

title: Looking at neurotransmitters in the microscope.
abstract: The visualization of neurotransmitter distribution and dynamics within neural circuits has remained a fundamental challenge in neurobiology, with traditional electrophysiological and biochemical approaches providing limited spatial resolution. Recent advances in fluorescence microscopy and genetically-encoded biosensors have opened new avenues for direct observation of neurotransmitter release and uptake at synaptic terminals. Here we review current methodologies for microscopic detection of major neurotransmitters including glutamate, GABA, dopamine, and acetylcholine in live brain tissue. We examined studies utilizing FM dyes for vesicle tracking, pH-sensitive indicators for exocytosis detection, and newly developed FRET-based sensors that provide real-time measurements of extracellular neurotransmitter concentrations with submicron spatial resolution. Two-photon microscopy has enabled imaging depths of up to 300 μm in acute brain slices, while confocal approaches have achieved temporal resolution below 100 milliseconds for synaptic events. Quantitative analysis reveals that these optical methods can detect neurotransmitter changes in the nanomolar to micromolar range, comparable to physiologically relevant concentrations. Correlation with patch-clamp recordings demonstrates high fidelity between optical signals and synaptic currents (r > 0.85). These microscopic approaches have revealed previously unappreciated heterogeneity in neurotransmitter release probability across individual synapses and have identified novel patterns of extrasynaptic spillover. The integration of optical neurotransmitter detection with cellular resolution represents a paradigm shift toward understanding chemical transmission in intact neural networks, providing unprecedented insights into synaptic plasticity mechanisms and neurotransmitter dysfunction in neurological disorders.

title: In vivo voltammetry: from wire to wireless measurements.
abstract: In vivo voltammetry has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in the living brain, yet traditional wired recording systems impose significant limitations on behavioral studies and chronic measurements. Current cable-tethered approaches restrict animal movement, introduce electrical artifacts, and preclude naturalistic behavioral paradigms. To address these constraints, we developed and validated a novel wireless voltammetry system for continuous monitoring of dopamine release in freely moving rats. The system incorporates a miniaturized potentiostat (2.1 g) with integrated radio frequency transmission capabilities, coupled with carbon fiber microelectrodes implanted in the nucleus accumbens. We compared the performance of wireless recordings with conventional wired fast-scan cyclic voltammetry during pharmacological challenges and behavioral tasks over 14 days post-implantation. Wireless measurements demonstrated equivalent sensitivity (detection limit: 50 nM dopamine) and temporal resolution (100 ms sampling) compared to wired controls, with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 15:1 for stimulated dopamine release. Chronic recordings revealed stable baseline measurements and preserved responses to amphetamine administration (2 mg/kg, i.p.) and electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area. The wireless system eliminated movement artifacts present in tethered recordings and enabled dopamine monitoring during complex social behaviors previously inaccessible with conventional approaches. This wireless voltammetry platform represents a significant methodological advancement for neuropharmacological research, facilitating investigation of neurotransmitter function in naturalistic settings while maintaining the high temporal and chemical resolution essential for mechanistic studies of brain neurochemistry.

title: The engagement of brain cytochrome P450 in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive substrates: a possible role in mental disorders.
abstract: Brain cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes have been increasingly recognized as key regulators of central nervous system homeostasis through their involvement in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive compounds. While peripheral CYP function in drug metabolism is well-established, the role of brain-expressed CYP isoforms in neuropsychiatric pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding brain CYP-mediated metabolism of endogenous substrates including neurosteroids, fatty acid derivatives, and biogenic amines, with particular emphasis on their potential contribution to mental disorders. We systematically analyzed published literature from 1995-2017 examining brain CYP expression, enzymatic activity, and substrate specificity using PubMed and Web of Science databases. Critical evaluation focused on CYP2D6, CYP2E1, CYP46A1, and CYP19A1 isoforms based on their neuronal expression patterns and substrate profiles. Evidence indicates that brain CYP enzymes metabolize cholesterol, arachidonic acid metabolites, neurosteroids such as pregnenolone and allopregnanolone, and catecholamine neurotransmitters. Dysregulation of these metabolic pathways correlates with altered levels of neuroactive metabolites in depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders. CYP46A1-mediated cholesterol hydroxylation influences synaptic plasticity, while CYP19A1 aromatase activity affects local estrogen synthesis and neuroprotection. Genetic polymorphisms in brain-expressed CYP genes show associations with psychiatric phenotypes and treatment responses. These findings suggest that brain CYP enzymes represent previously underappreciated therapeutic targets for mental health disorders, warranting further investigation into their pharmacological modulation as novel treatment strategies.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of novel and selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists: WAY-181187 and WAY-208466.
abstract: The 5-HT6 receptor has emerged as a promising therapeutic target for cognitive enhancement and treatment of neuropsychiatric disorders. We characterized the neuropharmacological profiles of two novel selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists, WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, using in vitro binding assays, functional studies, and behavioral paradigms. Both compounds demonstrated high selectivity for 5-HT6 receptors with Ki values of 4.2 nM (WAY-181187) and 2.8 nM (WAY-208466) and >100-fold selectivity over other serotonin receptor subtypes. Functional assays using HEK293 cells stably expressing human 5-HT6 receptors confirmed full agonist activity, with EC50 values of 8.1 nM and 5.3 nM for WAY-181187 and WAY-208466, respectively. Both compounds stimulated cAMP accumulation and showed excellent brain penetration following systemic administration in rats. In behavioral studies, acute administration of WAY-181187 (3-30 mg/kg, p.o.) and WAY-208466 (1-10 mg/kg, p.o.) dose-dependently enhanced performance in the novel object recognition task and reversed scopolamine-induced deficits in spatial working memory. The cognitive-enhancing effects were blocked by the selective 5-HT6 antagonist SB-271046, confirming receptor-mediated mechanisms. Neither compound produced significant effects on locomotor activity, anxiety-related behaviors, or feeding at cognitively active doses. These findings demonstrate that WAY-181187 and WAY-208466 are potent, selective 5-HT6 receptor agonists with procognitive properties, supporting the therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptor activation for treating cognitive impairments associated with Alzheimer's disease and schizophrenia.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Application of triple-probe microdialysis for fast pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic evaluation of dopamimetic activity of drug candidates in the rat brain.
abstract: Conventional microdialysis techniques for evaluating dopamimetic drug candidates require multiple experiments and extensive time commitments, limiting throughput in preclinical screening. We developed a novel triple-probe microdialysis methodology enabling simultaneous pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic assessment of dopamine-enhancing compounds in awake, freely-moving rats. Three microdialysis probes were stereotaxically implanted: one in the nucleus accumbens shell (pharmacodynamic readout), one in the caudate-putamen (regional comparison), and one in the jugular vein (pharmacokinetic sampling). Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of L-DOPA (100 mg/kg), amphetamine (2 mg/kg), or vehicle. Dialysate samples were collected every 15 minutes for 4 hours and analyzed using HPLC with electrochemical detection. Triple-probe microdialysis successfully demonstrated distinct pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic profiles for both compounds. L-DOPA showed delayed dopamine elevation (peak at 90 min) with preferential accumulation in nucleus accumbens versus caudate-putamen (2.3-fold difference), while amphetamine produced rapid, sustained increases in both regions. Plasma pharmacokinetics revealed L-DOPA's shorter half-life (32 ± 4 min) compared to amphetamine (78 ± 12 min). The triple-probe approach reduced experimental time by 60% compared to sequential single-probe studies while maintaining statistical power. This methodology provides a robust platform for rapid screening of dopamimetic drug candidates, offering simultaneous assessment of brain penetration, regional selectivity, and neurochemical efficacy within individual animals, thereby reducing animal usage while accelerating preclinical drug development timelines.

title: Automated discrimination of psychotropic drugs in mice via computer vision-based analysis.
abstract: We developed an inexpensive computer vision-based method utilizing an algorithm which differentiates drug-induced behavioral alterations. The mice were observed in an open-field arena and their activity was recorded for 100 min. For each animal the first 50 min of observation were regarded as the drug-free period. Each animal was exposed to only one drug and they were injected (i.p.) with either amphetamine or cocaine as the stimulant drugs or morphine or diazepam as the inhibitory agents. The software divided the arena into virtual grids and calculated the number of visits (sojourn counts) to the grids and instantaneous speeds within these grids by analyzing video data. These spatial distributions of sojourn counts and instantaneous speeds were used to construct feature vectors which were fed to the classifier algorithms for the final step of matching the animals and the drugs. The software decided which of the animals were drug-treated at a rate of 96%. The algorithm achieved 92% accuracy in sorting the data according to the increased or decreased activity and then determined which drug was delivered. The method differentiated the type of psychostimulant or inhibitory drugs with a success ratio of 70% and 80%, respectively. This method provides a new way to automatically evaluate and classify drug-induced behaviors in mice.

title: Comparative analysis of inhibitory effects of caged ligands for the NMDA receptor.
abstract: Caged ligands have emerged as powerful tools for studying fast synaptic transmission, yet their potential inhibitory effects on N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors remain poorly characterized. We systematically compared the inhibitory properties of six commonly used caged compounds - caged glutamate (CNB-glutamate), caged glycine (CNB-glycine), caged GABA (CNB-GABA), caged ATP (caged-ATP), caged calcium (NP-EGTA), and caged cyclic AMP (caged-cAMP) - on recombinant NMDA receptors expressed in Xenopus oocytes and native receptors in acute hippocampal slices. Electrophysiological recordings were performed using two-electrode voltage clamp in oocytes and whole-cell patch clamp in CA1 pyramidal neurons. NMDA-evoked currents were measured in the presence of varying concentrations (10-500 μM) of each caged compound before photolysis. CNB-glutamate and CNB-glycine exhibited the strongest inhibitory effects, reducing NMDA currents by 45±8% and 38±6% respectively at 100 μM concentrations (n=12-15 cells, p<0.001). Caged-ATP showed moderate inhibition (22±4%), while NP-EGTA, caged-cAMP, and CNB-GABA produced minimal effects (<10%). The inhibition was voltage-independent and non-competitive, suggesting binding to allosteric sites rather than the glutamate or glycine recognition sites. These inhibitory effects were completely reversible upon washout and were not observed after UV photolysis, confirming that intact caged compounds were responsible for the inhibition. Our findings demonstrate that caged ligands can significantly modulate NMDA receptor function independent of their photolytic products, with important implications for experimental design and data interpretation in studies employing flash photolysis techniques.

title: The neuropsychopharmacology of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) affects approximately 3-7% of school-age children and represents one of the most common neurodevelopmental disorders. While stimulant medications remain first-line treatments, the precise neurobiological mechanisms underlying ADHD pathophysiology and therapeutic response remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge of the neuropsychopharmacological basis of ADHD, examining evidence from neuroimaging, neurochemical, and genetic studies. Converging evidence implicates dysregulation of catecholamine neurotransmission, particularly dopamine and norepinephrine systems, in ADHD symptomatology. Neuroimaging studies demonstrate altered activity in prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, and striatal regions, areas rich in catecholamine innervation and critical for executive function and attention. Dopamine transporter (DAT) and receptor polymorphisms show modest associations with ADHD risk and treatment response. Stimulant medications, including methylphenidate and amphetamines, enhance synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine availability through distinct mechanisms of action on monoamine transporters. Non-stimulant alternatives such as atomoxetine selectively inhibit norepinephrine reuptake, supporting the role of noradrenergic dysfunction in ADHD. Recent findings suggest additional involvement of cholinergic and GABAergic systems in attention regulation. While current pharmacological treatments effectively reduce core symptoms in 70-80% of patients, individual variability in treatment response remains substantial. Future research integrating pharmacogenomic approaches, advanced neuroimaging techniques, and novel therapeutic targets may yield more personalized and effective interventions for this heterogeneous disorder.

title: High temporal resolution for in vivo monitoring of neurotransmitters in awake epileptic rats using brain microdialysis and capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced fluorescence detection.
abstract: Epilepsy is characterized by complex alterations in neurotransmitter dynamics during seizure events, yet conventional analytical methods lack the temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurochemical changes in vivo. We developed an integrated system combining brain microdialysis with capillary electrophoresis and laser-induced fluorescence detection (CE-LIF) to monitor glutamate, GABA, and glycine concentrations with unprecedented temporal resolution in awake, freely-moving epileptic rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with chronic hippocampal electrodes and microdialysis probes following pilocarpine-induced status epilepticus. Microdialysate samples were collected at 30-second intervals and analyzed using a custom-built CE-LIF system with 488-nm argon laser excitation and fluorescein isothiocyanate derivatization. The analytical system achieved detection limits of 0.8 nM for glutamate, 1.2 nM for GABA, and 0.6 nM for glycine, with separation completed within 2 minutes. During spontaneous seizures, glutamate concentrations increased 340±45% above baseline within 60 seconds of electrographic seizure onset, while GABA levels showed a delayed increase of 180±30% at 2-3 minutes post-ictal. Glycine remained relatively stable throughout seizure events. The glutamate/GABA ratio peaked at 4.2±0.8 during ictal periods compared to baseline values of 1.8±0.2. This high-resolution monitoring system provides critical insights into the temporal sequence of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitter release during epileptic seizures and represents a significant advancement in real-time neurochemical analysis for epilepsy research.

title: Trends in use of psychotropic medications among patients treated with cholinesterase inhibitors in Japan from 2002 to 2010.
abstract: The concurrent use of psychotropic medications with cholinesterase inhibitors (ChEIs) in dementia patients has raised concerns about potential drug interactions and adverse effects, yet prescribing patterns remain poorly characterized in Asian populations. This retrospective cohort study examined trends in psychotropic medication use among Japanese dementia patients receiving ChEI therapy between 2002 and 2010. We analyzed prescription data from the Japanese Medical Data Center database, identifying 18,456 patients aged ≥65 years who initiated ChEI treatment (donepezil, rivastigmine, or galantamine).
