Concurrent psychotropic medications were categorized as antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, or hypnotics. Temporal trends were assessed using Cochran-Armitage tests, and logistic regression models identified factors associated with polypharmacy. Overall, 64.2% of patients received at least one psychotropic medication concurrently with ChEIs. Antipsychotic use increased significantly from 28.1% in 2002 to 41.7% in 2010 (p<0.001), while antidepressant use rose from 22.3% to 35.4% (p<0.001). Anxiolytic and hypnotic prescribing remained stable at approximately 45% and 38%, respectively. Atypical antipsychotics comprised 78% of antipsychotic prescriptions by 2010. Factors associated with increased psychotropic use included younger age, male gender, and residence in urban areas. The high prevalence and increasing trend of psychotropic co-prescribing with ChEIs in Japanese dementia patients highlights the need for careful monitoring of potential drug interactions and development of evidence-based prescribing guidelines for this vulnerable population.

title: Neuropharmacological modulation of cognitive deficits after brain damage.
abstract: This review discusses recent studies that have implications for potential neuropharmacological interventions which target cognitive deficits resulting from traumatic brain injury or stroke. An important new study concerning the activity of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors after brain injury reveals that previous influential hypotheses about an increase in glutamate triggering neuronal death may need to be revised. Furthermore, the study suggests that cognitive function may be best preserved by stimulation of NMDA receptors with agonists rather than by the use of antagonists, as previously believed. Investigations of animal models of stroke and traumatic brain injury have further demonstrated the possibility of intervening in the acute and sub-acute stages to protect specific brain systems, such as preservation of the cholinergic system (via cholinesterase inhibitors) and hippocampal neurons (via a D2 agonist). Clinical trials in humans indicate it is also possible to target these neurotransmitter systems to enhance cognitive performance in patients with chronic deficits. In particular, recent studies demonstrated that it is possible to ameliorate the effects of two common cognitive syndromes, visual neglect and aphasia. Cognitive deficits are an extremely common consequence of either traumatic brain injury or stroke. Recent studies demonstrate the potential for using neuropharmacological intervention after acquired brain injury to prevent or ameliorate the effects of cognitive impairments. These treatments, however, are still in their preliminary stages and further research is required to identify the most effective compounds.

title: [Psychopharmacological Studies in Mice].
abstract: Psychoactive compounds continue to pose significant challenges in understanding their mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic applications. This study investigated the behavioral and neurochemical effects of novel synthetic compounds XM-47 and XM-52 in male ICR mice to elucidate their psychopharmacological profiles. Mice (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of test compounds (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0 mg/kg) or vehicle control. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, elevated plus-maze testing, forced swim test, and rotarod performance over 120 minutes post-administration. Brain tissue samples were analyzed for monoamine neurotransmitter levels using high-performance liquid chromatography. XM-47 demonstrated dose-dependent increases in locomotor activity (peak at 1.0 mg/kg, 247% of control, p<0.01) and significant anxiolytic-like effects in the elevated plus-maze (increased open arm time, p<0.05). XM-52 exhibited primarily sedative properties with decreased locomotor activity at higher doses and impaired rotarod performance. Neurochemical analysis revealed XM-47 significantly elevated striatal dopamine levels (168% of control, p<0.01) and reduced serotonin turnover, while XM-52 increased GABA concentrations in the hippocampus and cortex. Neither compound showed antidepressant-like activity in the forced swim test. These findings suggest XM-47 acts primarily through dopaminergic mechanisms with stimulant and anxiolytic properties, whereas XM-52 demonstrates GABAergic activity consistent with its sedative profile. Further investigation into receptor binding specificity and therapeutic potential is warranted.
journal: Yakugaku zasshi : Journal of the Pharmaceutical Society of Japan

title: Psychopharmacology of autistic spectrum disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorders (ASDs) affect approximately 1 in 110 children and are characterized by impaired social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors. While behavioral interventions remain the cornerstone of treatment, pharmacological management of associated symptoms has become increasingly important in clinical practice. This review examines the current evidence base for psychopharmacological interventions in children and adolescents with ASDs. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using PubMed, Cochrane Library, and PsycINFO databases from 1990 to 2011, focusing on randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews of pharmacological treatments in pediatric ASD populations. Key outcome measures included core autism symptoms, associated behavioral problems, and adverse effects. Evidence supports the use of atypical antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, for managing irritability, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors, with both agents receiving FDA approval for these indications in pediatric ASD patients. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show mixed efficacy for repetitive behaviors and anxiety symptoms, with fluoxetine demonstrating some benefit in adolescents. Stimulant medications can effectively treat comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms, though response rates may be lower than in typically developing children. Alpha-2 agonists show promise for hyperactivity and sleep disturbances. However, no medications have demonstrated significant improvement in core social communication deficits. While pharmacological interventions can provide meaningful symptom relief for associated behavioral problems in children and adolescents with ASDs, treatment decisions must carefully weigh potential benefits against risks of adverse effects, with close monitoring essential for optimal outcomes.

title: Volume versus wiring transmission in the brain: a new theoretical frame for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Traditional models of neural communication have emphasized point-to-point synaptic transmission, yet accumulating evidence suggests that neurotransmitter signaling extends beyond the synaptic cleft through volume transmission mechanisms. This theoretical review examines the dual nature of neurochemical communication and its implications for understanding drug action in the central nervous system. We propose a comprehensive framework distinguishing between "wiring transmission" - the classical synaptic model involving rapid, spatially restricted neurotransmitter release - and "volume transmission" - a slower, more diffuse process where neurotransmitters, neuropeptides, and neuromodulators spread through extracellular space to reach distant targets. Analysis of anatomical, physiological, and pharmacological data reveals that many neurotransmitter systems, particularly dopaminergic, serotonergic, and cholinergic pathways, operate through both mechanisms simultaneously. Volume transmission appears especially prominent in subcortical regions and involves specialized anatomical features including mismatched distributions of neurotransmitter release sites and receptor locations. This dual transmission model provides new insights into the therapeutic mechanisms of psychotropic drugs, particularly those affecting monoaminergic systems. The framework helps explain the delayed onset of antidepressant efficacy, the widespread effects of neuroleptics, and the complex dose-response relationships observed with many neuropsychopharmacological agents. Understanding the relative contributions of wiring versus volume transmission may guide the development of more selective therapeutic interventions and advance our comprehension of psychiatric disorder pathophysiology.

title: Investigation of Potential Antioxidant, Thrombolytic and Neuropharmacological Activities of Homalomena aromatica Leaves Using Experimental and In Silico Approaches.
abstract: Homalomena aromatica (Schott) Hook.f. is a traditional medicinal plant widely used in Southeast Asian folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, but its pharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study aimed to evaluate the antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological properties of H. aromatica leaf extracts through combined experimental and computational approaches. Methanol and aqueous extracts were prepared and subjected to phytochemical screening, DPPH radical scavenging assays, and thrombolytic activity evaluation using human blood clots. Neuropharmacological activities were assessed using hole-board, open-field, and forced swimming tests in Swiss albino mice (n=6 per group). Molecular docking studies were performed against key neurological targets including acetylcholinesterase, GABA receptors, and monoamine oxidases. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds. The methanol extract demonstrated significant antioxidant activity (IC₅₀ = 47.32 ± 2.1 μg/mL) compared to ascorbic acid standard. Thrombolytic activity showed 23.4% clot lysis at 200 μg/mL concentration. In behavioral studies, both extracts (200 and 400 mg/kg) exhibited anxiolytic effects in the hole-board test and antidepressant-like activity in the forced swimming test, with the methanol extract showing superior efficacy. Molecular docking revealed strong binding affinities of identified compounds to acetylcholinesterase (-8.2 kcal/mol) and GABA-A receptors (-7.6 kcal/mol). These findings suggest that H. aromatica possesses promising antioxidant, thrombolytic, and neuropharmacological activities, supporting its traditional use and warranting further investigation for developing novel neurotherapeutic agents.

title: Neuropharmacology of 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), Its Metabolites, and Related Analogs.
abstract: 3,4-Methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV) is a synthetic cathinone that emerged as a major constituent of "bath salts" and has been associated with severe psychostimulant toxicity and addiction liability. Despite its classification as a controlled substance, limited research exists on the neuropharmacological properties of MDPV, its metabolites, and structurally related analogs. This study investigated the neurochemical mechanisms underlying MDPV's psychoactive effects through comprehensive in vitro and in vivo analyses. We examined monoamine transporter binding affinities using radioligand competition assays in HEK-293 cells expressing human dopamine (DAT), norepinephrine (NET), and serotonin (SERT) transporters. Microdialysis was performed in freely moving rats to assess extracellular neurotransmitter levels in the nucleus accumbens following acute administration. Additionally, we evaluated the pharmacological profiles of three primary MDPV metabolites and four structural analogs. MDPV demonstrated potent inhibition of DAT (Ki = 4.2 nM) and NET (Ki = 26 nM) with minimal SERT activity (Ki = >10,000 nM). In vivo microdialysis revealed dose-dependent increases in extracellular dopamine (300-800% of baseline) and norepinephrine (200-450% of baseline) lasting 2-4 hours. The primary metabolite 3,4-dihydroxypyrovalerone retained significant DAT affinity (Ki = 89 nM), while structural analogs showed varying potencies correlating with substitution patterns on the aromatic ring. These findings demonstrate that MDPV functions as a highly selective catecholamine reuptake inhibitor, explaining its potent psychostimulant effects and abuse potential. The retained activity of certain metabolites may contribute to MDPV's prolonged behavioral effects and toxicity profile.

title: Commentary: physician-scientist's frustrations fester.
abstract: The declining population of physician-scientists in neuropharmacology research represents a critical threat to translational medicine and drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders. This commentary examines the systemic barriers contributing to career dissatisfaction among physician-scientists in academic neuropharmacology from 2005-2008. We conducted structured interviews with 47 physician-scientists at various career stages across 12 academic medical centers, supplemented by analysis of NIH funding patterns and publication metrics. Participants reported increasing administrative burdens (89%), inadequate protected research time (83%), and insufficient institutional support for translational research (76%) as primary sources of frustration. Clinical revenue pressures resulted in mean research time declining from 65% to 41% over the study period. Early-career physician-scientists expressed particular concern about competing with PhD scientists for limited grant funding while maintaining clinical responsibilities. Senior investigators noted decreased mentorship capacity due to expanding administrative duties. NIH R01 success rates for physician-scientists in neuropharmacology fell from 23% to 17% during this period, compared to relatively stable rates for PhD investigators. Publication output per physician-scientist decreased by 28%, while time from bench discovery to clinical application increased significantly. These findings highlight the urgent need for institutional reforms including enhanced protected time policies, dedicated physician-scientist funding mechanisms, and reduced administrative burdens. Without systematic intervention, the continued exodus of physician-scientists threatens to create an irreversible gap between neuroscience discovery and clinical application, ultimately compromising patient care and therapeutic innovation in neuropsychiatric medicine.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
abstract: The novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines 6a-c and their rigid congeners 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydro-chromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines 7a,b were synthesized in order to obtain dopamine D2-like receptor ligands. The new compounds were evaluated for their in vitro binding affinities, in vivo behavioral activities on rats, and for their effects on rat brain neurochemistry. Compounds 6b (toward both D2 and D3 dopamine receptors) and 7a,b (toward D3 only dopamine receptors) showed the most significant affinities. However none of the new compounds was able to stimulate behavioral activity in non pre-treated rats, nor to influence brain neurochemistry.

title: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology, yesterday, today and tomorrow. A conversation with Michel Hamon.
abstract: For this issue, Luc Zimmer, professor of pharmacology and chair of the Neuropsychopharmacology Committee of the French Society of Pharmacology and Therapeutics, talked with Michel Hamon, honorary director of research at the French National Institute of Health and Medical Research (INSERM) and honorary professor of neuropharmacology at Paris-Sorbonne (Pierre et Marie Curie) University. Some of the leading names in neuropsychopharmacology research are mentioned, pointing to significant conceptual advances that founded this discipline. The links between psychopharmacology and neuropharmacology are also discussed in the light of past collaborations. Finally, priorities are proposed for the emergence of the psychopharmacology of the future.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology: are we ready for a paradigm shift?
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has traditionally relied on monoaminergic hypotheses to explain psychiatric disorders and guide drug development. However, accumulating evidence suggests that current paradigms may be insufficient to address the complexity of neuropsychiatric conditions. This review examines emerging conceptual frameworks that may fundamentally alter our approach to understanding brain-behavior relationships and psychopharmacological interventions. We analyzed recent advances in molecular neurobiology, receptor pharmacology, and clinical psychopharmacology from 1990-1996, focusing on developments that challenge existing theoretical models. Key areas examined include glutamatergic and GABAergic neurotransmission, neuropeptide systems, intracellular signaling cascades, and gene expression regulation. Our analysis reveals several paradigm-shifting developments: the recognition of NMDA receptor hypofunction in schizophrenia, the role of neurotrophic factors in depression, the importance of neuroplasticity in therapeutic responses, and the emergence of pharmacogenomics. Additionally, advances in neuroimaging and electrophysiology have provided new insights into circuit-based dysfunction underlying psychiatric symptoms. Clinical evidence suggests that novel therapeutic targets, including metabotropic glutamate receptors, sigma receptors, and second messenger systems, may offer superior efficacy compared to traditional monoaminergic agents. These findings indicate that the field is approaching a critical juncture where fundamental assumptions about psychiatric pathophysiology and drug action require re-evaluation. We conclude that neuropsychopharmacology is indeed ready for a paradigm shift toward more sophisticated, multi-target approaches that integrate molecular, cellular, and systems-level perspectives. This transition will likely accelerate the development of more effective treatments for neuropsychiatric disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Noradrenergic dysfunction and the psychopharmacology of posttraumatic stress disorder.
abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is characterized by dysregulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems, with growing evidence implicating noradrenergic hyperactivity in core symptom manifestation. While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain first-line pharmacotherapy, their limited efficacy suggests involvement of additional neurochemical pathways. This study examined noradrenergic system alterations in PTSD and evaluated targeted pharmacological interventions. We conducted a comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical literature from 1990-2007, analyzing noradrenergic biomarkers, neuroimaging findings, and treatment response data. Additionally, we performed a 12-week open-label trial of prazosin, an α1-adrenergic antagonist, in 28 veterans with combat-related PTSD experiencing prominent hyperarousal symptoms and sleep disturbances. Literature analysis revealed consistent evidence of elevated norepinephrine levels in cerebrospinal fluid and plasma of PTSD patients, along with altered α2-adrenergic receptor sensitivity and enhanced noradrenergic responsivity to trauma-related stimuli. Neuroimaging studies demonstrated hyperactivation in noradrenergic projection areas including the amygdala and prefrontal cortex. In our clinical trial, prazosin treatment (mean dose 6.8±2.4 mg/day) resulted in significant improvements in Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale scores (baseline 78.3±12.1 vs. endpoint 52.7±15.8, p<0.001), with particularly robust effects on sleep quality and nightmare frequency. Treatment was well-tolerated with minimal adverse effects. These findings support a central role for noradrenergic dysfunction in PTSD pathophysiology and suggest that α1-adrenergic antagonism represents a promising therapeutic strategy, particularly for hyperarousal symptoms resistant to conventional serotonergic interventions.

title: Voluntary wheel running ameliorates depression-like behaviors and brain blood oxygen level-dependent signals in chronic unpredictable mild stress mice.
abstract: Physical exercise has been long recognized for its therapeutic effects on depressive disorders, but the underlying mechanisms remain largely unknown. In the study, we investigated whether the physical exercise by voluntary wheel running (VWR) alters depression-like behaviors and its impact on brain blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signals in mice. Adult male C57BL/6 mice were assigned to one of the following groups; (1) no exercise control (noEx), housed in a standard cage; (2) exercise (Ex), 2h/day in a running wheel apparatus; (3) chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS), which was imitating adult stress; and (4) CUMS+Ex. The differences in functional brain changes were determined by BOLD functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI). The results showed that VWR exercise significantly reversed the CUMS-induced behavioral abnormalities. Base on the fMRI amplitude of low-frequency fluctuation (ALFF) analysis, we found that VWR exercise could restore the CUMS-induced excessive BOLD activation in parts of limbic system, such as cortex, hippocampus and corpus callosum. Furthermore, CUMS-induced BOLD suppressive regions were also partially attenuated by VWR exercise, such as amygdala, cerebellum anterior lobe, thalamus, midbrain, and pontine. Most of these regions are involved in mood-regulating circuit, suggesting dysfunction of the circuit in CUMS model of depression, and VWR exercise could adjust the mood-regulating circuit. These results suggested that VWR exercise ameliorated depression-like behaviors and brain BOLD signals in CUMS induced depression mice.

title: Regional difference in stainability with calcium-sensitive acetoxymethyl-ester probes in mouse brain slices.
abstract: Loading neurons with membrane permeable Ca2+ indicators is a core experimental procedure in functional multineuron Ca2+ imaging (fMCI), an optical technique for monitoring multiple neuronal activities. Although fMCI has been applied to several brain networks, including cerebral cortex, hippocampus, and cerebellum, no studies have systematically addressed the dye-loading efficiency in different brain regions. Here, we describe the stainability of Oregon Green 488 BAPTA-1AM in mouse acute brain slice preparations. The data are suggestive of the potential usability of fMCI in many brain regions, including olfactory bulb, thalamus, dentate gyrus, habenular nucleus, and pons.

title: Astra Award Lecture. The psychopharmacology of 5-HT3 receptors.
abstract: The 5-HT3 receptor represents a unique member of the serotonin receptor family, distinguished by its ligand-gated ion channel structure rather than the G-protein coupling characteristic of other 5-HT receptor subtypes. Recent advances in molecular pharmacology have enabled detailed characterization of this receptor's distribution, function, and therapeutic potential. This review synthesizes current knowledge of 5-HT3 receptor psychopharmacology based on radioligand binding studies, electrophysiological recordings, and behavioral pharmacology in rodent models. 5-HT3 receptors demonstrate highest density in limbic structures including the hippocampus and amygdala, with moderate expression in cortical areas and brainstem nuclei involved in emesis and autonomic control. Selective antagonists such as ondansetron and tropisetron exhibit nanomolar affinity and demonstrate anxiolytic-like effects in conflict paradigms, reduce ethanol consumption in voluntary drinking models, and block chemotherapy-induced emesis in ferret studies. Conversely, 5-HT3 receptor agonists including 2-methyl-5-HT produce anxiogenic responses and facilitate dopamine release in mesolimbic pathways. Electrophysiological studies reveal that 5-HT3 activation produces rapid membrane depolarization through sodium and calcium influx, particularly in GABAergic interneurons. These findings suggest 5-HT3 receptors modulate anxiety, addiction, and emetic responses through distinct neural circuits. The development of highly selective 5-HT3 antagonists represents a promising therapeutic approach for anxiety disorders, substance abuse, and chemotherapy-induced nausea, warranting continued investigation of this receptor system's clinical potential.

title: Transport mechanisms governing serotonin clearance in vivo revealed by high-speed chronoamperometry.
abstract: The serotonin transporter (SERT) is the primary mechanism for terminating serotonergic neurotransmission and represents a major target for antidepressant drugs. However, the kinetics of serotonin clearance in living brain tissue remain poorly characterized due to technical limitations in measuring rapid neurotransmitter dynamics. We employed high-speed chronoamperometry with carbon fiber microelectrodes to directly monitor serotonin clearance in the CA3 region of hippocampal slices from adult Sprague-Dawley rats. Serotonin (1-10 μM) was applied by pressure ejection while recording oxidation currents at 10 ms intervals. Clearance kinetics were analyzed using Michaelis-Menten modeling under control conditions and following pharmacological manipulations. Under control conditions, serotonin clearance followed saturable kinetics with an apparent Km of 1.8 ± 0.3 μM and Vmax of 0.45 ± 0.08 μM/s. The selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor citalopram (1 μM) reduced clearance rate by 78 ± 6%, while the norepinephrine transporter blocker desipramine had no significant effect. The non-selective monoamine uptake inhibitor cocaine (10 μM) inhibited clearance by 85 ± 4%. Removal of extracellular Na+ or addition of the metabolic inhibitor 2,4-dinitrophenol significantly impaired serotonin clearance, confirming dependence on active transport mechanisms. These findings demonstrate that SERT-mediated uptake is the dominant mechanism governing serotonin clearance kinetics in hippocampal tissue, with minimal contribution from other transporters. This high-temporal resolution approach provides a valuable tool for investigating serotonergic dysfunction and antidepressant drug action in real-time.

title: Serial neurochemical measurement of cerebrospinal fluid during the human sexual response cycle.
abstract: The neurochemical basis of human sexual response remains poorly understood due to methodological limitations in studying central nervous system dynamics during sexual arousal. Previous animal studies have implicated dopamine, oxytocin, and endogenous opioids in sexual behavior, but direct measurement in humans has been limited to peripheral samples or post-coital assessments. We developed a novel protocol to serially sample cerebrospinal fluid during controlled sexual stimulation in healthy volunteers. Eight participants (4 male, 4 female, ages 22-34) underwent lumbar catheter placement under local anesthesia, followed by standardized visual and tactile sexual stimulation while monitoring physiological arousal markers. CSF samples (2 ml) were collected at baseline, during early arousal, at peak arousal, and during resolution phases. High-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection was used to quantify dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites. Oxytocin and β-endorphin levels were measured using radioimmunoassay. Dopamine concentrations increased 340% from baseline during peak arousal (p<0.001), while norepinephrine showed biphasic elevation during arousal and resolution phases. Oxytocin levels rose significantly in all participants during peak arousal (280% increase, p<0.01), with higher absolute concentrations in females. β-endorphin demonstrated delayed elevation during the resolution phase. Serotonin levels remained relatively stable throughout the response cycle. These findings provide the first direct neurochemical evidence of dopaminergic and oxytocinergic activation during human sexual response, supporting translational relevance of animal models and offering insights into potential therapeutic targets for sexual dysfunction.

title: Future treatments for Parkinson's disease: surfing the PD pipeline.
abstract: Current therapeutic approaches for Parkinson's disease (PD) remain largely symptomatic, with long-term dopaminergic therapy complicated by motor fluctuations and dyskinesias. This review examines emerging therapeutic strategies currently in preclinical and clinical development pipelines. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of novel PD therapeutics from 2008-2011, evaluating mechanism-based approaches including neuroprotective agents, disease-modifying therapies, and alternative neurotransmitter system modulators. Database searches of ClinicalTrials.gov, PubMed, and pharmaceutical industry reports identified 47 compounds in various development phases. Neuroprotective strategies represent the largest category, with GDNF gene therapy, rasagiline combination protocols, and mitochondrial-targeted antioxidants showing promise in Phase II trials. Novel dopaminergic approaches include continuous subcutaneous apomorphine delivery systems and modified-release levodopa formulations demonstrating improved motor control in clinical studies. Non-dopaminergic targets have emerged as particularly promising, with adenosine A2A receptor antagonists (istradefylline) and glutamate modulators (safinamide) advancing to Phase III development. Gene therapy approaches using viral vectors for aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase and neurturin delivery have shown preliminary efficacy in small patient cohorts. Additionally, alpha-synuclein immunotherapy and glucocerebrosidase enhancement represent innovative disease-modifying strategies entering clinical evaluation. Despite challenges in translating preclinical neuroprotection to clinical benefit, the current PD pipeline demonstrates unprecedented diversity in mechanistic approaches. These emerging therapies offer potential for addressing unmet clinical needs including disease progression, non-motor symptoms, and treatment-related complications, suggesting a paradigm shift toward personalized, mechanism-based PD therapeutics within the next decade.

title: Rats can learn a temporal task in a single session.
abstract: Temporal processing is fundamental to adaptive behavior, yet the neural mechanisms underlying rapid acquisition of timing tasks remain poorly understood. Previous studies have shown that rodents can learn to time intervals over multiple training sessions, but whether rats can acquire temporal discrimination within a single session and the neurochemical basis of such rapid learning has not been systematically investigated. Here, we examined the ability of male Wistar rats (n=24) to learn a peak-interval timing task within a single 90-minute session using a novel fixed-interval 20-second schedule with unpredictable probe trials. Additionally, we investigated the role of dopaminergic signaling by administering either saline or haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes prior to training. Behavioral performance was assessed using peak time, peak rate, and coefficient of variation measures. Results demonstrated that control rats rapidly acquired temporal control, showing significant peak timing behavior within the first 30 minutes of training (p<0.001). Peak times averaged 19.8±2.1 seconds by session end, with coefficient of variation decreasing from 0.45 to 0.23 across the session. Haloperidol-treated animals showed significantly impaired acquisition, with delayed emergence of temporal control and higher variability throughout training (p<0.01). These findings reveal that rats possess remarkable capacity for rapid temporal learning within a single session and highlight the critical role of dopaminergic neurotransmission in the acquisition of timing behavior. This rapid learning paradigm provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms of interval timing.

title: Current state of the use of neuroimaging techniques to understand and alter appetite control in humans.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying appetite regulation have become increasingly accessible through advanced neuroimaging techniques, offering unprecedented insights into human feeding behavior and potential therapeutic targets. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), positron emission tomography (PET), and single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) have emerged as powerful tools to investigate the complex interplay between homeostatic and hedonic appetite control systems. This review synthesizes recent evidence from neuroimaging studies examining appetite-regulating brain circuits and interventions designed to modulate these networks. Key findings demonstrate that the hypothalamus, insula, orbitofrontal cortex, and striatum show distinct activation patterns in response to food cues, nutritional states, and pharmacological interventions. Resting-state connectivity analyses reveal altered network dynamics in obesity and eating disorders, particularly involving default mode and salience networks. Recent intervention studies utilizing real-time fMRI neurofeedback, transcranial magnetic stimulation, and deep brain stimulation show promising results in modulating appetite-related brain activity. Pharmacological neuroimaging studies of GLP-1 agonists, naltrexone-bupropion combinations, and novel neuropeptide targets demonstrate measurable changes in central appetite circuits that correlate with clinical outcomes. However, significant methodological challenges remain, including standardization of hunger states, food stimuli, and analysis approaches across studies. Future directions include the development of personalized neuroimaging biomarkers for treatment selection and the integration of multimodal imaging approaches with metabolic measurements to better understand individual differences in appetite control mechanisms.
journal: Current opinion in clinical nutrition and metabolic care

title: Integral methods for automatic quantification of fast-scan-cyclic-voltammetry detected neurotransmitters.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as the gold standard for real-time detection of neurotransmitter release in brain tissue, yet current quantification methods rely heavily on manual peak identification and baseline correction, introducing operator bias and limiting throughput. Existing automated approaches often fail to accurately detect transient dopamine signals in noisy biological recordings, particularly during complex behavioral paradigms. We developed a novel integral-based computational framework for automated FSCV signal quantification that leverages continuous wavelet transforms and machine learning algorithms to identify and quantify dopamine release events. Our method was validated using synthetic datasets with known signal characteristics and tested on ex vivo brain slice recordings from rat nucleus accumbens (n=8) and in vivo recordings from freely-moving mice during reward-seeking behavior (n=12). The integral-based approach demonstrated superior performance compared to traditional peak-detection methods, achieving 94.3% accuracy in synthetic data validation and significantly reducing false positive rates (2.1% vs 8.7%, p<0.001) in biological recordings. Quantification precision improved by 35% compared to manual analysis, with inter-rater reliability increasing from r=0.76 to r=0.94. Processing time was reduced from 45 minutes to 3 minutes per dataset. The algorithm successfully detected dopamine transients with amplitudes as low as 50 nM and temporal resolution of 100 ms, while maintaining robust performance across different electrode types and experimental conditions. This automated quantification framework represents a significant advancement in FSCV data analysis, enabling high-throughput screening applications and reducing analytical bias in neurochemical measurements.

title: Chapter 11 - Solid lipid nanoparticles for brain tumors therapy: State of the art and novel challenges.
abstract: The treatment of brain tumors remains one of the most challenging aspects of oncology due to the blood-brain barrier (BBB) limiting drug penetration and the need for targeted delivery to minimize systemic toxicity. Solid lipid nanoparticles (SLNs) have emerged as promising drug delivery systems that can enhance brain bioavailability of therapeutic agents while reducing adverse effects. This review examines current developments in SLN-based drug delivery systems for brain tumor therapy and identifies key challenges for clinical translation. We analyzed recent literature on SLN formulations, surface modifications, and targeting strategies for brain tumor treatment, focusing on studies published between 2005-2009. Various approaches for BBB penetration have been investigated, including receptor-mediated transcytosis using transferrin and lactoferrin conjugation, adsorptive-mediated transcytosis through cationic surface modifications, and disruption of tight junctions. SLNs loaded with chemotherapeutic agents such as doxorubicin, paclitaxel, and temozolomide have demonstrated enhanced brain accumulation and improved therapeutic efficacy in preclinical models compared to conventional formulations. Surface functionalization with polyethylene glycol has shown to prolong circulation time and reduce recognition by the reticuloendothelial system. However, several challenges remain, including optimization of particle size and surface properties for maximum BBB permeability, prevention of drug leakage during storage and circulation, and scale-up manufacturing processes. Additionally, limited understanding of SLN biodistribution and long-term safety profiles necessitates comprehensive toxicological evaluation. Future research should focus on developing multifunctional SLNs combining therapeutic and diagnostic capabilities for personalized brain tumor therapy.

title: New vistas in immunopsychopharmacology.
abstract: The emerging field of immunopsychopharmacology represents a paradigm shift in understanding the complex interactions between the immune system, central nervous system, and psychoactive drugs. Growing evidence suggests that traditional psychopharmacological agents may exert therapeutic effects partially through modulation of immune function, while immune mediators reciprocally influence neurochemical processes underlying psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes current knowledge from both clinical and preclinical studies examining bidirectional neuroimmune communication in the context of psychotropic drug action. We analyzed data from recent investigations utilizing cytokine measurements, lymphocyte proliferation assays, and neuroendocrine challenge tests in patients receiving antidepressants, antipsychotics, and anxiolytics. Additionally, we examined animal studies employing immune stimulation models and psychopharmacological interventions. Results demonstrate that selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants significantly alter interleukin-1β, tumor necrosis factor-α, and interleukin-6 levels in depressed patients, correlating with clinical improvement. Antipsychotic medications modulate T-helper cell responses and natural killer cell activity in schizophrenic patients. Benzodiazepines influence immunoglobulin production and complement activation. Conversely, immune challenges with lipopolysaccharide or interleukin-1 administration in animal models produce depression-like behaviors reversible by antidepressant treatment. These findings suggest that immunomodulation may represent a novel therapeutic target for psychiatric disorders. Understanding neuroimmune mechanisms could lead to development of more effective treatments combining traditional psychopharmacological approaches with immunotherapeutic strategies, potentially revolutionizing psychiatric medicine in the next millennium.

title: Distribution and Neurochemistry of the Porcine Ileocaecal Valve Projecting Sensory Neurons in the Dorsal Root Ganglia and the Influence of Lipopolysaccharide from Different Serotypes of Salmonella spp. on the Chemical Coding of DRG Neurons in the Cell Cultures.
abstract: The ileocecal valve (ICV)-a sphincter muscle between small and large intestine-plays important roles in the physiology of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, but many aspects connected with the innervation of the ICV remain unknown. Thus, the aim of this study was to investigate the localization and neurochemical characterization of neurons located in the dorsal root ganglia and supplying the ICV of the domestic pig. The results have shown that such neurons mainly located in the dorsal root ganglia (DRG) of thoracic and lumbar neuromers show the presence of substance P (SP), calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), and galanin (GAL). The second part of the experiment consisted of a study on the influence of a low dose of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Salmonella serotypes Enteritidis Minnesota and Typhimurium on DRG neurons. It has been shown that the LPS of these serotypes in studied doses does not change the number of DRG neurons in the cell cultures, but influences the immunoreactivity to SP and GAL. The observed changes in neurochemical characterization depend on the bacterial serotype. The results show that DRG neurons take part in the innervation of the ICV and may change their neurochemical characterization under the impact of LPS, which is probably connected with direct actions of this substance on the nervous tissue and/or its pro-inflammatory activity.

title: Advancing equity, diversity, and inclusion in the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP): advances, challenges, and opportunities to accelerate progress.
abstract: The American College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ACNP) has historically faced challenges in achieving equitable representation across gender, racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic dimensions within its membership and leadership structure. To assess current diversity metrics and identify barriers to inclusion, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of ACNP membership demographics, leadership composition, and meeting participation patterns from 2015-2020. We surveyed 1,247 members regarding perceived barriers to advancement and collected demographic data on annual meeting speakers, award recipients, and council members. Additionally, we analyzed citation patterns and publication metrics across demographic groups to examine potential disparities in career advancement opportunities. Our analysis revealed significant underrepresentation of women (32% of membership), racial minorities (18% combined), and early-career investigators (23%) relative to the broader neuroscience workforce. Leadership positions showed even greater disparities, with women holding 28% of council positions and racial minorities comprising only 12% of committee chairs over the study period. Survey responses identified key barriers including limited mentorship opportunities, geographic constraints, financial barriers to meeting attendance, and perceived "old boys' network" dynamics. However, targeted initiatives implemented since 2018, including mentorship programs, travel awards, and structured networking events, demonstrated measurable improvements in diversity metrics. These findings highlight the need for sustained, multi-faceted approaches to advance equity in neuropsychopharmacology, including policy reforms, resource allocation, and cultural transformation to ensure the field benefits from diverse perspectives and talent.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Improving the reproducibility of hydrogel-coated glutamate microsensors by using an automated dipcoater.
abstract: Hydrogel-coated microsensors based on carbon fiber electrodes (CFEs) are promising tools for in vivo analysis of endogeneous compounds such as glutamate. However, their construction generally depends on manual fabrication, which often results in poor reproducibility. The aim of this study was to improve the reproducibility and performance of glutamate microsensors. CFEs (10 microm diameter, 300-500 microm long) were coated with a cross-linked redox-polymer hydrogel containing l-glutamate oxidase, horseradish peroxidase and ascorbate oxidase. Various parameters that are likely to influence the reproducibility of the glutamate microsensors were studied. It appeared that the most crucial step in determining the microsensor performance is the manual hydrogel-application procedure. To control this procedure an automated dipcoater was constructed, which allowed mechanical application of the hydrogel on the CFE under standardized conditions. Significant improvements in performance were seen when the CFEs were dipcoated for 10 min at 37 degrees C. Further improvements were obtained when the automated hydrogel application was combined with other cross-link methods, such as electrodeposition and electrostatic complexation. A crucial factor in determining the microsensor performance is the hydrogel thickness. Microscopic observations revealed that, despite the use of an automated dipcoater, the layer thickness was not constant. By combining the automated dipcoat technique with amperometry, the layer thickness could be indirectly monitored and controlled, which resulted in significant improvements of the reproducibility of the sensors.

title: The roles of dopamine and serotonin in decision making: evidence from pharmacological experiments in humans.
abstract: Decision-making processes involve complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems, with dopamine and serotonin playing particularly crucial roles. While dopamine has been implicated in reward prediction and reinforcement learning, serotonin's contribution to decision-making remains less well characterized. We investigated the distinct and interactive effects of dopaminergic and serotonergic manipulations on human decision-making using a double-blind, placebo-controlled, within-subjects pharmacological approach. Healthy volunteers (n=24) completed a probabilistic reversal learning task and temporal discounting paradigm under four conditions: placebo, the dopamine D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (2mg), the selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor citalopram (20mg), and combined haloperidol-citalopram administration. Haloperidol significantly impaired participants' ability to adapt to reward contingency reversals and increased preference for immediate over delayed rewards, consistent with dopamine's role in reward prediction error signaling and temporal discounting. Citalopram enhanced punishment sensitivity and reduced impulsive choice behavior, supporting serotonin's involvement in behavioral inhibition and impulse control. Notably, combined administration revealed opposing effects: citalopram partially rescued haloperidol-induced deficits in reversal learning while haloperidol attenuated citalopram's effects on punishment sensitivity. These findings demonstrate dissociable roles for dopamine and serotonin in human decision-making, with dopamine primarily mediating reward-based learning and temporal preferences, while serotonin modulates response to aversive outcomes and impulsivity. The antagonistic interactions between these systems suggest that balanced dopaminergic-serotonergic function is critical for optimal decision-making, with implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by decision-making abnormalities.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Effects of triiodothyronine and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity in rat brain: regional differences.
abstract: Thyroid hormones have been implicated in the modulation of serotonergic neurotransmission and may enhance the therapeutic efficacy of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in treating depression. However, the regional specificity of thyroid hormone effects on serotonin autoreceptor function remains poorly understood. We investigated the combined effects of triiodothyronine (T3) and fluoxetine on 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptor activity across different brain regions in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received daily injections of T3 (25 μg/kg, i.p.), fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), or their combination for 14 days. Autoreceptor function was assessed using in vivo microdialysis coupled with local perfusion of selective agonists 8-OH-DPAT (5-HT1A) and CP-93129 (5-HT1B) in the dorsal raphe nucleus, hippocampus, and frontal cortex. Extracellular 5-HT levels were measured by HPLC with electrochemical detection. Fluoxetine alone produced modest desensitization of 5-HT1A autoreceptors in all regions examined, while 5-HT1B autoreceptor sensitivity remained unchanged. T3 monotherapy had minimal effects on autoreceptor function. However, combined T3 and fluoxetine treatment resulted in marked desensitization of both 5-HT1A and 5-HT1B autoreceptors, with the most pronounced effects observed in the dorsal raphe nucleus and frontal cortex compared to hippocampus. The regional heterogeneity in autoreceptor desensitization correlated with differential expression of thyroid hormone receptors. These findings suggest that T3 enhances fluoxetine-induced autoreceptor desensitization in a region-specific manner, providing mechanistic insight into thyroid hormone augmentation strategies in antidepressant therapy.

title: Serotonin receptors of type 6 (5-HT6): what can we expect from them?
abstract: Serotonin 5-HT6 receptors represent one of the most recently discovered members of the serotonin receptor family and have emerged as promising therapeutic targets for cognitive disorders and neuropsychiatric conditions. Despite their restricted distribution primarily in the central nervous system, particularly in brain regions associated with learning and memory such as the hippocampus, cortex, and striatum, their precise physiological roles remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding 5-HT6 receptor pharmacology, distribution, and therapeutic potential based on preclinical and early clinical findings through 2011. 5-HT6 receptors are positively coupled to adenylyl cyclase and exhibit high constitutive activity, distinguishing them from other serotonin receptor subtypes. Accumulating evidence suggests these receptors modulate cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission, positioning them as important regulators of cognitive function. Selective 5-HT6 receptor antagonists, including SB-271046, SB-399885, and Ro 04-6790, have demonstrated procognitive effects in various animal models of learning and memory, including novel object recognition, Morris water maze, and attentional set-shifting tasks. Additionally, these compounds show promise in models of Alzheimer's disease, schizophrenia, and obesity. Early clinical trials with 5-HT6 antagonists have yielded mixed but encouraging results in cognitive enhancement and weight reduction. The unique pharmacological profile and therapeutic potential of 5-HT6 receptors suggest they may offer novel approaches for treating cognitive impairment associated with aging, dementia, and psychiatric disorders, warranting continued investigation and development of selective modulators.

title: Subcellular probes for neurochemical recording from multiple brain sites.
abstract: Dysregulation of neurochemicals, in particular, dopamine, is epitomized in numerous debilitating disorders that impair normal movement and mood aspects of our everyday behavior. Neurochemical transmission is a neuron-specific process, and further exhibits region-specific signaling in the brain. Tools are needed to monitor the heterogeneous spatiotemporal dynamics of dopamine neurotransmission without compromising the physiological processes of the neuronal environment. We developed neurochemical probes that are ten times smaller than any existing dopamine sensor, based on the size of the entire implanted shaft and its sensing tip. The microfabricated probe occupies a spatial footprint (9 μm) coordinate with the average size of individual neuronal cells (∼10 μm). These cellular-scale probes were shown to reduce inflammatory response of the implanted brain tissue environment. The probes are further configured in the form of a microarray to permit electrochemical sampling of dopamine and other neurotransmitters at unprecedented spatial densities and distributions. Dopamine recording was performed concurrently from up to 16 sites in the striatum of rats, revealing a remarkable spatiotemporal contrast in dopamine transmission as well as site-specific pharmacological modulation. Collectively, the reported platform endeavors to enable high density mapping of the chemical messengers fundamentally involved in neuronal communication through the use of minimally invasive probes that help preserve the neuronal viability of the implant environment.

title: A conversation with Paul Greengard. Interview by Eric J Nestler.
abstract: This interview with Nobel laureate Paul Greengard provides a comprehensive retrospective on five decades of pioneering research in neuropharmacology and signal transduction. Dr. Greengard discusses his transformative discovery that neurotransmitters function through second messenger systems involving protein phosphorylation, fundamentally changing our understanding of synaptic transmission from a purely electrical to a biochemical process. He recounts the early challenges in establishing the role of cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) and protein kinase A in neuronal signaling, work that initially faced significant skepticism from the neuroscience community in the 1970s. The conversation explores his laboratory's subsequent identification of DARPP-32 (dopamine- and cAMP-regulated phosphoprotein of 32 kDa) as a key integrator of dopaminergic and glutamatergic signaling in striatal neurons, elucidating mechanisms underlying psychiatric disorders and drug addiction. Dr. Greengard reflects on the evolution of neuropharmacological research methods, from early biochemical approaches to modern molecular and genetic techniques, emphasizing how technological advances enabled increasingly sophisticated investigations of neuronal signaling cascades. He discusses the clinical implications of his fundamental discoveries, particularly for developing treatments for Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and depression. The interview also addresses the importance of mentorship in scientific careers and the challenges of translating basic neuroscience research into therapeutic applications. This retrospective offers valuable insights into the intellectual development of modern neuropharmacology and highlights the critical role of protein phosphorylation in virtually all aspects of neuronal function and dysfunction.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: From the search for a molecular code of memory to the role of neurotransmitters: a historical perspective.
abstract: The molecular basis of memory formation has been a central pursuit in neuroscience for over five decades, evolving from early theories of protein synthesis to our current understanding of neurotransmitter-mediated synaptic plasticity. This historical review examines the paradigmatic shifts in memory research from 1950 to 2004, analyzing key experimental findings and theoretical frameworks that shaped the field. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of landmark studies, tracing the progression from Hyden's early RNA hypothesis through Kandel's work on Aplysia to contemporary investigations of NMDA receptor-dependent long-term potentiation. The search for a discrete "molecular code" of memory initially focused on stable protein modifications and RNA templates as information storage mechanisms. However, accumulating evidence revealed that memory consolidation involves dynamic, network-based processes mediated by neurotransmitter systems rather than static molecular engrams. Critical discoveries include the identification of CREB as a transcriptional switch for memory formation, the role of protein kinase A in synaptic strengthening, and the requirement for de novo protein synthesis during memory consolidation. The field has transitioned from reductionist models seeking single molecular determinants to systems-level approaches emphasizing neurotransmitter-receptor interactions, particularly glutamatergic and cholinergic signaling cascades. These findings demonstrate that memory formation emerges from coordinated neurotransmitter-mediated changes in synaptic efficacy rather than discrete molecular codes, fundamentally reshaping our understanding of neural plasticity mechanisms and providing new therapeutic targets for memory-related disorders.

title: [Regulatory molecular mechanisms of the neurochemical processes. History and the present time].
abstract: The understanding of regulatory molecular mechanisms governing neurochemical processes has evolved dramatically over the past century, from early observations of synaptic transmission to contemporary insights into complex signaling cascades. This review examines the historical development and current state of knowledge regarding molecular regulation in neurotransmission, focusing on key discoveries that have shaped modern neuropharmacology. We analyzed seminal publications from 1900-2003, tracing the progression from Dale's principle through the identification of neurotransmitter receptors, second messenger systems, and gene expression regulation. Historical analysis reveals three major evolutionary phases: the classical period (1900-1950) characterized by neurotransmitter identification, the receptor era (1950-1980) marked by pharmacological classification of binding sites, and the molecular biology revolution (1980-present) enabling detailed characterization of signaling mechanisms. Contemporary research demonstrates that neurochemical regulation involves intricate networks of G-protein coupled receptors, ion channels, protein kinases, and transcription factors operating across multiple temporal scales. Key regulatory mechanisms include presynaptic autoreceptor feedback, postsynaptic receptor desensitization, synaptic plasticity through CREB-mediated gene expression, and glial-neuronal interactions. Recent advances in molecular biology techniques have revealed previously unknown complexity in neurotransmitter systems, including receptor heteromerization, epigenetic modifications, and non-coding RNA regulation. Understanding these multilevel regulatory mechanisms provides crucial insights for developing targeted therapeutic interventions in neurological and psychiatric disorders, representing a paradigm shift from traditional pharmacological approaches toward precision neuromedicine based on molecular pathophysiology.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of an atypical antipsychotic, NRA0562.
abstract: The therapeutic limitations of conventional antipsychotics and the need for improved atypical agents with reduced side effect profiles have prompted continued investigation of novel dopaminergic compounds. NRA0562, a benzisoxazole derivative, was evaluated for its neuropharmacological properties as a potential atypical antipsychotic agent. Radioligand binding studies were performed using rat brain membranes to determine receptor affinity profiles. Behavioral assays included apomorphine-induced climbing, haloperidol-induced catalepsy, and prepulse inhibition tests in male Sprague-Dawley rats. Extracellular dopamine and serotonin levels were measured using in vivo microdialysis in the nucleus accumbens and medial prefrontal cortex. NRA0562 demonstrated high affinity for dopamine D2 (Ki = 2.1 nM) and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 0.8 nM), with a 5-HT2A/D2 ratio of 0.38. Moderate affinity was observed for α1-adrenergic (Ki = 15.3 nM) and histamine H1 receptors (Ki = 21.7 nM). The compound effectively blocked apomorphine-induced climbing (ED50 = 0.24 mg/kg, p.o.) while producing minimal catalepsy at antipsychotic doses. NRA0562 significantly improved prepulse inhibition deficits induced by apomorphine (p < 0.01). Microdialysis studies revealed preferential increases in cortical dopamine release compared to striatal regions, with concurrent elevation of prefrontal serotonin levels. These findings suggest that NRA0562 exhibits a favorable neuropharmacological profile characteristic of atypical antipsychotics, with potential advantages in treating both positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia while minimizing extrapyramidal side effects. Further preclinical and clinical evaluation is warranted.

title: Succi nervorum: a brief history of neurochemistry.
abstract: The study of neurochemistry has evolved dramatically since the early anatomical descriptions of nervous tissue, yet the historical development of our understanding of neural chemical processes remains poorly documented in contemporary literature. This review examines the progression of neurochemical research from the initial observations of "succi nervorum" (nerve juices) in the 18th century through the establishment of modern neurotransmitter theory. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of primary historical sources, including original manuscripts, laboratory notebooks, and published communications from 1750 to 1970, supplemented by bibliometric analysis of key publications in neurochemistry. Our investigation reveals three distinct phases in neurochemical discovery: the pre-electrical period (1750-1850) characterized by humoral theories of nerve function, the electrophysiological era (1850-1920) dominated by purely electrical models of neural transmission, and the modern chemical transmission period (1920-1970) establishing neurotransmitter mechanisms. Critical developments included du Bois-Reymond's early speculation about chemical transmission in 1877, Elliott's recognition of sympathetic nerve-adrenergic relationships in 1904, and Dale's definitive acetylcholine studies beginning in 1914. The transition from vague concepts of "nerve essences" to precise molecular mechanisms required fundamental advances in analytical chemistry, electrophysiology, and pharmacology. Understanding this historical progression illuminates current challenges in neuropsychopharmacology and provides context for emerging fields such as neuroproteomics and metabolomics, suggesting that historical perspective remains essential for interpreting contemporary neurochemical research paradigms.

title: Using a rate-frequency curve method to assess the rewarding properties of morphine in the intracranial self-stimulation paradigm in rats.
abstract: Intracranial self-stimulation (ICSS) is a sophisticated paradigm that can be used to assess the rewarding properties of drugs of abuse such as cocaine and amphetamine. Initial studies using this method to assess brain stimulation reward (BSR) examined the enhancement of response rates after drug administration. In the mid to late 1980s several groups began implementing paradigms to assess the direct rewarding effects of drugs of abuse that, for the most part, are independent of rate (i.e., progressive-ratio, auto-titration, rate-frequency curve), providing a representative assessment of a drugs rewarding effects. However, some drugs such as morphine and ethanol, which are known to have abuse potential, have sedative effects that may impede the ability to accurately quantify rewarding effects, even in these rate-independent paradigms. Few studies to date report effects of morphine in the ICSS paradigm and those that do appear to be inconsistent, lack robustness, have not been reproducible by other groups, or require inconvenient experimental designs. Here, we demonstrate a reliable and robust method to assess the rewarding effects of morphine using the rate-frequency curve paradigm.

title: An Update on the Neurochemistry of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the global population, yet its underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding neurotransmitter systems implicated in ET pathophysiology, with particular focus on recent advances in neurochemical research from 2015-2020. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of preclinical and clinical studies examining neurotransmitter dysfunction in ET, including pharmacological, neuroimaging, and post-mortem analyses. Emerging evidence suggests that ET involves complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems beyond the traditionally implicated GABAergic pathways. Recent studies demonstrate altered cerebellar GABA transmission, with reduced GABA-A receptor density in Purkinje cells and decreased GAD67 expression in ET patients. Additionally, dopaminergic dysfunction has been increasingly recognized, with PET imaging revealing reduced striatal dopamine transporter binding and altered D2 receptor availability in ET subjects compared to controls. Noradrenergic system abnormalities, including elevated plasma norepinephrine levels and altered α2-adrenoreceptor sensitivity, further contribute to tremor generation. Serotonergic involvement is supported by the therapeutic efficacy of certain SSRIs and altered 5-HT receptor binding in tremor-related brain regions. Glutamatergic hyperactivity in cerebellar-thalamic circuits has also been implicated through imaging and pharmacological studies. These findings reveal ET as a multisystem neurochemical disorder involving dysregulated inhibitory and excitatory neurotransmission across cerebellar-thalamic-cortical networks. Understanding these complex neurochemical interactions provides new therapeutic targets and may explain the heterogeneous clinical presentation and variable treatment responses observed in ET patients.

title: Effects of soybean peptide on immune function, brain function, and neurochemistry in healthy volunteers.
abstract:
**Background:** Soy-derived bioactive peptides have demonstrated immunomodulatory and neuroprotective properties in animal models, yet their effects on human brain function and neurochemistry remain poorly characterized. This study investigated whether oral supplementation with soybean peptide influences immune markers, cognitive performance, and neurotransmitter metabolism in healthy adults.

**Methods:** In this randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, 84 healthy volunteers (aged 25-55 years) received either soybean peptide (3g daily) or placebo for 8 weeks. Primary outcomes included serum cytokine levels (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IL-10), lymphocyte proliferation responses, and cognitive assessments using standardized neuropsychological batteries. Secondary measures comprised plasma amino acid profiles, urinary monoamine metabolites (3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid, 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid), and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) concentrations.

**Results:** Soybean peptide supplementation significantly increased serum IL-10 levels (p=0.032) and enhanced lymphocyte proliferation responses to mitogens (p=0.018) compared to placebo. Cognitive performance improved in attention and working memory domains (p=0.024 and p=0.041, respectively). Plasma tryptophan and tyrosine concentrations increased by 23% and 18%, respectively (both p<0.05), while urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid excretion rose by 34% (p=0.009). BDNF levels showed a modest but significant elevation (p=0.047) in the treatment group.

**Conclusions:** These findings suggest that soybean peptide supplementation enhances immune function and cognitive performance in healthy individuals, potentially through modulation of amino acid availability and neurotransmitter synthesis. Further research is warranted to elucidate optimal dosing strategies and long-term effects on neurological health.

title: Evaluation of metaquant microdialysis for measurement of absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in brain: a viable method for assessing pharmacokinetic profile of drugs in the brain.
abstract: Accurate measurement of absolute drug concentrations in brain tissue remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional microdialysis techniques provide relative concentration changes but yield recovery-dependent values that complicate pharmacokinetic interpretation. This study evaluated the metaquant microdialysis approach for determining absolute concentrations of amphetamine and dopamine in rat striatum. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with CMA/12 probes in the dorsal striatum. Following amphetamine administration (2.5 mg/kg i.p.), metaquant calibration was performed using retrodialysis of known concentrations of amphetamine (10-1000 nM) and dopamine (1-100 nM) through the probe membrane. Extraction fraction was determined by measuring the difference between perfusate input and output concentrations. Dialysate samples were analyzed using HPLC-ED with detection limits of 0.5 nM for amphetamine and 0.2 nM for dopamine. The metaquant method yielded extraction fractions of 0.18 ± 0.03 for amphetamine and 0.22 ± 0.04 for dopamine in vivo. Peak absolute amphetamine concentrations reached 285 ± 45 nM at 45 minutes post-injection, while dopamine concentrations increased from baseline 12 ± 3 nM to 89 ± 18 nM at 30 minutes. Pharmacokinetic analysis revealed amphetamine half-life of 78 ± 12 minutes in striatal extracellular fluid. These results demonstrate that metaquant microdialysis provides reliable absolute concentration measurements, enabling accurate assessment of drug pharmacokinetics in discrete brain regions and offering significant advantages over conventional relative microdialysis approaches for neuropharmacological investigations.

title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) is a complex neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by chronic motor and vocal tics, with emerging evidence implicating both neurodevelopmental abnormalities and immune dysfunction in its pathogenesis. Recent neurobiological investigations have expanded our understanding of the cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical (CSTC) circuits underlying tic generation, while neuroimmunological studies suggest autoimmune mechanisms may contribute to symptom onset and exacerbation in susceptible individuals. We conducted a comprehensive review of recent neuroimaging, post-mortem, and immunological studies published between 1995-2003, analyzing data from over 2,400 TS patients and 1,800 controls across multiple research centers. Structural and functional neuroimaging studies consistently demonstrate altered dopaminergic neurotransmission within the caudate nucleus and putamen, with increased D2 receptor binding density correlating with tic severity (r=0.67, p<0.001). Post-mortem analyses reveal decreased interneuron populations in the striatum, particularly parvalbumin-positive GABAergic cells, suggesting disinhibition of motor programs. Immunological investigations demonstrate elevated anti-neuronal antibodies in 34% of TS patients versus 8% of controls (p<0.0001), with molecular mimicry between Group A streptococcal antigens and basal ganglia proteins. Cytokine profiling reveals increased IL-12 and TNF-α levels during symptom exacerbations. These findings support a multifactorial model wherein genetic susceptibility, environmental triggers, and immune dysregulation converge to disrupt CSTC circuit function. The identification of specific neuroimmune biomarkers may facilitate early diagnosis and guide development of targeted immunomodulatory therapies for TS management.
journal: Cellular and molecular life sciences : CMLS

title: Structure of the catalytic pore of gamma-secretase probed by the accessibility of substituted cysteines.
abstract: Several single-span membrane proteins are cleaved within their transmembrane domains (TMDs) by intramembrane-cleaving proteases, although the structure of the active site executing intramembrane cleavage remains unknown. Here we use the substituted cysteine accessibility method to examine the structure of presenilin-1, a catalytic subunit of gamma-secretase, involved in amyloid beta protein generation in Alzheimer's disease and Notch signaling. We show that TMD6 and TMD7 of presenilin-1 contribute to the formation of a hydrophilic pore within the membrane. Residues at the luminal portion of TMD6 are predicted to form a subsite for substrate or inhibitor binding on the alpha-helix facing a hydrophilic milieu, whereas those around the GxGD catalytic motif within TMD7 are highly water accessible, suggesting formation of a hydrophilic structure within the pore. Collectively, our data suggest that the active site of gamma-secretase resides in a catalytic pore filled with water within the lipid bilayer and is tapered around the catalytic aspartates.
journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience

title: Psychopharmacology and ethnicity: A comparative study on Senegalese and Italian men.
abstract: Ethnic variations in drug metabolism and response represent critical considerations in psychopharmacological treatment, yet remain understudied in African populations. This study examined pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic differences in psychotropic medication response between Senegalese and Italian men. We conducted a randomized, double-blind crossover trial with 84 healthy male volunteers (42 Senegalese, 42 Italian) aged 18-35 years. Participants received single doses of risperidone (2mg), sertraline (50mg), and lorazepam (1mg) in separate sessions with 14-day washout periods. Blood samples were collected at predetermined intervals for 48 hours post-administration to assess plasma concentrations via LC-MS/MS. Cognitive and psychomotor assessments were performed using standardized batteries. Genotyping for CYP2D6, CYP2C19, and CYP3A4 polymorphisms was conducted. Senegalese participants demonstrated significantly higher plasma concentrations of risperidone (AUC: 1.47-fold increase, p<0.001) and its active metabolite, with delayed clearance compared to Italian subjects. Sertraline metabolism showed similar patterns (AUC: 1.32-fold increase, p<0.01). CYP2D6 poor metabolizer phenotypes were more prevalent in Senegalese participants (18.2% vs 7.1%, p<0.05). Lorazepam pharmacokinetics showed no significant ethnic differences. Cognitive performance decrements correlated with higher drug exposures in both groups. These findings highlight substantial ethnic differences in psychotropic drug metabolism between West African and European populations, primarily attributed to genetic polymorphisms in cytochrome P450 enzymes. Results support the need for ethnicity-guided dosing strategies and personalized medicine approaches in global psychiatric practice.
journal: The world journal of biological psychiatry : the official journal of the World Federation of Societies of Biological Psychiatry

title: Changes in striatal electroencephalography and neurochemistry induced by kainic acid seizures are modified by dopamine receptor antagonists.
abstract: The excitotoxic effects of kainic acid (KA) involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, particularly in the striatum where these neurotransmitter pathways extensively converge. This study investigated whether dopamine receptor antagonists could modulate KA-induced electrophysiological and neurochemical alterations in the rat striatum. Adult male Wistar rats (n=8 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of KA (10 mg/kg) alone or in combination with the D1 receptor antagonist SCH23390 (0.5 mg/kg) or the D2 receptor antagonist haloperidol (1.0 mg/kg). Striatal electroencephalographic activity was continuously monitored for 6 hours post-injection, and neurochemical analyses were performed using high-performance liquid chromatography to measure dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and GABA concentrations at 24 hours. KA administration produced characteristic epileptiform discharges in the striatum within 30 minutes, accompanied by significant depletion of striatal dopamine (-67%, p<0.001) and GABA (-43%, p<0.01) levels. Pretreatment with SCH23390 significantly attenuated the duration and amplitude of KA-induced seizure activity and partially protected against dopamine depletion (-31%, p<0.05 vs KA alone). Haloperidol pretreatment showed less pronounced protective effects on both electrophysiological and neurochemical parameters. These findings suggest that D1 receptor-mediated mechanisms contribute to KA-induced striatal excitotoxicity, and that selective D1 antagonism may offer neuroprotection against glutamate-mediated seizure damage in this brain region.

title: Efficient measurement of endogenous neurotransmitters in small localized regions of central nervous systems in vitro with HPLC.
abstract: Traditional methods for measuring endogenous neurotransmitters in discrete brain regions have been limited by tissue volume requirements and analytical sensitivity. This study describes an optimized high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system with electrochemical detection for quantifying dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites from microdissected brain regions weighing 0.5-2.0 mg. Brain slices (300 μm) from adult male Sprague-Dawley rats were prepared using a vibratome, and specific nuclei including the nucleus accumbens, caudate-putamen, and substantia nigra were microdissected under stereomicroscopic guidance. Tissue samples were homogenized in 0.1 M perchloric acid containing 0.1 mM EDTA and 10 ng/ml dihydroxybenzylamine as internal standard. The HPLC system employed a C18 reverse-phase column with a mobile phase consisting of 75 mM sodium phosphate buffer, 1.7 mM octanesulfonic acid, 25 μM EDTA, and 10% acetonitrile at pH 3.0. Detection limits were 0.5 pg for dopamine, 1.0 pg for norepinephrine, and 0.8 pg for serotonin. Intra-assay coefficient of variation was <5% for all compounds. Regional neurotransmitter distributions obtained were consistent with known neuroanatomical patterns, with dopamine levels highest in striatal regions and serotonin predominant in raphe-projecting areas. This method provides reliable quantification of multiple neurotransmitters from small tissue samples, enabling detailed neurochemical mapping studies and reducing the number of animals required for neurochemical analyses while maintaining analytical precision suitable for pharmacological investigations.

title: [New directions in GABAergic pharmacology of anxiety and depression].
abstract: GABAergic dysfunction has been increasingly implicated in the pathophysiology of anxiety and depressive disorders, yet traditional benzodiazepine therapies present significant limitations including tolerance, dependence, and cognitive impairment. This review examines emerging therapeutic approaches targeting the GABAergic system through novel mechanisms. We analyzed preclinical and clinical data from 1995-2003 investigating selective GABA receptor subunit modulators, neurosteroid analogues, and GABA transporter inhibitors. Particular attention was given to compounds demonstrating anxiolytic or antidepressant properties without classical benzodiazepine side effects.
