Selective α2/α3 subunit-preferring compounds showed promising anxiolytic efficacy in animal models while producing minimal sedation and reduced tolerance liability compared to non-selective agents. Synthetic neurosteroids, particularly 3α-hydroxy ring A-reduced pregnane derivatives, demonstrated rapid-onset antidepressant effects in forced swim and learned helplessness paradigms, potentially mediated through positive allosteric modulation of GABAA receptors and HPA axis regulation. GABA transporter-1 selective inhibitors exhibited both anxiolytic and antidepressant properties in multiple preclinical models, suggesting therapeutic potential through enhanced GABAergic tone. Clinical pilot studies with several compounds confirmed preliminary efficacy signals, though larger controlled trials remain necessary. These findings indicate that subunit-selective GABAA receptor modulators, neurosteroid-based therapies, and GABA reuptake inhibitors represent promising new directions for treating anxiety and depression. Such approaches may overcome limitations of current GABAergic medications while exploiting the therapeutic potential of this crucial inhibitory neurotransmitter system.

title: Oral famotidine: a potential treatment for children with autism.
abstract: Autism is a pervasive developmental disorder characterized by impaired social interaction, communication deficits, and repetitive behaviors. Recent neurochemical investigations have implicated abnormal histaminergic neurotransmission in autistic pathophysiology, with elevated brain histamine levels reported in post-mortem studies. Histamine H2 receptors are widely distributed throughout the central nervous system and modulate neurotransmitter release, synaptic plasticity, and behavioral responses. We hypothesize that famotidine, a selective histamine H2 receptor antagonist commonly used for gastric acid suppression, may ameliorate core autistic symptoms through central histaminergic blockade. This hypothesis is supported by several converging lines of evidence: (1) famotidine crosses the blood-brain barrier and achieves therapeutically relevant CNS concentrations, (2) H2 receptor antagonism normalizes excessive histamine-mediated glutamate release implicated in autistic stereotypies, and (3) preliminary case reports suggest behavioral improvements in autistic children receiving famotidine for gastrointestinal symptoms. The proposed therapeutic mechanism involves famotidine's ability to modulate histamine-dopamine interactions in striatal and limbic circuits, potentially reducing repetitive behaviors and improving social responsiveness. Additionally, famotidine may indirectly enhance GABAergic inhibition through histaminergic disinhibition, addressing the excitatory-inhibitory imbalance proposed in autism. Given famotidine's established safety profile in pediatric populations and its unique mechanism of action distinct from current autism interventions, controlled clinical trials investigating oral famotidine as an adjunctive treatment for childhood autism are warranted. Such studies could provide novel insights into histaminergic contributions to autistic pathophysiology while offering a potentially accessible therapeutic option.

title: A review of monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology in zebrafish.
abstract: The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a powerful vertebrate model for investigating monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems and their pharmacological modulation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic pathways in zebrafish and their relevance to neuropsychiatric drug discovery. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature from 2000-2010 examining monoamine neurotransmitter distribution, receptor pharmacology, and behavioral responses to psychoactive compounds in zebrafish. Immunohistochemical and in situ hybridization studies reveal that zebrafish possess conserved monoaminergic nuclei analogous to mammalian systems, including dopaminergic clusters in the posterior tuberculum and ventral diencephalon, serotonergic raphe-like neurons, and noradrenergic locus coeruleus homologs. Pharmacological characterization demonstrates that zebrafish monoamine receptors exhibit similar ligand binding profiles and G-protein coupling patterns to their mammalian counterparts. Behavioral assays measuring locomotion, anxiety-like responses, and social behaviors show dose-dependent effects of classical psychotropic drugs including SSRIs, dopamine receptor agonists/antagonists, and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors that parallel mammalian responses. Importantly, genetic mutants affecting monoamine synthesis and metabolism display behavioral phenotypes consistent with human neuropsychiatric conditions. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable translational model bridging molecular mechanisms and behavioral outcomes in monoaminergic neuropsychopharmacology. The conservation of monoamine systems across vertebrates, combined with zebrafish experimental advantages including genetic tractability, optical transparency, and high-throughput screening capabilities, positions this model organism as an important tool for understanding monoamine function and developing novel therapeutics for psychiatric disorders.

title: Strategies for Utilizing Neuroimaging Biomarkers in CNS Drug Discovery and Development: CINP/JSNP Working Group Report.
abstract: Central nervous system (CNS) drug development faces unprecedented challenges, with failure rates exceeding 90% and limited translation from preclinical models to clinical efficacy. Neuroimaging biomarkers offer promising strategies to bridge this translational gap by providing objective, quantifiable measures of CNS drug action. The CINP/JSNP Working Group convened to evaluate current applications and establish recommendations for integrating neuroimaging biomarkers into CNS drug discovery and development pipelines. Through systematic review of recent neuroimaging studies in CNS therapeutics and expert consensus sessions, we examined the utility of various imaging modalities including functional MRI, positron emission tomography, magnetic resonance spectroscopy, and diffusion tensor imaging across different phases of drug development. Our analysis identified key applications: target engagement assessment in Phase I trials, dose optimization through pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic modeling, patient stratification for enrichment strategies, and surrogate endpoint development for proof-of-concept studies. We found that successful implementation requires standardized acquisition protocols, robust quality control measures, and appropriate statistical frameworks for small sample sizes typical in early-phase trials. Critical recommendations include establishing imaging biomarker qualification pathways with regulatory agencies, developing companion diagnostics for patient selection, and creating multi-site consortiums to enable adequately powered studies. The Working Group concludes that strategic implementation of neuroimaging biomarkers can significantly enhance CNS drug development efficiency by enabling go/no-go decisions earlier in development, reducing late-stage failures, and facilitating personalized medicine approaches in psychiatric and neurological disorders.

title: A comprehensive regional neurochemical theory in depression: a protocol for the systematic review and meta-analysis of 1H-MRS studies in major depressive disorder.
abstract: Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) is a non-invasive analytical technique that investigates the presence and concentrations of brain metabolites. In the context of major depressive disorder (MDD), MRS has revealed regional biochemical changes in GABA, glutamate, and choline across different brain compartments. Technical and methodological advances in MRS data acquisition, in particular proton-based 1H-MRS, have resulted in a significant increase in the incidence of reports utilizing the technique for psychiatric disorder research and diagnosis. The most recent comprehensive meta-analysis reviewing MRS in MDD stems from 2006. Using contemporary systemic reviews and meta-analysis, the aim is to first test a neurochemical circuit-based theory of depression and then to determine if clinical scores relate to metabolite concentrations before and during treatment. Region-specific metabolite changes in MDD will be assessed by systematic review following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA). Inclusion criteria will include participant age (18 to 65), English language studies, known regions of interest, and detailed documentation of 1H-MRS procedures. Reported brain regions will be standardized according neuroanatomical expertise allowing increased power of the meta-analysis. Regions of interest will initially include the hippocampus, thalamus, prefrontal cortex, anterior and posterior cingulate gyri, parietal lobe, and basal ganglia. Exclusion criteria will include comorbid psychiatric illness and drug use. Two independent reviewers will undertake all data extraction, while a third reviewer will check for reviewer discrepancies. Statistical analysis will be performed using STATA supplemented by Metan software and SPSS. This data will shed new light on the biochemical basis of depression in different brain regions, thereby highlighting the potential of MRS in identifying biomarkers and generating models of MDD and treatment response. PROSPERO CRD42018091494.

title: Corticosterone urinalysis and nicotinic receptor modulation in rats.
abstract: Chronic stress and nicotine exposure produce complex neuroadaptations that are difficult to assess using invasive sampling methods. Non-invasive biomarkers would facilitate longitudinal studies of stress-nicotine interactions in rodent models. We developed and validated a urinalysis protocol for measuring corticosterone levels and examined the relationship between urinary corticosterone and nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) expression following chronic nicotine treatment. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were housed in metabolic cages and received either saline or nicotine (0.4 mg/kg, s.c.) twice daily for 14 days. Urine samples were collected every 48 hours, and corticosterone concentrations were determined using enzyme immunoassay. Following treatment, brain tissues were processed for α4β2 and α7 nAChR binding using [³H]-epibatidine and [¹²⁵I]-α-bungarotoxin autoradiography, respectively. Urinary corticosterone showed significant positive correlation with plasma levels (r=0.82, p<0.001) and exhibited circadian variation consistent with the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis rhythm. Chronic nicotine treatment elevated mean urinary corticosterone by 34% compared to controls (p<0.01). Nicotine-treated animals displayed upregulated α4β2 nAChR binding in cortical and hippocampal regions (25-40% increase, p<0.05) but unchanged α7 nAChR binding. Importantly, individual differences in urinary corticosterone levels significantly predicted the magnitude of α4β2 upregulation (r=0.71, p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that urinalysis provides a reliable, non-invasive method for monitoring corticosterone in rats and reveals a novel relationship between stress hormone levels and nicotinic receptor plasticity that may inform addiction vulnerability mechanisms.

title: The ethics of elective psychopharmacology.
abstract: The increasing availability of psychoactive medications for cognitive and mood enhancement in healthy individuals has raised significant ethical questions regarding elective psychopharmacology use outside traditional therapeutic contexts. This review examines the current landscape of off-label psychopharmacological interventions and evaluates key ethical frameworks applicable to elective use. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 2000-2012, surveying empirical studies on cognitive enhancers, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics used by healthy populations, alongside philosophical and bioethical perspectives on enhancement versus treatment paradigms. Our analysis identified four primary ethical domains: autonomy and informed consent, distributive justice and access, safety and long-term consequences, and societal pressure and coercion. Current evidence suggests 15-30% of university students have used prescription stimulants non-medically, while growing numbers of professionals report modafinil and other cognitive enhancers for performance enhancement. Key ethical tensions emerge between individual liberty and collective welfare, particularly regarding workplace competitiveness, academic fairness, and healthcare resource allocation. Safety profiles for long-term use in healthy individuals remain largely unknown, complicating risk-benefit analyses. We conclude that elective psychopharmacology requires robust regulatory frameworks balancing individual autonomy with societal considerations. Future research must address long-term safety profiles, develop guidelines for appropriate use, and establish ethical standards for prescribing practices. These findings have immediate implications for clinical practice, institutional policies, and public health governance as enhancement technologies become increasingly prevalent.

title: Role of glutamate and dopamine receptors in the psychopharmacological profile of the indole alkaloid psychollatine.
abstract: The indole alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from Psychotria species, has demonstrated psychoactive properties in traditional ethnobotanical preparations, yet its mechanism of action remains poorly characterized. This study investigated the neuropharmacological profile of psychollatine with particular emphasis on glutamate and dopamine receptor interactions. Psychollatine was purified from Psychotria carthaginensis using standard chromatographic techniques. Receptor binding assays were performed using rat brain membrane preparations to evaluate affinity for NMDA, AMPA, kainate, D1, and D2 receptor subtypes. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition, and conditioned avoidance response in male Sprague-Dawley rats following intraperitoneal administration of psychollatine (1-30 mg/kg). Electrophysiological recordings from hippocampal slices examined effects on glutamatergic neurotransmission. Psychollatine exhibited moderate binding affinity for NMDA receptors (Ki = 8.2 μM) and D2 receptors (Ki = 12.4 μM), with minimal affinity for other receptor subtypes tested. Behaviorally, psychollatine (10-30 mg/kg) produced dose-dependent hyperlocomotion and disrupted prepulse inhibition, effects that were attenuated by co-administration of the D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg) but not the NMDA antagonist MK-801. Electrophysiological studies revealed psychollatine significantly enhanced glutamate-induced excitatory postsynaptic potentials in CA1 pyramidal neurons. These findings suggest that psychollatine's psychoactive effects involve complex interactions between glutamatergic and dopaminergic systems, with primary behavioral effects mediated through dopamine D2 receptor activation. Further investigation of this novel indole alkaloid may provide insights into glutamate-dopamine interactions underlying psychosis-related behaviors.

title: Effects of soman (pinacolyl methylphosphonofluoridate) on coronary blood flow and cardiac function in swine.
abstract: Organophosphorus compounds such as soman pose significant toxicological threats due to their irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase, leading to cholinergic crisis. While the neurological and respiratory effects of soman poisoning are well-documented, the direct cardiovascular consequences remain poorly characterized. This study examined the acute effects of soman exposure on coronary hemodynamics and myocardial function in an anesthetized swine model. Twelve Yorkshire swine (25-30 kg) were instrumented with coronary flow probes, left ventricular pressure catheters, and ECG electrodes under pentobarbital anesthesia. Animals received either soman (0.5 × LD50, n=8) or saline control (n=4) via intravenous injection. Coronary blood flow, left ventricular pressure, heart rate, and cardiac output were continuously monitored for 120 minutes post-exposure. Soman administration produced rapid onset of cholinergic signs including bradycardia, hypotension, and cardiac arrhythmias within 5-10 minutes. Coronary blood flow decreased significantly by 35 ± 8% (p<0.01) compared to baseline, while coronary vascular resistance increased 2.3-fold. Left ventricular contractility (dP/dt max) declined by 42 ± 12% (p<0.001), and cardiac output fell to 60% of control values. These hemodynamic changes preceded the onset of severe respiratory distress and were partially reversed by atropine pretreatment in additional animals. The results demonstrate that soman exerts direct cardiotoxic effects independent of respiratory compromise, suggesting that cardiovascular support should be considered as an essential component of organophosphate poisoning treatment protocols.
journal: Fundamental and applied toxicology : official journal of the Society of Toxicology

title: In-vivo brain dialysis of neurotransmitters.
abstract: Recent advances in microdialysis technology have provided unprecedented opportunities to monitor neurotransmitter release in the living brain with high temporal resolution. This technique involves the stereotaxic implantation of a dialysis probe with a semipermeable membrane into specific brain regions, allowing continuous sampling of extracellular fluid. We evaluated the efficacy of in-vivo microdialysis for measuring dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and acetylcholine release in the striatum, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex of freely moving rats. Dialysis probes (2-4 mm membrane length, 20,000 Da molecular weight cutoff) were perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 1-2 μL/min, and samples were collected at 20-minute intervals for high-performance liquid chromatography analysis. Basal concentrations of dopamine (5-15 nM), norepinephrine (2-8 nM), and serotonin (1-5 nM) were successfully detected, representing approximately 10-20% of actual extracellular levels due to probe recovery limitations. Pharmacological validation experiments demonstrated that amphetamine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) increased striatal dopamine release by 400-600%, while haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a delayed elevation consistent with autoreceptor blockade. Local perfusion of tetrodotoxin abolished neurotransmitter release, confirming the neuronal origin of detected compounds. These findings establish microdialysis as a powerful tool for real-time neurochemical monitoring, offering significant advantages over traditional post-mortem analyses. The technique promises to advance our understanding of neurotransmitter dynamics in behavioral, pharmacological, and pathophysiological studies.

title: Neurochemical substrates linked to impulsive and compulsive phenotypes in addiction: A preclinical perspective.
abstract: Addiction is characterized by a transition from impulsive drug-seeking to compulsive drug-taking behaviors, yet the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this phenotypic shift remain poorly understood. We examined dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems in brain regions implicated in addiction using a well-validated rat self-administration model. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) underwent cocaine self-administration training followed by behavioral phenotyping using delay discounting and compulsivity tasks. Animals were classified as high-impulsive (HI) or low-impulsive (LI) based on delay discounting performance, and high-compulsive (HC) or low-compulsive (LC) based on punishment-resistant responding. Post-mortem neurochemical analysis was performed using high-performance liquid chromatography in the nucleus accumbens, prefrontal cortex, and dorsal striatum. HI animals showed significantly reduced dopamine transporter expression in the nucleus accumbens (p<0.01) and elevated dopamine D2 receptor binding in the prefrontal cortex compared to LI rats. HC animals exhibited decreased serotonin transporter density in the dorsal striatum (p<0.05) and reduced GABA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex relative to LC animals. Importantly, impulsive and compulsive phenotypes were associated with distinct neurochemical profiles, with minimal overlap between groups. Correlation analyses revealed that compulsivity scores negatively correlated with prefrontal GABA levels (r=-0.67, p<0.001), while impulsivity measures correlated with accumbal dopamine transporter expression (r=-0.58, p<0.01). These findings suggest that impulsive and compulsive addiction phenotypes are mediated by dissociable neurochemical substrates, providing potential targets for personalized therapeutic interventions in substance use disorders.

title: Cosmetic psychopharmacology and the President's Council on Bioethics.
abstract: The emerging field of cosmetic psychopharmacology—the use of psychoactive medications to enhance normal psychological functioning rather than treat diagnosed mental illness—has generated significant ethical and policy debates. The President's Council on Bioethics has raised concerns about the implications of pharmaceutical cognitive and mood enhancement for human nature, authentic selfhood, and social justice. This analysis examines the neurobiological basis for these concerns and evaluates current evidence regarding the efficacy and safety of off-label psychotropic use in healthy populations. We reviewed clinical trials of stimulants, antidepressants, and anxiolytics in non-clinical samples, along with neuroimaging studies of enhancement effects on brain function. Additionally, we analyzed bioethical arguments presented in the Council's reports and related policy documents. Current evidence suggests modest cognitive improvements from stimulants like modafinil and amphetamines in healthy individuals, with limited data on long-term safety. Antidepressants may enhance emotional resilience and social cognition in non-depressed populations, while anxiolytics can reduce performance anxiety. However, individual responses vary considerably, and mechanisms of action in normal brains remain poorly understood. The Council's concerns about authenticity and coercion appear philosophically complex but empirically unsubstantiated. More problematic are issues of equitable access and potential pressure for enhancement use in competitive environments. Rigorous long-term studies are needed to establish safety profiles for cosmetic psychopharmacological interventions. Regulatory frameworks should focus on evidence-based safety standards rather than essentialist notions of human nature, while addressing legitimate concerns about social justice and autonomous choice.

title: Emerging roles for novel antipsychotic medications in the treatment of schizophrenia.
abstract: Second-generation antipsychotic medications have emerged as promising alternatives to conventional neuroleptics in the treatment of schizophrenia, offering potential advantages in efficacy and tolerability. This review examines the clinical evidence supporting the use of clozapine, risperidone, and olanzapine in schizophrenic patients, with particular attention to their effects on positive symptoms, negative symptoms, and extrapyramidal side effects. A comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 1990 and 1997 was conducted, encompassing over 3,000 patients across multiple treatment centers. Clinical efficacy was assessed using the Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale (PANSS) and the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS), while extrapyramidal symptoms were evaluated using standardized rating instruments. Results demonstrate that novel antipsychotics produce superior improvements in negative symptoms compared to haloperidol and other typical agents, with effect sizes ranging from 0.4 to 0.8. Clozapine showed particular efficacy in treatment-resistant populations, with 30-40% of previously refractory patients achieving clinically meaningful improvement. Risperidone and olanzapine demonstrated comparable positive symptom control to conventional agents while producing significantly fewer extrapyramidal side effects at therapeutic doses. However, novel agents were associated with distinct adverse effect profiles, including weight gain, sedation, and potential metabolic disturbances. These findings suggest that second-generation antipsychotics represent a significant advancement in schizophrenia treatment, offering improved therapeutic outcomes and enhanced patient tolerability, though careful monitoring remains essential for optimal clinical management.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: The pediatric psychopharmacology of autism spectrum disorder: A systematic review - Part I: The past and the present.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children, yet evidence-based pharmacological interventions remain limited. While no medications are approved for core ASD symptoms, psychotropic drugs are frequently prescribed off-label to manage associated behavioral symptoms. This systematic review synthesizes the historical evolution and current state of pediatric psychopharmacological approaches in ASD. We conducted a comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane databases from inception through December 2020, including randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining pharmacological interventions in children and adolescents with ASD. Two independent reviewers extracted data on study design, participant characteristics, interventions, outcome measures, and safety profiles. Of 2,847 initially identified studies, 156 met inclusion criteria, encompassing 8,923 pediatric participants. Antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, demonstrated the strongest evidence for reducing irritability and aggressive behaviors, with FDA approval for these indications. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors showed mixed results for repetitive behaviors and anxiety, with concerns regarding behavioral activation in younger children. Stimulants effectively addressed comorbid attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder symptoms but required careful monitoring for adverse effects. Alpha-2 agonists and anticonvulsants showed promise in preliminary studies. However, significant heterogeneity in outcome measures, small sample sizes, and limited long-term safety data constrain clinical recommendations. Current pediatric psychopharmacology in ASD remains symptom-focused rather than addressing core deficits. Future research should prioritize standardized outcome measures, longer-term studies, and investigation of novel therapeutic targets to advance evidence-based treatment approaches for this vulnerable population.

title: Dopamine production in Enterococcus faecium: A microbial endocrinology-based mechanism for the selection of probiotics based on neurochemical-producing potential.
abstract: The mechanisms by which probiotics may influence host physiology are still incompletely understood. Microbial endocrinology, a field representing the union of microbiology, endocrinology and neurobiology, has theorized that microorganisms have the capacity to serve as neurochemical delivery vehicles [1]. According to microbial endocrinology, neurochemicals can serve as a common language between host and bacterium, enabling bidirectional communication. We report herein the first demonstration that Enterococcus sp. has the capacity to produce dopamine in a gastrointestinal-like environment when supplied with the dopamine precursor L-3,4 dihydroxyphenylalanine (L-dopa). The results presented herein provide a means to select probiotics based on neurochemical-producing potential and suggest the possibility that probiotics containing E. faecium may serve to influence the host through dopaminergic pathways.

title: Human Brain Neuropharmacology: A Platform for Translational Neuroscience.
abstract: The translational gap between preclinical neuropharmacological research and clinical outcomes remains a significant challenge in drug development for neuropsychiatric disorders. Traditional animal models, while valuable, often fail to recapitulate the complexity of human neural circuits and their responses to pharmacological interventions. We developed an integrated human brain neuropharmacology platform combining ex vivo human brain tissue analysis, induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neural networks, and advanced neuroimaging techniques to bridge this translational divide. The platform incorporates post-mortem human brain samples from neurologically healthy donors (n=45) and patients with major depressive disorder, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease (n=78), alongside patient-derived iPSC lines differentiated into cortical and subcortical neuronal subtypes. We employed high-throughput receptor binding assays, electrophysiological recordings, and calcium imaging to evaluate drug responses across multiple scales. Validation studies using 15 FDA-approved psychotropic medications demonstrated strong concordance between our platform predictions and known clinical efficacy profiles (r=0.82, p<0.001). Notably, the platform identified previously unrecognized off-target effects for three antipsychotic agents and predicted optimal dosing ranges that correlated with clinical therapeutic windows. Integration with functional MRI data from healthy volunteers (n=32) further validated network-level drug effects. This human brain neuropharmacology platform represents a paradigm shift toward human-centric drug discovery, offering unprecedented opportunities to accelerate the development of more effective neurotherapeutics while reducing reliance on animal models and improving clinical translation rates.

title: Artifact processing in topographic mapping of electroencephalographic activity in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Mapping of the electroencephalogram (EEG) has been found to be a valuable method in clinical neuropsychopharmacology. It is evident that careful treatment of artifacts is of utmost importance for EEG data processing, as artifacts that contaminate the EEG data can lead to spurious results. The artifact-processing method described in this article splits signal analysis into a preprocessing step, yielding individual electro-oculographic (EOG) regression factors for EOG minimization, and into a processing step, yielding target variables. The combination of avoiding, minimizing, and identifying artifacts, as well as visual checking of face validity, will help remove artifactual effects from the EEG.

title: Methodological optimization of applying neuroactive agents for the study of locomotor-like activity in the mudpuppies (Necturus maculatus).
abstract: The mudpuppy (Necturus maculatus) represents a valuable model for studying fundamental mechanisms of vertebrate locomotion due to its simplified neural circuitry and robust motor patterns. However, standardized protocols for pharmacological manipulation of locomotor networks in this species remain limited. We developed and validated an optimized methodology for applying neuroactive compounds to induce and modulate locomotor-like activity in the isolated mudpuppy brainstem-spinal cord preparation. Adult mudpuppies (n=24, 15-25 cm) were anesthetized and decerebrated, and brainstem-spinal cord preparations were maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 10°C. Locomotor-like rhythmic activity was monitored via extracellular recordings from ventral roots L2-L4. We systematically tested concentration-response relationships for N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA, 25-200 μM), 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT, 10-100 μM), and dopamine (DA, 50-300 μM) applied via bath perfusion. Optimal locomotor rhythm induction was achieved using NMDA (75 μM) combined with 5-HT (25 μM), generating stable alternating burst patterns with cycle periods of 2.8 ± 0.4 seconds and consistent left-right alternation (phase lag: 0.51 ± 0.03). Dopamine dose-dependently increased burst frequency without disrupting coordination. Application order significantly affected response magnitude, with 5-HT pre-treatment enhancing NMDA sensitivity by 35%. Temperature stability (±0.5°C) and gradual drug application (≤10 μM/min) were critical for reproducible results. This standardized protocol provides a reliable platform for investigating spinal locomotor networks and testing pharmacological interventions in an evolutionarily relevant vertebrate model, facilitating comparative studies of motor control mechanisms across species.

title: Effect of ethanolic extract of Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae) on monoamine-mediated behaviour.
abstract: Rubia peregrina L. (Rubiaceae), commonly known as wild madder, has been traditionally used in Mediterranean folk medicine for treating various neuropsychiatric disorders, yet its neuropharmacological properties remain largely unexplored. This study investigated the effects of ethanolic extract of R. peregrina aerial parts on monoamine-mediated behaviours in experimental models. The ethanolic extract was prepared using standardized procedures and subjected to preliminary phytochemical screening. Male Swiss albino mice (25-30 g) were administered R. peregrina extract (100, 200, and 400 mg/kg, p.o.) and evaluated using forced swimming test, tail suspension test, open field test, and elevated plus maze. Additionally, monoamine oxidase (MAO-A and MAO-B) inhibitory activity was assessed using fluorometric assays, while brain monoamine levels were determined by HPLC. Phytochemical analysis revealed the presence of anthraquinones, flavonoids, and phenolic compounds. R. peregrina extract (200 and 400 mg/kg) significantly reduced immobility time in both forced swimming test (p<0.01) and tail suspension test (p<0.05) compared to control, suggesting antidepressant-like activity. The extract increased locomotor activity and exploratory behaviour in open field test, while enhancing anxiolytic-like effects in elevated plus maze. Biochemical analysis demonstrated significant inhibition of MAO-A (IC₅₀ = 145.2 μg/ml) and moderate MAO-B inhibition (IC₅₀ = 298.7 μg/ml). Brain tissue analysis revealed increased serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine levels following extract treatment. These findings suggest that R. peregrina possesses significant psychoactive properties mediated through monoaminergic mechanisms, supporting its traditional use and warranting further investigation as a potential natural antidepressant.

title: The use of biomarkers in psychiatric research: how serotonin transporter occupancy explains the dose-response curves of SSRIs.
abstract: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) demonstrate considerable interindividual variability in therapeutic response and optimal dosing, with clinical dose-response relationships often appearing nonlinear or plateau-shaped. While therapeutic drug monitoring has shown limited utility for SSRIs due to poor correlation between plasma concentrations and clinical outcomes, neuroimaging biomarkers may provide superior insights into central nervous system target engagement. This study investigated whether serotonin transporter (SERT) occupancy measurements could explain the pharmacodynamic basis of SSRI dose-response curves and inform personalized dosing strategies. We conducted a systematic review of positron emission tomography (PET) studies measuring SERT occupancy across different SSRI doses, combined with meta-analysis of clinical efficacy data from randomized controlled trials. PET studies using [11C]DASB tracer were identified through PubMed and cross-referenced with corresponding clinical trials reporting Hamilton Depression Rating Scale scores. Twenty-three PET studies encompassing 312 subjects and 41 clinical trials with 8,847 patients were analyzed. SERT occupancy demonstrated a hyperbolic relationship with SSRI dose, reaching 50-80% occupancy at standard therapeutic doses across different agents. Clinical response curves paralleled occupancy data, with maximal efficacy occurring at 70-85% SERT occupancy. Higher occupancy levels (>85%) were associated with increased adverse effects without additional therapeutic benefit. These findings suggest that SERT occupancy represents a valuable biomarker for optimizing SSRI dosing, potentially explaining the plateau effect observed in clinical practice. Implementation of occupancy-guided dosing strategies may improve treatment outcomes while minimizing adverse effects, supporting the integration of neuroimaging biomarkers into psychiatric clinical practice.

title: History of Psychopharmacology.
abstract: We live in an age of psychopharmacology. One in six persons currently takes a psychotropic drug. These drugs have profoundly shaped our scientific and cultural understanding of psychiatric disease. By way of a historical review, we try to make sense of psychiatry's dependency on psychiatric drugs in the care of patients. Modern psychopharmacology began in 1950 with the synthesis of chlorpromazine. Over the course of the next 50 years, the psychiatric understanding and treatment of mental illness radically changed. Psychotropic drugs played a major part in these changes as state hospitals closed and psychotherapy gave way to drug prescriptions. Our review suggests that the success of psychopharmacology was not the consequence of increasingly more effective drugs for discrete psychiatric diseases. Instead, a complex mix of political economic realities, pharmaceutical marketing, basic science advances, and changes in the mental health-care system have led to our current infatuation with psychopharmacology.

title: Diagnosis and management of chronic daily headache.
abstract: Chronic daily headache (CDH) affects approximately 4-5% of the general population and represents a significant neurological burden with substantial impact on quality of life and healthcare utilization. The classification encompasses transformed migraine, chronic tension-type headache, new daily persistent headache, and hemicrania continua, each requiring distinct therapeutic approaches. This comprehensive review analyzes current diagnostic criteria and evidence-based management strategies for CDH syndromes. We systematically reviewed published literature from 1990-2009, focusing on randomized controlled trials and large observational studies examining diagnostic methodologies and therapeutic interventions. Clinical assessment should emphasize headache frequency, medication overuse patterns, and associated neurological symptoms to differentiate CDH subtypes. Neuroimaging is indicated when red flag symptoms are present or in cases of new-onset daily headache. Medication overuse headache, present in 30-50% of CDH patients, requires structured detoxification protocols. First-line preventive treatments include topiramate (50-100mg daily) and amitriptyline (25-75mg daily), with response rates of 40-60% in controlled trials. Botulinum toxin A shows emerging efficacy for chronic migraine, with significant headache reduction in 70% of patients. Behavioral interventions, including cognitive-behavioral therapy and biofeedback, provide additive benefits when combined with pharmacotherapy. Early recognition and appropriate management of CDH can substantially reduce headache frequency and improve functional outcomes. A multidisciplinary approach incorporating pharmacological and non-pharmacological strategies, with emphasis on medication overuse prevention, represents the optimal management paradigm for these challenging headache disorders.

title: Promising avenues of therapeutics for bipolar illness.
abstract: Bipolar disorder affects approximately 1-2% of the global population and represents a leading cause of disability worldwide, yet current pharmacological treatments remain suboptimal for many patients. Mood stabilizers such as lithium, valproate, and carbamazepine provide therapeutic benefit but are associated with significant side effects and limited efficacy in treatment-resistant cases. This review examines emerging therapeutic approaches that target novel neurobiological pathways implicated in bipolar disorder pathophysiology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies published between 2000-2008, focusing on glutamatergic modulators, neuroprotective agents, and circadian rhythm regulators. Glutamate NMDA receptor antagonists, including ketamine and memantine, demonstrate rapid antimanic and antidepressant effects in preliminary trials, with response rates of 60-75% within 72 hours. Neuroprotective compounds such as riluzole and N-acetylcysteine show promise in stabilizing mood episodes through modulation of glial function and oxidative stress pathways. Additionally, melatonin receptor agonists and light therapy interventions targeting circadian dysregulation yield significant improvements in sleep architecture and mood stability. Neuroimaging studies reveal that these novel interventions normalize aberrant activity in prefrontal-limbic circuits implicated in emotional regulation. These findings suggest that therapeutics targeting glutamatergic neurotransmission, cellular resilience mechanisms, and biological rhythm disturbances represent promising avenues for bipolar disorder treatment. Future research should prioritize large-scale randomized controlled trials to establish efficacy and safety profiles of these innovative approaches, potentially revolutionizing bipolar disorder management through precision medicine strategies.

title: Toward a neuropsychological theory of antidepressant drug action: increase in positive emotional bias after potentiation of norepinephrine activity.
abstract: Major depressive disorder is characterized by negative cognitive biases and blunted positive emotional processing, yet the mechanisms by which antidepressants restore normal emotional cognition remain poorly understood. While current theories focus primarily on monoamine receptor changes, emerging evidence suggests that antidepressants may act by modulating emotional information processing biases. This study examined whether acute potentiation of norepinephrine activity influences emotional bias in healthy volunteers using a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design. Twenty-four healthy adults received either reboxetine (4mg) or placebo before completing computerized tasks measuring emotional word recognition, facial expression categorization, and attentional bias toward positive versus negative stimuli. Participants also underwent functional magnetic resonance imaging during an emotional faces paradigm. Compared to placebo, reboxetine administration significantly increased recognition accuracy for positive emotional words (p<0.01) and shifted the threshold for categorizing ambiguous facial expressions toward happy rather than sad interpretations (p<0.05). Attentional bias measures revealed faster orienting toward positive compared to negative images following reboxetine treatment (p<0.05). Neuroimaging data showed increased amygdala-prefrontal coupling during positive emotion processing and reduced amygdala reactivity to negative faces in the reboxetine group. These findings demonstrate that acute norepinephrine enhancement produces measurable shifts toward positive emotional bias in healthy individuals, suggesting that antidepressants may exert therapeutic effects by correcting the negative cognitive biases characteristic of depression rather than simply through direct mood elevation. This neuropsychological model of antidepressant action has important implications for understanding therapeutic mechanisms and optimizing treatment approaches.

title: Analysis of neurotransmitter tissue content of Drosophila melanogaster in different life stages.
abstract: The neurotransmitter systems of Drosophila melanogaster undergo extensive remodeling during metamorphosis, yet comprehensive quantitative analysis across developmental stages remains limited. This study employed high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to quantify dopamine, serotonin, octopamine, tyramine, and GABA content in whole-body extracts of D. melanogaster across four distinct life stages: third-instar larvae, pupae (48-hour), newly eclosed adults (0-24 hours), and mature adults (7-day). Twenty replicate samples of each stage were analyzed, with neurotransmitter content normalized to total protein concentration. Dopamine levels showed a biphasic pattern, decreasing 3.2-fold from larvae to pupae (p<0.001), then increasing 4.7-fold in newly eclosed adults before stabilizing in mature adults. Serotonin content remained relatively constant through larval and pupal stages but increased 2.8-fold in adults (p<0.001). Octopamine exhibited the most dramatic developmental regulation, with barely detectable levels in larvae and pupae, followed by a 15-fold increase in adults (p<0.001). Tyramine showed similar but less pronounced changes, increasing 6.2-fold from pupae to adults. GABA content gradually declined throughout development, decreasing by 40% from larvae to mature adults (p<0.05). These findings reveal stage-specific neurotransmitter profiles that likely reflect the distinct behavioral and physiological requirements of each developmental phase, providing a quantitative foundation for future studies of neurotransmitter function during Drosophila development and metamorphosis.

title: The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project: revision of the algorithm for pharmacotherapy of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder.
abstract: **Background:** The Texas Children's Medication Algorithm Project (TCMAP) was developed to provide evidence-based pharmacotherapy guidelines for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) in children and adolescents. Since the original algorithm's publication in 2001, new medications have been approved and additional efficacy and safety data have emerged, necessitating algorithmic revision. **Objective:** To update the TCMAP ADHD algorithm incorporating recent clinical trial data and expert consensus recommendations. **Method:** A multidisciplinary panel of child psychiatrists, developmental pediatricians, and clinical researchers reviewed controlled trials of ADHD medications published between 2001-2006, focusing on stimulants, atomoxetine, and alpha-2 agonists. The panel utilized a modified Delphi consensus process to integrate new evidence with clinical expertise. Safety profiles, tolerability data, and real-world effectiveness studies were systematically evaluated. **Results:** The revised algorithm maintains stimulants as first-line treatment but incorporates atomoxetine as an alternative first-line option for patients with comorbid tic disorders, substance abuse concerns, or stimulant intolerance. Extended-release formulations are prioritized to improve adherence. The algorithm includes specific recommendations for preschool children, emphasizing lower starting doses and more frequent monitoring. Alpha-2 agonists are positioned as adjunctive therapy for patients with partial response to stimulants. Updated safety monitoring guidelines address cardiovascular screening and growth parameters. **Conclusions:** The revised TCMAP ADHD algorithm reflects current evidence-based practice, providing clinicians with updated guidance for medication selection, dosing strategies, and safety monitoring. Implementation of these guidelines may improve treatment outcomes and reduce practice variability in pediatric ADHD pharmacotherapy.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry

title: CSF neurochemicals during tryptophan depletion in individuals with remitted depression and healthy controls.
abstract: Tryptophan depletion has been shown to induce depressive relapse in individuals with remitted major depression, suggesting persistent alterations in serotonergic function. However, the neurochemical mechanisms underlying this vulnerability remain poorly understood. This study examined cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) concentrations of serotonin metabolites and related neurotransmitter systems during acute tryptophan depletion in remitted depressed patients compared to healthy controls. Twenty-four medication-free individuals with remitted major depression (≥6 months symptom-free) and 22 matched healthy controls underwent lumbar puncture before and 7 hours after consuming either a tryptophan-depleting amino acid mixture or balanced placebo mixture in a randomized, double-blind, crossover design. CSF samples were analyzed for 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), homovanillic acid (HVA), and 3-methoxy-4-hydroxyphenylglycol (MHPG). Mood ratings were obtained using the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale. Tryptophan depletion significantly reduced CSF 5-HIAA concentrations in both groups, but the reduction was 40% greater in remitted patients compared to controls (p=0.012). This differential response correlated with transient mood deterioration (r=0.48, p=0.019). CSF HVA and MHPG levels remained unchanged following depletion in both groups. No significant changes occurred during placebo sessions. These findings demonstrate that individuals with remitted depression exhibit heightened sensitivity to tryptophan depletion at the central serotonergic level, providing neurochemical evidence for persistent alterations in serotonin metabolism that may confer vulnerability to depressive relapse. The results support the serotonin hypothesis of depression and suggest that CSF 5-HIAA response to tryptophan depletion may serve as a biomarker for depression susceptibility.
journal: European neuropsychopharmacology : the journal of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Differential effects of acute administration of SCH-23390, a D₁ receptor antagonist, and of ethanol on swimming activity, anxiety-related responses, and neurochemistry of zebrafish.
abstract: **Background:** Dopaminergic neurotransmission plays a crucial role in motor behavior and anxiety-related responses across vertebrate species. The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has emerged as a valuable model for studying neuropsychopharmacology due to its conserved neurotransmitter systems and behavioral repertoire. While ethanol's effects on zebrafish behavior are well-documented, the specific contribution of D₁ dopamine receptors to locomotor and anxiety-like behaviors remains poorly understood. **Objective:** This study examined the comparative effects of D₁ receptor blockade and ethanol exposure on zebrafish swimming behavior, anxiety-related responses, and brain neurochemistry. **Methods:** Adult zebrafish were acutely exposed to SCH-23390 (10, 50, 100 μg/L), ethanol (0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0% v/v), or vehicle control for 60 minutes. Swimming activity was assessed using automated video tracking, while anxiety-related behaviors were evaluated in a novel tank diving test. Whole-brain levels of dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites were quantified using HPLC-ED. **Results:** SCH-23390 dose-dependently reduced total swimming distance (F₂,₄₅=12.4, p<0.001) and average velocity, while increasing bottom-dwelling time, indicative of heightened anxiety-like behavior. Ethanol produced biphasic effects, with low concentrations increasing activity and reducing anxiety-related responses, while high concentrations decreased locomotion. Neurochemically, SCH-23390 significantly elevated dopamine levels (142% of control, p<0.01) and reduced DOPAC/DA ratios, whereas ethanol decreased serotonin turnover at all concentrations tested. **Conclusions:** These findings demonstrate that D₁ receptor signaling is essential for normal locomotor activity and anxiety regulation in zebrafish, with distinct neurochemical profiles compared to ethanol-induced behavioral changes.

title: Microfluidics: a new cosset for neurobiology.
abstract: Recently, microfluidic systems have shown great potential in the study of molecular and cellular biology. With its excellent properties, such as miniaturization, integration and automation, to name just a few, microfluidics creates new opportunities for the spatial and temporal control of cell growth and environmental stimuli in vitro. In the field of neuroscience, microfluidic devices offer precise control of the microenvironment surrounding individual cells, and the delivery of biochemical or physical cues to neural networks or single neurons. The intent of this review is to outline recent advances in microfluidic-based applications in neurobiology, with emphasis on neuron culture, neuron manipulation, neural stem cell differentiation, neuropharmacology, neuroelectrophysiology, and neuron biosensors. It also aims to stimulate development of microfluidic-based applications in neurobiology by involving scientists from various disciplines, especially neurobiology and microtechnology.

title: Plate reader-based assays for measuring cell viability, neuroprotection and calcium in primary neuronal cultures.
abstract: Drug discovery and development efforts critically rely on cell-based assays for high-throughput screening. These assay systems mostly utilize immortalized cell lines, such as human embryonic kidney cells, and can provide information on cytotoxicity and cell viability, permeability and uptake of compounds as well as receptor pharmacology. While this approach has proven extremely useful for single-target pharmacology, there is an urgent need for neuropharmacological studies to screen novel drug candidates in a cellular environment resembles neurons in vivo more closely, in order to gain insight into the involvement of multiple signaling pathways. Primary cultured neuronal cells, such as cortical neurons, have long been used for basic research and low-throughput screening and assay development, and may thus be suitable candidates for the development of neuropharmacological high-throughput screening approaches. We here developed and optimized protocols for the use of primary cortical neuronal cells in high-throughput assays for neuropharmacology and neuroprotection, including calcium mobilization, cytotoxicity and viability as well as ion channel pharmacology. Our data show low inter-experimental variability and similar reproducibility as conventional cell line assays. We conclude that primary neuronal cultures provide a viable alternative to cell lines in high-throughput assay systems by providing a cellular environment more closely resembling physiological conditions in the central nervous system.

title: An Update on the Neurochemistry of Essential Tremor.
abstract: The pathophysiology and neurochemical mechanisms of essential tremor (ET) are not fully understood, because only a few post-mortem studies have been reported, and there is a lack of good experimental model for this disease. The main aim of this review is to update data regarding the neurochemical features of ET. Alterations of certain catecholamine systems, the dopaminergic, serotonergic, GABAergic, noradrenergic, and adrenergic systems have been described, and are the object of this revision. For this purpose, we performed a literature review on alterations of the neurotransmitter or neuromodulator systems (catecholamines, gammaaminobutyric acid or GABA, excitatory amino acids, adenosine, T-type calcium channels) in ET patients (both post-mortem or in vivo) or in experimental models resembling ET. The most consistent data regarding neurochemistry of ET are related with the GABAergic and glutamatergic systems, with a lesser contribution of adenosine and dopaminergic and adrenergic systems, while there is not enough evidence of a definite role of other neurotransmitter systems in ET. The improvement of harmaline-induced tremor in rodent models achieved with T-type calcium channel antagonists, cannabinoid 1 receptor, sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor agonists, and gap-junction blockers, suggests a potential role of these structures in the pathogenesis of ET.

title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning II: nicotinic and muscarinic modulations of atropine-induced disruption of the degraded contingency effect.
abstract: **Background and rationale:** Previous studies have demonstrated that systemic atropine administration disrupts the degraded contingency effect in auditory fear conditioning, suggesting that cholinergic signaling is critical for detecting predictive relationships between conditioned and unconditioned stimuli. However, the relative contributions of nicotinic and muscarinic receptor subtypes to this phenomenon remain unclear. **Objectives:** We investigated whether selective nicotinic or muscarinic receptor manipulations could modulate atropine-induced deficits in degraded contingency learning. **Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received auditory fear conditioning under either paired or degraded contingency conditions (50% CS-US pairing probability with additional unpaired USs). Animals received systemic injections of atropine (50 mg/kg), nicotine (0.5 mg/kg), scopolamine (1.0 mg/kg), mecamylamine (3.0 mg/kg), or vehicle 30 minutes prior to training. Freezing responses were measured during CS presentations 24 hours post-training. **Results:** Vehicle-treated rats showed significantly reduced freezing to the CS under degraded versus paired conditions, confirming intact contingency detection. Atropine eliminated this discrimination, with degraded contingency animals showing freezing levels comparable to paired controls. Co-administration of nicotine partially rescued the degraded contingency effect in atropine-treated rats, while scopolamine alone replicated atropine's effects. Mecamylamine had no significant impact on contingency learning. **Conclusions:** These findings suggest that muscarinic receptors are primarily responsible for contingency detection in fear learning, while nicotinic receptor activation can partially compensate for broad cholinergic blockade. This supports a model where cholinergic modulation of attention and associative learning involves complementary muscarinic and nicotinic mechanisms.

title: Cognitive and neurodevelopmental effects of antiepileptic drugs.
abstract: Antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) are essential for seizure control in pediatric epilepsy, yet concerns persist regarding their potential impact on cognitive development. This systematic review examined the neurocognitive effects of commonly prescribed AEDs in children and adolescents with epilepsy. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of studies published between 1990-2010, identifying 47 controlled studies examining cognitive outcomes in pediatric patients receiving monotherapy with phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, or topiramate. Primary outcomes included measures of IQ, memory, attention, executive function, and academic achievement. Secondary analyses examined dose-dependent effects and reversibility of cognitive impairment. Results demonstrated significant heterogeneity across AEDs in their cognitive profiles. Traditional AEDs, particularly phenytoin and high-dose valproate, were associated with decreased processing speed (effect size d = -0.42, p < 0.01) and impaired verbal memory (d = -0.38, p < 0.05). Topiramate showed the most pronounced negative effects on language function and working memory (d = -0.65, p < 0.001). In contrast, lamotrigine and levetiracetam exhibited minimal cognitive impact, with some studies suggesting modest improvements in attention. Dose-response relationships were evident for most AEDs, with cognitive impairment becoming more pronounced at higher serum concentrations. Importantly, cognitive effects were largely reversible upon drug discontinuation, though recovery was variable. These findings underscore the importance of considering cognitive outcomes when selecting AEDs for pediatric patients, favoring newer agents with more favorable neurocognitive profiles when clinically appropriate.

title: Three-dimensional patterning in biomedicine: Importance and applications in neuropharmacology.
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) patterning technologies have emerged as transformative tools in neuropharmacology research, enabling precise spatial control over drug delivery and cellular microenvironments. Traditional two-dimensional culture systems inadequately recapitulate the complex architecture of neural tissues, limiting the translation of pharmacological findings to clinical applications. This review examines recent advances in 3D biomaterial patterning techniques and their applications in neuropharmacological studies. We analyzed peer-reviewed literature from 2010-2018 focusing on 3D printing, microfluidics, hydrogel patterning, and electrospinning approaches for neural drug delivery systems. Key applications include gradient-based drug screening platforms, spatially-controlled release systems for neuroprotective agents, and biomimetic blood-brain barrier models. 3D-printed polylactic acid scaffolds demonstrated sustained release of brain-derived neurotrophic factor over 28 days, while microfluidic devices enabled real-time monitoring of drug penetration across engineered neural barriers. Hydrogel-based systems showed superior cell viability (>85%) compared to conventional cultures (65%) when evaluating neuroprotective compounds. Electrospun nanofiber matrices facilitated directional axonal growth and localized delivery of anti-inflammatory drugs in spinal cord injury models. These technologies significantly improved drug screening accuracy, reduced animal testing requirements, and enhanced our understanding of neuropharmacological mechanisms. Future developments in 4D printing and smart biomaterials promise even greater precision in temporal drug release patterns. 3D patterning represents a paradigm shift in neuropharmacology, offering unprecedented control over drug-tissue interactions and accelerating the development of targeted neurotherapeutics.
journal: Journal of biomedical materials research. Part B, Applied biomaterials

title: Alotamide A, a novel neuropharmacological agent from the marine cyanobacterium Lyngbya bouillonii.
abstract: Alotamide A (1), a structurally intriguing cyclic depsipeptide, was isolated from the marine mat-forming cyanobacterium Lyngbya bouillonii collected in Papua New Guinea. It features three contiguous peptidic residues and an unsaturated heptaketide with oxidations and methylations unlike those found in any other marine cyanobacterial metabolite. Pure alotamide A (1) displays an unusual calcium influx activation profile in murine cerebrocortical neurons with an EC50 of 4.18 microM.

title: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology, yesterday, today and tomorrow. A conversation with Michel Hamon.
abstract: Experimental neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation over the past five decades, evolving from rudimentary behavioral observations to sophisticated molecular investigations. This retrospective analysis examines the field's historical trajectory, current methodological advances, and future directions through comprehensive review of seminal contributions and emerging paradigms. We conducted a systematic evaluation of neuropsychopharmacological research milestones spanning 1970-2020, analyzing publications, methodological innovations, and translational outcomes. Key developments were assessed across three temporal phases: classical pharmacological approaches (1970-1990), molecular revolution period (1990-2010), and contemporary integrative era (2010-present). Historical analysis revealed fundamental shifts from receptor binding studies and behavioral pharmacology toward multi-omics approaches, optogenetics, and precision medicine strategies. The integration of neuroimaging, electrophysiology, and genetic tools has enabled unprecedented mechanistic insights into drug action and neural circuit function. Contemporary methodologies including CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing, single-cell transcriptomics, and computational modeling have revolutionized target identification and validation processes. Current challenges include translational gaps between preclinical models and clinical efficacy, personalized treatment approaches, and ethical considerations in neuropharmacological research. Future directions emphasize systems-level understanding of brain-drug interactions, artificial intelligence-driven drug discovery, and biomarker-guided therapeutic development. The field's evolution demonstrates successful transition from empirical observations to mechanistically-informed drug development, positioning experimental neuropsychopharmacology as a cornerstone of modern psychiatric and neurological therapeutics. Continued integration of technological advances with rigorous experimental design will be essential for addressing unmet clinical needs in brain disorders.

title: Migraine-associated vertigo: diagnosis and treatment.
abstract: Migraine-associated vertigo (MAV) represents a frequently underdiagnosed neurological condition affecting approximately 1% of the general population, with higher prevalence among migraine sufferers. The pathophysiological mechanisms linking vestibular dysfunction to migraine remain poorly understood, complicating both diagnosis and therapeutic management. This comprehensive review examines current diagnostic criteria and evidence-based treatment approaches for MAV based on analysis of 127 published studies from 2000-2009. We evaluated diagnostic methodologies including vestibular testing, neuroimaging, and clinical assessment tools, alongside therapeutic interventions ranging from lifestyle modifications to pharmacological treatments. Our analysis reveals significant heterogeneity in diagnostic approaches, with only 34% of studies utilizing standardized criteria such as those proposed by the International Headache Society. Vestibular function testing showed abnormalities in 68% of MAV patients, most commonly involving reduced caloric responses and abnormal vestibulo-ocular reflex parameters. Treatment efficacy varied considerably across interventions, with tricyclic antidepressants demonstrating response rates of 65-78%, anticonvulsants showing 52-71% efficacy, and calcium channel blockers achieving 45-62% improvement in vertigo symptoms. Combination therapies incorporating dietary modifications and vestibular rehabilitation exercises enhanced overall outcomes by 23-35%. These findings highlight the need for standardized diagnostic criteria and individualized treatment protocols. Future research should focus on elucidating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying MAV and developing targeted therapeutic strategies to improve patient outcomes in this challenging clinical population.

title: A Focused Review on the Treatment of Pediatric Patients with Atypical Antipsychotics.
abstract: Atypical antipsychotics are increasingly prescribed to pediatric patients for various psychiatric conditions, yet comprehensive safety and efficacy data in this population remain limited. The unique developmental neurobiology of children and adolescents may confer differential treatment responses and adverse event profiles compared to adults. This focused review synthesizes current evidence regarding the clinical use of second-generation antipsychotics in pediatric populations aged 6-17 years. A systematic literature search was conducted using PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases from 2008-2017, identifying randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series examining aripiprazole, olanzapine, quetiapine, risperidone, paliperidone, and ziprasidone in pediatric patients. Primary outcomes included efficacy measures for FDA-approved indications (schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, autism spectrum disorders, and Tourette's disorder) and safety profiles including metabolic parameters, extrapyramidal symptoms, and prolactin elevation. Twenty-three studies encompassing 2,847 pediatric patients were included. Risperidone and aripiprazole demonstrated robust efficacy for irritability associated with autism spectrum disorders, while olanzapine and quetiapine showed comparable efficacy to adults for bipolar mania. Significant safety concerns emerged, including substantial weight gain (mean 4.2-8.7 kg over 6-12 weeks), metabolic dysregulation in 15-35% of patients, and hyperprolactinemia with risperidone and paliperidone. Extrapyramidal symptoms occurred less frequently than with typical antipsychotics but remained clinically significant. These findings support careful risk-benefit consideration when prescribing atypical antipsychotics to pediatric patients, emphasizing the need for regular metabolic monitoring and exploration of non-pharmacological interventions as first-line treatments when appropriate.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology

title: Recent Updates in Psychopharmacology for the Core and Associated Symptoms of Autism Spectrum Disorder.
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1 in 54 children and is characterized by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction alongside restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior. While no FDA-approved medications exist for core ASD symptoms, psychopharmacological interventions are frequently employed to manage associated symptoms including irritability, hyperactivity, anxiety, and sleep disturbances. This comprehensive review examines recent developments in ASD psychopharmacology from 2018-2021, analyzing emerging therapeutic targets and novel treatment approaches. We conducted a systematic literature review of randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published in major psychiatric and neurodevelopmental journals. PubMed, Embase, and PsycINFO databases were searched using terms related to autism, psychopharmacology, and specific medication classes. Recent evidence supports continued use of risperidone and aripiprazole for severe irritability and aggression, with emerging data on lurasidone and cariprazine showing promise. Novel approaches targeting core social communication deficits include oxytocin analogs, with mixed results from recent trials. Cannabidiol has shown preliminary efficacy for irritability and hyperactivity in small controlled studies. Melatonin receptor agonists demonstrate consistent benefits for sleep disorders, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain controversial for repetitive behaviors due to activation concerns in pediatric populations. Glutamate modulators and gamma-aminobutyric acid enhancers represent emerging therapeutic frontiers. Current evidence emphasizes individualized treatment approaches targeting specific symptom domains rather than global ASD features. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided therapy selection and combination treatment strategies to optimize outcomes while minimizing adverse effects.

title: Neuropharmacologic correlates of deglutition: lessons from fictive swallowing.
abstract: The neurochemical mechanisms underlying deglutition remain poorly understood despite the critical importance of swallowing function. Recent advances in fictive swallowing preparations have provided new opportunities to examine the neuropharmacologic basis of this complex motor behavior. We investigated the role of various neurotransmitter systems in the generation and modulation of fictive swallowing patterns using an in vitro brainstem preparation from adult cats. Fictive swallowing was induced by electrical stimulation of the superior laryngeal nerve while recording from hypoglossal and recurrent laryngeal nerve branches. Bath application of neurotransmitter agonists and antagonists was used to assess their effects on swallowing motor patterns. Serotonin (5-HT) application significantly enhanced the amplitude and duration of fictive swallowing bursts, while the 5-HT2 receptor antagonist ketanserin abolished these facilitatory effects. GABA application dose-dependently suppressed swallowing activity, with complete inhibition occurring at concentrations above 100 μM. The GABAA receptor antagonist bicuculline partially reversed GABA-induced suppression and spontaneously increased swallowing frequency. Dopamine produced biphasic effects, initially facilitating then suppressing swallowing patterns at higher concentrations. Acetylcholine consistently enhanced swallowing activity through muscarinic mechanisms, as atropine blocked these effects while nicotinic antagonists had minimal impact. These findings demonstrate that fictive swallowing is modulated by multiple neurotransmitter systems, with serotonin and acetylcholine providing excitatory drive while GABA exerts inhibitory control. Understanding these neuropharmacologic correlates may inform therapeutic approaches for dysphagia associated with neurologic disorders.

title: Subcellular probes for neurochemical recording from multiple brain sites.
abstract: Spatially resolved neurochemical measurements are essential for understanding complex brain signaling networks, yet current approaches are limited by probe dimensions and multiplexing capabilities. Traditional microelectrodes lack the spatial resolution to distinguish subcellular compartments, while existing microsensor arrays cannot simultaneously monitor neurochemicals across distant brain regions. Here we report the development of ultrasmall carbon fiber microelectrodes (2-5 μm diameter) integrated with multiplexed recording capabilities for real-time neurochemical detection from multiple brain sites. We fabricated 16-channel arrays using photolithographic patterning of carbon fiber bundles coupled to miniaturized potentiostat circuits. The devices were characterized using cyclic voltammetry and demonstrated selective detection of dopamine, serotonin, and adenosine with sub-second temporal resolution. In vivo validation was performed in anesthetized rats (n=8) with simultaneous recordings from striatum, nucleus accumbens, and prefrontal cortex during electrical stimulation and behavioral paradigms. Our microsensor arrays detected stimulus-evoked dopamine release with 50 nM sensitivity and spatial resolution sufficient to distinguish synaptic versus extrasynaptic signaling within individual brain nuclei. Cross-correlation analysis revealed coordinated neurochemical dynamics between brain regions that were previously undetectable with single-site measurements. The multiplexed recordings demonstrated region-specific differences in neurotransmitter kinetics and revealed novel patterns of inter-regional neurochemical communication. This technology enables unprecedented spatial and temporal mapping of brain neurochemistry, providing new insights into distributed neural network function and offering significant advantages for neuropharmacological research and therapeutic monitoring applications.

title: Past and future of neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The field of neuropsychopharmacology has undergone remarkable transformation since the serendipitous discovery of chlorpromazine's antipsychotic properties in the 1950s. This review examines the historical development of psychotropic medications and projects future directions based on emerging neuroscientific understanding. We analyzed the evolution of major drug classes including antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers through comprehensive literature review spanning 1950-1989, evaluating therapeutic efficacy, mechanism elucidation, and adverse effect profiles. Additionally, we assessed contemporary research trends in receptor pharmacology, neurotransmitter systems, and molecular biology techniques. Historical analysis reveals three distinct phases: the empirical discovery period (1950s-1960s), characterized by phenothiazines and tricyclic antidepressants; the mechanistic understanding era (1970s-1980s), marked by dopamine hypothesis development and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor introduction; and the emerging molecular phase (late 1980s), featuring receptor subtype characterization. Current limitations include incomplete understanding of psychiatric disease etiology, significant adverse effects, and variable therapeutic response. Future developments likely include: selective receptor subtype modulators based on advancing molecular pharmacology; neuropeptide-targeted therapeutics; drugs addressing specific neurotransmitter pathway dysfunctions; and personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenetic principles. The integration of modern neuroscience methodologies with traditional pharmacological approaches promises more effective, better-tolerated treatments. Success will depend upon continued collaboration between clinical psychiatry, basic neuroscience, and pharmaceutical research, potentially revolutionizing psychiatric therapeutics within the next two decades.

title: Neuropharmacological properties of Trichosanthes dioica root.
abstract: The roots of Trichosanthes dioica Roxb. have been traditionally used in Chinese folk medicine for treating neurological disorders, yet their neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the neuroprotective and psychoactive properties of T. dioica root extracts using established in vivo and in vitro models. Aqueous and methanolic extracts were prepared from dried T. dioica roots and standardized using HPLC analysis. Male Wistar rats (n=8 per group) received oral administration of extracts (100, 200, and 400 mg/kg) for behavioral assessments including elevated plus maze, forced swimming test, and Morris water maze. Neuroprotective effects were evaluated using H2O2-induced oxidative stress in primary cortical neurons. GABA and dopamine receptor binding affinities were determined through radioligand binding assays. Phytochemical analysis revealed significant concentrations of cucurbitacins, flavonoids, and triterpene saponins. The methanolic extract demonstrated dose-dependent anxiolytic activity in the elevated plus maze test (p<0.01) and improved spatial memory performance in the Morris water maze compared to controls. In the forced swimming test, both extracts significantly reduced immobility time, suggesting antidepressant-like effects. Neuronal viability increased by 34% and 28% with aqueous and methanolic extracts respectively against H2O2-induced cytotoxicity. Moderate binding affinity was observed for GABAA receptors (Ki = 145 μM) but not dopaminergic receptors. These findings suggest that T. dioica root possesses significant neuropharmacological properties, potentially mediated through GABAergic modulation and antioxidant mechanisms, supporting its traditional therapeutic applications in neuropsychiatric conditions.

title: Neurogenic bladder model for spinal cord injury: spinal cord microdialysis and chronic urodynamics.
abstract: Neurogenic bladder dysfunction represents a major clinical complication following spinal cord injury (SCI), yet the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. We developed a combined approach utilizing spinal cord microdialysis with chronic urodynamic monitoring to investigate neurotransmitter dynamics in a rat model of SCI-induced neurogenic bladder. Adult female Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) underwent complete T10 spinal cord transection, with sham-operated controls (n=12). Four weeks post-injury, microdialysis probes were implanted in the L6-S1 spinal cord segments, and chronic bladder catheters were placed for simultaneous urodynamic recordings.
