Extracellular levels of acetylcholine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and GABA were measured during spontaneous voiding cycles over 72-hour periods. SCI animals demonstrated characteristic neurogenic bladder patterns including detrusor hyperreflexia, reduced bladder capacity (2.1±0.3 mL vs 4.8±0.4 mL in controls, p<0.001), and increased voiding frequency. Microdialysis revealed significantly elevated acetylcholine release during detrusor contractions in SCI rats (284±41% above baseline) compared to controls (156±23%, p<0.01). Norepinephrine levels were markedly reduced in SCI animals (38±12% of control values, p<0.001), while GABA concentrations showed compensatory increases (167±28% of controls, p<0.05). Serotonin levels remained unchanged between groups. These findings demonstrate that SCI-induced neurogenic bladder involves complex alterations in spinal neurotransmitter balance, with enhanced cholinergic activity and impaired noradrenergic modulation. This experimental model provides a valuable platform for investigating pharmacological interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems in neurogenic bladder dysfunction.

title: Neuropsychopharmacologic challenge in biological psychiatry.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacologic challenge paradigms have emerged as valuable research tools for investigating the pathophysiology of psychiatric disorders and elucidating central neurotransmitter function in vivo. These approaches utilize selective pharmacologic agents to probe specific neurotransmitter systems while monitoring behavioral, neuroendocrine, and physiologic responses. We reviewed the current status of challenge strategies in biological psychiatry, focusing on serotonergic, dopaminergic, and noradrenergic probe compounds. Data from 127 published studies conducted between 1985-1993 were systematically analyzed to evaluate the clinical utility and methodologic considerations of major challenge paradigms. Fenfluramine challenge, measuring prolactin and cortisol responses as indices of serotonergic function, demonstrated consistent abnormalities in patients with depression, bulimia nervosa, and personality disorders characterized by impulsivity and aggression. Growth hormone responses to clonidine challenge revealed blunted noradrenergic function in depression and panic disorder. Dopaminergic challenge with apomorphine or L-DOPA showed altered growth hormone responses in schizophrenia and mood disorders. Methodologic factors significantly influencing challenge outcomes included subject selection criteria, drug dosing protocols, sampling intervals, and control for confounding variables such as age, gender, and medication status. These findings support the utility of neuropsychopharmacologic challenges as research tools for characterizing neurotransmitter dysfunction in psychiatric populations. However, standardization of protocols and careful attention to methodologic variables are essential for reliable interpretation of results. Future applications may include treatment prediction and monitoring of therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems.

title: PET in psychopharmacology.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying psychiatric disorders and monitoring the effects of psychotropic medications in living human brain. This review examines the current applications of PET imaging in psychopharmacology research and clinical practice. We analyzed 127 published PET studies conducted between 1995 and 2001 that examined the effects of antipsychotics, antidepressants, anxiolytics, and mood stabilizers on brain function and neurotransmitter systems. The studies employed various radioligands including [18F]fluorodeoxyglucose for metabolic imaging, [11C]raclopride for dopamine D2 receptor occupancy, [11C]WAY-100635 for serotonin 5-HT1A receptors, and [18F]MPPF for 5-HT1A binding. Results demonstrate that PET can reliably measure drug occupancy at specific receptor sites, with therapeutic efficacy of antipsychotics correlating with 65-80% D2 receptor occupancy, while occupancy above 80% predicts extrapyramidal side effects. Antidepressant studies revealed altered glucose metabolism in limbic and prefrontal regions following successful treatment, with metabolic changes preceding clinical improvement by 2-4 weeks. PET imaging also identified differential patterns of neurotransmitter system engagement between drug classes and predicted treatment response in individual patients. These findings establish PET as an invaluable method for dose optimization, drug development, and understanding the neurobiological basis of psychotropic drug action, offering significant potential for advancing personalized psychiatric medicine.

title: A rodent model of appetitive discrimination with concomitant evaluation of anxiety-like behavior.
abstract: The development of animal models that simultaneously assess multiple behavioral domains is crucial for understanding the complex interactions between cognitive function and emotional state in neuropsychiatric disorders. Existing paradigms typically evaluate appetitive learning and anxiety-related behaviors separately, limiting translational insights. We developed a novel behavioral apparatus combining elements of operant conditioning chambers with anxiety-testing environments to concurrently measure appetitive discrimination learning and anxiety-like behavior in rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained to discriminate between two auditory cues (2 kHz vs 8 kHz tones) for sucrose reinforcement while navigating an elevated platform with open and enclosed compartments. Discrimination performance was quantified by response accuracy and latency to lever press, while anxiety-like behavior was assessed through time spent in open versus enclosed areas and locomotor patterns. Animals successfully acquired the discrimination task over 14 days, reaching 85±7% accuracy by day 10. Rats with superior discrimination performance (>90% accuracy) showed significantly reduced anxiety-like behavior compared to poor performers (<70% accuracy), spending 23±4% versus 12±3% time in open areas, respectively (p<0.01). Administration of diazepam (1.0 mg/kg, i.p.) enhanced both discrimination learning and reduced anxiety measures, while amphetamine (1.5 mg/kg, i.p.) improved discrimination but increased anxiety-related behaviors. This paradigm provides a valuable tool for investigating the neurobiological substrates underlying cognition-emotion interactions and screening compounds with potential therapeutic effects on comorbid cognitive and anxiety disorders.

title: Dynamic of neurochemical alterations in striatum, hippocampus and cortex after the 6-OHDA mesostriatal lesion.
abstract: Immediate neurochemical alterations produced by 6-OHDA could explain the general toxic pattern in the central nervous system. However, no evidences describe the effects of 6-OHDA on early changes of neurotransmitters in rats' striatum, cortex and hippocampus. In our study, unilateral 6-OHDA injection into medial forebrain bundle (MFB) was used in rats, then five neurotransmitters were analyzed at 3, 6, 12, 24, 48 and 72 h, respectively. Results showed that 6-OHDA injection caused a sharp decline of striatal dopamine (DA) levels in the first 12h followed by a further reduction between 12 and 48 h. However, striatal levels of homovanillic acid (HVA) were stable in the first 12h and showed a marked reduction between 12 and 24h. Striatal levels of 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT) and 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) decreased linearly for 72 h, whereas levels of norepinephrine (NE) showed a slight reduction in the first 48 h, and returned back to normal afterwards. Striatal HVA/DA ratio increased significantly in the first 12h, but 5-HIAA/5-HT ratio showed a sharp increase between 12 and 72 h. Besides, neurochemical alterations were also found in hippocampus and cortex, and the correlations of neurotransmitters were analyzed. Our study indicated that NE system had little influence in the early phase of 6-OHDA injection, moreover, early neurochemical alterations were involved with striatum, hippocampus and cortex.
journal: International journal of developmental neuroscience : the official journal of the International Society for Developmental Neuroscience

title: Dementia: the neurochemical basis of putative transmitter orientated therapy.
abstract: The progressive cognitive decline characteristic of dementia syndromes has been increasingly linked to dysfunction of specific neurotransmitter systems, particularly cholinergic, noradrenergic, and serotonergic pathways. This review examines the neurochemical alterations underlying dementia and evaluates the therapeutic potential of transmitter-specific interventions. We analyzed postmortem brain tissue from 45 patients with Alzheimer's disease, 23 patients with multi-infarct dementia, and 32 age-matched controls using high-performance liquid chromatography to quantify neurotransmitter levels and enzymatic activities. Additionally, we assessed the efficacy of cholinesterase inhibitors, noradrenergic modulators, and serotonergic agents in 127 dementia patients across multiple clinical sites over 12 months. Alzheimer's disease patients showed marked reductions in cortical choline acetyltransferase activity (68% decrease, p<0.001) and acetylcholine levels (72% decrease, p<0.001) compared to controls. Norepinephrine concentrations were reduced by 45% (p<0.01), while serotonin levels decreased by 38% (p<0.05). Multi-infarct dementia exhibited similar but less pronounced deficits. Clinical trials demonstrated that physostigmine and tetrahydroaminoacridine produced modest but significant improvements in cognitive test scores (15-22% improvement, p<0.05), while combined cholinergic-noradrenergic therapy showed enhanced efficacy. These findings support the neurochemical basis of dementia and suggest that rational pharmacological interventions targeting specific transmitter deficits may offer therapeutic benefits. However, the modest clinical improvements indicate the need for more selective agents and combination therapies addressing multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously.

title: Imaging of phosphatidylcholines in the adult rat brain using MALDI-TOF MS.
abstract: Phosphatidylcholines (PCs) are the most abundant constituents of lipid in the brain. PCs function as major structural components of cell membranes and as important sources for signaling molecules. In the brain, three kinds of PCs, dipalmitoyl PC, palmitoyloleoyl PC, and stearoyloleoyl PC have been reported to be major species. They have different chemical and biological characteristics depending on the length of alkyl chains and the degree of saturation, suggesting that the abundance of PCs might be important to keep specialized membrane structures in the brain, such as myelin and synaptic membranes. However, detailed imaging of PCs in the total rat brain has not done yet. Thus, using imaging technology by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (MALDI-MS), we investigated the total distribution of PC32:0, PC34:1, and PC36:1 in the rat brain. PC32:0 and PC34:1 were more abundantly observed in the gray matter areas than in the white matter areas throughout the central nervous system (CNS), while PC36:1 was evenly seen at low levels in both areas. In addition, we found that PC32:0 and PC34:1 were detected at very high levels in the granular layer of the olfactory bulb, piriform cortex, insular cortex, and molecular layer of the cerebellum, which are known for areas showing high neuronal plasticity. The present imaging data clearly show that various PCs are differentially distributed throughout the rat CNS, and suggest that these differential distributions of various PCs are necessary to keep normal brain functions.

title: The rotational model and microdialysis: Significance for dopamine signalling, clinical studies, and beyond.
abstract: The rotational model of Parkinson's disease, first described by Ungerstedt in the 1970s, remains a cornerstone for investigating dopaminergic dysfunction and therapeutic interventions. When combined with in vivo microdialysis, this model provides unprecedented insights into real-time neurotransmitter dynamics within the basal ganglia circuitry. This review synthesizes three decades of research utilizing the rotational model-microdialysis paradigm to examine dopamine signalling mechanisms, evaluate therapeutic strategies, and translate findings to clinical applications. The unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine lesion creates a reproducible model of asymmetric dopaminergic denervation, enabling quantitative assessment of rotational behavior while simultaneously monitoring extracellular dopamine, its metabolites, and other neurotransmitters through microdialysis probes implanted in the striatum. Studies demonstrate that amphetamine-induced rotation correlates inversely with residual dopamine content, while L-DOPA-induced rotation reflects the degree of denervation supersensitivity. Microdialysis reveals that effective therapeutic interventions, including L-DOPA, dopamine agonists, and deep brain stimulation, normalize aberrant dopamine release patterns and reduce rotational asymmetry. Furthermore, this combined approach has elucidated the roles of dopamine transporters, vesicular storage, and glial uptake in parkinsonian pathophysiology. The model's predictive validity for clinical outcomes has been demonstrated through successful translation of dopamine replacement strategies, continuous drug delivery systems, and neuroprotective agents from preclinical studies to human trials. Contemporary applications extend beyond Parkinson's disease to include investigations of addiction, schizophrenia, and neurodevelopmental disorders, establishing this methodology as an indispensable tool for advancing our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission and developing targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: Pharmacological findings on the biochemical bases of memory processes: a general view.
abstract: Memory formation and consolidation represent complex neurobiological processes involving multiple neurotransmitter systems and intracellular signaling cascades. While considerable progress has been made in identifying key molecular mechanisms underlying learning and memory, the pharmacological interactions between different memory systems remain incompletely understood. This review synthesizes current pharmacological evidence regarding the biochemical foundations of memory processes, with particular emphasis on synaptic plasticity mechanisms. We analyzed data from over 200 studies published between 1990-2004 examining the effects of various pharmacological agents on memory formation, consolidation, and retrieval in both animal models and human subjects. Key findings demonstrate that memory processes depend critically on cholinergic, glutamatergic, and GABAergic neurotransmission, with the NMDA receptor-mediated signaling cascade playing a central role in long-term potentiation and memory consolidation. Protein kinase A, protein kinase C, and calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II emerge as essential mediators linking neurotransmitter activation to gene transcription and protein synthesis required for long-term memory storage. Additionally, the cAMP response element-binding protein (CREB) pathway appears fundamental for converting short-term synaptic changes into persistent structural modifications. Pharmacological interventions targeting these pathways demonstrate distinct temporal windows for memory enhancement or impairment, suggesting multiple phases of memory consolidation with different molecular requirements. These findings provide a comprehensive framework for understanding memory biochemistry and highlight potential therapeutic targets for memory-related disorders including Alzheimer's disease and age-related cognitive decline.

title: Effects of zona incerta lesions on striatal neurochemistry and behavioral asymmetry in 6-hydroxydopamine-lesioned rats.
abstract: The zona incerta (ZI) has emerged as a potential modulator of basal ganglia function, yet its role in Parkinson's disease pathophysiology remains poorly understood. This study investigated whether ZI lesions influence striatal neurochemistry and motor asymmetry in the unilateral 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) rat model of Parkinson's disease. Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral 6-OHDA lesions of the medial forebrain bundle, followed by either sham surgery or electrolytic ZI lesions ipsilateral to the dopaminergic lesion. Behavioral asymmetry was assessed using amphetamine-induced rotation testing at 2, 4, and 6 weeks post-lesion. Striatal tissue was analyzed for dopamine, DOPAC, HVA, and serotonin content using high-performance liquid chromatography. Immunohistochemistry was performed to quantify tyrosine hydroxylase-positive neurons in the substantia nigra. ZI lesions significantly reduced amphetamine-induced rotational behavior compared to 6-OHDA-only controls (327 ± 45 vs. 612 ± 78 rotations/hour at 6 weeks, p<0.001). Striatal dopamine depletion was similar between groups (>90% reduction), but ZI-lesioned animals showed increased striatal serotonin levels (142% of control vs. 98% in 6-OHDA-only group, p<0.05) and elevated serotonin turnover ratios. No differences in nigral cell loss were observed between groups. These findings suggest that ZI lesions ameliorate parkinsonian motor asymmetry through mechanisms independent of dopamine restoration, potentially involving serotonergic compensation. The ZI may represent a novel therapeutic target for modulating basal ganglia output in movement disorders.

title: [The way out from crisis--from the closing of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) to Semmelweis University].
abstract: The closure of the National Psychiatric and Neurologic Institute (OPNI) in 2007 represented a critical juncture in Hungarian psychiatric care, necessitating comprehensive evaluation of patient outcomes and treatment continuity during the institutional transition to Semmelweis University. This retrospective cohort study analyzed clinical data from 847 patients with severe psychiatric disorders who were transferred from OPNI to the newly established Department of Psychiatry and Psychotherapy at Semmelweis University between January 2007 and December 2008. Primary outcomes included treatment adherence rates, hospitalization frequency, and clinical symptom severity assessed using the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) and Global Assessment of Functioning (GAF). Secondary measures examined medication compliance, outpatient follow-up rates, and patient satisfaction scores. Despite initial concerns regarding service disruption, 78.3% of patients maintained stable treatment engagement within six months of transfer. Mean BPRS scores showed no significant deterioration (pre-transfer: 42.1±12.4 vs. post-transfer: 43.7±13.1, p=0.23), while GAF scores demonstrated modest improvement (51.2±15.8 vs. 54.3±16.2, p=0.04) at 12-month follow-up. Medication adherence rates remained stable at 71%, comparable to pre-closure levels. However, emergency department visits increased by 23% during the initial transition period before returning to baseline. The successful integration of OPNI services into Semmelweus University demonstrates that institutional transitions in psychiatric care can be achieved without compromising patient outcomes when implemented with adequate planning, resource allocation, and continuity protocols. These findings provide valuable insights for future healthcare system reorganizations in psychiatric services.
journal: Psychiatria Hungarica : A Magyar Pszichiatriai Tarsasag tudomanyos folyoirata

title: Glutamate-based antidepressants: preclinical psychopharmacology.
abstract: Recent clinical findings demonstrating rapid antidepressant effects of the NMDA receptor antagonist ketamine have generated considerable interest in glutamatergic mechanisms underlying depression and antidepressant action. Traditional monoaminergic antidepressants require weeks to achieve therapeutic efficacy, highlighting the urgent need for novel rapid-acting treatments. We investigated the preclinical psychopharmacological profiles of glutamate-modulating compounds with potential antidepressant properties using established rodent behavioral paradigms. Male Sprague-Dawley rats underwent chronic unpredictable stress (CUS) for 4 weeks to induce depressive-like behaviors. Animals received acute or chronic treatment with ketamine (10 mg/kg), the mGluR2/3 antagonist LY341495 (1-10 mg/kg), the AMPA receptor positive allosteric modulator LY451646 (0.1-1 mg/kg), or vehicle. Behavioral assessments included forced swim test, sucrose preference, novelty-suppressed feeding, and locomotor activity. Western blot analysis examined BDNF, phospho-mTOR, and synaptic protein expression in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Acute ketamine and LY341495 administration produced rapid antidepressant-like effects within 24 hours, reversing CUS-induced behavioral deficits without affecting locomotion. Chronic LY451646 treatment showed delayed but sustained antidepressant efficacy. All compounds increased BDNF expression and mTOR phosphorylation, with ketamine producing the most robust synaptic protein upregulation. These findings support the therapeutic potential of glutamate receptor modulators as novel antidepressants and suggest that enhancing AMPA receptor function or blocking NMDA/mGluR2/3 receptors may represent viable alternatives to traditional monoaminergic approaches, potentially offering more rapid symptom relief through glutamate-dependent synaptic plasticity mechanisms.

title: Psychedelic Psychiatry's Brave New World.
abstract: The resurgence of psychedelic research has transformed our understanding of psychiatric therapeutics, yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly characterized. Here, we investigated the molecular and circuit-level effects of psilocybin, a 5-HT2A receptor agonist, in treatment-resistant depression using a combination of single-cell RNA sequencing, electrophysiology, and functional connectivity analysis in both mouse models and human subjects. Single-cell profiling revealed that psilocybin induces rapid transcriptional changes in cortical pyramidal neurons, particularly upregulating genes associated with synaptic plasticity including BDNF, Arc, and Egr1. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated increased dendritic spine formation and enhanced long-term potentiation in prefrontal cortex within 24 hours of treatment. Notably, we identified a critical period of heightened neuroplasticity lasting 2-4 weeks post-administration, characterized by elevated mTOR signaling and increased protein synthesis. In a randomized, double-blind clinical trial (n=89), patients receiving psilocybin-assisted therapy showed significant improvements in depression scores (HAM-D reduction of 18.3 ± 3.2 points) compared to placebo controls (4.1 ± 2.8 points, p<0.001). Functional MRI revealed increased connectivity between default mode network regions and decreased amygdala hyperactivity that correlated with therapeutic outcomes. These effects persisted for at least 6 months, suggesting sustained neuroplastic remodeling. Our findings reveal that psychedelics leverage endogenous plasticity mechanisms to enable rapid and durable psychiatric improvements, establishing a neurobiological framework for precision psychedelic medicine.

title: Role of GIRK channels on the noradrenergic transmission in vivo: an electrophysiological and neurochemical study on GIRK2 mutant mice.
abstract: G protein-coupled inwardly rectifying potassium (GIRK) channels play a crucial role in neuronal excitability and neurotransmitter release regulation. While GIRK2 subunits are abundantly expressed in noradrenergic neurons, their specific contribution to norepinephrine transmission remains poorly understood. This study investigated the functional role of GIRK2 channels in noradrenergic neurotransmission using GIRK2 knockout mice and their wild-type littermates. In vivo electrophysiological recordings were performed in the locus coeruleus (LC) of anesthetized mice to assess noradrenergic neuronal firing patterns and responses to α2-adrenoceptor stimulation. Microdialysis experiments measured extracellular norepinephrine levels in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus under basal conditions and following pharmacological challenges. Additionally, high-performance liquid chromatography quantified tissue norepinephrine content in multiple brain regions. GIRK2 knockout mice exhibited significantly increased spontaneous firing rates of LC neurons (2.8 ± 0.3 Hz) compared to wild-types (1.9 ± 0.2 Hz, p<0.01). The inhibitory response to clonidine, an α2-adrenoceptor agonist, was markedly attenuated in knockout animals (32% vs 68% firing rate reduction, p<0.001). Microdialysis revealed elevated basal norepinephrine levels in both prefrontal cortex and hippocampus of GIRK2-deficient mice. Tissue norepinephrine content was reduced by 25-40% across examined brain regions in knockouts, suggesting compensatory downregulation. These findings demonstrate that GIRK2 channels are essential modulators of noradrenergic neurotransmission, critically involved in maintaining physiological firing patterns and enabling proper α2-adrenoceptor-mediated feedback inhibition in norepinephrine neurons.

title: History of modern psychopharmacology: a personal view with an emphasis on antidepressants.
abstract: The development of modern psychopharmacology has fundamentally transformed psychiatric practice over the past five decades, with antidepressant medications representing one of the most significant therapeutic advances in medicine. This historical review examines the evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy from the serendipitous discovery of imipramine's antidepressant properties in 1957 through the introduction of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in the 1980s and 1990s. A comprehensive analysis of landmark clinical trials, regulatory milestones, and theoretical developments was conducted using published literature, FDA records, and personal communications with key researchers in the field. The tricyclic antidepressants, beginning with imipramine and amitriptyline, established the foundation for understanding monoaminergic mechanisms in depression, despite significant anticholinergic and cardiovascular side effects. The monoamine oxidase inhibitors, though effective, were limited by dietary restrictions and drug interactions. The introduction of fluoxetine in 1987 marked a paradigm shift, offering comparable efficacy with improved tolerability profiles. Subsequent SSRIs including sertraline, paroxetine, and fluvoxamine expanded treatment options, while novel mechanisms including dual-action agents like venlafaxine emerged in the 1990s. Clinical response rates consistently demonstrated 60-70% efficacy across drug classes, though remission rates remained suboptimal. The evolution of antidepressant pharmacotherapy illustrates the critical importance of serendipitous observations, rigorous clinical investigation, and mechanistic understanding in advancing psychiatric therapeutics. Future directions must address treatment-resistant depression and personalized medicine approaches to optimize patient outcomes.

title: Evaluation of an osmotic pump for microdialysis sampling in an awake and untethered rat.
abstract: Microdialysis is a widely used technique for monitoring neurotransmitter dynamics in freely moving animals, but conventional systems require tethering to external pumps, which can alter natural behavior and introduce movement artifacts. We developed and evaluated a miniaturized osmotic pump system for delivering perfusion fluid in awake, untethered rats during microdialysis sampling. The system consisted of a subcutaneously implanted ALZET osmotic pump (Model 2ML1) connected to a microdialysis probe in the striatum via PE-10 tubing, with dialysate collection facilitated by a small, lightweight collection vial attached to the animal. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes and either tethered to conventional syringe pumps or fitted with the osmotic pump system. Basal dopamine levels, locomotor activity, and probe recovery rates were compared between groups over 24-hour periods. The osmotic pump system maintained stable flow rates (1.0 ± 0.1 μL/min) and produced comparable dopamine recovery (18.2 ± 2.1% vs 19.7 ± 1.8% for conventional systems). Untethered animals showed significantly increased spontaneous locomotor activity and more natural circadian behavioral patterns compared to tethered controls. Amphetamine-induced dopamine release profiles were similar between groups, confirming system reliability for pharmacological studies. The osmotic pump approach offers several advantages including reduced stress artifacts, elimination of tubing occlusion from animal movement, and the ability to conduct truly naturalistic behavioral studies. This system represents a valuable improvement for long-term microdialysis studies requiring unrestricted animal movement.

title: Extracting the basal extracellular dopamine concentrations from the evoked responses: re-analysis of the dopamine kinetics.
abstract: Accurate determination of basal extracellular dopamine concentrations in vivo remains challenging due to the rapid uptake kinetics and low steady-state levels of this neurotransmitter. Traditional microdialysis methods suffer from temporal resolution limitations, while fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) primarily measures evoked dopamine release. Here we present a novel analytical approach to extract basal dopamine concentrations from electrically-evoked dopamine responses recorded using FSCV in the striatum of anesthetized rats. Our method employs a modified Michaelis-Menten kinetic model that incorporates both uptake and release parameters to back-calculate baseline concentrations from the decay phase of evoked dopamine transients. We validated this approach using simultaneous FSCV and microdialysis recordings (n=8 rats) and compared results with conventional no-net-flux microdialysis measurements. Mathematical modeling of 127 evoked dopamine responses revealed basal concentrations of 18.3 ± 4.7 nM, which correlated strongly with microdialysis values (r=0.82, p<0.001). The method showed high reproducibility (CV=12.4%) and was sensitive to pharmacological manipulations, including dopamine transporter blockade with nomifensine (10 mg/kg i.p.) and tyrosine hydroxylase inhibition with α-methyl-p-tyrosine (200 mg/kg i.p.). Re-analysis of existing datasets using this approach suggests that previous estimates of dopamine uptake parameters may have been confounded by inaccurate baseline assumptions. This methodology provides a valuable tool for determining physiological dopamine concentrations with high temporal resolution and may improve our understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

title: Emerging trends in in vivo neurochemical monitoring by microdialysis.
abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a pivotal technique for real-time monitoring of neurotransmitter dynamics in freely moving animals, providing unprecedented insights into neurochemical processes underlying behavior and disease. Recent technological advances have significantly enhanced the temporal resolution, sensitivity, and analytical capabilities of this approach. We review current innovations in microdialysis methodology, including the development of novel probe designs with improved biocompatibility, miniaturized sampling systems, and enhanced perfusion protocols that minimize tissue damage while maximizing recovery rates. Integration with advanced analytical techniques such as capillary electrophoresis, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and biosensor arrays has enabled simultaneous monitoring of multiple neurotransmitters including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and glutamate with sub-minute temporal resolution. Recent applications demonstrate the utility of these approaches in characterizing neurochemical alterations in models of Parkinson's disease, depression, and addiction, revealing dynamic patterns of neurotransmitter release that correlate with specific behavioral phenotypes. Furthermore, the combination of microdialysis with optogenetics and pharmacogenetics has provided new opportunities to establish causal relationships between neurochemical changes and neural circuit function. These methodological advances position microdialysis as an increasingly powerful tool for translational neuroscience research, offering potential applications in drug development and personalized medicine approaches for neuropsychiatric disorders. Future developments focusing on wireless telemetry and chronic monitoring capabilities promise to further expand the clinical relevance of in vivo neurochemical assessment.

title: Psychoneuroimmunology meets neuropsychopharmacology: translational implications of the impact of inflammation on behavior.
abstract: The bidirectional communication between the immune system and central nervous system has emerged as a critical pathway linking peripheral inflammation to neuropsychiatric disorders. Growing evidence indicates that pro-inflammatory cytokines can access the brain and alter neurotransmitter metabolism, neuroendocrine function, and synaptic plasticity, ultimately leading to behavioral changes reminiscent of depression and anxiety. This review synthesizes current understanding of inflammatory mechanisms underlying mood disorders and examines therapeutic implications for neuropsychopharmacology. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of preclinical and clinical studies investigating cytokine-induced behavioral alterations and anti-inflammatory treatment strategies. Animal models utilizing lipopolysaccharide, cytokine administration, and chronic stress paradigms consistently demonstrate that peripheral immune activation produces depressive-like behaviors including anhedonia, social withdrawal, and cognitive impairment. Mechanistically, inflammatory mediators disrupt monoaminergic signaling through enhanced tryptophan catabolism via indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase, reduced tetrahydrobiopterin availability, and increased monoamine transporter expression. Clinical studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers in subsets of patients with major depression, particularly those with treatment-resistant illness. Importantly, anti-inflammatory interventions including cyclooxygenase-2 inhibitors, tumor necrosis factor-α antagonists, and minocycline show therapeutic efficacy in both preclinical models and preliminary clinical trials. These findings suggest that inflammatory biomarkers may identify patient subgroups most likely to respond to anti-inflammatory augmentation strategies. The convergence of psychoneuroimmunology and neuropsychopharmacology offers promising avenues for developing personalized medicine approaches targeting immune-mediated mechanisms in psychiatric disorders, potentially improving outcomes for treatment-refractory patients.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Reduced computational models of serotonin synthesis, release, and reuptake.
abstract: Serotonergic dysfunction underlies numerous psychiatric and neurological disorders, yet computational modeling of serotonin (5-HT) dynamics remains limited by model complexity and computational cost. We developed reduced-order mathematical models of 5-HT synthesis, release, and reuptake to enable efficient simulation of serotonergic neurotransmission while preserving biological fidelity. Starting from detailed biophysical models incorporating tryptophan hydroxylase kinetics, vesicular packaging, calcium-dependent exocytosis, and serotonin transporter (SERT) function, we applied model reduction techniques including quasi-steady-state approximations and singular perturbation analysis. The resulting reduced models were validated against experimental data from rat dorsal raphe nucleus recordings and compared to full-order simulations. Our simplified models captured 5-HT synthesis dynamics with 85% accuracy while reducing computational time by two orders of magnitude. The reduced release model accurately reproduced stimulus-evoked 5-HT transients measured by fast-scan cyclic voltammetry, including both peak amplitudes and decay kinetics. Reuptake dynamics were well-preserved across physiological and pharmacologically-relevant SERT inhibition conditions, with less than 10% deviation from full models. Parameter sensitivity analysis revealed that synthesis rate and SERT density were the most critical determinants of steady-state 5-HT levels. These computationally efficient models enable real-time simulation of serotonergic circuits and provide a foundation for closed-loop neuromodulation systems targeting 5-HT dysfunction. The reduced-order framework presented here can be readily extended to other monoaminergic systems and integrated into larger network models of brain function.

title: Extracellular trypsin increases ASIC1a selectivity for monovalent versus divalent cations.
abstract: Acid-sensing ion channels (ASICs) are proton-gated cation channels widely distributed throughout the nervous system and implicated in synaptic transmission, mechanosensation, and pathological conditions including ischemic brain injury. ASIC1a, the predominant brain isoform, exhibits relatively low selectivity between monovalent and divalent cations under physiological conditions. Previous studies have shown that extracellular proteases can modulate ASIC function, though the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. Here we investigated whether trypsin treatment affects the ionic selectivity of recombinant human ASIC1a channels expressed in Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cells using whole-cell patch-clamp electrophysiology. Cells were treated with bovine pancreatic trypsin (10-100 μg/ml) for 2-15 minutes before recording acid-evoked currents (pH 6.0) in solutions containing various concentrations of Na+, K+, Ca2+, or Mg2+. Relative permeability ratios were calculated from reversal potential measurements using the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation. Trypsin treatment (50 μg/ml, 5 min) significantly increased PNa+/PCa2+ from 2.1 ± 0.3 to 8.7 ± 1.2 (n=12, p<0.001) and PK+/PMg2+ from 1.8 ± 0.4 to 6.9 ± 0.8 (n=10, p<0.001). The effect was dose- and time-dependent, reversible upon trypsin removal, and blocked by the serine protease inhibitor PMSF. Current amplitude and pH sensitivity remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that extracellular trypsin selectively enhances ASIC1a permeability to monovalent cations while reducing divalent cation flux, suggesting proteolytic modification of channel selectivity filter regions. This mechanism may be physiologically relevant during tissue injury when both acidosis and protease release occur simultaneously.

title: "Novel Psychopharmacology for Depressive Disorders".
abstract: Depression affects over 300 million individuals worldwide, yet current antidepressant therapies demonstrate limited efficacy and significant latency to therapeutic response. Traditional monoaminergic approaches fail in approximately 30-40% of patients, necessitating exploration of novel pharmacological targets. This study investigated the therapeutic potential of selective modulation of AMPA receptor positive allosteric modulators (AMPAkines) combined with low-dose ketamine in treatment-resistant depression models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were subjected to chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) for 6 weeks to induce depression-like behaviors. Animals were randomized into four groups: vehicle control, ketamine alone (2.5 mg/kg), AMPAkine CX717 alone (10 mg/kg), and combination therapy. Behavioral assessments included forced swim test, sucrose preference test, and elevated plus maze. Neurochemical analyses measured hippocampal BDNF, synaptic plasticity markers, and glutamate receptor expression via Western blot and RT-PCR. Combination therapy significantly reduced immobility time in forced swim test (p<0.001) and restored sucrose preference (p<0.01) compared to monotherapies. Hippocampal BDNF expression increased 2.3-fold with combination treatment, while GluA1 and PSD-95 protein levels were significantly elevated. Synergistic effects were observed in long-term potentiation enhancement and dendritic spine density restoration. The combination demonstrated rapid-acting antidepressant properties with sustained effects lasting 14 days post-treatment. These findings suggest that AMPAkine-ketamine combination therapy represents a promising novel approach for treatment-resistant depression, potentially offering superior efficacy through complementary glutamatergic mechanisms promoting synaptic plasticity and neuroplasticity.
journal: Advances in experimental medicine and biology

title: [Nanopsychiatry. The potential role of nanotechnologies in the future of psychiatry. A systematic review].
abstract: Psychiatric disorders represent a major global health burden, yet current pharmacotherapeutic approaches remain limited by poor blood-brain barrier penetration, systemic side effects, and suboptimal drug targeting. Nanotechnology offers promising solutions through enhanced drug delivery systems and novel therapeutic modalities. This systematic review aimed to evaluate the current state and future potential of nanotechnology applications in psychiatric treatment. We conducted a comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science databases from inception through December 2012, using terms related to nanotechnology, nanoparticles, drug delivery, and psychiatric disorders. Studies investigating nanocarrier systems, targeted drug delivery, and nanotechnology-based diagnostics in psychiatric contexts were included. Forty-three studies met inclusion criteria, comprising 28 preclinical studies and 15 theoretical frameworks. Polymeric nanoparticles, liposomes, and solid lipid nanoparticles demonstrated enhanced central nervous system penetration for antidepressants, antipsychotics, and anxiolytics in animal models. Targeted delivery systems using transferrin and lactoferrin receptors showed 2-4 fold increases in brain bioavailability compared to conventional formulations. Nanoparticle-mediated delivery reduced peripheral side effects by 40-60% while maintaining therapeutic efficacy. Emerging applications included quantum dots for neuroimaging and carbon nanotubes for neural interface technologies. Despite promising preclinical results, clinical translation remains limited by safety concerns and regulatory challenges. Nanopsychiatry represents an emerging field with significant potential to revolutionize psychiatric treatment through improved drug delivery, reduced side effects, and enhanced therapeutic precision. However, extensive safety evaluations and standardized regulatory frameworks are essential before clinical implementation.

title: Lobeline inhibits the neurochemical and behavioral effects of amphetamine.
abstract: Lobeline, an alkaloid derived from Lobelia inflata, has been shown to interact with neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors and vesicular monoamine transporters. Given the structural similarities between lobeline and amphetamine, we investigated whether lobeline could modulate amphetamine's neurochemical and behavioral effects. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received intraperitoneal injections of lobeline (1, 3, or 10 mg/kg) 30 minutes prior to d-amphetamine (2 mg/kg). Extracellular dopamine levels in the nucleus accumbens were measured using in vivo microdialysis, while locomotor activity was assessed in automated activity chambers. Additionally, we examined lobeline's effects on amphetamine-induced stereotypy and conditioned place preference. Pretreatment with lobeline dose-dependently attenuated amphetamine-induced increases in accumbal dopamine release, with the highest dose reducing peak dopamine levels by 68% compared to amphetamine alone. Correspondingly, lobeline significantly inhibited amphetamine-induced hyperlocomotion and stereotypic behaviors in a dose-related manner. The 10 mg/kg dose of lobeline completely blocked the development of amphetamine-conditioned place preference. When administered alone, lobeline produced minimal effects on dopamine release or locomotor activity. These inhibitory effects were not attributable to general motor impairment, as lobeline did not affect rotarod performance or food-motivated behavior. Our findings suggest that lobeline acts as a functional antagonist of amphetamine's rewarding and stimulant properties, possibly through interference with vesicular dopamine storage or release mechanisms. These results support the potential therapeutic application of lobeline or related compounds in treating psychostimulant abuse disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: Amino acid neurochemistry of the vertebrate retina.
abstract: The vertebrate retina contains a diverse array of amino acid neurotransmitters and neuromodulators that mediate synaptic transmission between photoreceptors, bipolar cells, horizontal cells, amacrine cells, and ganglion cells. While glutamate and GABA have been extensively characterized as the primary excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters respectively, the functional roles of glycine, taurine, aspartate, and other amino acids remain incompletely understood. We employed high-performance liquid chromatography, immunocytochemistry, and electrophysiological recordings to comprehensively map amino acid distribution and function across retinal layers in cat, rabbit, and goldfish preparations. Glutamate immunoreactivity was predominantly localized to photoreceptor terminals and ON-bipolar cells, with tissue concentrations reaching 8-12 mM in outer plexiform layer. GABA was concentrated in horizontal cells and a subset of amacrine cells, showing species-specific distribution patterns. Glycine demonstrated robust immunostaining in narrow-field amacrine cells and exhibited co-localization with GABA in 23% of GABAergic neurons. Taurine, present at unexpectedly high concentrations (15-20 mM), showed uniform distribution across all retinal layers with particular enrichment in Müller cells. Electrophysiological analysis revealed that glycine mediates crossover inhibition in OFF-pathway ganglion cells, while taurine modulates calcium homeostasis and exhibits neuroprotective properties under ischemic conditions. These findings demonstrate that amino acid neurotransmission in the vertebrate retina involves complex, overlapping systems that extend beyond classical glutamatergic and GABAergic pathways, suggesting novel therapeutic targets for retinal degenerative diseases.
journal: Progress in retinal and eye research

title: CNS Targets for multi-functional drugs in the treatment of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases.
abstract: The progressive neurodegenerative nature of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD) involves multiple pathological mechanisms including cholinergic dysfunction, oxidative stress, protein aggregation, and neuroinflammation. Current monotherapeutic approaches provide limited clinical benefits, suggesting that multi-target drug strategies may offer superior therapeutic outcomes. This study investigated the potential CNS targets for developing multi-functional compounds that could simultaneously address multiple pathogenic pathways in both diseases. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of shared molecular targets between AD and PD using computational modeling and in vitro screening approaches. Primary neuronal cultures from transgenic mouse models expressing mutant APP/presenilin-1 (AD) and α-synuclein (PD) were treated with a library of 147 small molecules designed to interact with multiple targets including acetylcholinesterase, monoamine oxidase-B, glutamate receptors, and antioxidant pathways. Cell viability, neurite outgrowth, protein aggregation, and mitochondrial function were assessed. Results identified 23 compounds showing significant neuroprotective effects in both disease models. The most promising candidates demonstrated dual acetylcholinesterase/monoamine oxidase-B inhibition coupled with antioxidant properties and reduced β-amyloid and α-synuclein aggregation. Lead compound MT-7741 showed 67% reduction in protein aggregation, 45% improvement in mitochondrial function, and enhanced neurite density compared to controls. These findings support the feasibility of multi-functional drug approaches targeting shared pathogenic mechanisms in AD and PD, potentially offering more effective therapeutic strategies than current single-target interventions for these devastating neurodegenerative disorders.

title: Manipulating neuropeptidergic pathways in humans: a novel approach to neuropharmacology?
abstract: Given the tremendous number of neuropeptides, which are synthesized in the central nervous system, the brain can be viewed as one of the most prominent endocrine organs. Elucidation of the functions of these peptides is hampered by the facts that after intravenous administration access to brain receptors is prevented or impaired by the blood-brain barrier. Here, we provide evidence that intranasal administration can be a way to circumvent the blood-brain barrier. Selected experiments will be reported indicating that peptides after intranasal administration in humans can specifically alter a great variety of brain functions. For vasopressin, we demonstrated improving effects of long-term intranasal treatment on sleep in elderly people. Insulin showed improving effects of short-term memory functions. For adrenocorticotropin/melanocyte stimulating hormone, ACTH/MSH-(4-10), a twofold action was isolated: The melanocortin fragment diminished selective attention and, with subchronic administration, reduced body fat. These results could provide the basis for developing a new, specific, and "soft" neuropharmacology.

title: Drug discovery in neurodegenerative diseases.
abstract: This meeting report documents the exciting ongoing research aimed at developing new therapeutic agents for neurodegenerative diseases at the interface between academia and industry, and illustrates the direction the NIH "roadmap" for medical research is taking. Some of the projects have resulted in very promising drugs, identified and developed by academic centers, entering clinical trials.
journal: Science of aging knowledge environment : SAGE KE

title: The nature of dopamine dysfunction in schizophrenia and what this means for treatment.
abstract: Dopamine dysfunction has long been implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, yet the precise nature of these alterations and their therapeutic implications remain incompletely understood. We conducted a comprehensive meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies examining dopamine function in schizophrenia patients and investigated the relationship between dopamine dysfunction patterns and treatment response. We analyzed data from 847 patients with schizophrenia and 623 healthy controls across 34 positron emission tomography and single-photon emission computed tomography studies measuring dopamine synthesis capacity, receptor availability, and synaptic dopamine levels in striatal and extrastriatal regions. Additionally, we examined treatment response data from 312 patients receiving antipsychotic medications over 12 weeks. Results demonstrated significant elevations in presynaptic dopamine synthesis capacity in the associative striatum (effect size = 0.79, p < 0.001) and reduced dopamine D1 receptor availability in prefrontal cortex (effect size = -0.65, p < 0.001) in patients versus controls. Dopamine dysfunction was most pronounced in treatment-resistant patients, who showed 23% greater striatal dopamine synthesis compared to treatment-responsive patients (p = 0.008). Prefrontal D1 receptor deficits correlated significantly with negative symptom severity (r = -0.42, p < 0.001) and cognitive impairment (r = 0.38, p = 0.003). These findings suggest schizophrenia involves a dual dopamine dysfunction pattern characterized by striatal hyperactivity and prefrontal hypoactivity. The magnitude of these alterations predicts treatment resistance and symptom domains, supporting the development of regionally-targeted therapeutic approaches that address both striatal dopamine excess and prefrontal dopamine deficiency in schizophrenia treatment.

title: Using a rate-frequency curve method to assess the rewarding properties of morphine in the intracranial self-stimulation paradigm in rats.
abstract: Intracranial self-stimulation (ICSS) paradigms provide valuable models for studying drug reward mechanisms, yet traditional threshold-based analyses may not capture the full spectrum of reward enhancement. This study evaluated a rate-frequency curve method as an alternative approach to assess morphine's rewarding properties in the ICSS paradigm. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=12) were implanted with bipolar electrodes targeting the medial forebrain bundle and trained to lever-press for electrical brain stimulation. Following stable baseline performance, rats underwent testing sessions where stimulation frequency was systematically varied (25-158 Hz) to generate rate-frequency curves. Morphine (1.0, 3.0, and 5.6 mg/kg, s.c.) or saline was administered 30 minutes prior to testing in a counterbalanced design. Response rates were recorded across all frequencies and curve-shift analysis was performed using nonlinear regression modeling. Morphine produced dose-dependent leftward and upward shifts in rate-frequency curves, indicating enhanced reward sensitivity. The 3.0 and 5.6 mg/kg doses significantly decreased the frequency required to maintain half-maximal responding (F50) by 18% and 32%, respectively, compared to saline controls (p<0.01). Maximum response rates were increased by 15-25% at the higher doses. The rate-frequency curve method demonstrated superior sensitivity compared to traditional threshold measures and provided additional information about changes in reward magnitude. These findings validate the rate-frequency curve approach as a robust method for characterizing drug reward effects in ICSS paradigms, offering enhanced temporal resolution and pharmacological sensitivity for neuropharmacological investigations.

title: Sedative choice in drug-induced sleep endoscopy: A neuropharmacology-based review.
abstract: To examine the suitability of commonly used agents for drug-induced sleep endoscopy (DISE) based on agent-specific neuropharmacology. PubMed. A literature search of the PubMed database was performed on January 1, 2016. A two-layered search strategy was performed to identify relevant pharmacologic agents and articles related to neuropharmacology for these agents. The first search identified relevant pharmacologic agents; the second search examined agents with greater than five results from search 1, along with medical subject headings "respiration," "sleep," "pharmacology," and/or "[respective agent] (e.g., propofol)." Articles not in English were excluded. Bibliographies of pertinent articles were hand-searched for additional articles. Three agents were commonly identified from search 1: propofol, midazolam, and dexmedetomidine with 44, 13, and 6 results, respectively. Of note, 11 results utilized coinduction with midazolam and propofol. Search 2 for propofol, midazolam, and dexmedetomidine retrieved 219, 220, and 26 results, respectively. Eleven results for propofol, 4 for midazolam, and 9 for dexmedetomidine were found to be related to their neuropharmacology. The current review demonstrates relatively few investigations seeking to characterize the neuropharmacologic suitability of DISE agents. Compared to propofol and midazolam, dexmedetomidine's mechanism of action appears most likely to induce natural sleep pathways. Further study of its effect on upper airway collapsibility (critical closing pressure) and pharyngeal muscle tone (genioglossus electrode electromyography) are needed. Laryngoscope, 2016 Laryngoscope, 127:273-279, 2017.

title: Legal aspects of zebrafish neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research.
abstract: The increasing utilization of zebrafish (Danio rerio) as a model organism in neuropharmacology and neurotoxicology research has raised important regulatory and ethical considerations that require systematic examination. While zebrafish offer significant advantages including genetic tractability, optical transparency, and high-throughput screening capabilities, the legal framework governing their use in research remains complex and varies across jurisdictions. This review analyzes current regulatory requirements, institutional oversight mechanisms, and emerging legal challenges in zebrafish-based neurotoxicological studies. We examined regulatory guidelines from major international bodies including the EU, FDA, and OECD, alongside institutional animal care and use committee (IACUC) protocols from 45 research institutions conducting zebrafish neuropharmacological research between 2015-2018. Our analysis reveals significant inconsistencies in regulatory interpretation, particularly regarding the developmental stage at which zebrafish acquire legal protection status. While most jurisdictions consider larvae beyond 5 days post-fertilization as requiring formal ethical approval, 23% of surveyed institutions applied more stringent criteria. Neurotoxicology studies involving controlled substances presented additional regulatory complexities, with 67% of institutions reporting delays in protocol approval due to unclear guidelines for Schedule II compounds in aquatic models. We identified critical gaps in current regulatory frameworks, particularly regarding high-throughput behavioral assays and novel psychoactive substances testing. These findings highlight the urgent need for harmonized international guidelines specific to zebrafish neuropharmacological research to ensure both scientific rigor and appropriate ethical oversight while facilitating regulatory acceptance of zebrafish-derived safety data.

title: Psychopharmacology of combined activation of the serotonin1A and σ1 receptors.
abstract: The serotonin1A (5-HT1A) receptor and sigma-1 (σ1) receptor represent distinct neurotransmitter systems that have independently shown therapeutic potential in neuropsychiatric disorders. While 5-HT1A receptors modulate serotonergic signaling and are targeted by anxiolytics and antidepressants, σ1 receptors function as molecular chaperones regulating calcium homeostasis and synaptic plasticity. Given their overlapping expression in limbic and cortical regions, we investigated the psychopharmacological effects of combined 5-HT1A and σ1 receptor activation using selective pharmacological tools. Male Wistar rats received acute or chronic treatment with 8-OH-DPAT (5-HT1A agonist, 0.1-1.0 mg/kg), PRE-084 (σ1 agonist, 0.3-3.0 mg/kg), or their combination. Behavioral assessments included elevated plus maze, forced swim test, novel object recognition, and prepulse inhibition paradigms. Neurochemical analyses measured monoamine levels and cAMP signaling in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Combined receptor activation produced synergistic anxiolytic effects in the elevated plus maze and enhanced antidepressant-like responses in forced swim testing compared to individual treatments. Cognitive performance was significantly improved only with dual activation, accompanied by increased hippocampal acetylcholine release and enhanced cAMP/CREB signaling. The combination also normalized sensorimotor gating deficits more effectively than either compound alone. Neurochemically, co-activation increased cortical dopamine and norepinephrine levels beyond additive effects. These findings demonstrate that simultaneous 5-HT1A and σ1 receptor activation produces synergistic psychopharmacological benefits across multiple behavioral domains, suggesting potential therapeutic advantages for dual-targeting approaches in treating complex neuropsychiatric conditions characterized by mood, cognitive, and sensory processing dysfunction.

title: Alotamide A, a novel neuropharmacological agent from the marine cyanobacterium Lyngbya bouillonii.
abstract: Marine cyanobacteria have emerged as prolific sources of structurally diverse bioactive compounds with significant therapeutic potential. During our ongoing investigation of secondary metabolites from tropical marine cyanobacteria, we isolated a novel alkaloid, alotamide A, from cultured Lyngbya bouillonii collected from Hawaiian waters. The planar structure of alotamide A was elucidated through extensive spectroscopic analysis including 1D and 2D NMR, HRMS, and IR spectroscopy, revealing a unique indole-containing framework with an unprecedented cyclic peptide moiety. The absolute configuration was determined by advanced Mosher's method and confirmed by total synthesis. Alotamide A exhibited potent and selective antagonistic activity at NMDA receptors in primary rat cortical neurons with an IC₅₀ of 2.3 μM, demonstrating 15-fold selectivity over AMPA and kainate receptors. Electrophysiological patch-clamp studies revealed that alotamide A functions as a non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist, likely binding to the channel pore region. In vivo studies using the mouse forced swim test showed significant antidepressant-like effects at 10 mg/kg (i.p.), comparable to ketamine treatment. Importantly, alotamide A displayed minimal psychotomimetic side effects and improved safety profile compared to conventional NMDA antagonists. Molecular docking studies suggested binding interactions with the GluN2B subunit, consistent with observed subunit selectivity. These findings establish alotamide A as a promising lead compound for developing novel antidepressant therapeutics targeting the glutamatergic system, while expanding the chemical diversity of marine-derived NMDA receptor modulators.

title: Time-dependent behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic dysregulation in female C57BL/6 mice caused by chronic high-fat diet intake.
abstract: Obesity and metabolic dysfunction are increasingly recognized as risk factors for neuropsychiatric disorders, yet the temporal relationship between diet-induced metabolic changes and behavioral alterations remains poorly understood, particularly in females. This study investigated time-dependent effects of chronic high-fat diet (HFD) consumption on behavioral, neurochemical, and metabolic parameters in female C57BL/6 mice. Eight-week-old female mice were fed either standard chow (10% fat) or HFD (60% fat) for 2, 8, or 16 weeks (n=12-15/group). Behavioral assessments included open field test, elevated plus maze, forced swim test, and novel object recognition. Neurochemical analyses measured monoamine levels in prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum using HPLC. Metabolic parameters included body weight, glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, and serum leptin levels. HFD mice showed progressive weight gain and glucose intolerance by 2 weeks, with insulin resistance developing by 8 weeks. Anxiety-like behavior emerged at 8 weeks, accompanied by reduced dopamine and serotonin levels in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus. Depressive-like behavior and cognitive impairment were evident only after 16 weeks of HFD exposure, coinciding with further reductions in hippocampal BDNF expression and elevated neuroinflammatory markers. Serum leptin levels were elevated throughout all time points but showed diminished central leptin sensitivity after 8 weeks. These findings demonstrate that chronic HFD consumption in female mice produces sequential metabolic dysfunction, anxiety, and finally depression with cognitive deficits, suggesting distinct temporal windows for intervention strategies targeting diet-induced neuropsychiatric complications.

title: New approaches to chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting: from neuropharmacology to clinical investigations.
abstract: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) remains a significant dose-limiting toxicity affecting patient quality of life and treatment compliance. While 5-HT3 receptor antagonists have improved antiemetic therapy, breakthrough emesis continues to affect 30-50% of patients receiving highly emetogenic chemotherapy. This review examines emerging neuropharmacological targets and translational approaches for CINV management. We analyzed preclinical data from animal models of cisplatin and cyclophosphamide-induced emesis, focusing on novel receptor systems including neurokinin-1 (NK-1), cannabinoid CB1/CB2, and GABA-B receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and nucleus tractus solitarius. Clinical trial data from 2000-2006 were systematically reviewed, emphasizing phase II and III studies of combination antiemetic regimens. Preclinical studies demonstrate that NK-1 receptor antagonists significantly reduce both acute and delayed emesis when combined with 5-HT3 antagonists, with aprepitant showing 65-80% complete response rates in delayed CINV. Cannabinoid receptor modulators exhibit promise in refractory cases, while GABA-B agonists show efficacy in anticipatory nausea models. Clinical investigations reveal that triple combination therapy (5-HT3 antagonist, NK-1 antagonist, and dexamethasone) achieves complete protection rates exceeding 70% for highly emetogenic regimens. Patient stratification based on genetic polymorphisms in CYP2D6 and 5-HT3 receptor subunits may optimize individual responses. These findings support a multi-target approach to CINV, with NK-1 receptor antagonists representing the most significant advance since 5-HT3 antagonists. Future directions include personalized antiemetic therapy and investigation of central sensitization mechanisms in treatment-refractory patients.

title: On the path from chemistry to neuroscience: early explorations in chemical medicine under the mentorship of Dr. Erminio Costa, a neuroscientist with a big brain and a bigger heart.
abstract: In this short note in tribute to Dr. Erminio Costa, I reflect upon the sabbatical period that I spent with him during his early years at the Fidia-Georgetown Institute for the Neurosciences [FGIN] at the Georgetown University Medical Center. I detail some of the interesting projects that we were involved in at that time, that led us to create strong bridges between chemistry and the neurosciences, and that led in turn to the discovery of various classes of interesting molecular tools such as the peripheral benzodiazepine receptor ligand FGIN-1-27.

title: Mercury, selenium and neurochemical biomarkers in different brain regions of migrating common loons from Lake Erie, Canada.
abstract: Mercury (Hg) contamination in aquatic ecosystems poses significant neurological risks to piscivorous birds, while selenium (Se) may provide neuroprotective effects through antagonistic interactions. Common loons (Gavia immer) are particularly vulnerable due to their high trophic position and reliance on fish consumption. This study investigated regional brain distribution of Hg and Se, and associated neurochemical alterations in migrating loons from Lake Erie. Brain tissues from 24 adult loons collected during fall migration (2009-2010) were analyzed for total Hg and Se concentrations in cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem using cold vapor atomic absorption spectrophotometry and hydride generation atomic absorption spectrophotometry, respectively. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity, dopamine, serotonin, and their metabolites were quantified using spectrophotometric and HPLC methods. Mercury concentrations ranged from 0.89-4.2 μg/g wet weight, with highest levels in cerebellum, followed by cerebrum and brainstem. Selenium concentrations (0.34-1.1 μg/g wet weight) showed similar regional patterns and were positively correlated with Hg (r=0.72, p<0.001). Significant decreases in AChE activity were observed in cerebrum (23% reduction) and cerebellum (31% reduction) relative to control values. Dopamine concentrations were reduced by 18% in cerebrum, while serotonin metabolite ratios indicated altered neurotransmitter turnover. Se:Hg molar ratios below 1.0 in 67% of individuals suggested inadequate Se protection. These findings demonstrate region-specific neurotoxic effects of Hg exposure in loons, with potential implications for motor function and behavior during critical migration periods.

title: Targeted gene expression in dopamine and serotonin neurons of the mouse brain.
abstract: We used a knock-in strategy to generate two lines of mice expressing Cre recombinase under the transcriptional control of the dopamine transporter promoter (DAT-cre mice) or the serotonin transporter promoter (SERT-cre mice). In DAT-cre mice, immunocytochemical staining of adult brains for the dopamine-synthetic enzyme tyrosine hydroxylase and for Cre recombinase revealed that virtually all dopaminergic neurons in the ventral midbrain expressed Cre. Crossing DAT-cre mice with ROSA26-stop-lacZ or ROSA26-stop-YFP reporter mice revealed a near perfect correlation between staining for tyrosine hydroxylase and beta-galactosidase or YFP. YFP-labeled fluorescent dopaminergic neurons could be readily identified in live slices. Crossing SERT-cre mice with the ROSA26-stop-lacZ or ROSA26-stop-YFP reporter mice similarly revealed a near perfect correlation between staining for serotonin-synthetic enzyme tryptophan hydroxylase and beta-galactosidase or YFP. Additional Cre expression in the thalamus and cortex was observed, reflecting the known pattern of transient SERT expression during early postnatal development. These findings suggest a general strategy of using neurotransmitter transporter promoters to drive selective Cre expression and thus control mutations in specific neurotransmitter systems. Crossed with fluorescent-gene reporters, this strategy tags neurons by neurotransmitter status, providing new tools for electrophysiology and imaging.

title: Can voltammetry measure nitrogen monoxide (NO) and/or nitrites?
abstract: Nitrogen monoxide (NO) and its oxidation products play critical roles in neuronal signaling, vascular regulation, and synaptic plasticity. Current methods for measuring NO in biological systems, including chemiluminescence and fluorescent indicators, have limitations in temporal resolution and spatial specificity. This study evaluated the feasibility of using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to detect NO and nitrite species in vitro and in brain tissue preparations. Carbon fiber microelectrodes (7 μm diameter) were fabricated and characterized for electrochemical detection of authentic NO solutions (1-100 μM) and sodium nitrite (10-500 μM) in phosphate-buffered saline and artificial cerebrospinal fluid. Voltammetric measurements were performed using triangular waveforms (-0.4 to +1.3 V, 400 V/s) and compared with established amperometric techniques. FSCV detected NO with an oxidation peak at approximately +0.9 V versus Ag/AgCl, showing linear responses from 5-75 μM (r² = 0.94) with detection limits of 2.3 μM. Nitrite exhibited distinct voltammetric signatures with oxidation occurring at +1.1 V. However, electrode fouling occurred within 15-20 minutes of continuous measurements, and significant interference from ascorbic acid, dopamine, and other neurochemicals was observed. While FSCV demonstrated capability for NO detection under controlled conditions, poor selectivity, electrode stability issues, and interference from endogenous compounds limit its practical application for real-time NO monitoring in complex biological environments. Alternative electrode materials and surface modifications may improve performance for future neurochemical applications.

title: The presence of comorbidity in Tourette syndrome increases the need for pharmacological treatment.
abstract: Tourette syndrome (TS) is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by motor and vocal tics, with up to 90% of patients presenting comorbid psychiatric conditions including attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), and anxiety disorders. While tic severity alone has traditionally guided pharmacological intervention decisions, the impact of comorbid conditions on treatment necessity remains poorly understood. We conducted a retrospective analysis of 247 pediatric patients (ages 6-17 years) diagnosed with TS at our tertiary care center between 2005-2008. Patients were categorized into TS-only (n=61) and TS-comorbid groups (n=186), with comprehensive assessment using the Yale Global Tic Severity Scale, Children's Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale, ADHD Rating Scale-IV, and Beck Youth Inventories. Treatment decisions and medication utilization were analyzed using logistic regression models controlling for tic severity, age, and socioeconomic status. Patients with TS plus comorbidities were significantly more likely to receive pharmacological intervention than those with TS alone (78.5% vs 41.0%, p<0.001). This association remained significant after controlling for tic severity (OR=3.24, 95% CI: 1.82-5.77, p<0.001). The presence of ADHD (OR=2.89) and OCD symptoms (OR=2.15) were the strongest predictors of medication use. Functional impairment scores were significantly higher in the comorbid group (p<0.001), correlating with increased polypharmacy requirements. These findings suggest that comorbid psychiatric conditions, rather than tic severity alone, primarily drive pharmacological treatment decisions in pediatric TS, highlighting the importance of comprehensive psychiatric evaluation in treatment planning.

title: Neuroethics and psychiatry.
abstract: The rapid advancement of neuroscience technologies and their clinical applications in psychiatry has generated unprecedented ethical challenges that require systematic examination. This review synthesizes current literature on neuroethical considerations in psychiatric practice, focusing on emerging diagnostic and therapeutic modalities. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed publications from 2000-2008, examining ethical frameworks applied to neuroimaging-based diagnosis, deep brain stimulation, transcranial magnetic stimulation, and pharmacogenomic approaches in psychiatric treatment. Key databases including PubMed, PsycINFO, and Cochrane Library were searched using terms related to neuroethics, psychiatric treatment, and moral considerations in neuroscience applications. Our analysis reveals five primary ethical domains: patient autonomy and informed consent in the context of altered mental states, privacy and confidentiality of neurobiological data, justice in access to expensive neurotechnologies, potential for coercion in involuntary treatment scenarios, and the fundamental question of personal identity when directly modulating brain function. Neuroimaging presents particular challenges regarding predictive information and its implications for insurance coverage and employment. Deep brain stimulation and other invasive procedures raise concerns about personality changes and authentic selfhood. The integration of pharmacogenomics introduces questions about genetic discrimination and therapeutic obligation. Current regulatory frameworks appear insufficient to address these evolving challenges. We conclude that psychiatry requires specialized neuroethical guidelines that balance therapeutic innovation with protection of vulnerable populations, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary collaboration between clinicians, ethicists, and policymakers to establish comprehensive governance structures.

title: The use of imaging in the early development of neuropharmacological drugs: a survey of approved NDAs.
abstract: The aim of the study was to evaluate the use of imaging in the development of neuropharmacological drugs. All New Drug Applications (NDAs) approved from 1995 through 2004 in the Division of Neuropharmacological Drug Products at the Food and Drug Administration were surveyed for imaging studies. Imaging literature was also reviewed with respect to antipsychotics and antidepressants. One hundred and six NDAs (35 new molecular entities (NMEs)) were approved; 15 of these NDAs (10 NMEs) had imaging studies. The primary imaging modality was positron emission tomography. Imaging was primarily conducted for drugs used in schizophrenia, depression, multiple sclerosis, and migraine. The majority evaluated receptor occupancy or proof of concept. Examples (including literature) are discussed as pertinent to dosage, efficacy, safety, or further development of a drug or class of drugs. Imaging contributes to optimal clinical development of central nervous system (CNS)-active drugs. Opportunities are available for its broader use, contributing to improved understanding of the clinical pharmacology of CNS-active drugs.

title: Unraveling neuronal dopamine transporter mechanisms with rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: Herein we describe how the rotating disk electrode voltammetric technique can be used to examine the mechanism(s) of the inward transport of dopamine by the neuronal transporter for dopamine (DAT). The usefulness of making measurements kinetically resolving dopamine transport, interpretations of changes in Km and Vmax, approaches to defining pre-steady-state binding of dopamine to DAT, interactions between competing inhibitors, chemical modification of functional groups within DAT, and a presentation of a hypothetical multi-state model of dopamine transport are presented and discussed.

title: Mentored and inspired by Mimo: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has been profoundly shaped by the pioneering contributions of Erminio Costa, whose seminal work over five decades established fundamental principles of GABAergic neurotransmission and neurosteroid modulation. This tribute examines Costa's transformative impact on our understanding of anxiety disorders, epilepsy, and psychiatric conditions through his innovative research methodologies and mentorship philosophy. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of Costa's scientific legacy, reviewing 347 peer-reviewed publications, examining the career trajectories of his 89 doctoral and postdoctoral trainees, and assessing the translational impact of his discoveries on modern therapeutics. Costa's laboratory pioneered the characterization of GABA receptor subtypes, elucidated the molecular mechanisms of benzodiazepine action, and identified endogenous neurosteroids as key modulators of GABAergic function. His work established the theoretical framework for understanding how allopregnanolone and related compounds regulate neuronal excitability and behavior. Remarkably, Costa's trainees have collectively published over 12,000 research articles and hold leadership positions at major academic institutions worldwide. His discoveries directly contributed to the development of novel anxiolytic and anticonvulsant medications currently in clinical use. Costa's integrative approach, combining molecular biology, electrophysiology, and behavioral pharmacology, created a methodological template that continues to guide contemporary neuropharmacological research.
