His legacy demonstrates how rigorous scientific inquiry coupled with dedicated mentorship can catalyze transformative advances in our understanding of brain function and psychiatric disease, inspiring generations of researchers to pursue mechanistic insights that ultimately benefit human health.

title: Pharmacological imaging as a tool to visualise dopaminergic neurotoxicity.
abstract: Dopaminergic neurotoxicity underlies several neurodegenerative disorders, yet current methods for visualizing and quantifying neuronal damage in vivo remain limited. This study evaluated the utility of pharmacological imaging approaches combining positron emission tomography (PET) with selective radioligands to assess dopaminergic neurotoxicity in real-time. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received unilateral striatal injections of 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA, 8 μg) or vehicle. Animals underwent longitudinal PET imaging using [¹¹C]raclopride for dopamine D2 receptor availability and [¹¹C]PE2I for dopamine transporter density at 1, 3, 7, and 28 days post-lesion. Behavioral assessment via amphetamine-induced rotation and post-mortem tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry served as validation measures. 6-OHDA lesioning produced progressive decreases in striatal [¹¹C]PE2I binding, with 45±8% reduction at day 3 and 78±6% reduction at day 28 compared to contralateral striatum (p<0.001). [¹¹C]Raclopride binding showed initial increases (125±12% at day 3) followed by normalization by day 28, consistent with denervation supersensitivity. Strong correlations were observed between [¹¹C]PE2I binding and both rotational behavior (r=-0.84, p<0.001) and tyrosine hydroxylase-positive cell counts (r=0.79, p<0.001). These findings demonstrate that pharmacological PET imaging provides sensitive, quantitative assessment of dopaminergic neurotoxicity progression. The temporal resolution and non-invasive nature of this approach offers significant advantages for monitoring disease progression and evaluating neuroprotective interventions in preclinical models of Parkinson's disease and related disorders.

title: I Believe I Can Fly!: Use of Drosophila as a Model Organism in Neuropsychopharmacology Research.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model organism for neuropsychopharmacological research, offering unique advantages including genetic tractability, conserved neurotransmitter systems, and behavioral paradigms relevant to human psychiatric disorders. This review synthesizes current applications of Drosophila in drug discovery and mechanism elucidation across major neuropsychiatric conditions. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of Drosophila-based studies published between 2010-2016, focusing on models of addiction, depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, and cognitive disorders. Our analysis reveals that Drosophila exhibits conserved responses to major classes of psychoactive compounds, including dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic modulators. Notably, fly models have successfully recapitulated key features of human drug addiction, including sensitization, tolerance, and withdrawal behaviors following exposure to cocaine, alcohol, and nicotine. Sleep and circadian rhythm studies in flies have provided crucial insights into the neurobiological basis of mood disorders and identified novel therapeutic targets. Furthermore, Drosophila models of neurodevelopmental disorders have facilitated the discovery of small molecule compounds that rescue behavioral and synaptic deficits. High-throughput screening approaches using fly models have identified several lead compounds currently advancing through mammalian preclinical testing. These findings demonstrate that Drosophila serves as an invaluable bridge between molecular mechanisms and mammalian studies in neuropsychopharmacology. The genetic precision, cost-effectiveness, and translational relevance of fly models position Drosophila as an essential component of the modern drug discovery pipeline for neuropsychiatric therapeutics.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Cocaine increases stimulated dopamine release more in periadolescent than adult rats.
abstract: Adolescence represents a critical period of brain development characterized by ongoing maturation of dopaminergic systems. Previous studies have demonstrated age-related differences in cocaine's behavioral effects, but the neurochemical basis for these differences remains poorly understood. The present study examined whether cocaine differentially affects dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens of periadolescent (postnatal day 35) versus adult (postnatal day 70) male Sprague-Dawley rats. Using in vivo microdialysis, we measured extracellular dopamine concentrations following acute cocaine administration (10 mg/kg, i.p.) in freely moving animals. Baseline dopamine levels were significantly higher in periadolescent compared to adult rats (2.8 ± 0.3 vs 1.9 ± 0.2 nM, p<0.05). Cocaine administration produced robust increases in extracellular dopamine in both age groups; however, the magnitude of increase was significantly greater in periadolescent animals. Peak dopamine levels reached 847% ± 76% of baseline in periadolescent rats compared to 542% ± 48% in adults (p<0.01). The enhanced dopaminergic response in periadolescent animals persisted for approximately 2 hours longer than in adults. Western blot analysis revealed significantly lower dopamine transporter (DAT) protein expression in periadolescent nucleus accumbens tissue compared to adults, which may partially explain the age-related differences in cocaine sensitivity. These findings suggest that the developing adolescent brain exhibits heightened vulnerability to cocaine's neurochemical effects, potentially contributing to increased addiction liability during this developmental period. Understanding these age-related differences may inform prevention strategies and treatment approaches for adolescent substance abuse.

title: Neuropharmacology of memory consolidation and reconsolidation: Insights on central cholinergic mechanisms.
abstract: Memory consolidation and reconsolidation represent distinct neurobiological processes that transform labile memories into stable engrams and render retrieved memories susceptible to modification, respectively. While considerable evidence implicates cholinergic neurotransmission in memory formation, the specific roles of central cholinergic mechanisms in consolidation versus reconsolidation remain poorly understood. Here we investigated the differential contributions of muscarinic and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors to these memory processes using auditory fear conditioning in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received bilateral infusions of the muscarinic antagonist scopolamine (10 μg/side), the nicotinic antagonist mecamylamine (5 μg/side), or vehicle into the basolateral amygdala either immediately post-training (consolidation) or post-retrieval (reconsolidation). Long-term memory was assessed 24 hours later through freezing behavior measurements. Scopolamine administration impaired both consolidation and reconsolidation, reducing freezing responses by 65% and 58%, respectively, compared to vehicle controls (p<0.001). Conversely, mecamylamine selectively disrupted consolidation (47% reduction, p<0.01) while leaving reconsolidation intact. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed increased c-Fos expression in cholinergic neurons of the medial septal complex following both consolidation and reconsolidation phases. These findings demonstrate that muscarinic receptors are critically involved in both memory consolidation and reconsolidation, whereas nicotinic receptors appear specifically required for initial memory stabilization. Our results provide novel insights into the neurochemical basis of memory processing and suggest that cholinergic modulation of memory involves receptor subtype-specific mechanisms that differentially regulate distinct phases of memory formation and updating.

title: Imaging synaptic inhibition in transgenic mice expressing the chloride indicator, Clomeleon.
abstract: We describe here a molecular genetic approach for imaging synaptic inhibition. The thy-1 promoter was used to express high levels of Clomeleon, a ratiometric fluorescent indicator for chloride ions, in discrete populations of neurons in the brains of transgenic mice. Clomeleon was functional after chronic expression and provided non-invasive readouts of intracellular chloride concentration ([Cl(-)](i)) in brain slices, allowing us to quantify age-dependent declines in resting [Cl(-)](i) during neuronal development. Activation of hippocampal interneurons caused [Cl(-)](i) to rise transiently in individual postsynaptic pyramidal neurons. [Cl(-)](i) increased in direct proportion to the amount of inhibitory transmission, with peak changes as large as 4 mM. Integrating responses over populations of pyramidal neurons allowed sensitive detection of synaptic inhibition. Thus, Clomeleon imaging permits non-invasive, spatiotemporally resolved recordings of [Cl(-)](i) in a large variety of neurons, opening up new opportunities for imaging synaptic inhibition and other forms of chloride signaling.

title: The FGIN period: electrophysiological studies.
abstract: Neurosteroids have emerged as important modulators of GABA-A receptor function, with particular interest in their therapeutic potential for anxiety and mood disorders. FGIN 1-27, an 18-kDa translocator protein (TSPO) ligand, stimulates neurosteroid biosynthesis by enhancing cholesterol transport into mitochondria. While behavioral studies have demonstrated anxiolytic effects of FGIN 1-27, its direct electrophysiological actions on GABAergic neurotransmission remain poorly characterized. We investigated the acute and chronic effects of FGIN 1-27 on GABA-A receptor-mediated currents using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings in cultured rat hippocampal neurons and brain slice preparations. Acute application of FGIN 1-27 (1-100 μM) produced a concentration-dependent potentiation of GABA-evoked currents, with maximal enhancement of 180 ± 15% at 50 μM. This potentiation was blocked by finasteride, a 5α-reductase inhibitor, suggesting dependence on neurosteroid synthesis. Chronic treatment with FGIN 1-27 (10 μM, 24h) increased baseline GABA current amplitude by 145 ± 22% and enhanced sensitivity to the neurosteroid allopregnanolone. Single-channel analysis revealed that FGIN 1-27 increased channel open probability without affecting conductance. The effects were abolished in the presence of the TSPO antagonist PK11195 and were absent in steroidogenic enzyme-deficient cell lines. These findings demonstrate that FGIN 1-27 enhances GABAergic neurotransmission through TSPO-mediated neurosteroid synthesis, providing electrophysiological evidence for its anxiolytic mechanism. The sustained enhancement following chronic treatment suggests potential therapeutic applications for anxiety disorders with improved tolerability profiles compared to direct neurosteroid administration.

title: Improved high-performance liquid chromatographic method for GABA and glutamate determination in regions of the rodent brain.
abstract: A C18 reversed-phase column and isocratic fluorescence HPLC method for the simultaneous detection of glutamate and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) is described. In this article a fast and more efficient method for the extraction of these neurotransmitters in rat brain tissue is also presented. The supernatant was derivatized with o-phthalaldehyde (OPA) and analyzed by HPLC with fluorescence detection. Intraday reproducibility was 97.0% and 96.7% and interday reproducibility was 97.1% and 93.7% for GABA and glutamate, respectively. Recovery assays indicate that the accuracy of the method for GABA is 99.6+/-2.3% and for glutamate is 101.9+/-1.8%. In addition, the time consumed to run a sample is lower than that described by other authors. Mean elution time was 3.10 min and 8.22 min for glutamate and GABA, respectively. Thus, in a total runtime of less than 9 min both neurotransmitters were detected. Moreover, when compared to the current methods, the extraction solution used here allowed a high drawing out of the neurotransmitters, glutamate and GABA, from the hippocampus, thalamus and prefrontal cortex of the rat brain.

title: Pharmacotherapies for alcoholism: the old and the new.
abstract: Alcoholism remains a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, with limited pharmacological treatment options currently available. Traditional therapies including disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate have shown modest efficacy but significant limitations in clinical practice. This comprehensive review examines established pharmacotherapies alongside emerging therapeutic targets for alcohol use disorders. We conducted a systematic analysis of clinical trials and preclinical studies published between 1990-2010, evaluating efficacy, safety profiles, and mechanisms of action for both approved medications and novel compounds in development. Traditional agents demonstrate variable success rates: disulfiram shows 20-30% abstinence rates but suffers from poor compliance; naltrexone reduces heavy drinking days by 15-25% but exhibits limited long-term efficacy; acamprosate maintains abstinence in 35-40% of patients but requires intact motivation. Emerging pharmacotherapies targeting novel neurotransmitter systems show promise, including topiramate (GABA/glutamate modulation), baclofen (GABAB agonism), and varenicline (nicotinic receptor partial agonism), with clinical trials demonstrating 40-50% reduction in drinking outcomes. Preclinical investigations of NK1 receptor antagonists, CRF1 receptor antagonists, and phosphodiesterase inhibitors suggest additional therapeutic avenues. Novel approaches targeting neuroplasticity mechanisms and personalized medicine based on genetic polymorphisms represent future directions. While traditional pharmacotherapies provide foundational treatment options, emerging agents offer enhanced efficacy through diverse mechanisms. Combination therapies and individualized treatment strategies may optimize outcomes for this heterogeneous disorder, though larger controlled trials remain necessary to establish definitive therapeutic protocols.

title: Neuropharmacology of cognition and memory: a unifying theory of neuromodulator imbalance in psychiatry and amnesia.
abstract: The neurobiological mechanisms underlying cognitive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders and amnesia remain poorly understood, despite growing evidence that multiple neurotransmitter systems are involved. We propose a unifying theoretical framework suggesting that cognitive impairments across diverse conditions result from imbalances in key neuromodulatory systems rather than discrete neurotransmitter deficits. This hypothesis integrates emerging data on acetylcholine, dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin interactions in hippocampal and prefrontal circuits critical for memory formation and executive function. We systematically reviewed neurochemical, pharmacological, and neuroimaging studies published between 1990-2005 examining cognitive dysfunction in major depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and various amnesias. Converging evidence suggests that optimal cognitive performance requires precise ratios between cholinergic enhancement and dopaminergic modulation in the hippocampus, while prefrontal executive functions depend on balanced noradrenergic and serotonergic tone. Disruption of these neuromodulatory balances, rather than absolute neurotransmitter levels, appears to underlie memory consolidation deficits and attentional impairments observed across psychiatric conditions. In amnesia syndromes, similar imbalances occur secondary to structural damage affecting neuromodulatory projections. This framework predicts that therapeutic interventions targeting neuromodulatory ratios rather than individual neurotransmitter systems may prove more effective for cognitive enhancement. The model suggests novel combination therapies and provides testable hypotheses for future pharmacological studies aimed at restoring optimal neuromodulatory balance in cognitive disorders.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology at the new millennium: new industry directions.
abstract: The neuropharmaceutical industry stands at a critical juncture as we enter the new millennium, with traditional drug discovery approaches yielding diminishing returns and escalating development costs threatening innovation in central nervous system therapeutics. This review examines emerging paradigms in neuropsychopharmacology research and development based on analysis of industry trends, patent filings, and clinical trial data from 1990-1999. We surveyed major pharmaceutical companies (n=25) and biotechnology firms (n=47) regarding research priorities, technological adoptions, and strategic directions for the next decade. The genomics revolution has fundamentally altered target identification strategies, with 73% of surveyed companies now incorporating pharmacogenomic approaches compared to 12% in 1995. High-throughput screening capabilities have expanded 50-fold on average, while companies are increasingly focusing on mechanism-based drug design rather than serendipitous discovery. Neuroinformatics and computational modeling have emerged as essential tools, with 68% of firms establishing dedicated bioinformatics divisions. Clinical development strategies are shifting toward earlier proof-of-concept studies using biomarkers and surrogate endpoints, potentially reducing late-stage failures. Therapeutic areas showing greatest industry investment include Alzheimer's disease, depression-resistant conditions, and schizophrenia cognitive deficits. Strategic alliances between pharmaceutical companies and academic institutions have increased 340% since 1995. These findings suggest that successful neuropsychopharmacology in the coming decade will require integration of genomics, advanced screening technologies, and novel clinical trial designs to address the growing burden of neuropsychiatric disorders while maintaining economic viability.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Neurobiological models of obsessive-compulsive disorder.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) represents a prevalent psychiatric condition characterized by intrusive thoughts and repetitive behaviors, yet its underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Recent advances in neuroimaging and pharmacological research have implicated dysfunction within specific brain circuits, particularly involving the orbitofrontal cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, and caudate nucleus. The present review synthesizes current neurobiological models of OCD based on clinical, pharmacological, and emerging neuroimaging evidence. We examined data from positron emission tomography studies, cerebrospinal fluid analyses, and treatment response patterns to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in OCD patients. Converging evidence supports a model in which hyperactivity within the orbitofrontal-subcortical circuit, specifically the orbitofrontal cortex-caudate-thalamic loop, underlies the pathophysiology of OCD. Neurochemical investigations reveal dysregulation of serotonergic transmission, with secondary involvement of dopaminergic and GABAergic systems. PET studies consistently demonstrate increased metabolic activity in the orbitofrontal cortex and caudate nucleus that normalizes following successful treatment with SSRIs or behavioral therapy. Additionally, pharmacological challenge studies with meta-chlorophenylpiperazine suggest heightened sensitivity of postsynaptic serotonin receptors in OCD patients. These findings support a neurobiological model wherein serotonergic dysfunction leads to disinhibition of the orbitofrontal-subcortical circuit, resulting in the repetitive thoughts and behaviors characteristic of OCD. This model provides a framework for understanding treatment mechanisms and developing novel therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems and brain circuits in obsessive-compulsive disorder.

title: The Influence of Prolonged Acetylsalicylic Acid Supplementation-Induced Gastritis on the Neurochemistry of the Sympathetic Neurons Supplying Prepyloric Region of the Porcine Stomach.
abstract: This experiment was designed to establish the localization and neurochemical phenotyping of sympathetic neurons supplying prepyloric area of the porcine stomach in a physiological state and during acetylsalicylic acid (ASA) induced gastritis. In order to localize the sympathetic perikarya the stomachs of both control and acetylsalicylic acid treated (ASA group) animals were injected with neuronal retrograde tracer Fast Blue (FB). Seven days post FB injection, animals were divided into a control and ASA supplementation group. The ASA group was given 100 mg/kg of b.w. ASA orally for 21 days. On the 28th day all pigs were euthanized with gradual overdose of anesthetic. Then fourteen-micrometer-thick cryostat sections were processed for routine double-labeling immunofluorescence, using primary antisera directed towards tyrosine hydroxylase (TH), dopamine β-hydroxylase (DβH), neuropeptide Y (NPY), galanin (GAL), neuronal nitric oxide synthase (nNOS), leu 5-enkephalin (LENK), cocaine- and amphetamine- regulated transcript peptide (CART), calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), substance P (SP) and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP). The data obtained in this study indicate that postganglionic sympathetic nerve fibers supplying prepyloric area of the porcine stomach originate from the coeliac-cranial mesenteric ganglion complex (CCMG). In control animals, the FB-labelled neurons expressed TH (94.85 ± 1.01%), DβH (97.10 ± 0.97%), NPY (46.88 ± 2.53%) and GAL (8.40 ± 0.53%). In ASA group, TH- and DβH- positive nerve cells were reduced (85.78 ± 2.65% and 88.82 ± 1.63% respectively). Moreover, ASA- induced gastritis resulted in increased expression of NPY (76.59 ± 3.02%) and GAL (26.45 ± 2.75%) as well as the novo-synthesis of nNOS (6.13 ± 1.11%) and LENK (4.77 ± 0.42%) in traced CCMG neurons. Additionally, a network of CART-, CGRP-, SP-, VIP-, LENK-, nNOS- immunoreactive (IR) nerve fibers encircling the FB-positive perikarya were observed in both intact and ASA-treated animals. The results of this study indicate involvement of these neuropeptides in the development or presumably counteraction of gastric inflammation.

title: Partial response, nonresponse, and relapse with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in major depression: a survey of current "next-step" practices.
abstract: Despite the widespread use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) as first-line treatment for major depressive disorder, substantial numbers of patients experience partial response, nonresponse, or relapse during treatment. However, limited data exist regarding clinicians' approaches to these treatment challenges in routine clinical practice. We conducted a nationwide survey of 847 psychiatrists and primary care physicians to examine current "next-step" strategies when patients fail to achieve or maintain adequate response to SSRI therapy. Participants completed a structured questionnaire detailing their management approaches for three clinical scenarios: partial response after 8-12 weeks of adequate SSRI dosing, complete nonresponse after 8-12 weeks, and relapse during maintenance therapy. Response rate was 73% (n=618). For partial responders, 54% of clinicians reported dose optimization as their preferred strategy, followed by augmentation with bupropion (23%) or lithium (18%). In cases of nonresponse, 67% favored switching to another SSRI, while 28% preferred switching to a non-SSRI antidepressant. For relapse scenarios, 71% increased the SSRI dose, 19% added augmentation therapy, and 10% switched medications. Significant differences emerged between psychiatrists and primary care physicians, with psychiatrists more likely to utilize augmentation strategies and combination therapies. Geographic variations were also observed, with urban practitioners showing greater willingness to employ complex regimens. These findings highlight substantial heterogeneity in clinical decision-making for SSRI treatment failures and underscore the need for evidence-based guidelines to optimize outcomes in this challenging patient population.

title: Effect of short-term intake of high- and low-concentrations of sucrose solution on the neurochemistry of male and female mice.
abstract: Chronic high sucrose consumption has been extensively studied for its neurochemical effects, yet the acute impact of varying sucrose concentrations on brain neurotransmitter systems remains poorly characterized. This study investigated sex-specific neurochemical responses following short-term exposure to different sucrose concentrations in adult C57BL/6J mice. Male and female mice (n=12 per group) were randomly assigned to receive either high-concentration sucrose (20% w/v), low-concentration sucrose (5% w/v), or water for 7 days ad libitum. Brain tissue samples from the prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and striatum were analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography to quantify dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and their metabolites. Behavioral assessments included sucrose preference tests and locomotor activity measurements. High-concentration sucrose significantly increased dopamine levels in the striatum of both sexes (p<0.01), with males showing greater elevation than females (2.3-fold vs. 1.7-fold increase). Serotonin turnover in the prefrontal cortex was enhanced in female mice receiving high-concentration sucrose (p<0.05) but remained unchanged in males. Low-concentration sucrose produced minimal neurochemical alterations compared to controls. Locomotor activity increased dose-dependently with sucrose concentration, particularly in female subjects. These findings demonstrate that short-term sucrose exposure induces rapid, concentration-dependent neurochemical changes with notable sex differences in neurotransmitter responses. The differential sensitivity between males and females suggests distinct neural mechanisms underlying sugar reward processing, with implications for understanding sex-specific vulnerabilities to dietary sugar overconsumption and related metabolic disorders.

title: Endocannabinoids and the neurochemistry of gluttony.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical mediator of feeding behavior and energy homeostasis, yet its role in pathological overeating remains poorly understood. We investigated the neurochemical mechanisms underlying cannabinoid-induced hyperphagia in the hypothalamic feeding circuits of adult male Wistar rats (n=48). Animals received bilateral microinjections of the CB1 receptor agonist WIN55,212-2 (0.1-1.0 μg) or vehicle into the paraventricular nucleus (PVN) or lateral hypothalamus (LH) during scheduled feeding periods. Food intake, meal microstructure, and tissue levels of anandamide (AEA) and 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG) were measured using HPLC-MS/MS. Immunohistochemistry and in situ hybridization examined neuropeptide Y (NPY), agouti-related peptide (AgRP), and proopiomelanocortin (POMC) expression. WIN55,212-2 administration in the LH, but not PVN, significantly increased 24-hour food intake (vehicle: 24.3±1.8g vs. high dose: 41.7±3.2g, p<0.001) and meal frequency without affecting meal size. This hyperphagic response was blocked by the CB1 antagonist rimonabant. Hypothalamic AEA levels were elevated 3-fold in food-deprived animals compared to ad libitum fed controls (p<0.01), while 2-AG remained unchanged. CB1 activation upregulated NPY and AgRP mRNA expression in the arcuate nucleus by 180% and 220% respectively, while suppressing POMC expression by 60% (all p<0.05). These findings demonstrate that endocannabinoids promote excessive food intake through CB1-mediated disinhibition of orexigenic pathways in the lateral hypothalamus, suggesting therapeutic targets for treating pathological overeating disorders.

title: The neuropharmacological basis for the use of memantine in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by cognitive decline, memory impairment, and eventual loss of functional independence. Current therapeutic approaches have focused primarily on cholinesterase inhibition, yet this strategy addresses only one aspect of the complex pathophysiology underlying AD. Emerging evidence suggests that excitotoxicity mediated by excessive N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor activation contributes significantly to neuronal death in AD. Memantine, a low-affinity, voltage-dependent NMDA receptor antagonist, offers a novel therapeutic mechanism by selectively blocking pathological receptor overactivation while preserving physiological glutamatergic neurotransmission. This review examines the neuropharmacological rationale for memantine use in AD treatment through analysis of preclinical studies, receptor binding assays, electrophysiological recordings, and clinical trial data from recent phase II and III studies. Preclinical investigations demonstrate that memantine exhibits neuroprotective properties in models of excitotoxic neuronal injury and reduces amyloid-beta-induced toxicity. Clinical studies involving moderate to severe AD patients reveal significant improvements in cognitive function, activities of daily living, and global clinical measures compared to placebo treatment. The drug's unique pharmacological profile, characterized by fast blocking kinetics and moderate affinity for NMDA receptors, allows for therapeutic intervention without interfering with normal synaptic transmission. These findings support memantine as a promising therapeutic agent that targets the glutamatergic system in AD, representing a complementary approach to existing cholinergic therapies and potentially offering disease-modifying benefits in addition to symptomatic improvement.

title: [The neurochemistry and neuropharmacology of frontotemporal dementia].
abstract: Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) represents a heterogeneous group of neurodegenerative disorders characterized by progressive deterioration of the frontal and temporal cortices, leading to profound changes in behavior, personality, and language. Despite its clinical significance as the second most common cause of presenile dementia, the neurochemical basis of FTD remains poorly understood compared to Alzheimer's disease. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neurochemical alterations and pharmacological interventions in FTD. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of postmortem studies, neuroimaging investigations, and clinical trials published between 1990 and 2005, focusing on neurotransmitter systems, protein aggregation patterns, and therapeutic approaches. The neurochemical profile of FTD reveals significant disruptions in multiple neurotransmitter systems, including reduced cholinergic and GABAergic activity in affected regions, altered serotonergic function correlating with behavioral symptoms, and dysregulation of dopaminergic pathways contributing to motor manifestations. Protein aggregation studies demonstrate distinct patterns of tau and ubiquitin-positive inclusions, differentiating FTD subtypes from other tauopathies. Current pharmacological interventions show limited efficacy, with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors providing modest benefits for behavioral symptoms and cholinesterase inhibitors showing inconsistent results. Novel therapeutic targets include modulation of tau phosphorylation, enhancement of GABAergic transmission, and neuroprotective strategies targeting oxidative stress. The complex neurochemical landscape of FTD necessitates development of targeted therapies addressing specific neurotransmitter deficits and pathological protein accumulation, moving beyond traditional approaches used in Alzheimer's disease.

title: Serial neurochemical measurement of cerebrospinal fluid during the human sexual response cycle.
abstract: Recent studies examining the neuroendocrine response pattern underlying the human sexual response cycle revealed transient activation of the sympathoadrenal system and a substantial, long-lasting increase in plasma prolactin concentrations following orgasm in men and women. Prolactin has been discussed as being part of a feedback mechanism that signals centers in the central nervous system, such as the dopaminergic system controlling sexual arousal. To further elucidate the central role of neuropeptides, biogenic monoamines and neurotransmitters in human sexual behavior, a serial cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)-sampling technique was implemented using a previously established experimental paradigm for sexual activity in a laboratory setting. In parallel with peripheral endocrine measures, lumbar CSF was drawn via an indwelling spinal catheter during the sexual response cycle in 10 healthy males and 10 age-matched controls, and analysed for prolactin, oxytocin, biogenic monoamines and/or their metabolites as well as inhibitory and excitatory neurotransmitter concentrations. Parallel to raised peripheral sympathetic activity, norepinephrine also increased in CSF during audiovisual, masturbation-induced sexual arousal and orgasm, and remained elevated for the remainder of the session (F(4,72) = 8.79, P = 0.000). In contrast, none of the other measures, in particular prolactin and dopamine or its metabolites, reflected significant alteration. In conclusion, the human sexual response cycle is characterized by an increase in sympathetic activity in plasma and CSF, and by pronounced secretion of plasma prolactin after orgasm. However, alterations in dopaminergic or peptidergic activity are not found in lumbar CSF, possibly due to local and restricted release in diencephalic and mesencephalic brain regions.

title: Dorsal raphe vs. median raphe serotonergic antagonism. Anatomical, physiological, behavioral, neuroendocrinological, neuropharmacological and clinical evidences: relevance for neuropharmacological therapy.
abstract: The serotonergic system comprises anatomically and functionally distinct dorsal raphe (DR) and median raphe (MR) nuclei that exhibit differential responses to pharmacological interventions and may contribute differently to psychiatric disorders. This study examined the contrasting effects of selective serotonergic modulation in DR versus MR regions using combined anatomical, electrophysiological, behavioral, and neuroendocrine approaches. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received microinjections of the 5-HT1A receptor antagonist WAY-100635 (0.1-1.0 μg) into either DR or MR nuclei, followed by assessment of anxiety-related behaviors in elevated plus maze and open field tests, measurement of plasma corticosterone and ACTH levels, and single-unit recordings from hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed distinct 5-HT1A receptor distribution patterns between DR and MR regions. DR antagonism produced anxiogenic effects, elevated stress hormone levels, and increased hippocampal theta rhythm power, while MR antagonism resulted in anxiolytic responses, reduced HPA axis activation, and decreased theta activity. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated that DR manipulation primarily affected dorsal hippocampal neuronal firing, whereas MR modulation influenced ventral hippocampal activity. These findings provide compelling evidence for functional antagonism between DR and MR serotonergic systems, with DR primarily mediating stress responses and anxiety, while MR appears to regulate emotional processing and behavioral inhibition. The differential therapeutic targets identified suggest that anatomically-selective serotonergic interventions may offer improved treatment strategies for anxiety disorders and depression, potentially explaining variable clinical responses to current serotonergic medications.

title: Comparison of the effects of clozapine and 8-hydroxy-2-(di-n-propylamino)tetralin (8-OH-DPAT) on progressive ratio schedule performance: evidence against the involvement of 5-HT1A receptors in the behavioural effects of clozapine.
abstract:
**Background:** Clozapine, an atypical antipsychotic with unique clinical efficacy, exhibits complex pharmacological properties including interactions with multiple neurotransmitter systems. While its therapeutic effects are primarily attributed to dopaminergic and serotonergic mechanisms, the specific contribution of 5-HT1A receptor activity to clozapine's behavioral profile remains unclear. Progressive ratio (PR) schedules, which measure motivation and reward sensitivity, provide a valuable tool for examining the behavioral effects of psychoactive compounds.

**Methods:** Male Sprague-Dawley rats were trained on a PR schedule for food reinforcement until stable baseline performance was achieved. Separate groups received acute injections of clozapine (1.25, 2.5, 5.0 mg/kg, i.p.), the selective 5-HT1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle. Breakpoint (highest ratio completed) and response rate were measured as primary outcome variables.

**Results:** Clozapine dose-dependently decreased breakpoint and response rate, with significant effects observed at 2.5 and 5.0 mg/kg. In contrast, 8-OH-DPAT produced a biphasic response pattern: low doses (0.025-0.05 mg/kg) slightly increased breakpoint, while higher doses (0.1-0.2 mg/kg) markedly decreased performance. The temporal pattern and magnitude of behavioral disruption differed substantially between compounds. Pretreatment with the 5-HT1A antagonist WAY-100635 blocked 8-OH-DPAT effects but did not alter clozapine's actions.

**Conclusions:** These findings demonstrate distinct behavioral profiles for clozapine and 8-OH-DPAT on PR performance, suggesting that 5-HT1A receptor activation does not significantly contribute to clozapine's motivational effects. The results challenge previous hypotheses regarding 5-HT1A involvement in atypical antipsychotic action and support alternative mechanisms underlying clozapine's unique pharmacological properties.

title: Comparative study of chemical neuroanatomy of the olfactory neuropil in mouse, honey bee, and human.
abstract: Despite divergent evolutionary origins, the organization of olfactory systems is remarkably similar across phyla. In both insects and mammals, sensory input from receptor cells is initially processed in synaptically dense regions of neuropil called glomeruli, where neural activity is shaped by local inhibition and centrifugal neuromodulation prior to being sent to higher-order brain areas by projection neurons. Here we review both similarities and several key differences in the neuroanatomy of the olfactory system in honey bees, mice, and humans, using a combination of literature review and new primary data. We have focused on the chemical identity and the innervation patterns of neuromodulatory inputs in the primary olfactory system. Our findings show that serotonergic fibers are similarly distributed across glomeruli in all three species. Octopaminergic/tyraminergic fibers in the honey bee also have a similar distribution, and possibly a similar function, to noradrenergic fibers in the mammalian OBs. However, preliminary evidence suggests that human OB may be relatively less organized than its counterparts in honey bee and mouse.

title: Mass spectrometry imaging, an emerging technology in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry imaging (MALDI-MSI) represents a transformative analytical approach for investigating drug distribution and neurochemical changes in brain tissue with unprecedented spatial resolution. Traditional neuropsychopharmacological studies rely on tissue homogenization techniques that obscure regional drug localization and metabolite distribution patterns critical for understanding therapeutic mechanisms. This review synthesizes current applications of MALDI-MSI in neuropsychopharmacology research, examining methodological advances and their implications for drug discovery and development. We analyzed peer-reviewed publications from 2008-2014 utilizing MSI techniques for central nervous system drug studies, focusing on spatial resolution capabilities, quantitative accuracy, and biological relevance. MALDI-MSI enables direct detection and mapping of pharmaceuticals, neurotransmitters, and their metabolites at cellular-level resolution (10-50 μm) while preserving tissue architecture. Recent studies demonstrate successful imaging of antipsychotics, antidepressants, and anxiolytics across discrete brain regions, revealing heterogeneous distribution patterns not detectable through conventional methods. Quantitative improvements through internal standard incorporation and matrix optimization have enhanced analytical reliability, with detection limits reaching femtomolar concentrations for select compounds. Temporal pharmacokinetic studies show dynamic drug accumulation in target regions, providing mechanistic insights into therapeutic onset and duration. These findings establish MALDI-MSI as a powerful complement to traditional pharmacological approaches, offering unique capabilities for understanding drug action in complex neural networks. Integration of MSI technology with existing neuropsychopharmacological methodologies promises to accelerate drug development through improved target validation and biomarker identification, ultimately enhancing therapeutic strategies for psychiatric and neurological disorders.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: A primary culture system for biochemical analyses of neuronal proteins.
abstract: Low-density cultures of embryonic rat hippocampal neurons have been widely used to investigate localization and function of neuronal proteins using immunocytochemistry and electrophysiology. These cultures provide a relatively homogeneous population of hippocampal pyramidal neurons and interneurons compared to post-natal mixed neuron/glial cultures from hippocampus, cerebral cortex, and cerebellum. However, the limited quantity of neurons and the difficulty in harvesting adequate amounts makes biochemical analyses of endogenous neuronal proteins in these low-density cultured neurons difficult. Here, we provide detailed methods to prepare cultures of embryonic rat hippocampal neurons suitable for biochemical analyses of both endogenously and exogenously expressed proteins. The procedures described here are also suitable for comprehensive studies of expression, localization, post-translational modification, and function of neuronal proteins in the same neuronal culture system.

title: The utility of functional interaction and cluster analysis in CNS proteomics.
abstract: Proteomic studies offer enormous potential for gaining insight into cellular dynamics and disease processes. An immediate challenge for enhancing the utility of proteomics in translational research lies in methods of handling and interpreting the large datasets generated. Publications rarely extend beyond lists of proteins, putatively altered derived from basic statistics. Here we describe two additional distinct approaches (with particular strengths and limitations) that will enhance the analysis of proteomic datasets. Arithmetic and functional cluster analyses have been performed on proteins found differentially regulated in human glioma. These two approaches highlight (i) subgroups of proteins that may be co-regulated and play a role in glioma pathophysiology, and (ii) functional protein interactions that may improve comprehension of the biological mechanisms involved. A coherent proteomic strategy which involves both arithmetic and functional clustering, (together with careful consideration of conceptual limitations), is imperative for quantitative proteomics to deliver and advance the biological understanding of disease of the CNS. A strategy which combines arithmetic analysis and bioinformatics of protein-protein interactions is both generally applicable and will facilitate the interpretation of proteomic data.

title: Neuropathology in Alzheimer's disease: target of pharmacotherapy.
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) represents the most prevalent form of dementia, affecting approximately 4.5 million Americans and characterized by progressive cognitive decline, memory impairment, and eventual loss of functional independence. The neuropathological hallmarks of AD include extracellular amyloid-β (Aβ) plaques, intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein, synaptic dysfunction, and widespread neuronal death. Current pharmacotherapeutic approaches primarily target the cholinergic deficit through acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and the glutamatergic system via NMDA receptor antagonism (memantine). This review synthesizes current understanding of AD neuropathology and evaluates emerging therapeutic targets based on analysis of recent clinical trials, postmortem studies, and preclinical research from 1995-2005. Evidence demonstrates that while cholinesterase inhibitors provide modest symptomatic benefits in mild-to-moderate AD, they do not address underlying disease mechanisms. Promising therapeutic targets include β-secretase and γ-secretase enzymes involved in amyloid processing, tau kinases responsible for neurofibrillary tangle formation, inflammatory mediators, and oxidative stress pathways. Clinical trials investigating Aβ immunotherapy, secretase inhibitors, and anti-inflammatory agents show preliminary efficacy in slowing cognitive decline. However, the complex, multifactorial nature of AD neuropathology suggests that combination therapies targeting multiple pathways may be necessary for disease modification. Future pharmacological interventions must address both symptomatic management and disease-modifying mechanisms to meaningfully impact the trajectory of this devastating neurodegenerative disorder.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Nurse Practitioners

title: Antidepressant-related sexual dysfunction - perspectives from neuroimaging.
abstract: Antidepressant-induced sexual dysfunction (AISD) affects 30-70% of patients treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study employed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to examine neural activation patterns associated with sexual arousal in healthy controls versus patients experiencing AISD. Twenty-four healthy volunteers and eighteen patients with major depressive disorder experiencing AISD following chronic SSRI treatment (mean duration 8.3 ± 3.2 months) underwent fMRI scanning while viewing erotic visual stimuli. Blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal changes were measured in regions of interest including the anterior cingulate cortex, insula, amygdala, hypothalamus, and genital sensory cortex. Subjective arousal ratings and physiological measures of sexual response were also collected. Compared to controls, AISD patients showed significantly reduced activation in the hypothalamus (p < 0.01), anterior cingulate cortex (p < 0.05), and right insula (p < 0.05) during erotic stimulus presentation. These neural differences correlated with decreased subjective arousal ratings (r = 0.68, p < 0.001) and diminished physiological responses. Notably, hypothalamic hypoactivation was most strongly associated with reported sexual dysfunction severity (r = -0.72, p < 0.001). These findings suggest that SSRI-induced alterations in serotonergic neurotransmission may disrupt normal hypothalamic and limbic processing of sexual stimuli, providing neurobiological evidence for the mechanisms underlying AISD. Understanding these neural substrates may inform the development of targeted therapeutic interventions for this prevalent and distressing side effect.

title: An integrative dynamic model of brain energy metabolism using in vivo neurochemical measurements.
abstract: Brain energy metabolism is critical for normal neuronal function, yet the dynamic relationships between glucose utilization, oxygen consumption, and neurotransmitter cycling remain poorly understood in vivo. We developed an integrative computational model that combines real-time neurochemical measurements with metabolic flux analysis to characterize brain energy dynamics during physiological states. Using microdialysis probes in awake, freely-moving rats (n=12), we simultaneously measured extracellular glucose, lactate, glutamate, and GABA concentrations in the hippocampus during rest, mild stress, and recovery periods. Oxygen tension was monitored using polarographic electrodes, while local field potentials were recorded to assess neural activity. These experimental data were incorporated into a multi-compartment mathematical model describing neuronal and glial metabolic interactions, including the astrocyte-neuron lactate shuttle and glutamate-glutamine cycle. The model successfully reproduced observed metabolic patterns, revealing that astrocytic glucose uptake increased 340% during neural activation while neuronal glucose utilization rose only 180%. Lactate production by astrocytes peaked within 2 minutes of stimulus onset, preceding maximal neuronal lactate consumption by 45 seconds. The model predicted that glutamate clearance accounted for 60-70% of total energy expenditure during high activity states. Sensitivity analysis identified astrocytic glucose transport and glutamate uptake kinetics as critical parameters governing metabolic responses. This integrative approach provides quantitative insights into brain bioenergetics and establishes a framework for investigating metabolic dysfunction in neurological disorders. The model's predictive capability may prove valuable for optimizing neuroprotective strategies and understanding energy-related pathophysiology.

title: Discovery of novel tetrahydro-pyrazolo [4,3-c] pyridines for the treatment of neuropathic pain: synthesis and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Neuropathic pain represents a significant therapeutic challenge with limited treatment options displaying both efficacy and tolerability. Voltage-gated sodium channels, particularly Nav1.7 and Nav1.8, have emerged as promising targets for neuropathic pain management due to their preferential expression in nociceptive neurons. In this study, we report the discovery and characterization of a novel series of tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridine derivatives as selective sodium channel modulators. A library of 47 compounds was synthesized through a convergent approach involving cyclization of appropriately substituted hydrazines with β-ketoesters, followed by subsequent reduction and functionalization. Lead optimization focused on substitutions at the N1 and C6 positions to enhance potency and selectivity. Electrophysiological evaluation using whole-cell patch-clamp techniques on recombinant Nav channels revealed that compound 23c exhibited potent inhibition of Nav1.7 (IC₅₀ = 180 nM) and Nav1.8 (IC₅₀ = 95 nM) with >50-fold selectivity over cardiac Nav1.5. Structure-activity relationships indicated that electron-withdrawing groups at the C6 position and lipophilic substituents at N1 were crucial for activity. In vivo studies using the chronic constriction injury model in rats demonstrated significant anti-allodynic effects for compound 23c (ED₅₀ = 12 mg/kg, p.o.) without motor impairment or cardiovascular effects at therapeutic doses. These results establish tetrahydro-pyrazolo[4,3-c]pyridines as a promising new chemotype for developing next-generation analgesics targeting peripheral sodium channels in neuropathic pain conditions.

title: Molecular neurochemistry of the lanthanides.
abstract: Lanthanides, once termed rare-earth elements, are not as sparce in the environment as their traditional name suggests. Mean litospheric concentrations are in fact comparable to the physiologically fundamental elements such as iodine, cobalt, and selenium. Recent advances in medical technology have resulted in accumulation of lanthanides presenting potential exposure to both our central and peripheral nervous systems. Extensive and detailed studies on these peculiar active metals in the context of their influence on neural functions are therefore urgently required. Almost all neurochemical effects of trivalent lanthanide ions appear to result from the similarity of their radii to the key signaling ion calcium. Lanthanides, especially La3+ and Gd3+ block different types of calcium, potassium, and sodium channels in human and animal neurons, regulate neurotransmitter turnover and release, as well as synaptic activity. Lanthanides also act as modulators of several ionotropic receptors, e.g., GABA, NMDA, and kainate and can also affect numerous signaling mechanisms including NF-κB and apoptotic-related endoplasmic reticulum IRE1-XBP1, PERK, and ATF6 pathways. Several lanthanide ions may cause oxidative neuronal injuries and functional impairment by promoting reactive oxygen species production. However, cerium and yttrium oxides have some unique and promising neuroprotective properties, being able to decrease free radical cell injury and even alleviate motor impairment and cognitive function in animal models of multiple sclerosis and mild traumatic brain damage, respectively. In conclusion, lanthanides affect various neurophysiological processes, altering a large spectrum of brain functions. Thus, a deeper understanding of their potential mechanistic roles during disease and as therapeutic agents requires urgent elucidation.

title: Neurochemical signatures revealed by glutamine labeling in the chicken retina.
abstract: The retina contains multiple neuronal populations that utilize glutamate as their primary neurotransmitter, including photoreceptors, bipolar cells, and ganglion cells. While previous studies have characterized glutamate immunoreactivity in retinal neurons, the metabolic pathways underlying glutamate synthesis and cycling remain poorly understood. We employed [³H]glutamine autoradiography combined with selective pharmacological treatments to investigate glutamine uptake and metabolism in the chicken retina, a well-established model for vertebrate retinal organization. Eyecups from 14-day-old white leghorn chickens were incubated with [³H]glutamine (50 μCi/ml) for 30 minutes, either alone or in the presence of methionine sulfoximine (MSO), an inhibitor of glutamine synthetase, or aminooxyacetic acid (AOAA), an inhibitor of aminotransferases. Autoradiographic analysis revealed intense [³H]glutamine labeling in the inner nuclear layer, particularly in putative Müller glial cells, with moderate labeling in the ganglion cell layer and outer plexiform layer. MSO treatment significantly reduced labeling in Müller cells by 73% while enhancing labeling in bipolar and amacrine cell populations. AOAA treatment produced a distinct laminar pattern, with preferential accumulation in the inner plexiform layer and reduced labeling in photoreceptor terminals. Quantitative densitometry demonstrated that glutamine uptake varies significantly across retinal cell types, with Müller cells showing the highest capacity for glutamine metabolism. These findings suggest that glutamine serves as a precursor for multiple neurotransmitter pools in the retina and that glial-neuronal metabolic coupling plays a critical role in maintaining glutamatergic transmission in retinal circuits.

title: Psychopharmacology of traumatic brain injury.
abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) represents a significant clinical challenge with complex pathophysiology involving excitotoxicity, neuroinflammation, and disrupted neurotransmitter systems. Current pharmacological interventions remain limited, necessitating comprehensive evaluation of existing psychotropic medications in TBI populations. This systematic review analyzed clinical trials and observational studies from 2010-2018 examining psychopharmacological treatments in TBI patients (n=4,847 across 23 studies). We evaluated efficacy and safety profiles of antidepressants, anticonvulsants, dopaminergic agents, and cognitive enhancers for post-TBI neuropsychiatric symptoms and cognitive dysfunction. Sertraline and citalopram demonstrated superior efficacy for post-TBI depression compared to tricyclic antidepressants, with fewer anticholinergic side effects potentially impairing cognitive recovery. Methylphenidate showed modest improvements in attention and processing speed (effect size 0.42, p<0.05) but increased seizure risk in acute phases. Levetiracetam emerged as the preferred anticonvulsant, showing neuroprotective properties and lower cognitive impairment compared to phenytoin or valproate. Amantadine administration within 30 days post-injury accelerated functional recovery in severe TBI patients (median recovery time 14 vs 21 days, p<0.01). However, modafinil showed inconsistent results for fatigue management. Notable safety concerns included increased intracranial pressure with certain antipsychotics and delayed wound healing with high-dose corticosteroids. These findings support a precision medicine approach to post-TBI psychopharmacology, emphasizing careful drug selection based on injury severity, timeline, and individual risk factors. Further research is needed to establish optimal dosing protocols and long-term safety profiles in this vulnerable population.

title: Identifying novel protein-protein interactions using co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectroscopy.
abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to neuronal signaling cascades and synaptic plasticity, yet many critical interactions remain uncharacterized. Traditional biochemical approaches have limited sensitivity for detecting transient or low-abundance protein complexes in neural tissues. We developed an optimized protocol combining co-immunoprecipitation (co-IP) with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) to systematically identify novel PPIs in mouse brain tissue. Adult C57BL/6 mice were sacrificed and cortical, hippocampal, and striatal regions were rapidly dissected and homogenized under native conditions. Co-IP was performed using antibodies against known synaptic scaffolding proteins including PSD-95, NMDAR1, and mGluR5. Immunoprecipitated complexes were resolved by SDS-PAGE, subjected to in-gel trypsin digestion, and analyzed by high-resolution LC-MS/MS using a linear ion trap mass spectrometer. Database searches identified peptides with >95% confidence scores. We identified 127 high-confidence protein interactions, including 43 novel PPIs not previously reported in neuronal tissues. Notable discoveries included interactions between mGluR5 and several previously uncharacterized membrane-associated proteins, and a novel binding partner for PSD-95 with predicted kinase activity. Validation by reciprocal co-IP confirmed 89% of identified interactions. Brain region-specific analysis revealed distinct interaction profiles, suggesting specialized protein complex assembly across neural circuits. This optimized co-IP/MS protocol provides a robust platform for discovering functionally relevant protein interactions in neural tissues and may facilitate identification of new therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

title: Drugs used to treat Parkinson's disease, present status and future directions.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) affects approximately 1% of individuals over 65 years of age and represents one of the most prevalent neurodegenerative disorders worldwide. Current pharmacological interventions primarily target the dopaminergic deficit characteristic of PD, yet significant limitations remain in achieving optimal symptom control while minimizing adverse effects. This comprehensive review examines the current therapeutic armamentarium for PD management and evaluates emerging treatment paradigms. We systematically analyzed clinical efficacy data from randomized controlled trials published between 1990-2008 for established PD medications, including levodopa/carbidopa, dopamine agonists, monoamine oxidase-B inhibitors, and catechol-O-methyltransferase inhibitors. Additionally, we reviewed preclinical and early clinical data for novel therapeutic approaches targeting non-dopaminergic systems and neuroprotective strategies. Levodopa remains the gold standard for symptomatic treatment, providing superior motor benefit compared to dopamine agonists, though long-term use is associated with motor fluctuations and dyskinesias in 50-80% of patients within 5-10 years. Dopamine agonists demonstrate efficacy in early PD with reduced dyskinesia risk but increased psychiatric adverse effects. Emerging therapies show promise, including adenosine A2A receptor antagonists, glutamate modulators, and compounds targeting α-synuclein aggregation. Novel drug delivery systems, such as continuous dopaminergic stimulation via pumps and patches, may address motor fluctuations. Future PD therapeutics will likely emphasize combination approaches targeting multiple pathophysiological mechanisms, including neuroinflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and protein aggregation, potentially offering disease modification beyond current symptomatic treatments.

title: Current evaluation of the millennium phytomedicine- ginseng (II): Collected chemical entities, modern pharmacology, and clinical applications emanated from traditional Chinese medicine.
abstract: Ginseng, particularly Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer, represents one of the most extensively studied phytomedicines of the new millennium, with over 2000 years of documented use in traditional Chinese medicine. This comprehensive review evaluates the current understanding of ginseng's chemical constituents, pharmacological mechanisms, and clinical applications in neurological and psychiatric disorders. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2008, focusing on ginsenosides and their neurotropic effects. Over 150 distinct ginsenosides have been isolated and characterized, with Rb1, Rg1, and Re demonstrating the most significant neuroactive properties. Mechanistic studies reveal that ginsenosides modulate multiple neurotransmitter systems, including cholinergic, GABAergic, and dopaminergic pathways, while exhibiting neuroprotective effects through antioxidant mechanisms and mitochondrial stabilization. Preclinical investigations demonstrate cognitive enhancement, anti-fatigue properties, and neuroprotection against ischemic injury in rodent models. Clinical trials involving 2,847 participants across 23 randomized controlled studies show modest but statistically significant improvements in cognitive performance, mood regulation, and stress adaptation compared to placebo controls. Meta-analysis indicates effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6 for various neuropsychological parameters. Safety profiles remain favorable with minimal adverse events reported at therapeutic doses (200-400 mg daily standardized extract). These findings support ginseng's potential as an adjunctive therapy for age-related cognitive decline and stress-related disorders, though larger long-term studies are needed to establish definitive clinical efficacy and optimal dosing regimens.

title: Differential neuropsychopharmacological influences of naturally occurring tropane alkaloids anisodamine versus scopolamine.
abstract: Tropane alkaloids derived from Solanaceae plants have been extensively studied for their anticholinergic properties, yet differential neuropsychopharmacological effects among structurally similar compounds remain poorly characterized. This study compared the behavioral and neurochemical effects of anisodamine, a naturally occurring tropane alkaloid from Anisodus tanguticus, with scopolamine in adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals received intraperitoneal injections of anisodamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), scopolamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg), or saline vehicle. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity monitoring, Morris water maze performance, and prepulse inhibition testing. Neurochemical analyses measured acetylcholine, dopamine, and serotonin levels in hippocampus, striatum, and prefrontal cortex using HPLC. Both compounds dose-dependently impaired spatial learning and memory consolidation, with scopolamine producing more severe deficits at equivalent doses. Anisodamine induced moderate hyperlocomotion at higher doses, whereas scopolamine caused initial hypoactivity followed by delayed hyperactivity. Prepulse inhibition was disrupted by both alkaloids, but anisodamine showed less pronounced effects. Neurochemically, scopolamine produced greater acetylcholine depletion in hippocampus and prefrontal cortex compared to anisodamine. Interestingly, anisodamine selectively increased striatal dopamine levels, an effect not observed with scopolamine. These findings suggest that despite structural similarity, anisodamine exhibits a distinct neuropsychopharmacological profile characterized by milder cognitive impairment and unique dopaminergic interactions, potentially offering therapeutic advantages over conventional anticholinergic agents in certain clinical applications.

title: [Research progress in neuropsychopharmacology updated for the post-genomic era].
abstract: The completion of the Human Genome Project in 2003 has fundamentally transformed neuropsychopharmacology research, shifting the paradigm from traditional phenotype-driven approaches to genotype-based drug discovery and personalized medicine strategies. This review examines the current state of neuropsychopharmacological research in the post-genomic era, analyzing developments from 2004 to 2009. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of major neuropsychopharmacology publications, focusing on genomics-based drug discovery, pharmacogenomics applications, and novel therapeutic targets identified through genomic approaches. Database searches included PubMed, Web of Science, and Japanese medical databases, encompassing studies on major psychiatric disorders including schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and anxiety disorders. Our analysis reveals significant progress in several key areas: identification of over 150 novel CNS drug targets through genome-wide association studies (GWAS), development of pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressant and antipsychotic medications, and emergence of epigenetic-based therapeutic strategies. Notably, cytochrome P450 genotyping has become clinically relevant for personalizing psychotropic drug dosing, while GWAS have identified new susceptibility loci for psychiatric disorders. However, the translation of genomic discoveries into approved therapeutics remains limited, with only three genomics-derived neuropsychiatric drugs reaching clinical trials by 2009. The post-genomic era has revolutionized our understanding of psychiatric disorders' molecular basis, yet significant challenges remain in bridging genomic knowledge to clinical applications. Future research must focus on functional validation of genomic targets and development of more sophisticated personalized treatment algorithms to fully realize the potential of genomics-based neuropsychopharmacology.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: The neuropharmacology of sleep paralysis hallucinations: serotonin 2A activation and a novel therapeutic drug.
abstract: Sleep paralysis (SP) is a parasomnia characterized by temporary inability to move accompanied by vivid and often terrifying hallucinations, affecting 7-8% of the general population. While the cholinergic mechanisms underlying SP atonia are well-established, the neuropharmacological basis of SP hallucinations remains poorly understood. Recent evidence suggests involvement of serotonergic pathways, particularly 5-HT2A receptors, which mediate hallucinogenic effects of psychedelics. This study investigated the role of 5-HT2A receptor activation in SP hallucinations and evaluated a novel therapeutic intervention. We administered the 5-HT2A agonist 25I-NBOMe (0.3 mg/kg) to sleep-deprived C57BL/6 mice during REM sleep rebound periods and measured behavioral correlates of hallucination-like states using the head-twitch response (HTR) paradigm modified for paralyzed animals. SP episodes were induced using the REM sleep fragmentation protocol combined with physostigmine administration. 5-HT2A activation significantly increased HTR frequency during SP episodes (p<0.001) and enhanced c-Fos expression in visual and auditory cortices. The selective 5-HT2A antagonist MDL 11939 (2 mg/kg) reduced both HTR responses and hallucination-associated cortical activity by 68% compared to vehicle controls. Chronic MDL 11939 treatment over 14 days decreased SP episode frequency from 4.2±0.8 to 1.6±0.4 episodes per week in a clinical pilot study of 24 recurrent SP patients. These findings demonstrate that 5-HT2A receptor hyperactivation contributes to SP hallucinations and suggest that selective 5-HT2A antagonists may represent a promising therapeutic approach for managing this distressing parasomnia.

title: Human Brain Neuropharmacology: A Platform for Translational Neuroscience.
abstract: Central nervous system (CNS) drug development has been plagued by a failure to translate effective therapies from the lab to the clinic. There are many potential reasons for this, including poor understanding of brain pharmacokinetic (PK) and pharmacodynamic (PD) factors, preclinical study flaws, clinical trial design issues, the complexity and variability of human brain diseases, as well as species differences. To address some of these problems, we have developed a platform for CNS drug discovery comprising: drug screening of primary adult human brain cells; human brain tissue microarray analysis of drug targets; and high-content phenotypic screening methods. In this opinion, I summarise the theoretical basis and the practical development and use of this platform in CNS drug discovery.

title: Beyond the reward hypothesis: alternative functions of nucleus accumbens dopamine.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAcc) has been predominantly studied within the framework of reward processing and addiction, where dopamine release is viewed as encoding reward prediction error and motivational salience. However, accumulating evidence suggests that NAcc dopamine serves broader computational functions beyond hedonic processing. This review synthesizes recent findings challenging the traditional reward hypothesis and proposes alternative roles for accumbens dopamine signaling. We examined literature from 2000-2005 encompassing electrophysiological recordings, microdialysis studies, and behavioral pharmacology experiments in rodents and non-human primates. Lesion studies utilizing 6-OHDA and pharmacological manipulations with dopamine receptor antagonists were analyzed alongside emerging optogenetic approaches. Our analysis reveals that NAcc dopamine responds robustly to aversive stimuli, uncertainty, and novelty independent of reward value. Dopaminergic signaling in the shell and core subregions exhibits distinct temporal dynamics during action selection, with phasic release preceding motor initiation rather than following reward delivery. Furthermore, dopamine depletion impairs effort-based decision making and behavioral switching without abolishing reward preference. These findings indicate that NAcc dopamine functions as a general motivational signal that energizes behavior and facilitates adaptive responding to environmental demands. Rather than simply encoding "wanting" or reward prediction, accumbens dopamine appears to regulate behavioral activation, attention allocation, and cognitive flexibility. This reconceptualization has important implications for understanding psychiatric disorders characterized by motivational deficits, including depression and schizophrenia, and suggests novel therapeutic targets beyond traditional reward-based frameworks.

title: Neurochemically distinct myenteric neurone populations containing calbindin have specific distribution patterns around the circumference of the gastric corpus.
abstract: The myenteric plexus of the stomach contains neurochemically diverse populations of neurons that regulate gastric motility and secretion. While calbindin-D28k (CB) has been identified as a calcium-binding protein expressed in enteric neurons, the precise distribution and neurochemical phenotypes of CB-immunoreactive neurons in different regions of the gastric wall remain poorly characterized. We investigated the circumferential distribution patterns of CB-containing myenteric neurons in the gastric corpus of adult Wistar rats using immunohistochemistry and confocal microscopy. Whole-mount preparations of the gastric corpus were processed for CB immunoreactivity and double-labeled with markers for cholinergic (choline acetyltransferase), nitrergic (neuronal nitric oxide synthase), and peptidergic (vasoactive intestinal peptide, substance P) neurons. CB-immunoreactive neurons comprised 23.4 ± 2.1% of total myenteric neurons and exhibited distinct regional variations around the gastric circumference. The density of CB-positive neurons was significantly higher along the greater curvature (31.2 ± 3.4%) compared to the lesser curvature (16.8 ± 2.2%) and anterior wall (19.1 ± 2.6%). Double-labeling revealed three neurochemically distinct CB-positive populations: CB/choline acetyltransferase-positive neurons (42% of CB cells), CB/neuronal nitric oxide synthase-positive neurons (28%), and CB/vasoactive intestinal peptide-positive neurons (18%). Each subpopulation showed specific circumferential distribution patterns, with CB/cholinergic neurons predominating along the greater curvature. These findings demonstrate that calbindin-containing myenteric neurons comprise functionally distinct subpopulations with region-specific distributions in the gastric corpus, suggesting specialized roles in the neural control of gastric function that vary according to anatomical location.

title: Methodological approaches to exploring epileptic disorders in the human brain in vitro.
abstract: The investigation of epileptic mechanisms in human brain tissue represents a critical yet methodologically challenging aspect of neuropharmacological research. While animal models have provided valuable insights, species differences in neuronal architecture and neurotransmitter systems necessitate direct examination of human epileptic tissue. We describe a comprehensive methodological framework for maintaining viable human cortical specimens obtained from surgical resections of epileptogenic foci in patients with medically refractory epilepsy. Brain slices (400-500 μm) were prepared within 30 minutes of surgical removal and maintained in oxygenated artificial cerebrospinal fluid at 32°C. Electrophysiological recordings were performed using multielectrode arrays and patch-clamp techniques to characterize spontaneous and evoked epileptiform activity. Pharmacological interventions included application of various antiepileptic drugs, glutamate receptor antagonists, and GABA modulators. Tissue viability was assessed through cellular morphology, membrane potential stability, and metabolic indicators over 8-12 hour recording periods. Our protocol consistently yielded robust epileptiform discharges in 78% of specimens (n=45 patients), with maintained neuronal responsiveness to pharmacological manipulation for up to 10 hours post-resection. AMPA/kainate receptor blockade reduced seizure-like activity by 65±12%, while gabazine enhanced discharge frequency by 180±34%. This methodology provides a reliable platform for investigating human epileptic mechanisms and screening potential therapeutic compounds directly in pathological human neural networks, offering advantages over conventional animal models for translational epilepsy research.

title: A novel method for monitoring the cell surface expression of heteromeric protein complexes in dispersed neurons and acute hippocampal slices.
abstract: The study of heteromeric protein complexes at neuronal cell surfaces has been limited by technical challenges in monitoring their native expression and localization in physiologically relevant preparations. Traditional approaches using overexpression systems or isolated membrane preparations often fail to recapitulate the endogenous stoichiometry and trafficking of multi-subunit complexes. Here we describe a novel dual-labeling approach combining biotinylation-based surface labeling with proximity ligation assays to specifically detect heteromeric complexes at the plasma membrane of live neurons. Primary hippocampal neurons (DIV 14-21) and acute hippocampal slices (300 μm) from P14-P21 rats were surface-biotinylated using membrane-impermeant NHS-SS-biotin at 4°C, followed by fixation and proximity ligation using antibodies against different subunits of target complexes. We validated this method using NMDA receptors containing GluN1/GluN2A subunits and GABAA receptors containing α1/β2/γ2 subunits. Surface biotinylation efficiency exceeded 85% as confirmed by streptavidin-fluorophore labeling, while proximity ligation signals showed >95% colocalization with surface markers. The method successfully detected developmental changes in NMDA receptor subunit composition and revealed distinct subcellular distributions of GABAA receptor subtypes in CA1 pyramidal neurons. Background signal in non-biotinylated controls remained below 5% of experimental values. This approach provides a powerful tool for investigating the cell surface expression and localization of endogenous heteromeric protein complexes in native neuronal preparations, enabling more physiologically relevant studies of synaptic protein trafficking and organization.

title: Translational neuropharmacology: the use of human isolated gastrointestinal tissues.
abstract: Translational sciences increasingly emphasize the measurement of functions in native human tissues. However, such studies must confront variations in patient age, gender, genetic background and disease. Here, these are discussed with reference to neuromuscular and neurosecretory functions of the human gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Tissues are obtained after informed consent, in collaboration with surgeons (surgical techniques help minimize variables) and pathologists. Given the difficulties of directly recording from human myenteric neurones (embedded between muscle layers), enteric motor nerve functions are studied by measuring muscle contractions/relaxations evoked by electrical stimulation of intrinsic nerves; responses are regionally dependent, often involving cholinergic and nitrergic phenotypes. Enteric sensory functions can be studied by evoking the peristaltic reflex, involving enteric sensory and motor nerves, but this has rarely been achieved. As submucosal neurones are more accessible (after removing the mucosa), direct neuronal recordings are possible. Neurosecretory functions are studied by measuring changes in short-circuit current across the mucosa. For all experiments, basic questions must be addressed.
