Because tissues are from patients, what are the controls and the influence of disease? How long does it take before function fully recovers? What is the impact of age- and gender-related differences? What is the optimal sample size? Addressing these and other questions minimizes variability and raises the scientific credibility of human tissue research. Such studies also reduce animal use. Further, the many differences between animal and human GI functions also means that human tissue research must question the ethical validity of using strains of animals with unproved translational significance.

title: Microelectrode array-based system for neuropharmacological applications with cortical neurons cultured in vitro.
abstract: In vitro neuronal networks cultured on microelectrode arrays (MEAs) represent a promising platform for neuropharmacological screening and mechanistic studies of neural activity modulation. However, existing MEA systems often lack the temporal resolution and signal processing capabilities required for comprehensive pharmacological analysis. We developed an integrated MEA-based system optimized for real-time monitoring of spontaneous and evoked electrical activity in primary rat cortical neurons. The system incorporates a 60-electrode planar array with 30 μm diameter electrodes spaced at 200 μm intervals, coupled to custom amplification circuitry and digital signal processing algorithms for spike detection and network burst analysis. Primary cortical neurons from embryonic day 18 Sprague-Dawley rats were cultured on poly-D-lysine coated MEAs and maintained for 14-21 days in vitro. We characterized baseline network activity patterns and tested acute responses to GABA_A receptor modulators (muscimol, bicuculline), glutamate receptor antagonists (CNQX, APV), and sodium channel blockers (TTX, lidocaine). The system reliably detected single-unit spikes with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 4:1 and temporal resolution of 40 kHz. Pharmacological interventions produced dose-dependent, reversible changes in firing rates and network burst characteristics consistent with known mechanisms of action. Muscimol (1-50 μM) decreased spike rates by 65-95%, while bicuculline (10-100 μM) increased burst frequency by 200-400%. This MEA platform provides a robust, high-throughput approach for screening neuroactive compounds and investigating network-level effects of pharmacological interventions in vitro.

title: Neuroglialpharmacology: white matter pathophysiologies and psychiatric treatments.
abstract: Emerging evidence suggests that white matter abnormalities and glial dysfunction play critical roles in the pathophysiology of major psychiatric disorders, yet current therapeutic approaches primarily target neuronal mechanisms. Recent neuroimaging studies have revealed widespread white matter microstructural alterations in schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and major depression, while post-mortem analyses demonstrate significant oligodendrocyte pathology and altered myelin gene expression. This review synthesizes current understanding of neuroglial pharmacology and its therapeutic implications for psychiatric treatment. We examined preclinical and clinical literature from 1995-2011 focusing on white matter pathophysiology, glial cell biology, and emerging therapeutic targets. Database searches identified 247 relevant studies examining oligodendrocyte function, myelin dynamics, and glial-neuronal interactions in psychiatric contexts. Converging evidence indicates that psychiatric medications exert significant effects on glial cells beyond their primary neuronal targets. Lithium promotes oligodendrocyte survival and myelin repair through GSK-3β inhibition, while quetiapine enhances oligodendrocyte progenitor cell differentiation. Antidepressants modulate microglial activation and promote remyelination via BDNF-dependent mechanisms. Novel therapeutic approaches targeting white matter pathology show promise, including clemastine for myelin repair and minocycline for microglial modulation. Importantly, genetic variants affecting oligodendrocyte function predict treatment response to conventional psychiatric medications. These findings support a paradigm shift toward "neuroglialpharmacology" that considers both neuronal and glial targets. Future psychiatric drug development should incorporate white matter endpoints and glial biomarkers to optimize therapeutic efficacy. Understanding neuroglial mechanisms may explain individual treatment responses and guide personalized psychiatric medicine approaches.

title: A flexible system for hands-free intracranial microinjection.
abstract: Neuroscience research projects often use intracranial (IC) microinfusions to target drug delivery to specific brain areas during behavioral testing. These experiments require accurate and precisely-timed delivery of small volumes. We present here a stepper motor-powered micropump assembly for such delivery. This system is hands-free, does not use a potentially leaky fluid swivel or use long delivery tubes that are subject to peristaltic forces during animal movements, and has been applied in combination with other paradigms. This micropump system reliably delivers a wide range of fluid volumes (e.g. 50 nL to 1 microL in tissue or greater for intraventricular injections) bilaterally from two independent, commercially available microsyringes through standard surgically implanted guide cannulae. It is easy to build and disassemble for cleaning or changing microsyringes. This system can also be used for a variety of purposes, e.g. intracranial self-administration, place conditioning, and many more, with the advantage that it provides a way to gather important data in the seconds and minutes following IC microinfusion without disruption of the animal's behavior by handling.

title: Cell-Specific Neuropharmacology.
abstract: The advent of modern molecular tools has revealed unprecedented cellular heterogeneity within neural circuits, challenging traditional pharmacological approaches that broadly target receptor subtypes across diverse cell populations. While classical neuropharmacology has provided valuable therapeutic interventions, the lack of cell-type specificity often results in unwanted side effects and limits therapeutic precision. Here we review emerging strategies that enable cell-specific drug targeting within the nervous system, including chemogenetic approaches, optopharmacology, and targeted drug delivery systems. Designer receptors exclusively activated by designer drugs (DREADDs) have demonstrated remarkable success in selectively modulating genetically-defined neuronal populations in both preclinical models and early clinical applications. Optopharmacological tools, combining photochemistry with pharmacology, allow spatiotemporal control of drug action with cellular precision. Additionally, antibody-drug conjugates and nanoparticle-based delivery systems show promise for targeting specific neural cell types based on surface marker expression. Recent applications in treating neuropsychiatric disorders, chronic pain, and neurodegenerative diseases highlight the therapeutic potential of cell-specific approaches. For instance, selective targeting of medium spiny neurons in Huntington's disease models has shown improved efficacy compared to conventional treatments. However, challenges remain in translation to clinical settings, including delivery across the blood-brain barrier, potential immunogenicity, and long-term safety profiles. Cell-specific neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine in neurology and psychiatry, offering opportunities to enhance therapeutic efficacy while minimizing off-target effects. Continued technological advances and interdisciplinary collaboration will be essential for realizing the full potential of this rapidly evolving field.

title: Photolysis of caged neurotransmitters: theory and procedures for light delivery.
abstract: Caged neurotransmitters have emerged as powerful tools for investigating synaptic transmission and neural circuit function with high spatial and temporal resolution. However, optimal application of these photolabile compounds requires precise understanding of photolysis kinetics and standardized protocols for light delivery systems. We present a comprehensive theoretical framework for caged neurotransmitter photolysis and describe validated procedures for implementing UV flash photolysis in acute brain slice preparations. Photolysis efficiency was characterized using caged glutamate (CNB-glutamate) and caged GABA (CNB-GABA) in hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons through whole-cell patch-clamp recordings. Flash lamp systems delivering 1-5 ms UV pulses (peak wavelength 365 nm) achieved 60-80% uncaging efficiency with spatial resolution of 10-20 μm when coupled to appropriate objective lenses. Theoretical modeling revealed that photolysis follows first-order kinetics with rate constants dependent on photon flux density, cage photolysis cross-section, and quantum yield. Practical considerations include cage compound concentration (50-500 μM), flash intensity calibration, and optical pathway optimization to minimize tissue photodamage while maximizing neurotransmitter release. Validation experiments demonstrated reliable induction of fast synaptic responses with rise times of 2-8 ms, comparable to natural synaptic transmission. These standardized protocols enable reproducible caged compound experiments across laboratories and provide essential technical foundations for advanced applications including synaptic plasticity studies, circuit mapping, and investigation of neurotransmitter receptor kinetics in native tissue environments.

title: Neuropharmacology of performance monitoring.
abstract: Performance monitoring, the ability to detect and respond to errors and conflicts during goal-directed behavior, represents a fundamental cognitive control mechanism mediated by the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and associated neural networks. While extensive neuroimaging and electrophysiological studies have characterized the neural correlates of performance monitoring, including the error-related negativity (ERN) and conflict-related activity, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neurochemical basis of performance monitoring, with particular emphasis on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems. We examine evidence from pharmacological challenge studies in humans and animals, focusing on how acute manipulations of neurotransmitter systems affect behavioral and neural indices of error detection and conflict monitoring. Dopamine appears to play a critical role in signaling prediction errors and modulating ACC activity during performance monitoring tasks, with D2 receptor blockade consistently impairing error detection. GABAergic modulation through benzodiazepine administration reduces conflict-related ACC activation and impairs behavioral adjustments following errors. Glutamatergic NMDA receptor antagonism similarly disrupts performance monitoring, suggesting involvement of cortical excitatory networks. Additionally, we review emerging evidence for acetylcholine and norepinephrine contributions to attention-related aspects of performance monitoring. The convergent findings indicate that performance monitoring depends on complex interactions between multiple neurotransmitter systems, with dopamine and GABA playing particularly prominent roles. Understanding these neurochemical mechanisms has important implications for psychiatric disorders characterized by performance monitoring deficits, including schizophrenia, ADHD, and obsessive-compulsive disorder.

title: Schizophrenia: a review of neuropharmacology.
abstract: Schizophrenia represents one of the most challenging psychiatric disorders, affecting approximately 1% of the global population and imposing substantial burdens on healthcare systems. Despite decades of research, the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain incompletely understood, limiting therapeutic advances. This comprehensive review examines current knowledge of neurotransmitter systems implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology and evaluates existing pharmacological interventions. A systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 1990-2003 was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases, focusing on dopaminergic, glutamatergic, GABAergic, and serotonergic mechanisms in schizophrenia. Additional emphasis was placed on emerging theories regarding N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction and its relationship to dopaminergic dysfunction. The traditional dopamine hypothesis, while foundational, appears insufficient to explain the full spectrum of schizophrenia symptoms, particularly negative symptoms and cognitive deficits. Evidence increasingly supports glutamatergic hypofunction as a primary mechanism, potentially upstream of dopaminergic abnormalities. NMDA receptor antagonist models demonstrate remarkable similarity to the full symptom profile of schizophrenia, including positive symptoms, negative symptoms, and cognitive impairments. Current antipsychotic medications, predominantly dopamine D2 receptor antagonists, show limited efficacy against negative symptoms and cognitive dysfunction. Atypical antipsychotics demonstrate modest improvements through multi-receptor targeting, but significant unmet therapeutic needs persist. Future neuropharmacological strategies should focus on glutamatergic enhancement, particularly NMDA receptor co-agonists and metabotropic glutamate receptor modulators. Novel approaches targeting GABAergic interneuron dysfunction and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors also warrant investigation. Understanding these complex neurotransmitter interactions may facilitate development of more effective therapeutic interventions.

title: Neuropharmacology of Cevimeline and Muscarinic Drugs-Focus on Cognition and Neurodegeneration.
abstract: Muscarinic acetylcholine receptors play crucial roles in cognitive processes and are implicated in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease. Cevimeline, a selective M1/M3 muscarinic receptor agonist clinically approved for Sjögren's syndrome, has emerged as a potential therapeutic candidate for cognitive enhancement and neuroprotection. This review synthesizes current understanding of cevimeline's neuropharmacological properties and compares its mechanisms with other muscarinic modulators in the context of neurodegeneration. We conducted a comprehensive literature analysis of preclinical and clinical studies examining cevimeline and related muscarinic drugs, focusing on their effects on synaptic plasticity, neuroinflammation, and cognitive performance. Preclinical studies demonstrate that cevimeline enhances long-term potentiation in hippocampal neurons and promotes acetylcholine release, while reducing amyloid-β accumulation and tau hyperphosphorylation in transgenic mouse models. Clinical trials in mild cognitive impairment patients showed modest improvements in memory and attention tasks following 12-week cevimeline treatment, with minimal anticholinergic side effects compared to non-selective muscarinic agonists. Mechanistic studies reveal that cevimeline activates PKC/MAPK signaling pathways and upregulates BDNF expression, contributing to synaptic strengthening and neuronal survival. Additionally, cevimeline exhibits anti-inflammatory properties by modulating microglial activation through M1 receptor-mediated pathways. These findings suggest that selective muscarinic receptor modulation, particularly through cevimeline, represents a promising therapeutic strategy for addressing cognitive decline and neurodegeneration. Further clinical investigations are warranted to establish optimal dosing regimens and long-term efficacy in neurodegenerative disorders.

title: A conversation with Paul Greengard. Interview by Eric J Nestler.
abstract: Paul Greengard was born in New York City in 1925. After completing high school, he served three years in the US Navy during World War II and then completed his bachelor's degree at Hamilton College where he majored in physics and mathematics. He obtained a PhD in biophysics from Johns Hopkins University in 1953 and pursued postdoctoral training with Wilhelm Feldberg at the National Institute for Medical Research in England. After eight years as head of biochemistry at Geigy, and sabbaticals at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and Vanderbilt University, he joined the Yale University faculty as a full professor of pharmacology in 1968. While he was at Yale, Greengard's laboratory performed groundbreaking research, which demonstrated a role for cyclic nucleotides, protein kinases and protein phosphatases, and their protein substrates in the regulation of synaptic transmission. In 1983, Greengard moved to The Rockefeller University, where he has since served as the Vincent Astor Professor and Head of the Laboratory of Molecular and Cellular Neuroscience. Greengard's paradigm-shifting research has continued at Rockefeller and has informed our understanding and possible treatment of a host of brain disorders, including schizophrenia, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and depression. He is the author of more than 950 research articles and reviews. Greengard has received numerous awards and honors, including the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2000, the Metropolitan Life Foundation Award for Medical Research, The National Academy of Sciences Award in Neuroscience, the Ralph W. Gerard Prize in Neuroscience for the Society for Neuroscience, and the Karolinska Institutet's Bicentennial Gold Medal. He is a member of the US National Academy of Sciences and the Institute of Medicine of the National Academies. The following interview was conducted on May 29, 2012.
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology

title: Annual Research Review: New frontiers in developmental neuropharmacology: can long-term therapeutic effects of drugs be optimized through carefully timed early intervention?
abstract: Recent advances in developmental neuroscience suggest that early pharmacological interventions may have enduring effects on neural development that extend beyond immediate symptom relief. This review examines emerging evidence for critical periods in neurodevelopment where therapeutic drug administration could optimize long-term outcomes in pediatric psychiatric and neurological conditions. We conducted a comprehensive review of preclinical and clinical studies published between 2005-2011 investigating the developmental timing of psychoactive drug interventions, focusing on antidepressants, stimulants, antipsychotics, and mood stabilizers. Particular attention was given to studies examining neuroplasticity markers, synaptic development, and long-term behavioral outcomes following early versus delayed treatment initiation. Converging evidence from animal models demonstrates that pharmacological interventions during specific developmental windows can permanently alter neural circuitry through effects on synaptogenesis, myelination, and neurotransmitter system maturation. Clinical data, while more limited, suggest that early intervention with certain medications may confer greater therapeutic benefits and potentially reduce long-term medication requirements compared to delayed treatment approaches. However, concerning findings also indicate that some drug classes may interfere with normal developmental processes when administered during vulnerable periods, potentially leading to adverse long-term consequences. These findings highlight the critical importance of developmental timing in pediatric neuropharmacology and suggest that optimizing therapeutic outcomes requires careful consideration of both the benefits and risks of early intervention. Future research should prioritize identifying specific critical periods for different drug classes and developing biomarkers to guide personalized timing of therapeutic interventions in developing populations.
journal: Journal of child psychology and psychiatry, and allied disciplines

title: Neuropharmacological effects of Nigerian honey in mice.
abstract: Honey is a natural sweet substance that bees produce by transforming flower nectar or other sweet secretions of plants. It has widespread use in traditional medicine in various parts of the world. It has been reported to assist in building the entire central nervous system. The beneficial effects of honey have been attributed to the possible polyphenolic contents and some other constituents. The geographical locations and the sources of plant nectars may contribute to the effects of honey samples. Thus, we evaluated the neuropharmacological effects of six samples of honey (10%, 20% and 40%(V)/v, p.o.) from three geographical locations of Nigeria using the following behavioral models: Novelty-induced behaviors (NIB), learning and memory, pentobarbital-induced hypnosis, anxiolytic, anticonvulsant, analgesic and antidepressant models in mice. The results showed that honey significantly (p< 0.05) decreased locomotion and rearing behaviors in NIB and amphetamine-induced locomotor activity when compared to the control group. Exploratory behavior was significantly increased in both holeboard and elevated plus maze but had no significant effect on spatial working memory. Honey sample from Umudike has significant hypnotic and anticonvulsant effects. The antinociceptive models (hot plate and tail flick tests) showed that the honey samples significantly increased the pain reaction time and naloxone blocked these central antinociceptive effects. The force swimming test showed that only the Idanre (ID) honey sample had antidepressant effect. In conclusion, some of these honey samples have central inhibitory property, anxiolytic, antinociceptive, anticonvulsant and antidepressant effects, thus may be used as nutraceutic. It can also be inferred that some of these effects are probably mediated through dopaminergic and opioidergic systems.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: Pharmacotherapy in children and adolescents with pervasive developmental disorders.
abstract: Pervasive developmental disorders (PDDs), including autistic disorder, Asperger's disorder, and pervasive developmental disorder not otherwise specified, affect approximately 0.2-0.6% of children and present significant challenges in behavioral management and therapeutic intervention. While no medications are specifically approved for core symptoms of PDDs, various psychotropic agents have shown promise in managing associated behavioral disturbances. This comprehensive review examines the current literature on pharmacological interventions for children and adolescents with PDDs from 1990 to 1999, analyzing efficacy and safety profiles of commonly prescribed medications. Systematic analysis of 47 published studies, including 12 controlled trials and 35 open-label studies, encompassing 892 patients aged 2-18 years with diagnosed PDDs was conducted. Primary outcome measures included reduction in stereotypic behaviors, aggression, self-injurious behavior, hyperactivity, and social withdrawal. Results demonstrate that selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly fluoxetine and sertraline, showed moderate efficacy in reducing repetitive behaviors and improving social interaction in 60-70% of patients. Neuroleptics, including haloperidol and risperidone, were effective for managing severe aggression and self-injury, with risperidone showing superior tolerability profiles. Stimulant medications provided modest improvements in attention and hyperactivity in select patients without autism. Anticonvulsants showed limited benefit except in patients with comorbid seizure disorders. Adverse effects were generally consistent with known medication profiles, though children with PDDs appeared more sensitive to extrapyramidal symptoms. These findings suggest that targeted pharmacotherapy can be a valuable adjunct to behavioral interventions in managing specific symptom clusters in pediatric PDD populations.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: Psychopharmacological profile of the alkaloid psychollatine as a 5HT2A/C serotonin modulator.
abstract: The naturally occurring indole alkaloid psychollatine, isolated from the Amazonian plant Psychotria psychollatrensis, has been traditionally used by indigenous communities for its psychoactive properties. Recent ethnobotanical surveys have identified this compound as a potential modulator of serotonergic neurotransmission, yet its specific receptor binding profile and behavioral effects remain uncharacterized. We investigated the in vitro binding affinity and functional activity of psychollatine at human 5-HT2A and 5-HT2C receptors expressed in HEK-293 cells, along with its behavioral effects in rodent models. Radioligand binding assays revealed that psychollatine exhibits high affinity for 5-HT2A receptors (Ki = 12.3 ± 2.1 nM) and moderate affinity for 5-HT2C receptors (Ki = 89.4 ± 8.7 nM), with minimal binding to other serotonin receptor subtypes. Functional calcium mobilization assays demonstrated that psychollatine acts as a partial agonist at 5-HT2A receptors (EC50 = 24.8 nM, Emax = 67% of serotonin) and as a weak partial agonist at 5-HT2C receptors (EC50 = 156 nM, Emax = 34% of serotonin). In vivo studies showed that psychollatine (0.5-2.0 mg/kg, i.p.) dose-dependently induced head-twitch responses in mice, an effect completely blocked by the selective 5-HT2A antagonist MDL 100907. The compound also modulated prepulse inhibition and locomotor activity in a manner consistent with 5-HT2A/2C activation. These findings establish psychollatine as a novel naturally occurring 5-HT2A/2C receptor modulator with potential applications in understanding serotonergic neurotransmission and developing new therapeutic agents.

title: Generalized anxiety disorder. An important clinical concern.
abstract: GAD is common, often follows a chronic course, and usually is associated with extensive psychiatric and medical comorbidity. This disorder often presents in a primary care setting, commonly with somatic symptoms; it is important for primary care physicians to be aware of its existence, forms of presentation, and different treatments. GAD appears to be twice as common in women than men. There is some evidence that the neurobiologic basis of GAD may involve abnormalities in neurochemical, neuroendocrine, neurophysiologic, and neuroanatomic factors. Research on psychosocial treatment of GAD has favored the combination of cognitive therapy and relaxation techniques or anxiety management training. It also appears that relapse rates after termination of cognitive-behavioral therapy are low. Taking into consideration that GAD generally is chronic and associated frequently with depressive symptoms, the ideal pharmacotherapy may be a drug that can treat these comorbid disorders adequately. New antidepressants, especially those with action in the serotonin system and possibly noradrenergic, may be the appropriate option: They not only treat anxiety, but also treat or prevent the development of comorbid depression; they should be effective ideally during prolonged periods without risks related to addiction or withdrawal, such as may happen with benzodiazepines and some antidepressants. The role of newly emerging drugs, such as some anticonvulsants, in GAD needs to be defined more clearly. More research is warranted to address issues such as (1) whether pharmacotherapy is as effective, less effective, or more effective than cognitive-behavioral therapy; (2) whether antidepressants improve the rate of wellness or remission; and (3) whether prolonged antidepressant therapy for GAD protects against later emergent depression.
journal: The Medical clinics of North America

title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
abstract: Schizophrenia patients demonstrate consistent deficits in facial affect recognition that significantly impact social functioning and quality of life. While these impairments have been well-documented behaviorally, the underlying neural mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets remain poorly understood. This review synthesizes current neuroimaging and psychopharmacological evidence to elucidate the neurobiological basis of affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia and evaluate treatment implications. We conducted a comprehensive literature review of neuroimaging studies examining affect recognition in schizophrenia patients compared to healthy controls, and analyzed clinical trials investigating pharmacological interventions targeting these deficits. Converging neuroimaging evidence demonstrates abnormal activation patterns in the amygdala, fusiform face area, and prefrontal cortical regions during affect recognition tasks in schizophrenia patients. Structural abnormalities in these regions correlate with behavioral deficits, suggesting a neuroanatomical substrate. While typical antipsychotics show limited efficacy for improving affect recognition, emerging evidence suggests that atypical antipsychotics, particularly those with significant serotonergic activity, may provide modest benefits. Adjunctive treatments targeting cholinergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems show preliminary promise in small-scale studies. However, effect sizes remain modest and clinical significance unclear. Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia involve disrupted limbic-cortical circuitry, with the amygdala and prefrontal regions as key substrates. Current pharmacological approaches provide limited therapeutic benefit, highlighting the need for novel treatment strategies targeting specific neurotransmitter systems underlying social cognitive dysfunction.

title: Neuronal and glial cell lines as model systems for studying P2Y receptor pharmacology.
abstract: P2Y receptors are G protein-coupled receptors activated by extracellular nucleotides that play crucial roles in neurotransmission, glial cell activation, and neuroinflammation. While primary cell cultures have been traditionally used to study P2Y receptor function, immortalized cell lines offer advantages including reproducibility, genetic homogeneity, and ease of manipulation. We systematically characterized P2Y receptor expression and pharmacological profiles in five commonly used neuronal and glial cell lines: PC12, SH-SY5Y, U87-MG, C6, and 1321N1 astrocytoma cells. RT-PCR analysis revealed differential expression patterns, with PC12 cells predominantly expressing P2Y₁, P2Y₂, and P2Y₁₂ receptors, while U87-MG glioblastoma cells showed high levels of P2Y₂, P2Y₆, and P2Y₁₂. Functional responses were assessed using intracellular calcium mobilization assays and cAMP measurements following stimulation with selective agonists including ADP, UTP, UDP, and 2-MeSADP. SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells demonstrated robust calcium responses to ADP (EC₅₀ = 1.2 ± 0.3 μM) and UTP (EC₅₀ = 0.8 ± 0.2 μM), while C6 glioma cells showed preferential sensitivity to UDP stimulation. Pharmacological characterization using selective antagonists confirmed functional receptor subtypes, with suramin effectively blocking P2Y₂-mediated responses and MRS2179 specifically inhibiting P2Y₁ activation. These findings establish a comprehensive pharmacological profile of P2Y receptors in commonly used cell line models, providing valuable tools for investigating purinergic signaling mechanisms in neurodegenerative diseases, glial activation, and drug discovery applications.

title: Contemporary approaches to pharmacotherapy in Tourette's syndrome and obsessive-compulsive disorder.
abstract: Tourette's syndrome (TS) and obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) represent complex neuropsychiatric conditions that frequently co-occur in pediatric populations, presenting significant challenges for clinicians and families. Traditional pharmacological approaches have relied primarily on haloperidol and pimozide for tic suppression, while clomipramine has been the mainstay for OCD treatment. This comprehensive review examines emerging pharmacotherapeutic strategies and evaluates their efficacy and safety profiles in children and adolescents. A systematic analysis of clinical trials published between 1990-1996 was conducted, focusing on novel therapeutic agents including risperidone, olanzapine, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), and alpha-2 adrenergic agonists. Data from 24 controlled studies involving 1,847 pediatric patients were analyzed for treatment response rates, adverse effects, and long-term outcomes. Results indicate that risperidone demonstrates comparable efficacy to haloperidol for tic reduction (68% vs 71% response rates) with significantly fewer extrapyramidal side effects. Fluoxetine and sertraline showed superior tolerability profiles compared to clomipramine while maintaining therapeutic efficacy for OCD symptoms (62-78% improvement on Y-BOCS scores). Clonidine proved particularly beneficial for patients with comorbid attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder. These findings suggest that newer pharmacological agents offer promising alternatives to traditional treatments, potentially improving treatment adherence and quality of life in pediatric patients. However, long-term safety data remain limited, necessitating continued research and careful monitoring protocols for optimal therapeutic outcomes.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychiatric nursing : official publication of the Association of Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Nurses, Inc

title: The role of 5-HT₁A receptors in fish oil-mediated increased BDNF expression in the rat hippocampus and cortex: a possible antidepressant mechanism.
abstract: Omega-3 fatty acids have demonstrated antidepressant-like effects in both clinical and preclinical studies, yet the underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) upregulation and serotonergic system modulation have been proposed as potential mediators of these effects. This study investigated whether fish oil-induced increases in hippocampal and cortical BDNF expression are mediated through 5-HT₁A receptor signaling pathways. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received daily oral administration of fish oil (300 mg/kg) or vehicle for 21 days. To examine 5-HT₁A receptor involvement, separate groups received co-treatment with the selective 5-HT₁A antagonist WAY-100635 (0.5 mg/kg, s.c.). BDNF mRNA and protein levels were quantified using qRT-PCR and Western blot analysis in hippocampal and prefrontal cortical tissues. Immunohistochemical analysis was performed to assess regional BDNF distribution. Fish oil treatment significantly increased BDNF mRNA expression in the hippocampus (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and prefrontal cortex (1.8-fold, p<0.05) compared to controls. Corresponding increases in BDNF protein levels were observed in both regions. Co-administration of WAY-100635 completely abolished fish oil-induced BDNF upregulation in the hippocampus and partially attenuated the response in the prefrontal cortex. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that fish oil enhanced BDNF immunoreactivity primarily in CA1 and CA3 pyramidal neurons and dentate gyrus granule cells. These findings suggest that 5-HT₁A receptors play a critical role in mediating fish oil-induced BDNF expression, providing novel mechanistic insight into the antidepressant properties of omega-3 fatty acids.

title: A new cross-correlation algorithm for the analysis of "in vitro" neuronal network activity aimed at pharmacological studies.
abstract: In vitro neuronal networks cultured on microelectrode arrays (MEAs) have emerged as powerful tools for investigating network dynamics and drug effects on neural activity. However, existing cross-correlation analysis methods often fail to capture the complex spatiotemporal patterns of synchronized activity characteristic of these networks, particularly when evaluating pharmacological interventions. Here we present a novel cross-correlation algorithm specifically designed to analyze spike train data from MEA recordings with enhanced sensitivity to network-wide synchronization events. Our algorithm incorporates adaptive time-windowing and weighted correlation coefficients that account for electrode-specific firing rates and inter-electrode distances. We validated this method using simulated spike trains with known correlation structures and compared its performance against conventional cross-correlation approaches using recordings from primary cortical cultures (DIV 14-21) treated with various concentrations of bicuculline (10-50 μM), a GABA_A receptor antagonist known to enhance network synchronization. The new algorithm demonstrated superior detection of correlation changes, showing 40% improved sensitivity compared to standard methods in identifying dose-dependent increases in network synchronization following bicuculline application. Additionally, the algorithm successfully distinguished between global network effects and localized clustering patterns that emerged during drug treatment. Computational efficiency was maintained with processing times comparable to existing methods. This cross-correlation algorithm provides researchers with an improved analytical tool for characterizing network-level effects of pharmacological compounds in MEA-based assays, potentially accelerating drug discovery efforts targeting neurological disorders involving altered network connectivity such as epilepsy and autism spectrum disorders.

title: The origins and early history of neurochemistry and its societies.
abstract: The field of neurochemistry emerged in the early 20th century as investigators began applying biochemical techniques to understand brain function, yet a comprehensive historical analysis of its origins and institutional development remains lacking. This review examines the foundational period of neurochemistry from 1900-1970, analyzing primary sources including scientific publications, society records, and correspondence to trace the discipline's evolution and the establishment of its professional organizations. Systematic literature searches were conducted across historical databases, with particular attention to seminal works by pioneers such as Thudichum, McIlwain, and Richter. Institutional archives from the International Society for Neurochemistry (ISN) and American Society for Neurochemistry (ASN) provided insights into organizational founding principles and early membership patterns. Our analysis reveals that neurochemistry crystallized as a distinct discipline following three key developments: the application of enzymatic assays to neural tissue (1920s-1930s), the discovery of neurotransmitter synthesis pathways (1940s-1950s), and the advent of subcellular fractionation techniques (1950s-1960s). The ISN, established in 1963, emerged from informal networks of researchers who recognized the need for specialized forums to discuss brain chemistry distinct from general biochemistry or neurophysiology. Early society proceedings demonstrate rapid expansion from 200 founding members to over 1,200 by 1970, with significant geographic diversification. This historical framework demonstrates how neurochemistry's institutional identity was shaped by methodological innovations and the unique challenges of studying brain metabolism, providing context for understanding contemporary directions in neuropharmacological research and professional organization within the neurosciences.

title: Affinity labeling the dopamine transporter ligand binding site.
abstract: The dopamine transporter (DAT) plays a crucial role in terminating dopaminergic neurotransmission by removing dopamine from the synaptic cleft, and represents a primary target for psychostimulants such as cocaine and amphetamine. Despite extensive pharmacological characterization, the precise molecular architecture of the DAT ligand binding site remains poorly understood. We employed photoaffinity labeling techniques to identify amino acid residues within the cocaine and dopamine binding domains of the human DAT. A series of photoreactive analogues of cocaine and dopamine were synthesized, including [³H]3β-[4-(4-azido-3-iodophenyl)methyl]tropane-2β-carboxylic acid methyl ester (RTI-82) and [³H]2-azido-dopamine. Membrane preparations from HEK293 cells stably expressing human DAT were incubated with photolabels under UV irradiation conditions. Covalently modified transporters were isolated by immunoprecipitation and subjected to proteolytic digestion followed by peptide mapping and Edman degradation sequencing. RTI-82 specifically labeled Asp79 in transmembrane domain 1 and Phe320 in transmembrane domain 6, while 2-azido-dopamine primarily modified Ser422 in transmembrane domain 8. Competition experiments with unlabeled ligands confirmed specificity of labeling. Mutational analysis revealed that substitution of labeled residues significantly reduced binding affinity for both substrate and inhibitor ligands. These results provide direct biochemical evidence for a multi-domain binding pocket involving transmembrane segments 1, 6, and 8, and suggest overlapping but distinct binding sites for dopamine and cocaine within the DAT structure. This photoaffinity mapping approach offers valuable insights into transporter structure-function relationships and may facilitate rational drug design efforts.

title: Fundamental and clinical neuropharmacology, a terra incognita with constantly expanding frontiers.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology continues to evolve rapidly, yet significant knowledge gaps persist in understanding drug-brain interactions across clinical and preclinical domains. This comprehensive review synthesizes current literature from 2015-2021 to identify emerging trends, methodological advances, and translational challenges in fundamental and clinical neuropharmacology research. We conducted a systematic analysis of 847 peer-reviewed articles from major neuropharmacology journals, categorizing studies by therapeutic area, methodological approach, and translational outcomes. Additionally, we surveyed 156 researchers across 23 countries to assess perceived research priorities and barriers to clinical translation. Our analysis revealed exponential growth in neuroimaging-based pharmacodynamic studies (312% increase), optogenetic applications in drug discovery (287% increase), and precision medicine approaches targeting neuropsychiatric disorders (198% increase). However, translation rates from preclinical to clinical success remained low (8.3%), with significant disparities between therapeutic areas. Alzheimer's disease research showed the highest preclinical activity but lowest clinical success rates (3.1%), while addiction pharmacotherapy demonstrated improved translational efficiency (15.7%). Survey responses identified inadequate disease models (78% of respondents), insufficient funding for negative results (65%), and regulatory barriers (52%) as primary obstacles. Emerging areas including psychedelic-assisted therapy, neuromodulation pharmacology, and gut-brain axis interventions represent promising frontiers. These findings underscore neuropharmacology's dynamic landscape while highlighting persistent translational challenges. Enhanced collaboration between basic and clinical researchers, improved animal models, and standardized outcome measures are essential for advancing this rapidly expanding field toward meaningful therapeutic breakthroughs.

title: Microdialysis in freely moving mice: determination of acetylcholine, serotonin and noradrenaline release in galanin transgenic mice.
abstract: The neuropeptide galanin has been implicated in the modulation of cholinergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic neurotransmission, yet its precise effects on neurotransmitter release in vivo remain poorly characterized. We employed intracerebral microdialysis in freely moving mice to investigate the effects of galanin overexpression on basal and stimulated release of acetylcholine (ACh), serotonin (5-HT), and noradrenaline (NA) in the hippocampus and frontal cortex. Male transgenic mice overexpressing galanin under the platelet-derived growth factor-β promoter and wild-type littermates were implanted with CMA/7 microdialysis probes. Following a 24-hour recovery period, dialysate samples were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection for monoamines and radioenzymatic assay for ACh. Potassium-evoked neurotransmitter release was assessed by perfusion with 100 mM KCl for 20 minutes. Galanin transgenic mice exhibited significantly reduced basal ACh release in both hippocampus (-32%, p<0.01) and frontal cortex (-28%, p<0.05) compared to controls. Basal 5-HT release was decreased by 24% in the hippocampus (p<0.05) but unchanged in frontal cortex. NA release remained unaffected in both regions. Potassium-stimulated ACh release was markedly attenuated in transgenic mice (hippocampus: -45%, frontal cortex: -38%, both p<0.01), while 5-HT and NA responses were preserved. These findings demonstrate that chronic galanin overexpression selectively impairs cholinergic neurotransmission in vivo, providing insights into galanin's role in cognitive dysfunction and supporting its potential as a therapeutic target for cholinergic disorders.

title: William L. Woolverton: a case history in unraveling the behavioral pharmacology of stimulants.
abstract: The behavioral pharmacology of psychostimulants has been fundamentally shaped by the systematic research approach pioneered by William L. Woolverton over three decades. This retrospective analysis examines key methodological innovations and findings from Woolverton's laboratory that advanced our understanding of stimulant reinforcement mechanisms and abuse liability assessment. We reviewed seminal publications from 1978-2012 focusing on self-administration paradigms, choice procedures, and pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic relationships in nonhuman primate models. Woolverton's laboratory established critical methodological standards for intravenous self-administration studies, demonstrating that cocaine and amphetamine derivatives maintain responding across species through dopaminergic mechanisms. The introduction of concurrent schedules of reinforcement revealed that stimulant choice behavior follows matching law principles, with relative reinforcing efficacy determined by dose, alternative reinforcer magnitude, and pharmacokinetic profiles. Studies with novel stimulants, including modafinil, methylphenidate analogs, and dopamine transporter inhibitors, demonstrated predictive validity between preclinical self-administration data and clinical abuse potential. Investigations of pharmacokinetic variables revealed that rapid onset and short duration of action enhance reinforcing strength, explaining differential abuse liability among chemically similar compounds. The integration of behavioral economics principles further elucidated how environmental factors modulate stimulant reinforcement. Woolverton's systematic approach established the nonhuman primate self-administration model as the gold standard for abuse liability assessment, directly informing regulatory decisions for controlled substance scheduling. This body of work exemplifies how rigorous behavioral pharmacology research can bridge basic neuroscience mechanisms with clinical and public health applications in substance abuse.

title: Proteomic approaches in brain research and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Numerous applications of genomic technologies have enabled the assembly of unprecedented inventories of genes, expressed in cells under specific physiological and pathophysiological conditions. Complementing the valuable information generated through functional genomics with the integrative knowledge of protein expression and function should enable the development of more efficient diagnostic tools and therapeutic agents. Proteomic analyses are particularly suitable to elucidate posttranslational modifications, expression levels and protein-protein interactions of thousands of proteins at a time. In this review, two-dimensional polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (2D-PAGE) investigations of brain tissues in neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Down syndrome and schizophrenia, and the construction of 2D-PAGE proteome maps of the brain are discussed. The role of the Human Proteome Organization (HUPO) as an international coordinating organization for proteomic efforts, as well as challenges for proteomic technologies and data analysis are also addressed. It is expected that the use of proteomic strategies will have significant impact in neuropharmacology over the coming decade.

title: Pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain.
abstract: Refractory neuropathic pain can be devastating to a patient's quality of life. Ideally, the primary goal of therapy would be to prevent the pain, yet even the most appropriate treatment strategy may be only able to reduce the pain to a more tolerable level. Pharmacotherapy is currently the mainstay of treatment in patients with neuropathic pain, although at present the drugs are used on a mainly "off-label" basis. A wide variety of agents are used, especially antidepressants (ie, tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin-reuptake inhibitors) and anticonvulsants, but also opioids and tramadol, topical agents (eg, lidocaine), systemic local anesthetics, and anti-inflammatories. Even so, effective pain relief is achieved in less than half of patients with chronic neuropathic pain. In refractory patients, combination therapy using two agents with synergistic mechanisms of action may offer greater pain relief without compromising the side-effect profile of each agent.
journal: Pain practice : the official journal of World Institute of Pain

title: Optofluidic control of rodent learning using cloaked caged glutamate.
abstract: The precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter release remains a fundamental challenge in understanding neural circuit function and behavior. While optogenetics has revolutionized neuroscience research, existing approaches for controlling endogenous neurotransmitter signaling lack the temporal precision and spatial specificity required for dissecting complex learning behaviors. Here, we developed a novel optofluidic platform utilizing photocaged glutamate conjugated with near-infrared fluorescent cloaking molecules to achieve millisecond-precise, localized glutamate release in behaving rodents. The cloaking strategy prevents premature photolysis while enabling deep tissue penetration of 980-nm light for targeted uncaging. We validated this approach in adult C57BL/6 mice (n=48) performing a spatial memory task, delivering caged glutamate via chronically implanted microfluidic devices targeting the hippocampal CA1 region. Optical stimulation through implanted fiber optics triggered glutamate release with 2.3 ± 0.4 ms temporal precision and <50 μm spatial resolution. Mice receiving precisely timed glutamate uncaging during memory encoding showed significantly enhanced performance in the Morris water maze (escape latency: 18.2 ± 3.1 s vs 31.7 ± 4.8 s, p<0.001) compared to controls. Electrophysiological recordings confirmed selective enhancement of CA1 pyramidal cell activity and theta-gamma coupling during stimulation periods. Post-mortem immunohistochemistry revealed minimal tissue damage and preserved synaptic architecture. These results demonstrate that optofluidic control of caged neurotransmitters enables unprecedented precision in manipulating neural activity during complex behaviors, providing a powerful tool for dissecting the neurochemical basis of learning and memory with potential therapeutic applications for cognitive disorders.
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

title: Neuropharmacology of drugs and alcohol in mother and fetus.
abstract: Epidemiological evidence suggests that an adverse prenatal environment can have profound long-term health consequences throughout postnatal life. This chapter discusses the underlying mechanisms implicated in the consumption of mood-altering recreational drugs and teratogenicity in the fetus. The way metabolic parameters in pregnancy influence the pharmacokinetic characteristics of drugs and alcohol and the developmental stage of neurotoxicity are reviewed. The general underlying mechanisms that link multifaceted interactions between drug characteristics, gene polymorphisms, dietary deficiencies, changed endocrine indices and fetal programming are outlined, with specific examples throughout the text. As developmental injury is of significant social concern, the final section questions whether society provides adequate support for making appropriate and informed lifestyle choices to alleviate preventable transgenerational harm.

title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder and tic syndromes.
abstract: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) and tic syndromes, including Tourette's disorder, represent neuropsychiatric conditions with overlapping clinical features and proposed shared neurobiological substrates involving cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits. While selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) remain first-line treatment for OCD, their efficacy in tic disorders is limited, suggesting distinct pathophysiological mechanisms. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial examining the differential effects of sertraline versus haloperidol in 84 patients with comorbid OCD and chronic tic disorder. Participants were randomized to receive sertraline (50-200 mg daily), haloperidol (1-6 mg daily), combination therapy, or placebo for 12 weeks. Primary outcome measures included the Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS) and Yale Global Tic Severity Scale (YGTSS). Secondary measures assessed neuropsychological function and adverse effects. Sertraline significantly reduced obsessive-compulsive symptoms (mean Y-BOCS reduction: 8.4 points, p<0.001) but showed minimal impact on tic severity. Haloperidol effectively suppressed tics (mean YGTSS reduction: 12.7 points, p<0.001) with modest effects on compulsions. Combination therapy provided superior improvement in both symptom domains compared to monotherapy. Neuropsychological testing revealed preserved cognitive function with sertraline but attention deficits with haloperidol. These findings support the hypothesis that OCD and tic syndromes, while phenomenologically related, involve distinct neurotransmitter systems requiring targeted pharmacological approaches. Combination therapy may optimize treatment outcomes in patients with comorbid presentations while minimizing cognitive side effects associated with dopamine antagonists.
journal: The Medical clinics of North America

title: Combining metabolic flux analysis tools and 13C NMR to estimate intracellular fluxes of cultured astrocytes.
abstract: In this work, brain cell metabolism was investigated by (13)C NMR spectroscopy and metabolic flux analysis (MFA). Monotypic cultures of astrocytes were incubated with labeled glucose for 38 h, and the distribution of the label was analyzed by (13)C NMR spectroscopy. The analysis of the spectra reveals two distinct physiological states characterized by different ratios of pyruvate carboxylase to pyruvate dehydrogenase activities (PC/PDH). Intracellular flux distributions for both metabolic states were estimated by MFA using the isotopic information and extracellular rate measurements as constraints. The model was subsequently checked with the consistency index method. From a biological point of view, the occurrence of the two physiological states appears to be correlated with the presence or absence of extracellular glutamate. Concerning the model, it can be stated that the metabolic network and the set of constraints adopted provide a consistent and robust characterization of the astrocytic metabolism, allowing for the calculation of central intracellular fluxes such as pyruvate recycling, the anaplerotic flux mediated by pyruvate carboxylase, and the glutamine formation through glutamine synthetase.

title: Charles Darwin's emotional expression "experiment" and his contribution to modern neuropharmacology.
abstract: Charles Darwin's investigations into emotional expression, particularly his systematic observations documented in "The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals" (1872), represent an overlooked foundation for contemporary neuropharmacological research. While Darwin's work predates modern neurochemistry by nearly a century, his methodological approach and theoretical insights established crucial conceptual frameworks that continue to influence current understanding of emotion-drug interactions. This historical analysis examines Darwin's correspondence, experimental notebooks, and published observations to evaluate his contributions to the field. We analyzed Darwin's documentation of emotional responses across species, his early recognition of the physiological basis of emotional expression, and his proto-experimental methodology involving photographic documentation and cross-cultural surveys. Darwin's work demonstrated remarkable prescience in identifying the involuntary nature of emotional expressions, the role of the autonomic nervous system in emotional states, and the evolutionary conservation of emotional mechanisms across species. His observations of facial muscle contractions, cardiovascular responses, and respiratory changes during emotional states anticipated key targets of modern anxiolytic and antidepressant medications. Furthermore, his recognition that emotional expressions could be modified through conscious effort foreshadowed current research into cognitive-behavioral interventions and their neurochemical correlates. Darwin's systematic approach to studying emotional expression established methodological principles that remain relevant to contemporary neuropharmacological research, particularly in the development of animal models for psychiatric disorders and the assessment of drug efficacy in emotional regulation.
journal: Journal of the history of the neurosciences

title: A pre-clinical study showing how dopaminergic drugs administered during pre-exposure can impair or facilitate latent inhibition.
abstract: Latent inhibition (LI), the reduced conditioning to a stimulus following non-reinforced pre-exposure, is disrupted in schizophrenia and serves as a valuable model for studying attentional and associative learning deficits. While dopaminergic dysfunction has been implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, the specific role of dopamine receptor subtypes in LI remains poorly understood. This study examined how selective dopaminergic manipulations during pre-exposure affect LI acquisition in rats. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of saline, the D2/D3 agonist quinpirole (0.5 mg/kg), the D1/D5 agonist SKF-38393 (10 mg/kg), the D2 antagonist haloperidol (0.1 mg/kg), or the D1 antagonist SCH-23390 (0.05 mg/kg) 30 minutes before each of five tone pre-exposure sessions. Following a two-day interval, all animals underwent tone-shock conditioning and extinction testing. Vehicle-treated pre-exposed animals demonstrated robust LI compared to non-pre-exposed controls (p<0.01). Quinpirole administration during pre-exposure completely abolished LI (p<0.001), while SKF-38393 had no significant effect. Conversely, haloperidol enhanced LI magnitude (p<0.05), whereas SCH-23390 produced no change. D2-like receptor stimulation during pre-exposure impaired subsequent LI expression, while D2 blockade facilitated it. These findings suggest that D2-mediated dopaminergic activity during stimulus pre-exposure critically modulates attentional processing mechanisms underlying LI, supporting the hypothesis that excessive dopaminergic transmission may contribute to attentional deficits observed in schizophrenia through disruption of normal stimulus filtering processes.

title: Laboratory of receptor pharmacology.
abstract: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) represent the largest class of membrane proteins targeted by therapeutic agents, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying ligand-receptor interactions and downstream signaling remain incompletely understood. We investigated the pharmacological properties and signaling pathways of dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors using a combination of radioligand binding assays, functional cAMP measurements, and calcium mobilization studies in HEK293 cells stably transfected with human receptor constructs. Saturation binding experiments with [³H]spiperone and [³H]ketanserin revealed Kd values of 0.89 ± 0.12 nM and 2.3 ± 0.4 nM for D2 and 5-HT2A receptors, respectively. Competition binding studies with a panel of 15 structurally diverse ligands demonstrated significant differences in binding affinity profiles between receptor subtypes. Functional assays showed that D2 receptor activation by quinpirole inhibited forskolin-stimulated cAMP accumulation with an EC50 of 45 ± 8 nM, while 5-HT2A receptor stimulation by DOI increased intracellular calcium with an EC50 of 127 ± 23 nM. Pre-treatment with pertussis toxin abolished D2-mediated cAMP inhibition, confirming Gi/o protein coupling. Interestingly, several atypical antipsychotic drugs exhibited partial agonist properties at both receptors with varying efficacy profiles. These findings provide detailed pharmacological characterization of D2 and 5-HT2A receptors and support the hypothesis that functional selectivity at these targets may contribute to the therapeutic profiles of antipsychotic medications. Our receptor pharmacology platform offers valuable tools for drug discovery and mechanistic studies of GPCR signaling.

title: The 20th scientific conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology.
abstract: The 20th Scientific Conference of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology convened in Key Biscayne, Florida from April 2-5, 2014, bringing together 312 researchers from 28 countries to address critical advances in neuroimmune interactions and therapeutic interventions. This milestone conference featured 89 oral presentations, 156 poster sessions, and 4 keynote addresses focusing on emerging paradigms in neuroinflammation, blood-brain barrier dynamics, and immune-mediated neurological disorders. Major themes included novel therapeutic targets for multiple sclerosis, HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders, and neurodegenerative diseases. Significant attention was devoted to recent discoveries regarding microglial activation states, astrocyte-immune cell crosstalk, and the role of peripheral immune cells in CNS pathology. Breakthrough presentations highlighted the therapeutic potential of selective immunomodulators, including fingolimod analogs and novel complement inhibitors, in treating neuroinflammatory conditions. Data presented demonstrated promising preclinical results for targeting specific cytokine pathways, particularly IL-17 and TNF-α signaling cascades, in animal models of neurodegeneration. Clinical trial updates revealed encouraging outcomes for several investigational compounds, including a Phase II study of a CCR5 antagonist for HIV-associated dementia showing significant cognitive improvements. The conference emphasized the growing recognition of bidirectional brain-immune communication and its implications for drug development. These proceedings underscore the rapid evolution of neuroimmune pharmacology as a distinct therapeutic discipline and highlight promising avenues for translating mechanistic insights into clinical interventions for debilitating neurological conditions affecting millions worldwide.
journal: Journal of neuroimmune pharmacology : the official journal of the Society on NeuroImmune Pharmacology

title: Comparative Neuropharmacology and Pharmacokinetics of Methamphetamine and Its Thiophene Analog Methiopropamine in Rodents.
abstract: The escalating prevalence of novel psychoactive substances (NPS) has created significant challenges for both clinical toxicology and drug policy. Methiopropamine (MPA), a thiophene analog of methamphetamine (METH), emerged as a "legal high" alternative with limited pharmacological characterization. This study investigated the comparative neuropharmacological profiles and pharmacokinetic properties of METH and MPA in rodent models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) received intraperitoneal injections of METH or MPA (1, 3, or 10 mg/kg). Locomotor activity was monitored using automated activity chambers, while monoamine transporter interactions were assessed through in vitro binding assays and ex vivo neurochemical analyses. Pharmacokinetic parameters were determined using LC-MS/MS analysis of plasma and brain samples collected at multiple time points. Both compounds demonstrated dose-dependent hyperlocomotion, with MPA producing 65-70% of METH's peak stimulant effects. In vitro binding studies revealed MPA exhibited moderate affinity for dopamine (DAT) and norepinephrine transporters (NET) with Ki values of 245 ± 32 nM and 189 ± 28 nM, respectively, compared to METH (Ki = 125 ± 18 nM for DAT, 89 ± 15 nM for NET). MPA demonstrated slower brain penetration (Tmax = 45 min vs. 20 min for METH) but prolonged elimination half-life (t½ = 4.2 ± 0.6 h vs. 2.8 ± 0.4 h). These findings suggest MPA functions as a weaker but longer-acting psychostimulant compared to METH, providing crucial pharmacological data for forensic identification and clinical management of MPA intoxication cases.

title: How to design PET experiments to study neurochemistry: application to alcoholism.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating neurochemical alterations in alcohol use disorders, yet methodological considerations critically impact data interpretation and reproducibility. This review synthesizes current best practices for designing PET neurochemistry studies in alcoholism research, addressing common pitfalls and optimization strategies. We examined 127 published PET studies investigating dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic systems in alcohol-dependent subjects from 2000-2013, analyzing experimental design parameters including radioligand selection, scanning protocols, and subject characterization. Key design considerations include appropriate radioligand choice based on target receptor density and binding kinetics, standardized abstinence periods to minimize acute withdrawal effects, and careful matching of control subjects for age, sex, and smoking status. Quantification approaches significantly influenced effect sizes, with reference tissue methods showing greater sensitivity for detecting group differences in striatal dopamine D2/D3 receptor availability compared to arterial input function methods. Studies incorporating pharmacological challenges revealed enhanced detection of synaptic neurotransmitter changes, particularly for dopamine release paradigms. Sample size calculations indicated that detecting 15-20% group differences in receptor binding requires 12-15 subjects per group with 80% power. Standardized abstinence periods of 3-4 weeks optimized detection of trait-related neuroadaptations while minimizing acute withdrawal confounds. These methodological guidelines provide a framework for designing robust PET neurochemistry studies in alcoholism, facilitating more reliable characterization of addiction-related neurobiological alterations and improved translation to therapeutic interventions.
journal: The Yale journal of biology and medicine

title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
abstract: Public health policies targeting tobacco use have achieved significant population-level reductions in smoking prevalence, yet individual neurobiological mechanisms underlying nicotine dependence remain critical barriers to cessation success. This review synthesizes current psychopharmacological evidence with community-based intervention strategies to examine how multilevel approaches may optimize tobacco control outcomes. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 2005-2010 examining nicotine replacement therapies, bupropion, and varenicline in community settings, alongside population-based studies evaluating policy interventions including taxation, smoke-free laws, and media campaigns. Neurobiological studies demonstrate that chronic nicotine exposure produces persistent alterations in dopaminergic and cholinergic signaling pathways, particularly within mesocorticolimbic reward circuits, contributing to sustained craving and relapse vulnerability. Pharmacotherapies targeting these systems show 1.5-3.0 fold increases in quit rates compared to placebo in clinical trials. However, real-world effectiveness varies substantially across populations, with lower success rates observed in socioeconomically disadvantaged communities. Community-wide interventions combining pharmacotherapy access with environmental policy changes demonstrate enhanced outcomes, with population quit rates increasing 15-25% beyond individual treatment approaches alone. Smoke-free policies amplify pharmacotherapy effectiveness by reducing environmental cue exposure and social reinforcement of smoking behavior. These findings suggest that optimal tobacco control requires integrated strategies addressing both neurobiological dependence mechanisms through evidence-based pharmacotherapies and environmental factors through comprehensive community-level policies. Future research should prioritize implementation science approaches examining how to effectively deploy combined psychopharmacological and policy interventions across diverse populations.

title: The neurochemical mobile with non-linear interaction matrix: an exploratory computational model.
abstract: The complex interplay between neurotransmitter systems in psychiatric disorders remains poorly understood, with traditional linear models failing to capture the dynamic nature of neurochemical interactions. Recent advances in computational neuroscience suggest that non-linear mathematical frameworks may better represent the intricate feedback loops and emergent properties observed in neural networks. We developed a novel computational model termed the "neurochemical mobile" to simulate multi-dimensional neurotransmitter interactions using non-linear matrix algebra. The model incorporates five major neurotransmitter systems (dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, GABA, and glutamate) with dynamic weighting coefficients that adjust based on system-wide perturbations. Using published pharmacokinetic data and receptor binding affinities, we constructed interaction matrices with both positive and negative feedback loops. The model was validated against known drug effects from 127 psychoactive compounds across six therapeutic classes. Our simulations revealed three distinct stable states corresponding to euthymic, depressive, and manic-like neurochemical profiles. Notably, the model predicted previously uncharacterized drug interactions with 78% accuracy when compared to clinical reports. Phase-space analysis demonstrated critical transition points where small pharmacological interventions could trigger large-scale system reorganization. The neurochemical mobile framework successfully captured non-linear dose-response relationships and provided mechanistic insights into polypharmacy effects. This computational approach offers a promising tool for rational drug design and personalized medicine approaches in psychiatry, potentially reducing trial-and-error prescribing practices through predictive modeling of complex neurochemical interactions.

title: [Therapeutic drug monitoring in neuropsychopharmacology : Summary of the consensus guidelines 2017 of the TDM task force of the AGNP].
abstract: Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) has gained increasing importance in neuropsychopharmacology to optimize treatment outcomes and minimize adverse effects. The AGNP TDM task force developed updated consensus guidelines in 2017 to provide evidence-based recommendations for clinical practice. This summary reviews the key recommendations and their implementation in psychiatric treatment settings. The guidelines were developed through systematic literature review and expert consensus involving pharmacologists, psychiatrists, and laboratory specialists across German-speaking countries. Recommendations were categorized into four levels based on available evidence and clinical utility: strongly recommended, recommended, useful, and potentially useful. The 2017 guidelines provide specific recommendations for 60 psychotropic medications, including antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics. TDM is strongly recommended for tricyclic antidepressants, several atypical antipsychotics including clozapine and olanzapine, lithium, and anticonvulsant mood stabilizers. For newer antidepressants such as SSRIs and SNRIs, TDM is recommended in specific clinical situations including treatment-resistant cases, suspected non-adherence, drug interactions, and vulnerable populations. The guidelines emphasize proper timing of blood sampling, consideration of active metabolites, and interpretation within clinical context. Implementation of these evidence-based TDM recommendations can significantly improve therapeutic outcomes in psychiatric patients by enabling personalized dosing strategies, enhancing medication adherence monitoring, and reducing the risk of adverse drug reactions. Regular application of TDM principles represents a crucial step toward precision medicine in neuropsychopharmacology.

title: The neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review.
abstract: Agitation represents one of the most prevalent and distressing behavioral symptoms in Alzheimer's disease (AD), affecting up to 70% of patients and contributing significantly to caregiver burden and institutionalization. Despite its clinical importance, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. We conducted a systematic review to synthesize current knowledge on the neurochemical basis of agitation in AD. We searched PubMed, EMBASE, and PsycINFO databases from inception to December 2017 for studies investigating neurochemical correlates of agitation in AD patients. Studies were included if they examined cerebrospinal fluid, neuroimaging, or post-mortem brain tissue analyses in relation to validated agitation measures. Twenty-eight studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing 2,847 AD patients. Converging evidence implicated dysregulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems. Reduced cholinergic activity, particularly in frontal and temporal regions, was consistently associated with increased agitation severity across neuroimaging and post-mortem studies. Dopaminergic dysfunction, evidenced by decreased striatal dopamine transporter binding and altered D2 receptor density, correlated with agitated behaviors. Serotonergic abnormalities, including reduced 5-HT1A receptor binding and elevated inflammatory markers affecting serotonin metabolism, were linked to aggression and irritability. Additionally, increased noradrenergic activity and elevated cortisol levels suggested heightened stress responses in agitated patients. GABAergic deficits in frontal regions were associated with disinhibited behaviors. These findings suggest agitation in AD results from complex interactions between cholinergic depletion, monoaminergic imbalances, and stress system activation. Understanding these mechanisms may inform targeted therapeutic approaches for managing this challenging symptom.

title: Psychopharmacology and memory.
abstract: The therapeutic manipulation of memory through psychopharmacological interventions raises significant ethical questions regarding personal identity, autonomy, and the nature of authentic human experience. While memory-modulating drugs show promise for treating post-traumatic stress disorder, addiction, and neurodegenerative diseases, their potential applications extend beyond clear medical necessity into enhancement and selective memory editing. This analysis examines the ethical implications of three categories of memory-affecting agents: memory enhancers (cholinesterase inhibitors, ampakines), memory inhibitors (propranolol, anisomycin), and memory selectors (targeted reconsolidation blockers). Through systematic review of current literature and application of established bioethical frameworks, we evaluated the moral permissibility of memory modification across therapeutic, preventive, and enhancement contexts. Our analysis reveals a spectrum of ethical acceptability, with therapeutic applications for severe psychiatric conditions receiving strongest justification, while enhancement uses raise concerns about authenticity, fairness, and psychological continuity. Memory inhibition for trauma prevention shows promise but requires careful consideration of informed consent and long-term consequences. Selective memory editing presents the most complex ethical challenges, potentially undermining the integrated nature of personal narrative and moral development. We propose a graduated ethical framework emphasizing therapeutic necessity, proportionality, and reversibility as key criteria for evaluating memory modification interventions. Future research must address questions of distributive justice, coercion, and the societal implications of widespread memory manipulation technologies. Robust ethical oversight and public discourse are essential as these powerful interventions transition from experimental tools to clinical realities.

title: Adventures in neural plasticity, aging, and neurodegenerative disorders aboard the CWC beagle.
abstract: This article recounts some of the scientific endeavors of Carl W. Cotman (CWC) during his journeys through the cellular circuitry of the mammalian brain. I have selected for consideration his findings that have been an important impetus for my own research; in several cases our different experiments have provided complementary data to support an hypothesis. Three examples are (i) Carl's studies of the roles of glutamate in synaptic transmission and plasticity in the adult brain and my studies of how glutamate regulates neurite outgrowth and cell survival in brain development; (ii) his and our studies of the mechanisms whereby amyloid beta-peptide damages and kills neurons; and (iii) Carl's evidence that physical activity regulates neurotrophin levels in the brain and our evidence that dietary restriction has similar effects and is neuroprotective. In case you have not yet realized how I chose a title for this article it is because Carl has a (very distant) connection with Charles Darwin-Darwin sailed on a vessel called the Beagle and Carl has studied beagle dogs, establishing them as a model for understanding the neurobiology of human brain aging.

title: Psychopharmacology in autism.
