abstract: Autism is a pervasive developmental disorder characterized by impaired social interaction, communication deficits, and restricted repetitive behaviors. While the etiology remains unclear, neurochemical abnormalities involving serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems have been implicated. This study examined the efficacy and tolerability of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) in reducing maladaptive behaviors in children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorders. Twenty-eight subjects aged 6-17 years meeting DSM-IV criteria for autistic disorder were enrolled in a 12-week, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of fluoxetine (mean dose 0.43 mg/kg/day). Primary outcome measures included the Aberrant Behavior Checklist (ABC) and Clinical Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I) scale. Secondary measures assessed repetitive behaviors, social responsiveness, and adaptive functioning. Results demonstrated significant improvement in the fluoxetine group compared to placebo on ABC subscales for irritability (p=0.024), social withdrawal (p=0.031), and stereotypic behavior (p=0.047). Fifty-seven percent of fluoxetine-treated subjects were rated as much improved or very much improved on the CGI-I versus 14% of placebo recipients (p=0.018). Common adverse effects included decreased appetite (29%), sleep disturbance (21%), and behavioral activation (14%), but discontinuation rates were similar between groups. These findings suggest that low-dose fluoxetine may be beneficial for reducing core behavioral symptoms in autism, particularly repetitive behaviors and social withdrawal. Further research is needed to establish optimal dosing strategies and long-term safety profiles in this vulnerable population.

title: High-sensitive liquid chromatographic method for determination of neuronal release of serotonin, noradrenaline and dopamine monitored by microdialysis in the rat prefrontal cortex.
abstract: A high-sensitive liquid chromatographic method based on precolumn derivatization and fluorescence detection allowing simultaneous determination of serotonin (5-HT), noradrenaline (NA) and dopamine (DA) in brain microdialysis samples is described. 5-HT, NA and DA were derivatized with benzylamine and 1,2-diphenylethylenediamine in the presence of potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) and glycine, which yielded to highly fluorescent and stable benzoxazoles. The derivatized samples were separated on a microbore column (150 mm x 1.0mm i.d., packed with C18 silica, 5 microm) within 60 min. The mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile-Briton-Robinson buffer (pH 7.2) (32:68, v/v) containing 5 mM Na2EDTA and 5 mM octanesulfonic acid sodium salt. The detection limits (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) for 5-HT, NA and DA were 76, 42 and 95 amol/10 microl injected on-column, respectively. Microdialysis samples were collected at 10-min intervals from the probes implanted in the prefrontal cortex of awake rats. The basal levels of 5-HT, NA and DA were 7.3 +/- 0.7, 5.3 +/- 0.31 and 8.1 +/- 0.47 fmol/5 microl (mean +/- S.E.M., n = 5). Following 90-min perfusion with tetrodotoxin (1 microM) or calcium-free Ringer solution, the DA and NA levels were reduced to about 15 and 20%, respectively and the 5-HT levels to 45 and 60% of the basal levels, respectively. Reserpine, 12h after a dose of 5mg/kg i.p., reduced the extracellular 5-HT, NA and DA concentrations to about 34, 39 and 32% of the basal levels, respectively. In conclusion, the preset microdialysis/analytical method enables simultaneous monitoring of basal and pharmacologically reduced neuronal release of 5-HT, NA and DA in the rat brain.

title: Operant self-administration models for testing the neuropharmacological basis of ethanol consumption in rats.
abstract: Operant self-administration paradigms represent the gold standard for investigating the neurobiological mechanisms underlying ethanol reinforcement and consumption patterns. These models provide translational validity by allowing laboratory animals to voluntarily consume ethanol through learned behaviors that parallel human drinking patterns. The current protocol describes established methods for training rats to self-administer ethanol using operant conditioning chambers, enabling systematic evaluation of pharmacological interventions on ethanol-seeking and consumption behaviors. Male Wistar rats (n=48) were trained to lever-press for 10% ethanol solutions using a fixed-ratio 1 schedule with concurrent water availability. Following acquisition, animals underwent pharmacological challenges with the μ-opioid receptor antagonist naltrexone (0.1-3.0 mg/kg, s.c.), the dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonist raclopride (0.01-0.1 mg/kg, i.p.), and the GABAB receptor agonist baclofen (1.0-5.0 mg/kg, i.p.) administered 30 minutes prior to test sessions. Naltrexone dose-dependently reduced ethanol self-administration (F=12.4, p<0.001) with significant effects observed at 1.0 and 3.0 mg/kg doses without affecting water consumption. Raclopride similarly decreased ethanol lever-pressing (F=8.7, p<0.01) at 0.1 mg/kg. Baclofen produced mixed effects, reducing consumption at 2.5 mg/kg but causing motor impairment at higher doses. These findings demonstrate the utility of operant self-administration models for characterizing neurotransmitter systems mediating ethanol reinforcement, particularly opioidergic and dopaminergic pathways. This methodology provides a robust platform for screening potential pharmacotherapeutics for alcohol use disorders and investigating the neurochemical basis of ethanol reward.

title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
abstract: Upregulation of neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (AChRs) is a venerable result of chronic exposure to nicotine; but it is one of several consequences of pharmacological chaperoning by nicotine and by some other nicotinic ligands, especially agonists. Nicotinic ligands permeate through cell membranes, bind to immature AChR oligomers, elicit incompletely understood conformational reorganizations, increase the interaction between adjacent AChR subunits, and enhance the maturation process toward stable AChR pentamers. These changes and stabilizations in turn lead to increases in both anterograde and retrograde traffic within the early secretory pathway. In addition to the eventual upregulation of AChRs at the plasma membrane, other effects of pharmacological chaperoning include modifications to endoplasmic reticulum stress and to the unfolded protein response. Because these processes depend on pharmacological chaperoning within intracellular organelles, we group them as "inside-out pharmacology". This term contrasts with the better-known, acute, "outside-in" effects of activating and desensitizing plasma membrane AChRs. We review current knowledge concerning the mechanisms and consequences of inside-out pharmacology. This article is part of the Special Issue entitled 'The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor: From Molecular Biology to Cognition'.

title: Quantitative assessment of the microstructure of rat behavior: I, f(d), the extension of the scaling hypothesis.
abstract: Recent advances in behavioral pharmacology have emphasized the importance of analyzing fine-grained temporal patterns of behavior rather than relying solely on traditional rate measures. The scaling hypothesis, originally proposed for simple operant responses, suggests that drug effects on behavior follow predictable mathematical relationships across different time scales. This study extends this framework by introducing f(d), a novel quantitative measure that characterizes the microstructure of behavioral sequences as a function of temporal resolution (d). Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were trained on a free-operant lever pressing schedule and subsequently administered acute doses of amphetamine (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mg/kg, i.p.), chlorpromazine (1.0, 2.0, 4.0 mg/kg, i.p.), or saline vehicle. Behavioral sessions were recorded at 10-ms resolution and analyzed using computer-automated detection of response clusters, inter-response times, and pause durations. The f(d) function was calculated by measuring behavioral complexity across logarithmically spaced time windows from 0.01 to 100 seconds. Results demonstrated that f(d) follows a power-law relationship under control conditions, with amphetamine producing systematic shifts in the scaling exponent (increased from 0.73±0.08 to 1.24±0.12 at 2.0 mg/kg), while chlorpromazine decreased the exponent (0.51±0.09 at 4.0 mg/kg). These findings suggest that psychoactive drugs produce characteristic "fingerprints" in behavioral microstructure that are not apparent in conventional analyses. The f(d) measure provides a sensitive tool for detecting drug effects and may offer insights into the temporal organization of dopaminergic and other neurotransmitter systems underlying motivated behavior.

title: Metabolic changes detected by proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy in vivo and in vitro in a murin model of Parkinson's disease, the MPTP-intoxicated mouse.
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is characterized by selective dopaminergic neuronal loss in the substantia nigra, yet the underlying metabolic alterations remain poorly understood. The 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP) mouse model reproduces key features of PD pathology and provides an opportunity to investigate neurochemical changes associated with dopaminergic degeneration. We employed proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (¹H-MRS) to characterize metabolic profiles in MPTP-treated C57BL/6 mice both in vivo and in vitro. Adult male mice received four subcutaneous injections of MPTP (20 mg/kg) at 2-hour intervals. In vivo ¹H-MRS was performed at 9.4T focusing on striatal regions at 1, 7, and 21 days post-treatment. Ex vivo high-resolution magic angle spinning ¹H-MRS was conducted on striatal and nigral tissue extracts. MPTP treatment resulted in significant reductions in N-acetylaspartate (NAA) levels in the striatum by day 7 (23% decrease, p<0.01), which persisted through day 21. Lactate concentrations were elevated 2-fold at day 1 but normalized by day 7. In vitro analysis revealed decreased glutamate and increased taurine levels in both striatal and nigral regions. Creatine and choline-containing compounds showed region-specific alterations, with striatal creatine decreasing by 18% while nigral levels remained unchanged. These findings demonstrate that MPTP-induced dopaminergic toxicity produces distinct metabolic signatures detectable by ¹H-MRS, with NAA reduction likely reflecting neuronal dysfunction or loss. The metabolic profiles provide insights into the temporal progression of MPTP toxicity and may serve as biomarkers for monitoring disease progression and therapeutic interventions in experimental PD.

title: High frequency, real-time neurochemical and neuropharmacological measurements in situ in the living body.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in living neural tissue remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research, limiting our understanding of drug action mechanisms and therapeutic optimization. Traditional sampling methods lack the temporal resolution necessary to capture rapid neurotransmitter fluctuations and drug-target interactions occurring on millisecond timescales. We developed and validated a novel carbon fiber microelectrode array system coupled with fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) for simultaneous, high-frequency detection of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine in freely-moving rodents. The biosensor platform achieved sub-second temporal resolution (100 Hz sampling) with nanomolar sensitivity and minimal tissue damage. We tested the system by monitoring real-time neurochemical responses to acute administration of cocaine (10 mg/kg, i.p.), fluoxetine (20 mg/kg, i.p.), and L-DOPA (100 mg/kg, i.p.) in the striatum and nucleus accumbens of awake rats (n=8 per group). Results demonstrated rapid dopamine transporter blockade within 2.3 ± 0.4 seconds of cocaine administration, with peak concentrations occurring at 47 ± 12 seconds. Fluoxetine selectively elevated serotonin levels with a delayed onset of 23 ± 6 seconds, while L-DOPA produced sustained dopamine elevation lasting 180 ± 25 minutes. Cross-correlation analysis revealed previously undetected interactions between monoamine systems during pharmacological perturbation. This technology enables unprecedented temporal resolution for studying neuropharmacological mechanisms in vivo, providing critical insights for rational drug design and personalized medicine approaches in neurological and psychiatric disorders.
journal: Translational research : the journal of laboratory and clinical medicine

title: The discovery of chemical neurotransmitters.
abstract: The elucidation of chemical neurotransmission represents one of the most fundamental paradigm shifts in neuroscience, transitioning from purely electrical models of neural communication to our current understanding of synaptic transmission. This historical review examines the key experimental discoveries and methodological advances that established the chemical basis of interneuronal signaling from the early 20th century through the molecular era. We analyzed seminal studies beginning with Loewi's 1921 frog heart experiments demonstrating "Vagusstoff," through Dale's acetylcholine work, Eccles' intracellular recordings at the neuromuscular junction, and subsequent identification of major neurotransmitter systems including catecholamines, amino acids, and neuropeptides. Critical methodological developments included bioassay techniques, electron microscopy revealing synaptic vesicles, subcellular fractionation methods, and receptor binding assays. The convergence of evidence from these approaches definitively established that neurons communicate primarily through calcium-dependent vesicular release of specific chemical messengers that bind to postsynaptic receptors. Key findings included the quantal nature of transmitter release, the existence of distinct receptor subtypes, and the complex machinery governing synaptic vesicle fusion and recycling. These discoveries fundamentally transformed our understanding of neural computation, learning and memory, and neurological disease mechanisms. The identification of chemical neurotransmitters provided the conceptual framework for modern psychopharmacology and continues to drive therapeutic drug development. This historical perspective illuminates how methodological innovation and cross-disciplinary collaboration established the chemical foundations of nervous system function.

title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
abstract: Social hierarchies are fundamental organizational structures across mammalian species, yet the underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. The mesolimbic dopaminergic system, critical for reward processing and motivation, has been implicated in dominance behaviors, but its specific role in hierarchy establishment and maintenance requires further investigation. Here, we examined the contribution of dopamine D1 and D2 receptors within the nucleus accumbens (NAc) and ventral tegmental area (VTA) to social rank determination in adult male C57BL/6J mice. Using a tube test paradigm to assess dominance relationships, we administered selective D1 (SCH23390) and D2 (sulpiride) receptor antagonists via bilateral microinjections prior to social encounters. Additionally, we employed optogenetic stimulation of VTA dopamine neurons during hierarchy formation trials. Behavioral analysis revealed that D1 receptor blockade in the NAc significantly reduced dominance scores (p<0.01), while D2 antagonism had minimal effects. VTA dopamine neuron stimulation enhanced dominant behaviors in subordinate animals (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis showed increased c-Fos expression in NAc medium spiny neurons following successful dominance displays. Microdialysis measurements confirmed elevated extracellular dopamine levels in dominant animals during social interactions. These findings demonstrate that mesolimbic D1 receptor signaling is necessary for establishing social dominance, while VTA dopamine release is sufficient to promote hierarchical status. Our results provide novel insights into the neuropharmacological basis of social hierarchies and suggest potential therapeutic targets for disorders involving aberrant social behaviors.

title: Validation of a robust and sensitive method for detecting hydroxyl radical formation together with evoked neurotransmitter release in brain microdialysis.
abstract: Microdialysis has become an essential technique for monitoring neurotransmitter release in vivo, but simultaneous detection of oxidative stress markers remains technically challenging. Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are highly reactive oxygen species implicated in neurodegeneration, yet their detection alongside neurotransmitters requires methodological optimization due to their short half-life and low steady-state concentrations. We developed and validated a sensitive analytical approach combining high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) to simultaneously quantify •OH formation via 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,3-DHBA) trapping and dopamine release in rat striatal microdialysates. The method employed salicylic acid as a molecular probe for •OH detection, which forms 2,3-DHBA upon hydroxylation. Validation experiments demonstrated linear responses for both 2,3-DHBA (0.5-50 nM, r²=0.998) and dopamine (1-100 nM, r²=0.997) with detection limits of 0.2 nM and 0.5 nM, respectively. Recovery rates through microdialysis probes were 18±2% for 2,3-DHBA and 22±3% for dopamine. In anesthetized rats, potassium-evoked depolarization (100 mM KCl) increased extracellular dopamine by 340±45% while simultaneously elevating 2,3-DHBA formation by 280±38% compared to baseline. Iron-catalyzed Fenton reactions were confirmed as the primary source of •OH generation using deferoxamine pretreatment, which reduced 2,3-DHBA formation by 75±8%. This validated methodology provides a robust tool for investigating the relationship between neurotransmitter release and oxidative stress in neurochemical research, particularly relevant for studies of neurodegeneration and neuroprotection.

title: A beam-walking apparatus to assess behavioural impairments in MPTP-treated mice: pharmacological validation with R-(-)-deprenyl.
abstract: A beam-walking apparatus has been evaluated for its ability to detect motor impairments in mice acutely treated with the dopaminergic neurotoxin 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP, 30 mg/kg, s.c., single or double administration). Mice subjected to MPTP lesioning showed deficits in motor performance on the beam-walking task, for up to 6 days post-MPTP administration, as compared to saline-treated controls. In addition, MPTP-treated mice were detected to have a marked depletion in striatal dopamine levels and a concomitant reduction in substantia nigra (SN) tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) immunoreactivity, at 7 days post-MPTP administration, indicative of dopaminergic neuronal loss. Pre-administration of the potent MAO-B inhibitor R-(-)-deprenyl at 3 or 10 mg/kg, 30 min, s.c, significantly inhibited the MPTP-induced reduction in SN TH-immunoreactivity, striatal dopamine depletions and impairments in mouse motor function. The data described in the present study provides further evidence that functional deficits following an acute MPTP dosing schedule in mice can be quantified and are related to nigro-striatal dopamine function.

title: Frontal-subcortical neuronal circuits and clinical neuropsychiatry: an update.
abstract: Frontal-subcortical circuits form the principal network, which mediate motor activity and behavior in humans. Five parallel frontal-subcortical circuits link the specific areas of the frontal cortex to the striatum, basal ganglia and thalamus. These frontal-subcortical circuits originate from the supplementary motor area, frontal eye field, dorsolateral prefrontal region, lateral orbitofrontal region and anterior cingulate portion of the frontal cortex. The open afferent and efferent connections to the frontal-subcortical circuits mediate coordination between functionally similar areas of the brain. Specific chemoarchitecture and multiple neurotransmitter interactions modulate the functional activity of each circuit. Dorsolateral prefrontal circuit lesions cause executive dysfunction, orbitofrontal circuit lesions lead to personality changes characterized by disinhibition and anterior cingulate circuit lesions present with apathy. The neurobiological correlates of neuropsychiatric disorders including depression, obsessive-compulsive disorder, schizophrenia and substance abuse, imply involvement of frontal-subcortical circuits.

title: European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) - 23rd Congress.
abstract: The 23rd Congress of the European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) convened in Amsterdam, Netherlands, from August 28 to September 1, 2010, bringing together leading researchers in neuropsychopharmacology to present cutting-edge findings in psychiatric drug development and neuroscience. This comprehensive report summarizes key presentations and symposia focusing on novel therapeutic targets and investigational compounds for major psychiatric disorders. The congress featured 142 oral presentations, 756 poster presentations, and 28 satellite symposiums covering diverse areas including depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, anxiety disorders, and addiction medicine. Notable highlights included presentations on next-generation antidepressants targeting the glutamatergic system, including mGluR2/3 agonists and NMDA receptor modulators showing promise in treatment-resistant depression. Significant attention was devoted to novel antipsychotic agents with improved metabolic profiles, particularly partial dopamine D2 receptor agonists and dual-acting compounds modulating both dopaminergic and serotonergic pathways. Innovative approaches in addiction medicine featured presentations on phosphodiesterase inhibitors and GABA-B receptor agonists for alcohol and cocaine dependence. Emerging biomarker strategies for personalized psychiatric medicine were extensively discussed, emphasizing pharmacogenomic approaches and neuroimaging-guided treatment selection. The congress highlighted the industry's shift toward more sophisticated mechanistic approaches in psychiatric drug development, moving beyond traditional monoaminergic targets. These advances represent significant progress in addressing the substantial unmet medical needs in psychiatry, with several investigational compounds expected to enter Phase III trials within the next two years, potentially transforming treatment paradigms for millions of patients worldwide.

title: Chronic infusion of PCP via osmotic mini-pumps: a new rodent model of cognitive deficit in schizophrenia characterized by impaired attentional set-shifting (ID/ED) performance.
abstract: Schizophrenia is characterized by persistent cognitive deficits, particularly in executive function and attentional flexibility, which are poorly modeled by acute pharmacological interventions. The N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction hypothesis of schizophrenia suggests that chronic NMDA receptor blockade may better recapitulate the cognitive symptoms observed clinically. We developed a novel rodent model using continuous subcutaneous infusion of phencyclidine (PCP) via osmotic mini-pumps to examine sustained NMDA receptor antagonism effects on cognitive performance. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were implanted with 14-day osmotic pumps delivering either saline or PCP at doses of 2.58, 5.16, or 10.32 mg/kg/day. Following a 7-day recovery period, animals were trained on an intra-dimensional/extra-dimensional (ID/ED) attentional set-shifting task, a rodent analog of the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test sensitive to prefrontal cortical dysfunction. Chronic PCP infusion produced dose-dependent impairments in cognitive flexibility without affecting simple discrimination learning or reversal performance. Animals receiving 5.16 and 10.32 mg/kg/day PCP showed significantly increased trials to criterion and errors specifically during extra-dimensional set-shifting phases (p<0.01), while intra-dimensional shifting remained intact. These deficits emerged after 5-7 days of continuous infusion and persisted throughout the 14-day treatment period. Locomotor activity was minimally affected at the lower doses that produced cognitive impairment. This chronic infusion model provides a novel tool for investigating NMDA receptor hypofunction-mediated cognitive deficits relevant to schizophrenia and may facilitate development of procognitive therapeutic interventions targeting executive dysfunction.

title: High-sensitive liquid chromatographic method for determination of neuronal release of serotonin, noradrenaline and dopamine monitored by microdialysis in the rat prefrontal cortex.
abstract: Microdialysis coupled with liquid chromatography has become an essential technique for monitoring neurotransmitter release in vivo, but existing methods often lack the sensitivity required for simultaneous detection of multiple monoamines in small brain regions. We developed a highly sensitive HPLC method with electrochemical detection for the simultaneous quantification of serotonin (5-HT), noradrenaline (NA), and dopamine (DA) in microdialysate samples from rat prefrontal cortex. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8) were implanted with microdialysis probes (2 mm membrane length) in the medial prefrontal cortex. Dialysate samples (20 μL) were collected at 20-minute intervals and analyzed using a C18 reverse-phase column with a mobile phase containing sodium acetate buffer, methanol, and octanesulfonic acid at pH 4.2. Detection was performed using a glassy carbon electrode at +0.65V versus Ag/AgCl reference. The method demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.998) over the concentration range 0.1-50 fmol for all three neurotransmitters, with detection limits of 0.05 fmol for 5-HT, 0.08 fmol for NA, and 0.03 fmol for DA. Intra-day and inter-day precision were <8% for all compounds. Basal extracellular concentrations were 1.2 ± 0.3 fmol/20μL for 5-HT, 2.1 ± 0.4 fmol/20μL for NA, and 0.8 ± 0.2 fmol/20μL for DA. Pharmacological validation using selective reuptake inhibitors confirmed the neuronal origin of detected neurotransmitters. This sensitive method enables reliable monitoring of monoaminergic neurotransmission in the prefrontal cortex and should prove valuable for neuropharmacological studies investigating cognitive and emotional behaviors.

title: Pitfalls in presenting and interpreting clinical trial data.
abstract: Clinical trials in psychopharmacology have become increasingly complex, yet significant methodological and interpretive issues continue to compromise the validity of reported findings. This review examines common pitfalls encountered in the presentation and analysis of clinical trial data in neuropharmacological research. We analyzed 127 published clinical trials of psychotropic medications from 1990-1994, focusing on study design, statistical methodology, and data interpretation practices. Major deficiencies identified include inadequate sample size calculations (found in 43% of studies), inappropriate statistical analyses such as multiple comparisons without correction (38%), selective reporting of favorable outcomes (31%), and misinterpretation of statistical significance versus clinical significance (29%). Post-hoc analyses were frequently presented without appropriate caveats in 47% of trials. Dropout rates exceeding 30% were inadequately addressed in 52% of studies, with last-observation-carried-forward methods applied inappropriately. Placebo response rates varied dramatically (15-65%) across similar conditions, suggesting heterogeneous patient populations or study conduct issues. Adverse event reporting was inconsistent, with systematic collection methods described in only 34% of trials. Industry-sponsored studies showed significantly higher rates of positive primary outcomes compared to investigator-initiated trials (78% vs. 52%, p<0.01). These findings highlight critical areas requiring attention to enhance the reliability and clinical utility of psychopharmacological research. Standardized reporting guidelines, appropriate statistical consultation, and enhanced peer review processes are essential to address these systematic weaknesses in clinical trial methodology and interpretation.

title: Neuropsychopharmacology and the genetics of schizophrenia: a history of the diagnosis of schizophrenia.
abstract: The diagnostic conceptualization of schizophrenia has undergone substantial evolution since Kraepelin's initial description of dementia praecox, with profound implications for contemporary neuropsychopharmacological research and genetic studies. This historical review examines the progression of diagnostic criteria from classical descriptive approaches through modern operational definitions, and their impact on pharmacogenetic investigations. We analyzed key diagnostic frameworks from 1896 to 2000, including contributions by Kraepelin, Bleuler, Schneider, and the DSM/ICD systems, correlating these with major pharmacological discoveries and genetic findings. The transition from Kraepelin's deterioration-based model to Bleuler's broader concept of "split mind" fundamentally altered research populations and therapeutic approaches. Schneider's first-rank symptoms provided operational clarity but potentially narrowed the phenotypic scope for genetic studies. The introduction of chlorpromazine in the 1950s marked the beginning of rational psychopharmacology, while subsequent dopamine hypothesis development directly influenced both diagnostic conceptualization and drug discovery. Modern neuropsychopharmacological research has been significantly shaped by DSM-III's operational criteria, enabling more homogeneous study populations for pharmacogenetic investigations. However, this diagnostic refinement may have inadvertently excluded important phenotypic variants relevant to treatment response and genetic susceptibility. The evolution of schizophrenia diagnosis reflects the complex interplay between clinical observation, pharmacological discovery, and genetic understanding. Current neuropsychopharmacological research would benefit from reconsidering broader phenotypic definitions that encompass the full spectrum of schizophrenia-related disorders, particularly as pharmacogenetic studies reveal the heterogeneous nature of treatment response within traditional diagnostic boundaries.

title: Psychopharmacological treatment of schizophrenia: what do we have, and what could we get?
abstract: Current antipsychotic medications for schizophrenia primarily target dopamine D2 receptors, providing modest efficacy for positive symptoms but limited benefits for negative symptoms and cognitive deficits. This comprehensive review examines existing pharmacotherapeutic approaches and emerging therapeutic targets for schizophrenia treatment. We conducted a systematic analysis of published clinical trials, preclinical studies, and mechanistic investigations spanning 1990-2012, focusing on both established antipsychotics and novel compounds targeting alternative neurotransmitter systems. Current first- and second-generation antipsychotics demonstrate 60-70% response rates for positive symptoms but show minimal improvement in functional outcomes. Clozapine remains the gold standard for treatment-resistant cases, though its use is limited by serious adverse effects. Emerging approaches targeting glutamatergic hypofunction through NMDA receptor glycine transport inhibition (e.g., bitopertin) and mGluR2/3 agonism show promise in early trials. Cholinergic enhancement via α7 nicotinic receptor modulation demonstrates potential for cognitive improvement, while muscarinic M1/M4 receptor agonists offer antipsychotic efficacy with reduced extrapyramidal side effects. Additionally, compounds targeting histamine H3 receptors and trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) represent novel therapeutic avenues. Despite these advances, effect sizes for cognitive enhancement remain modest, and functional improvement continues to lag behind symptom reduction. Future therapeutic development should prioritize multi-target approaches addressing the complex pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with particular emphasis on procognitive agents and treatments that meaningfully improve quality of life and social functioning.

title: Academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: In the second half of the 19th century new drugs introduced by the pharmaceutical industry helped lead to the establishment of academic departments in psychiatry. Causal treatment of cerebral pellagra by nicotinic acid and cerebral syphilis by penicillin in the first half of the 20th century led to major changes in the diagnostic distribution of psychiatric patients. In the second half of the 20th century with the introduction of a rapidly growing number of psychotropic drugs, pharmacotherapy became the primary form of treatment in mental illness. Psychiatrists today perceive neuropharmacology as one of the basic sciences of psychiatry and psychopharmacology as the bridge between the mode of action and the clinical indications of psychotropic drugs. Pharmacotherapy with psychotropic drugs focused attention on the differential responsiveness to the same drug within the same diagnostic category. Yet, instead of re-evaluating psychiatric nosology and conducting research in psychopathology, a statistical methodology was adopted for the demonstration of therapeutic effectiveness in pharmacologically heterogeneous populations. Employment of consensus-based classifications and psychiatric rating scales in the clinical development of psychotropic drugs led to semi-finished products, which are prescribed indiscriminately. Replacement of single-center clinical trials by multi-center centrally coordinated clinical investigations led to the control of education in pharmacotherapy by the pharmaceutical industry. To separate education from marketing, the identification of the treatment-responsive forms of illness and the delineation of the therapeutic profile of psychotropic drugs are proposed with the employment of a new methodology, the "Composite Diagnostic Evaluation System." It is postulated that development of a pharmacologically valid psychiatric nosology with the employment of a "nosologic matrix" would provide the pharmaceutical industry with the necessary feedback to develop clinically selective drugs in mental illness and to break the impasse of progress in "translational research" in psychiatry.

title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
abstract: Low sexual desire is the most common sexual complaint in women. As a result, many women suffer from sexual dissatisfaction which often negatively interferes with their quality of life. These complaints have been classified as the condition Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder (HSDD), and have recently been merged with the condition Female Sexual Arousal Disorder (FSAD) into the diagnosis Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder (FSIAD) in the DSM-5. To date, no drug treatment approved by the U.S. Food & Drug Administration (FDA)/European Medicines Agency (EMA) is available to treat women with HSDD/FSIAD. As a result, there is an unmet need for a drug treatment for HSDD/FSIAD. In our search for an adequate treatment we followed a different approach compared to other pharmaceutical companies. Based on a personalized sexual medicine approach we proposed that different mechanisms cause low sexual desire in women, namely an insensitive system for sexual cues or dysfunctional activation of sexual inhibitory mechanisms. Subsequently we developed two new on-demand drug treatments for women with HSDD/FSIAD based on these different causal mechanisms. One treatment (testosterone combined with a phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitor) has been developed for women with HSDD/FSIAD due to a relatively insensitive system for sexual cues, while the second treatment (testosterone combined with a 5-HT₁A receptor agonist) has been developed for women with HSDD/FSIAD due to dysfunctional activation of sexual inhibitory mechanisms.

title: Psychopharmacology of the endocannabinoids: far beyond anandamide.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical neuromodulatory network, yet research has predominantly focused on anandamide (AEA) while neglecting other bioactive lipid mediators. This review synthesizes current understanding of the broader endocannabinoid pharmacology beyond classical eCB signaling. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent literature examining 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), palmitoylethanolamide (PEA), oleoylethanolamide (OEA), and N-arachidonoyldopamine (NADA) across preclinical models of neuropsychiatric disorders. Unlike AEA's predominant CB1 receptor activity, 2-AG demonstrates distinct biosynthetic pathways via diacylglycerol lipase and enhanced synaptic plasticity modulation. PEA and OEA, while lacking direct cannabinoid receptor affinity, exhibit potent anti-inflammatory and feeding regulatory properties through peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-α (PPAR-α) and GPR119 mechanisms. NADA uniquely activates both cannabinoid and vanilloid receptor systems, suggesting novel therapeutic targets. Pharmacokinetic analyses reveal differential enzymatic degradation patterns, with fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) and monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL) displaying substrate selectivity that impacts tissue-specific endocannabinoid tone. Behavioral studies demonstrate that selective modulation of non-AEA pathways produces distinct anxiolytic, analgesic, and cognitive effects, often with improved therapeutic windows compared to direct CB1 agonism. These findings highlight the endocannabinoid system's remarkable pharmacological diversity and suggest that therapeutic interventions targeting specific eCB metabolic pathways may offer superior clinical outcomes with reduced psychoactive liability compared to traditional cannabinoid approaches.

title: Compact 256-channel multi-well microelectrode array system for in vitro neuropharmacology test.
abstract: Microelectrode arrays (MEAs) have been extensively used to measure extracellular spike activity from cultured neurons using multiple electrodes embedded in a planar glass substrate. This system has been implemented to investigate drug effects by detecting pharmacological perturbation reflected in spontaneous network activity. By configuring multiple wells in an MEA, a high-throughput electrophysiological assay has become available, speeding up drug tests. Despite its merits in acquiring massive amounts of electrophysiological data, the high cost and the bulky size of commercial multi-well MEA systems and most importantly its lack of customizability prevent potential users from fully implementing the system in drug experiments. In this work, we have developed a microelectrode array based drug testing platform by incorporating a custom-made compact 256-channel multi-well MEA in a standard microscope slide and commercial application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) chip based recording system. We arranged 256 electrodes in 16 wells to maximize data collection from a single chip. The multi-well MEA in this work has a more compact design with reduced chip size compared to previously reported multi-well MEAs. Four synaptic modulators (NMDA, AMPA, bicuculline (BIC) and ATP) were applied to a multi-well MEA and neural spike activity was analyzed to study their neurophysiological effects on cultured neurons. Analyzing various neuropharmacological compounds has become much more accessible by utilizing commercially available digital amplifier chips and customizing a user-preferred analog-front-end interface design with additional benefits in reduced platform size and cost.

title: Brain imaging in migraine research.
abstract: Understanding the pathophysiology and pharmacology of migraine has been driven by astute clinical observations, elegant experimental medicine studies, and importantly by studying highly effective anti-migraine agents in the laboratory and the clinic. Significant progress has been made in the use of functional brain imaging to compliment observational studies of migraine phenotypes by highlighting pathways within the brain that may be involved in predisposition to migraine, modulating migraine pain or that could be sensitive to pharmacological or behavioral therapeutic intervention. In drug discovery, molecular imaging approaches compliment functional neuroimaging by visualizing migraine drug targets within the brain. Molecular imaging enables the selection and evaluation of drug candidates by confirming that they engage their targets sufficiently at well tolerated doses to test our therapeutic hypotheses. Migraine is a progressive disorder. Developing our knowledge of where drugs act in the brain and of how the brain is altered in both episodic migraine (interictal state and ictal state) and chronic migraine are important steps to understanding why there is such differential responsiveness to therapeutics among migraine patients and to improving how they are evaluated and treated.

title: Effects of dopamine D2/D3 receptor antagonism on human planning and spatial working memory.
abstract: Dopaminergic signaling in the prefrontal cortex and striatum plays a crucial role in executive functions, yet the specific contribution of D2/D3 receptors to higher-order cognitive processes remains poorly understood. This study investigated the effects of selective D2/D3 receptor antagonism on planning abilities and spatial working memory in healthy volunteers. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover study with 24 healthy adults (mean age 24.3±3.1 years, 12 female). Participants received either sulpiride (400mg) or placebo and completed the Tower of London task to assess planning performance and a spatial n-back task (1-back, 2-back, 3-back) to evaluate working memory capacity. Cognitive testing occurred 2-3 hours post-administration during peak plasma concentrations. Compared to placebo, sulpiride significantly impaired planning performance, increasing both solution time (p=0.012) and number of moves required (p=0.031) on complex Tower of London problems. Spatial working memory showed a differential pattern, with sulpiride selectively impairing 3-back performance accuracy (p=0.018) while leaving 1-back and 2-back performance intact. Reaction times were prolonged across all working memory conditions under sulpiride (p=0.007). These findings demonstrate that D2/D3 receptors are critical for optimal planning and high-demand spatial working memory in humans. The selective impairment of complex cognitive operations suggests that D2/D3 antagonism disrupts prefrontal-striatal circuits specifically when cognitive load is high, providing insight into the neurochemical mechanisms underlying executive dysfunction in psychiatric disorders characterized by altered dopaminergic transmission.

title: Consensus statements on the clinical uses of pregabalin for Hong Kong.
abstract:
**Background:** Pregabalin, a gabapentinoid with established efficacy in neuropathic pain and epilepsy, has gained increasing attention for psychiatric applications in Asia-Pacific regions. However, clinical practice guidelines specific to Hong Kong populations remain limited. This study aimed to develop evidence-based consensus statements on pregabalin's clinical uses in Hong Kong psychiatric practice.

**Methods:** A modified Delphi process was conducted involving 24 psychiatrists and neurologists from major Hong Kong medical institutions between January-September 2019. Three rounds of structured questionnaires examined pregabalin's efficacy, safety, dosing protocols, and clinical applications across anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder, and substance use disorders. Literature review encompassed randomized controlled trials and real-world evidence from Asian populations. Consensus was defined as ≥75% agreement among panelists.

**Results:** Strong consensus emerged supporting pregabalin as second-line treatment for generalized anxiety disorder (91.7% agreement) and panic disorder (87.5% agreement) when first-line agents prove inadequate. For bipolar disorder, 79.2% agreed pregabalin demonstrated utility as adjunctive treatment for anxiety symptoms and sleep disturbances. Recommended starting doses were 75mg twice daily for anxiety disorders and 150mg daily for bipolar applications, with gradual titration based on response and tolerability. Common adverse effects included dizziness (68% of patients), sedation (45%), and weight gain (32%). Panelists emphasized careful monitoring for dependency potential, particularly in patients with substance use history.

**Conclusions:** These consensus statements provide practical guidance for pregabalin use in Hong Kong clinical practice, emphasizing its role as adjunctive therapy while highlighting the importance of individualized treatment approaches and comprehensive patient monitoring in Chinese populations.
journal: Asia-Pacific psychiatry : official journal of the Pacific Rim College of Psychiatrists

title: Microdialysis applications in neuroscience.
abstract: Microdialysis has emerged as a powerful in vivo sampling technique that enables real-time monitoring of neurotransmitters, metabolites, and drug concentrations in discrete brain regions of awake, freely moving animals. This review examines recent applications of cerebral microdialysis in neuroscience research, highlighting methodological advances and novel experimental paradigms developed over the past decade. We analyzed 127 peer-reviewed studies published between 2000-2007 that employed microdialysis to investigate neurotransmitter systems, drug pharmacokinetics, and pathophysiological mechanisms. Major applications included monitoring dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, and glutamate release in response to pharmacological challenges, behavioral paradigms, and disease models. Technological improvements have enhanced temporal resolution to sub-minute sampling intervals and reduced probe size to minimize tissue damage. Novel probe designs incorporating biosensors and online analytical systems have enabled simultaneous measurement of multiple analytes with improved sensitivity. Microdialysis studies have provided critical insights into addiction mechanisms, demonstrating drug-induced alterations in mesolimbic dopamine signaling, and have elucidated neurotransmitter changes in models of Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and stroke. Recent applications in clinical settings show promise for monitoring brain chemistry in neurosurgical patients. Despite limitations including delayed equilibration and potential tissue trauma, microdialysis remains an invaluable tool for investigating dynamic neurochemical processes. Future developments in probe miniaturization, analytical sensitivity, and wireless telemetry systems will likely expand applications in translational neuroscience research and clinical neuropharmacology.

title: [The history of CINP between 1958 and 2000].
abstract: The Collegium Internationale Neuro-Psychopharmacologicum (CINP) was established in 1958 as the first international organization dedicated to advancing the scientific understanding of neuropsychopharmacology. This historical review examines the evolution and contributions of CINP during its formative decades from 1958 to 2000, analyzing its role in shaping modern psychopharmacology research and clinical practice. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of CINP archival materials, including congress proceedings, membership records, scientific publications, and correspondence from founding members. Historical documents were obtained from the CINP archives and supplemented with interviews of key figures who participated in the organization's early development. Between 1958 and 2000, CINP organized 22 biennial world congresses across four continents, facilitating international collaboration among 3,847 researchers from 67 countries. The organization played a pivotal role in establishing standardized methodologies for psychopharmacological research, promoting the development of rating scales for psychiatric disorders, and fostering the emergence of biological psychiatry as a distinct discipline. Notable achievements included the creation of task forces on depression, schizophrenia, and anxiety disorders, which produced influential consensus statements that guided clinical practice. CINP also contributed significantly to the training of young investigators through fellowship programs and mentorship initiatives. The period from 1958 to 2000 represents a foundational era for CINP, during which the organization established itself as the premier international forum for neuropsychopharmacology research. CINP's contributions during this period were instrumental in transforming psychopharmacology from an emerging field into a mature scientific discipline with global impact.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Zebrafish assessment of cognitive improvement and anxiolysis: filling the gap between in vitro and rodent models for drug development.
abstract: Traditional drug discovery for neuropsychiatric disorders relies heavily on in vitro assays followed by costly rodent studies, creating a significant gap in translational research. Zebrafish (Danio rerio) have emerged as a promising intermediate model organism, offering vertebrate complexity with high-throughput screening capabilities. This review examines the utility of zebrafish behavioral assays for evaluating cognitive enhancement and anxiolytic properties of novel compounds. We analyzed published studies from 2005-2011 employing standardized zebrafish protocols including novel tank diving, light-dark preference, shoaling behavior, and spatial learning paradigms. Pharmacological validation studies demonstrated that zebrafish exhibit dose-dependent responses to established anxiolytics (diazepam, buspirone) and cognitive enhancers (modafinil, piracetam) consistent with mammalian models. The novel tank test showed 89% concordance with rodent elevated plus-maze results for anxiolytic screening across 23 compounds tested. Cognitive assessment using T-maze and conditioned place preference protocols revealed significant correlations (r=0.76, p<0.001) with Morris water maze performance in corresponding rodent studies. Zebrafish demonstrated superior throughput capacity, requiring 48-72 hours versus 2-4 weeks for equivalent rodent protocols, while maintaining 85% translational predictivity for clinical outcomes. These findings establish zebrafish as a valuable bridge between cellular and mammalian models, offering rapid, cost-effective screening for neurotropic compounds. The high evolutionary conservation of neurotransmitter systems and behavioral responses supports zebrafish integration into tiered drug development strategies for anxiety and cognitive disorders.

title: Psychopharmacology: finding one's way.
abstract: The development of effective psychopharmacological treatments has been hampered by limited understanding of drug action mechanisms and inadequate predictive models for therapeutic efficacy. While traditional approaches have relied primarily on behavioral endpoints and gross neurochemical changes, recent advances in receptor binding techniques and microdialysis methodology offer new opportunities to elucidate drug-brain interactions. We examined the relationship between dopaminergic and serotonergic neurotransmission patterns and behavioral outcomes in a spatial navigation paradigm using male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-12 per group). Animals received acute or chronic administration of haloperidol (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), fluoxetine (10-40 mg/kg), or vehicle controls before testing in a Morris water maze. Concurrent in vivo microdialysis measured extracellular dopamine and serotonin levels in prefrontal cortex and striatum during task performance. Additionally, autoradiographic analysis quantified D2 and 5-HT2A receptor density following chronic treatment protocols. Acute haloperidol impaired spatial learning in a dose-dependent manner, correlating with 60-75% reductions in striatal dopamine release. Chronic fluoxetine enhanced acquisition rates while increasing cortical serotonin by 180-220% and downregulating 5-HT2A receptors by 35%. Combined treatments revealed complex interactions between dopaminergic and serotonergic systems in mediating cognitive performance. These findings demonstrate that spatial navigation paradigms, coupled with neurochemical monitoring, provide sensitive measures for characterizing psychoactive drug effects. This integrated approach may facilitate more rational drug development strategies and improve our understanding of the neurobiological basis of psychiatric therapeutics.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Applying small quantities of multiple compounds to defined locations of in vitro brain slices.
abstract: Local application of pharmacological agents to specific brain regions in slice preparations has traditionally been limited by drug diffusion and imprecise targeting. We developed a novel microinjection system for simultaneous delivery of multiple compounds to defined anatomical locations within acute brain slices. The apparatus consists of borosilicate micropipettes (tip diameter 2-5 μm) mounted on independent micromanipulators, connected to a pressure-controlled delivery system capable of ejecting 10-100 picoliter volumes. Adult rat hippocampal slices (400 μm) were prepared using standard techniques and maintained in artificial cerebrospinal fluid. Fluorescent dyes (rhodamine, FITSC) were used to validate spatial precision and diffusion characteristics. We tested the system by applying GABA (100 μM), bicuculline (20 μM), and glutamate (50 μM) to CA1 pyramidal cell dendrites while recording from soma via whole-cell patch clamp. Confocal microscopy confirmed drug application within 50 μm radius of target sites with minimal cross-contamination between injection sites separated by >200 μm. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrated rapid onset of pharmacological effects (200-500 ms) with duration proportional to injection volume. Sequential application of antagonists effectively blocked agonist responses, confirming maintained drug potency. This method enables precise spatiotemporal control over multiple drug applications in brain slices, facilitating investigation of localized neurotransmitter actions and receptor interactions. The system provides superior spatial resolution compared to bath application while maintaining tissue viability and allowing reversible pharmacological manipulations essential for mechanistic studies of synaptic transmission and plasticity.

title: Current and Future Neuropharmacological Options for the Treatment of Essential Tremor.
abstract: Essential tremor (ET) is the most prevalent movement disorder, affecting approximately 1% of the global population and up to 5% of individuals over 65 years of age. Despite its high prevalence, current pharmacological treatments remain limited and often provide suboptimal symptom control. This comprehensive review examines the current neuropharmacological landscape for ET management and explores emerging therapeutic targets. Traditional first-line treatments include propranolol, a non-selective β-adrenergic antagonist, and primidone, an anticonvulsant, which demonstrate moderate efficacy in 50-70% of patients but are associated with significant side effects limiting long-term adherence. Second-line options such as topiramate, gabapentin, and benzodiazepines show variable effectiveness with considerable inter-individual variability. Recent mechanistic insights into ET pathophysiology have identified cerebellar dysfunction and altered GABAergic/glutamatergic neurotransmission as key therapeutic targets. Novel approaches under investigation include T-type calcium channel modulators, particularly zonisamide and 3,4-diaminopyridine, which have shown promising results in early-phase clinical trials. GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulators and metabotropic glutamate receptor antagonists represent additional emerging strategies. Furthermore, personalized medicine approaches utilizing pharmacogenomic profiling may optimize treatment selection and dosing strategies. Deep brain stimulation remains the most effective intervention for medication-refractory cases, though surgical risks limit widespread application. The integration of precision medicine principles with novel pharmacological targets offers considerable promise for improving therapeutic outcomes in ET patients, potentially transforming the treatment paradigm for this common yet undertreated neurological condition.

title: Neurochemical and physiological correlates of a critical period of respiratory development in the rat.
abstract: Respiratory control undergoes dramatic reorganization during early postnatal development, with disruptions during critical periods potentially leading to sudden infant death syndrome and other breathing disorders. This study investigated the neurochemical and physiological changes occurring during the critical period of respiratory development in Sprague-Dawley rats between postnatal days 10-14 (P10-P14). We recorded respiratory frequency, tidal volume, and hypoxic ventilatory responses using whole-body plethysmography in conscious pups. Brainstem sections from the pre-Bötzinger complex and rostral ventrolateral medulla were analyzed for neurotransmitter receptor expression using quantitative immunohistochemistry and in situ hybridization. GABA-A and glycine receptor subunit mRNA levels were measured by real-time PCR. Respiratory frequency decreased significantly from P10 to P14 (184±12 to 156±8 breaths/min, p<0.01), while tidal volume increased proportionally with body weight. Hypoxic ventilatory responses showed biphasic changes, with peak sensitivity at P12 followed by depression at P14. GABA-A receptor α1 subunit expression increased 2.3-fold between P10-P14 (p<0.001), while glycine receptor α1 subunits showed more modest increases (1.4-fold, p<0.05). Serotonin 2A receptor density decreased by 35% during this period (p<0.01). These findings demonstrate that the P10-P14 period represents a critical developmental window characterized by enhanced inhibitory neurotransmission and altered chemosensitivity in brainstem respiratory circuits. The coordinated changes in GABAergic inhibition and serotoninergic modulation may contribute to the vulnerability of respiratory control during early postnatal life.

title: Discrete neurochemical coding of distinguishable motivational processes: insights from nucleus accumbens control of feeding.
abstract: The idea that nucleus accumbens (Acb) dopamine transmission contributes to the neural mediation of reward, at least in a general sense, has achieved wide acceptance. Nevertheless, debate remains over the precise nature of dopamine's role in reward and even over the nature of reward itself. In the present article, evidence is reviewed from studies of food intake, feeding microstructure, instrumental responding for food reinforcement, and dopamine efflux associated with feeding, which suggests that reward processing in the Acb is best understood as an interaction among distinct processes coded by discrete neurotransmitter systems. In agreement with several theories of Acb dopamine function, it is proposed here that allocation of motor effort in seeking food or food-associated conditioned stimuli can be dissociated from computations relevant to the hedonic evaluation of food during the consummatory act. The former appears to depend upon Acb dopamine transmission and the latter upon striatal opioid peptide release. Moreover, dopamine transmission may play a role in 'stamping in' associations between motor acts and goal attainment and perhaps also neural representations corresponding to rewarding outcomes. Finally, evidence is reviewed that amino acid transmission specifically in the Acb shell acts as a central 'circuit breaker' to flexibly enable or terminate the consummatory act, via descending connections to hypothalamic feeding control systems. The heuristic framework outlined above may help explain why dopamine-compromising manipulations that strongly diminish instrumental goal-seeking behaviors leave consummatory activity relatively unaffected.

title: Lactate dehydrogenase release is facilitated by brief sonication of rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas following acute neuronal damage.
abstract: Assessment of neuronal viability in acute brain slice preparations remains a critical methodological challenge in neuropharmacological research. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release has emerged as a reliable marker of cellular damage, but traditional measurement approaches may underestimate tissue injury due to incomplete enzyme release from damaged cells. We investigated whether brief sonication enhances LDH release detection following acute neuronal damage in rat hippocampal slices and isolated retinas. Hippocampal slices (400 μm) and retinal preparations were obtained from adult Sprague-Dawley rats and subjected to oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD) for 30-120 minutes or excitotoxic injury using 100 μM NMDA. Following injury protocols, tissues were either processed immediately for LDH assay or subjected to 10-second sonication (40 kHz, 0.2 W/cm²) prior to enzyme measurement. Sonication significantly increased detectable LDH release by 2.3-fold in hippocampal slices and 1.8-fold in retinal preparations compared to non-sonicated controls after equivalent injury periods. The enhancement was most pronounced following mild to moderate injury conditions, where traditional methods detected minimal enzyme release. Dose-response relationships between injury duration and LDH release showed improved linearity following sonication treatment. Cell viability assessed by trypan blue exclusion confirmed that sonication parameters did not induce additional cellular damage in healthy tissue. These findings demonstrate that brief sonication represents a simple and effective method for enhancing LDH release detection sensitivity in neural tissue preparations, potentially improving the accuracy of neuronal viability assessments in neuropharmacological studies investigating neuroprotective compounds and injury mechanisms.

title: Targeted disruption of cocaine-activated nucleus accumbens neurons prevents context-specific sensitization.
abstract: The nucleus accumbens (NAc) plays a critical role in drug addiction, yet the specific neuronal populations mediating different aspects of cocaine-induced plasticity remain poorly defined. Context-specific sensitization, in which repeated cocaine exposure in a particular environment leads to enhanced locomotor responses upon re-exposure to that context, represents a key model of addiction-related learning. Here we used activity-dependent genetic targeting to selectively ablate cocaine-activated NAc neurons and examine their contribution to sensitization. We employed transgenic mice expressing diphtheria toxin receptor under control of the immediate early gene c-fos promoter, allowing targeted elimination of neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure. Mice received cocaine (15 mg/kg, i.p.) or saline in distinct environmental contexts for five consecutive days, followed by diphtheria toxin administration to ablate c-fos-expressing NAc neurons. After a 10-day withdrawal period, animals were challenged with cocaine in both the drug-paired and unpaired contexts. Control mice exhibited robust context-specific sensitization, displaying significantly enhanced locomotor activity when cocaine was administered in the previously drug-paired environment compared to the unpaired context (2.8-fold vs 1.4-fold increase over saline baseline, p<0.001). In contrast, mice with targeted ablation of cocaine-activated NAc neurons showed equivalent, non-sensitized responses in both contexts, with locomotor activity comparable to drug-naive animals. Importantly, acute cocaine responses remained intact, indicating preserved basic drug sensitivity. These findings demonstrate that NAc neurons activated during initial cocaine exposure form a stable cellular engram essential for context-dependent sensitization, providing direct evidence for ensemble-specific encoding of addiction-related memories.

title: The psychopharmacology algorithm project at the Harvard South Shore Program: An algorithm for core symptoms of autism spectrum disorder in adults.
abstract:
**Background:** Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) affects approximately 1-2% of adults, yet evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions for core symptoms remain limited. Current treatment approaches often rely on off-label use of medications targeting associated comorbidities rather than core ASD symptomatology. The Harvard South Shore Program developed a systematic algorithm to guide clinicians in medication selection for core ASD symptoms in adults.

**Methods:** We conducted a retrospective analysis of 147 adults with ASD (mean age 28.4 ± 8.2 years, 68% male) treated using our standardized algorithm from 2017-2019. The algorithm prioritized selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) for repetitive behaviors and social communication deficits, followed by atypical antipsychotics for severe behavioral symptoms, and adjunctive treatments including oxytocin and N-acetylcysteine. Primary outcomes were measured using the Social Responsiveness Scale-2 (SRS-2) and Repetitive Behavior Scale-Revised (RBS-R) at baseline, 12, and 24 weeks.

**Results:** Algorithm implementation resulted in significant improvements in SRS-2 total scores (baseline 76.3 ± 12.1 vs. 24 weeks 68.9 ± 14.2, p<0.001) and RBS-R scores (baseline 42.7 ± 18.3 vs. 24 weeks 35.1 ± 16.8, p=0.003). Sertraline was most frequently prescribed (n=89, 61%), followed by risperidone (n=34, 23%). Treatment response rates were 58% for social communication symptoms and 52% for repetitive behaviors. Discontinuation due to adverse effects occurred in 18% of participants.

**Conclusions:** A systematic psychopharmacological algorithm demonstrated measurable improvements in core ASD symptoms among adults. These findings support the potential efficacy of structured medication approaches, though randomized controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.

title: Dynamic of neurochemical alterations in striatum, hippocampus and cortex after the 6-OHDA mesostriatal lesion.
abstract: Parkinson's disease is characterized by progressive degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to striatal dopamine depletion and widespread neurochemical alterations. The 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA) lesion model provides a valuable tool for investigating the temporal dynamics of neurochemical changes following dopaminergic denervation. We examined the time-course of neurotransmitter alterations in striatum, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex following unilateral 6-OHDA injection into the medial forebrain bundle of adult male Sprague-Dawley rats. Animals were sacrificed at 3, 7, 14, and 28 days post-lesion, and brain tissues were analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography for dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, and their metabolites. Tyrosine hydroxylase immunohistochemistry confirmed lesion extent. In the ipsilateral striatum, dopamine levels decreased by 85% at 3 days and remained depleted throughout the study period, while DOPAC and HVA showed initial increases followed by sustained reductions. Norepinephrine and serotonin exhibited compensatory increases peaking at 7-14 days post-lesion. In the hippocampus, modest but significant reductions in dopamine and norepinephrine emerged by 14 days, accompanied by elevated serotonin turnover. The prefrontal cortex showed delayed dopamine depletion (40% reduction at 28 days) and increased noradrenergic activity. These findings demonstrate that mesostriatal 6-OHDA lesions trigger complex, time-dependent neurochemical adaptations extending beyond the primary target region, involving multiple neurotransmitter systems across interconnected brain regions. Understanding these dynamic changes provides insight into the pathophysiology of Parkinson's disease and potential therapeutic targets for neuroprotective interventions.
journal: International journal of developmental neuroscience : the official journal of the International Society for Developmental Neuroscience

title: New treatment strategies in multiple sclerosis.
abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system affecting over 2.5 million people worldwide. Current disease-modifying therapies primarily target relapsing-remitting disease but show limited efficacy in progressive forms. This study investigated novel neuroprotective and remyelination-promoting strategies using experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) models in C57BL/6 mice. We evaluated three therapeutic approaches: (1) combination therapy with fingolimod (0.3 mg/kg) and the AMPA receptor antagonist perampanel (2 mg/kg), (2) intrathecal delivery of oligodendrocyte progenitor cells (OPCs) transduced with brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), and (3) oral administration of the sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator FTY720 combined with low-dose methylprednisolone. Clinical disability scores, magnetic resonance imaging, histopathological analysis, and electrophysiological measurements were assessed over 12 weeks post-immunization. The fingolimod-perampanel combination significantly reduced clinical severity scores by 40% compared to vehicle controls (p<0.001) and preserved myelin basic protein expression in spinal cord white matter. BDNF-transduced OPC therapy promoted remyelination, evidenced by increased oligodendrocyte marker expression and restored compound action potential conduction velocities. The FTY720-corticosteroid regimen demonstrated superior anti-inflammatory effects with reduced microglial activation and decreased pro-inflammatory cytokine levels. These findings suggest that combination neuroprotective strategies targeting both inflammatory and degenerative pathways may offer enhanced therapeutic benefit over current monotherapies, particularly for addressing the progressive component of MS pathology.

title: Attention: oscillations and neuropharmacology.
abstract: Attention-related neural oscillations in the gamma frequency range (30-100 Hz) have been implicated in selective attention and conscious perception, yet their underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. We investigated the effects of GABAergic and cholinergic modulation on gamma oscillations during visual attention tasks in awake, behaving macaque monkeys. Local field potentials were recorded from area V4 while animals performed a cued spatial attention task. Iontophoretic application of the GABA_A antagonist bicuculline (10-50 nA) significantly increased gamma power (147 ± 23% of baseline, p < 0.001) and enhanced attentional modulation of neuronal responses by 34 ± 12%. Conversely, muscimol (GABA_A agonist, 20-80 nA) suppressed gamma oscillations (62 ± 18% of baseline, p < 0.01) and reduced attention-related firing rate modulation. The acetylcholinesterase inhibitor physostigmine (15-40 nA) enhanced gamma synchronization during attended conditions (128 ± 19% of baseline, p < 0.05) and improved behavioral performance on attention-demanding trials. The nicotinic receptor antagonist mecamylamine (25-60 nA) disrupted attention-related gamma enhancement and impaired discrimination accuracy. Cross-correlation analysis revealed that pharmacologically induced changes in gamma power correlated strongly with attentional performance (r = 0.73, p < 0.001). These findings demonstrate that gamma oscillations underlying visual attention depend critically on the balance between GABAergic inhibition and cholinergic excitation. Our results suggest that attention-related gamma rhythms emerge from local GABAergic interneuron networks modulated by ascending cholinergic projections, providing a neurochemical framework for understanding attentional dysfunction in neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: New treatments for ocular hypertension.
abstract: Ocular hypertension remains a significant risk factor for the development of primary open-angle glaucoma, necessitating effective therapeutic interventions to reduce intraocular pressure (IOP). Current first-line treatments, including topical beta-blockers and prostaglandin analogs, demonstrate limitations in efficacy and tolerability in certain patient populations. This study investigated the ocular hypotensive effects of a novel dual-action compound, NK-347, which combines selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonism with nitric oxide donation properties. Twenty-four New Zealand white rabbits were randomized to receive topical NK-347 (0.1%, 0.2%, or 0.5%), timolol 0.5%, or vehicle control twice daily for 14 days. IOP measurements were obtained using Goldmann applanation tonometry at baseline and at 2, 4, 6, and 8 hours post-dosing on days 1, 7, and 14. Aqueous humor dynamics were assessed using fluorophotometry, and autonomic innervation of ciliary tissues was evaluated through immunohistochemistry for tyrosine hydroxylase and neuronal nitric oxide synthase. NK-347 0.2% and 0.5% demonstrated significant IOP reduction compared to vehicle (p<0.001), with maximal effects observed at 4 hours post-dosing (mean reduction: 6.2±1.4 mmHg and 8.1±1.8 mmHg, respectively). The compound enhanced aqueous humor outflow facility by 34% (p<0.01) and reduced aqueous humor production by 18% (p<0.05). Immunohistochemical analysis revealed preserved sympathetic and nitrergic innervation patterns. These findings suggest NK-347 represents a promising therapeutic approach for ocular hypertension management through dual mechanisms targeting both aqueous humor dynamics and vascular regulation.

title: Team Evans and Watkins: Excitatory Amino Acid research at Bristol University 1973-1981.
abstract: Between 1973 and 1981, the research teams led by Evans and Watkins at Bristol University made seminal contributions to understanding excitatory amino acid neurotransmission, fundamentally shaping modern neuropharmacology. This historical review examines their pioneering work identifying glutamate receptors and establishing the pharmacological framework that underlies contemporary NMDA, AMPA, and kainate receptor classification. The Bristol group's systematic investigation of L-glutamate and L-aspartate as excitatory neurotransmitters began with electrophysiological studies on spinal neurons, demonstrating that these amino acids produced dose-dependent depolarization distinct from other neurotransmitter systems. Their development of selective antagonists, including 2-amino-5-phosphonovalerate (AP5) and 6,7-dinitroquinoxaline-2,3-dione (DNQX), provided crucial pharmacological tools for dissecting glutamatergic transmission. Subsequent binding studies and receptor autoradiography revealed heterogeneous glutamate binding sites throughout the central nervous system, with particularly high densities in hippocampus, cerebral cortex, and cerebellum. The team's characterization of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) as a selective agonist and their discovery of the glycine modulatory site established the foundation for understanding NMDA receptor function in synaptic plasticity, learning, and excitotoxicity. These discoveries directly influenced the development of neuroprotective strategies for stroke and neurodegenerative diseases, while their receptor classification system remains the standard framework for glutamate pharmacology. The Evans-Watkins collaboration exemplifies how systematic neuropharmacological research can transform our understanding of brain function and provide therapeutic targets for neurological disorders.

title: Validation of a fluorescence-based high-throughput assay for the measurement of neurotransmitter transporter uptake activity.
abstract: Neurotransmitter transporters play critical roles in synaptic transmission by regulating extracellular concentrations of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine. Current methods for measuring transporter uptake activity, including radioligand binding assays and electrophysiological techniques, are often labor-intensive and unsuitable for high-throughput screening applications. We developed and validated a fluorescence-based assay using the fluorescent substrate 4-(4-(dimethylamino)styryl)-N-methylpyridinium (ASP+) to quantify uptake activity of the dopamine transporter (DAT), serotonin transporter (SERT), and norepinephrine transporter (NET) in a 96-well plate format. Human embryonic kidney 293 cells stably transfected with individual transporters were incubated with ASP+ (5 μM) for 30 minutes at 37°C, and fluorescence accumulation was measured using a microplate reader (excitation 485 nm, emission 590 nm). The assay demonstrated excellent linearity (r² > 0.95) across a range of cell densities and incubation times. Kinetic analysis revealed apparent Km values of 8.2 ± 1.4 μM for DAT, 12.1 ± 2.1 μM for SERT, and 9.7 ± 1.8 μM for NET. Selective inhibitors including GBR12909, fluoxetine, and desipramine produced concentration-dependent inhibition with IC50 values consistent with literature reports. The assay showed high reproducibility (CV < 10%) and was successfully used to screen a library of 480 compounds, identifying novel transporter modulators. This fluorescence-based method provides a robust, cost-effective alternative to radioligand assays for neurotransmitter transporter research and drug discovery applications.

title: How human electrophysiology informs psychopharmacology: from bottom-up driven processing to top-down control.
abstract: Recent advances in human electrophysiology have provided unprecedented insights into the neural mechanisms underlying psychoactive drug effects, bridging the gap between preclinical models and clinical outcomes. Understanding how pharmacological interventions modulate both bottom-up sensory processing and top-down cognitive control represents a critical frontier in neuropsychopharmacology. We reviewed electrophysiological studies employing event-related potentials (ERPs) and oscillatory measures to characterize drug effects across multiple neurotransmitter systems. A comprehensive analysis of 127 studies published between 2005-2010 examined how psychoactive compounds alter early sensory components (P50, N100, P300) reflecting bottom-up processing, and later cognitive components (N200, P300, error-related negativity) indexing top-down control mechanisms. Results demonstrate that dopaminergic agents primarily enhance early sensory gating and P300 amplitude, while GABAergic compounds attenuate both early and late ERP components in a dose-dependent manner.
