Glutamatergic modulators, particularly NMDA antagonists, selectively impair mismatch negativity and gamma oscillations without affecting primary sensory responses. Cholinergic enhancement selectively improved top-down attentional control as indexed by enhanced P300 and reduced distractor-related N200. Importantly, individual differences in baseline electrophysiological measures predicted treatment response magnitude across multiple drug classes. These findings establish human electrophysiology as a translational bridge between molecular pharmacology and behavioral outcomes, providing mechanistic insights into bottom-up versus top-down drug effects. Such approaches may facilitate more precise medication selection and dosing strategies in psychiatric and neurological disorders by identifying electrophysiological biomarkers of drug response.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Stereochemistry in clinical medicine: a neurological perspective.
abstract: Stereochemistry plays a crucial role in neuropharmacology, yet its clinical implications remain incompletely understood in neurological practice. Many neurotropic medications exist as stereoisomers with potentially different pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties. We conducted a comprehensive review of stereochemical considerations in commonly prescribed neurological medications and analyzed clinical outcomes data from 847 patients treated at our tertiary neurology center between 1998-2002. We examined racemic versus enantiopure formulations of key drug classes including anticonvulsants, anti-parkinsonian agents, and psychotropic medications, correlating stereochemical properties with therapeutic efficacy and adverse event profiles. Our analysis revealed significant differences in clinical outcomes between stereoisomeric formulations. S-citalopram demonstrated superior efficacy compared to racemic citalopram in treating post-stroke depression (response rate 73% vs 58%, p<0.05). Levodopa combined with carbidopa showed enhanced bioavailability when the L-enantiomer was used exclusively versus racemic preparations. Among anticonvulsants, the S-enantiomer of phenytoin exhibited reduced cognitive side effects while maintaining seizure control in 89% of patients switched from racemic formulations. Pharmacokinetic modeling revealed that hepatic metabolism, protein binding, and blood-brain barrier penetration varied significantly between enantiomers of the same compound. These findings suggest that stereochemical optimization of neurotropic medications could improve therapeutic outcomes while minimizing adverse effects. Greater attention to stereochemistry in drug development and clinical practice may enhance precision in neurological therapeutics, though larger controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.
journal: Journal of clinical neuroscience : official journal of the Neurosurgical Society of Australasia

title: Quantitative analysis of neurochemical panel in rat brain and plasma by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry.
abstract: The dopamine, serotonin, and kynurenine metabolic pathways play pivotal roles on brain function, and their disturbances are closely related to various neurological diseases. Comprehensive measurements of these metabolites is thus essential for monitoring the global neurochemical responses to pathological challenges or drug intervention. However, simultaneous measurement of various neurochemcial metabolites represents a great challenge. We developed herein an original and feasible method using high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). A chemical derivatization approach using benzoyl chloride (benzoylation) was developed to achieve better chromatographic behavior and mass-detecting sensitivity. The developed method enables a rapid quantification of 11 metabolites spanning dopamine, serotonin, and kynurenine metabolic pathways within 10.5 min. With this method, we were able to simultaneously monitor inflammation induced alternations of all these metabolites in a rat brain and in particular their dynamics in plasma matrix. The balance between the serotonin and kynurenine branches of tryptophan metabolism was disrupted by lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammation, characterized with the overproduction of neurotoxic metabolite 3-hydroxykynurenine and decreased levels of serotonin. The measured levels of this panel of neurotransimtters ranged from 4.3 ng to 10.6 μg per gram of brain tissue. All these results suggest that the presently developed method is sufficiently sensitive and robust to simultaneously monitor a large panel of metabolites with diverse properties and a large range of concentration differences. Therefore, this method will be expected to be highly useful for comprehensive studies of the pathophysiological roles and mechanisms of these critical neurotransmitters.

title: Linear quantitation of Abeta aggregation using Thioflavin T: reduction in fibril formation by colostrinin.
abstract: Thioflavin T (ThT) fluorescence is a commonly used method to monitor Abeta protein fibril formation. This method is particularly attractive since ThT fluoresces only when bound to fibrils, the reaction is completed within 1min and ThT does not interfere with aggregation of Abeta fibrils. One of the drawbacks of this method is the lack of a strict quantitative relationship between ThT fluorescence and fibril content. It was observed that, when the same gram molecular weight of Abeta (1-40) is dissolved into varying amounts of base then placed into a constant volume of aqueous buffer, a non-linear fluorescent response is obtained. By maintaining a strict relationship between Abeta content and the volume of base, this anomalous result can be alleviated and a linear dose response curve is obtained at much lower Abeta concentrations than is typically observed. In addition, differences in Abeta batch to batch preparations are alleviated. It was previously reported that colostrinin (CLN), a proline-rich peptide derived from colostrum, reduces fibril content and protects neuroblastoma cells against Abeta peptide-induced toxicity. The newly developed ThT fluorescence protocol was used to quantify Abeta fibril content after treatment with CLN. We also demonstrate that CLN, can solubilize Abeta fibrils in a dose and time-dependent fashion.

title: PET Neurochemical Imaging Modes.
abstract: Positron emission tomography (PET) has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating neurochemical processes in vivo, offering unprecedented insights into neurotransmitter systems and their alterations in neurological and psychiatric disorders. This comprehensive review examines current PET neurochemical imaging approaches and their clinical applications. We analyzed literature from 2010-2015 encompassing major neurotransmitter systems including dopaminergic, serotonergic, cholinergic, and GABAergic pathways, evaluating radiotracer development, imaging protocols, and quantitative methodologies. Key dopaminergic tracers such as [11C]raclopride and [18F]DOPA continue to provide robust measures of receptor availability and presynaptic function, while newer compounds like [11C]PE2I offer improved specificity for dopamine transporter imaging. Serotonergic imaging has advanced significantly with [11C]DASB for serotonin transporter quantification and [18F]altanserin for 5-HT2A receptor studies. Cholinergic system evaluation benefits from [18F]fluorobenzovesamicol for vesicular acetylcholine transporter imaging, particularly valuable in dementia research. GABAergic imaging remains challenging, though [11C]flumazenil provides reliable benzodiazepine receptor measurements. Quantitative analysis methods have evolved from simple standardized uptake values to sophisticated kinetic modeling approaches using arterial input functions and reference tissue methods. Recent developments in partial volume correction and motion compensation have enhanced accuracy. PET neurochemical imaging continues to expand our understanding of brain chemistry in health and disease, with emerging tracers for glutamatergic, histaminergic, and cannabinoid systems promising further advances in translational neuroscience research and drug development.

title: Neurochemical insights into the radiation protection of astronauts: Distinction between low- and moderate-LET radiation components.
abstract: Radiation protection of astronauts remains an ongoing challenge in preparation of deep space exploratory missions. Exposure to space radiation consisting of multiple radiation components is associated with a significant risk of experiencing central nervous system (CNS) detriments, potentially influencing the crew operational decisions. Developing of countermeasures protecting CNS from the deleterious exposure requires understanding the mechanistic nature of cognitive impairments induced by different components of space radiation. The current study was designed to identify differences in neurochemical modifications caused by exposure to low- and moderate-LET radiations and to elucidate a distinction between the observed outcomes. We exposed rats to accelerated protons (170 MeV; 0.5 keV/μm) or to carbon ions (12C; 500 MeV/u; 10.5 keV/μm) delivered at the same dose of 1 Gy. Neurochemical alterations were evaluated 1, 30, and 90 days after exposure via indices of the monoamine metabolism measured in five brain structures, including prefrontal cortex, hypothalamus, nucleus accumbens, hippocampus and striatum. We obtained the detailed patterns of neurochemical modifications after exposure to the mentioned radiation modalities. Our data show that the enhancement in the radiation LET from relatively low to moderate values leads to different neurochemical outcomes and that a particular effect depends on the irradiated brain structure. We also hypothesized that exposure to the moderate-LET radiations can induce a hyperactivation of feedback neurochemical mechanisms, which blur metabolic deviations and lead to the delayed impairments in brain functions. Based on our findings we discuss possible contribution of the observed changes to behavioural impairments.
journal: Physica medica : PM : an international journal devoted to the applications of physics to medicine and biology : official journal of the Italian Association of Biomedical Physics (AIFB)

title: Clinical practice with anti-dementia drugs: A revised (third) consensus statement from the British Association for Psychopharmacology.
abstract: The British Association for Psychopharmacology convened an expert panel to update evidence-based recommendations for anti-dementia drug prescribing, following significant developments in clinical practice since the previous 2011 consensus. A multidisciplinary group of clinicians and researchers systematically reviewed recent literature on cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and memantine, alongside emerging evidence on combination therapies and long-term treatment strategies. The panel evaluated randomized controlled trials, real-world effectiveness studies, and safety data published between 2011-2017, with particular attention to patient selection criteria, treatment optimization, and discontinuation practices. Updated recommendations emphasize earlier initiation of cholinesterase inhibitors in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease, with donepezil remaining first-line treatment due to superior tolerability and adherence. Memantine should be considered as add-on therapy in moderate disease or as monotherapy when cholinesterase inhibitors are contraindicated. New evidence supports continuing treatment into severe dementia stages, challenging previous recommendations for discontinuation. The panel identified improved cognitive and functional outcomes with optimized dosing regimens and highlighted the importance of regular monitoring for therapeutic response and adverse effects. Combination therapy with cholinesterase inhibitors plus memantine showed modest additional benefits in moderate-to-severe disease. Despite limited disease-modifying effects, current anti-dementia drugs provide clinically meaningful symptomatic benefits when appropriately prescribed. These revised guidelines emphasize individualized treatment approaches, incorporating patient preferences and comorbidities, while acknowledging the continued need for novel therapeutic approaches targeting underlying disease mechanisms in dementia.

title: Physical predictors, behavioural/emotional attributes and neurochemical determinants of dominant behaviour.
abstract: Significant differences in physical and behavioural/emotional/cognitive predictors and attributes, as well as of neurochemical inducers of behaviour, between dominant and subordinate animals are discussed. It is still unknown whether these factors are the causes of differences between dominants and subordinates, or vice versa whether the differences between dominants and subordinates are the origin of differences in these factors. The possibility is discussed that no differences exist among juveniles in the concentrations of neurochemical agents (known in the literature as determinants of dominance) between the brains of future dominants and future subordinates. We describe a study design that makes the assessment of the 'original' neurochemical profile of the brain possible.
journal: Biological reviews of the Cambridge Philosophical Society

title: Psychopharmacological possibilities in the acute disaster setting.
abstract: The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks and recent natural disasters have highlighted the urgent need for evidence-based psychopharmacological interventions in acute disaster settings. While psychological first aid remains the primary intervention, certain populations may benefit from targeted pharmacological support to prevent the development of chronic psychiatric sequelae. This review examines the current literature on acute pharmacological interventions following traumatic exposure and their potential applications in mass casualty events. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases from 1990-2004, focusing on randomized controlled trials and case series examining pharmacological interventions within 30 days of trauma exposure. Studies were evaluated for methodological quality and clinical applicability in disaster scenarios. Limited but promising evidence supports the use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, particularly sertraline and paroxetine, in preventing post-traumatic stress disorder when initiated within 72 hours of exposure in high-risk individuals. Beta-blockers, specifically propranolol, show potential for disrupting traumatic memory consolidation when administered within 6 hours of the event, though replication studies are needed. Short-term benzodiazepine use may provide symptomatic relief but should be limited to 2-3 days due to concerns about dependence and potential interference with natural recovery processes. Atypical antipsychotics may be beneficial for individuals experiencing acute psychotic symptoms or severe agitation. While pharmacological interventions should not replace psychological support, targeted use of specific agents in carefully selected populations may reduce the risk of chronic psychiatric morbidity following mass traumatic events.
journal: The Psychiatric clinics of North America

title: Gaddum and LSD: the birth and growth of experimental and clinical neuropharmacology research on 5-HT in the UK.
abstract: The discovery of lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) and John Henry Gaddum's pioneering work in the 1950s fundamentally transformed our understanding of serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) neuropharmacology in the United Kingdom. This historical review examines the development of 5-HT research from Gaddum's initial observations of LSD's antagonistic effects on smooth muscle preparations through to the establishment of modern clinical neuropharmacology programmes. We analysed primary literature, laboratory records, and correspondence from key British institutions including the National Institute for Medical Research, Edinburgh University, and the Medical Research Council between 1953-1980. Gaddum's seminal 1953 paper demonstrating LSD's ability to block 5-HT responses in isolated tissue preparations provided the first evidence linking indoleamine neurotransmission to psychoactive drug effects. This work catalysed subsequent investigations by Brodie, Woolley, and Shaw, leading to the identification of 5-HT in mammalian brain tissue and the development of the first selective serotonergic ligands. The establishment of the MRC Neuropsychopharmacology Unit in 1961 under Gaddum's influence created a framework for systematic clinical studies of 5-HT function in psychiatric disorders. By 1975, British researchers had characterised multiple 5-HT receptor subtypes, developed novel antidepressant compounds, and established serotonin's role in mood regulation. Gaddum's initial LSD studies represent a pivotal moment in neuropharmacology, demonstrating how serendipitous observations of psychedelic compounds can illuminate fundamental neurotransmitter mechanisms and drive therapeutic innovation in psychiatry and neurology.

title: The psychopharmacology of brain vascular disease/poststroke depression.
abstract: Poststroke depression (PSD) affects approximately 30% of stroke survivors and represents a complex neuropsychiatric condition involving disrupted neural circuits, neurotransmitter imbalances, and inflammatory processes. While traditional antidepressants show variable efficacy in PSD, the underlying cerebrovascular pathology may alter pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties of psychotropic medications. This review examines the current evidence for psychopharmacological interventions in PSD and their mechanisms of action in the context of compromised cerebral vasculature. We conducted a systematic analysis of randomized controlled trials and observational studies published between 2010-2018, focusing on selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and novel therapeutic approaches in stroke patients with depression. Seventeen studies encompassing 2,847 patients were analyzed. SSRIs, particularly escitalopram and sertraline, demonstrated superior efficacy compared to placebo (effect size 0.62, 95% CI 0.44-0.80, p<0.001) with response rates of 65-72% versus 35-42% for placebo. Importantly, early initiation within 3 months poststroke showed enhanced treatment response and potential neuroprotective effects. Tricyclic antidepressants showed comparable efficacy but increased cardiovascular risk in this vulnerable population. Emerging evidence suggests that antidepressants may promote neuroplasticity through BDNF upregulation and reduce neuroinflammation via cytokine modulation. Blood-brain barrier disruption following stroke may enhance drug penetration but also increase susceptibility to adverse effects. These findings support early, targeted psychopharmacological intervention in PSD, with SSRIs as first-line therapy, while highlighting the need for personalized treatment approaches considering individual cerebrovascular risk profiles.

title: A primer on confidence intervals in psychopharmacology.
abstract: The interpretation of clinical trial results in psychopharmacology has traditionally relied heavily on p-values and statistical significance testing, often leading to oversimplified binary conclusions about treatment efficacy. Confidence intervals (CIs) provide a more nuanced approach to data interpretation by estimating the range of plausible values for treatment effects, yet their application remains underutilized in psychiatric research. This primer examines the theoretical foundations and practical applications of confidence intervals in psychopharmacological studies. We reviewed methodological approaches for constructing and interpreting CIs across common study designs in psychopharmacology, including randomized controlled trials, meta-analyses, and dose-response studies. Using examples from recent antidepressant and antipsychotic trials, we demonstrate how CIs can enhance interpretation of treatment effects beyond traditional significance testing. Key applications include assessing clinical meaningfulness of observed differences, evaluating equivalence and non-inferiority in comparative effectiveness research, and improving transparency in meta-analytic syntheses. Our analysis reveals that CIs provide crucial information about precision of estimates and potential clinical significance that p-values alone cannot convey. For instance, a statistically non-significant result may still indicate clinically meaningful benefit when CI bounds encompass relevant effect sizes. Furthermore, CIs facilitate better communication of uncertainty to clinicians and patients, supporting evidence-based decision-making. We conclude that routine reporting and interpretation of confidence intervals alongside traditional statistical measures would substantially improve the quality and clinical utility of psychopharmacological research. Training programs should emphasize CI interpretation to enhance statistical literacy among psychiatric researchers and clinicians.

title: Carbon nanofiber electrode for neurochemical monitoring.
abstract: Real-time monitoring of neurochemical dynamics in the central nervous system remains a significant challenge due to limitations in electrode sensitivity, selectivity, and biocompatibility. Traditional carbon fiber microelectrodes, while widely used for fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV), exhibit suboptimal signal-to-noise ratios and limited detection capabilities for low-concentration neurotransmitters. Here we report the development and characterization of novel carbon nanofiber (CNF) electrodes for enhanced neurochemical detection. CNF electrodes were fabricated by chemical vapor deposition of carbon onto tungsten substrates, resulting in highly ordered nanostructured surfaces with increased electroactive area. Electrochemical characterization using cyclic voltammetry revealed 3.2-fold greater sensitivity for dopamine detection compared to conventional carbon fiber electrodes, with detection limits of 15 nM. In vitro validation demonstrated selective detection of dopamine in the presence of common interferents including ascorbic acid and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid. CNF electrodes exhibited stable performance over 200 consecutive voltammetric scans with less than 5% signal degradation. In vivo microdialysis experiments in anesthetized rats showed successful real-time monitoring of stimulated dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens following electrical stimulation of the medial forebrain bundle. The enhanced sensitivity enabled detection of pharmacologically-induced changes in extracellular dopamine concentrations as low as 25 nM following quinpirole administration. These findings demonstrate that CNF electrodes represent a significant advancement in neurochemical sensing technology, offering improved sensitivity and temporal resolution for investigating neurotransmitter dynamics in both physiological and pathological conditions.

title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
abstract: The synthesis of novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline derivatives was undertaken to explore their potential as central nervous system active compounds. Tetrahydroisoquinoline scaffolds have demonstrated significant pharmacological activity at dopaminergic and adrenergic receptors, warranting investigation of structurally related analogues. A series of twenty-three compounds was synthesized via Pictet-Spengler cyclization reactions starting from appropriately substituted phenethylamines and aldehydes, followed by phenolic coupling reactions to generate the chromeno-fused derivatives. All compounds were characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and mass spectrometry. Pharmacological evaluation included radioligand binding assays at dopamine D1 and D2 receptors, α1- and α2-adrenergic receptors, and serotonin 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors using rat brain membranes. Functional activity was assessed using cAMP accumulation assays and calcium mobilization studies. Several 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives exhibited moderate to high affinity for dopamine D2 receptors (Ki = 45-180 nM), with compound 7c showing selectivity over D1 receptors (>50-fold). The chromeno-fused analogues demonstrated preferential binding to α1-adrenergic receptors, with compound 12b displaying nanomolar affinity (Ki = 32 nM). Functional studies revealed that most active compounds behaved as partial agonists or antagonists at their respective targets. These results suggest that 4-phenoxy-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives represent promising lead compounds for the development of selective dopaminergic agents, while the chromeno-fused derivatives may serve as α1-adrenergic receptor modulators with potential cardiovascular applications.

title: Glutamate-based antidepressants: preclinical psychopharmacology.
abstract: Over the past 20 years, converging lines of evidence have both linked glutamatergic dysfunction to the pathophysiology of depression and demonstrated that the glutamatergic synapse presents multiple targets for developing novel antidepressants. The robust antidepressant effects of the N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonists ketamine and traxoprodil provide target validation for this family of ionotropic glutamate receptors. This article reviews the preclinical evidence that it may be possible to develop glutamate-based antidepressants by not only modulating ionotropic (N-methyl-D-aspartate and alpha-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazole-propionic acid) and metabotropic glutamate (mGlu) receptors, including mGlu2/3, mGLu5 and mGlu7 receptors, but also by altering synaptic concentrations of glutamate via specialized transporters such as glial glutamate transporter 1 (excitatory amino-acid transporter 2).

title: Pharmacotherapy of mental illness--a historical analysis.
abstract: The treatment of mental illness has evolved dramatically over the past century, with major paradigm shifts occurring in our understanding of neurobiological mechanisms and therapeutic approaches. This historical analysis examines the development of psychopharmacological interventions from the pre-pharmacological era through contemporary practice. We conducted a comprehensive review of psychiatric treatment modalities from 1900-2000, analyzing primary literature, clinical trial data, and epidemiological studies to trace the evolution of mental health pharmacotherapy. The pre-1950 era was characterized by non-specific treatments including barbiturates, insulin coma therapy, and early electroconvulsive approaches. The discovery of chlorpromazine in 1952 marked the beginning of modern psychopharmacology, leading to the development of phenothiazines and the first effective antipsychotic medications. The 1950s-1960s witnessed the introduction of tricyclic antidepressants and benzodiazepines, fundamentally altering treatment approaches for mood and anxiety disorders. The 1980s-1990s brought selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and atypical antipsychotics, offering improved efficacy and tolerability profiles. Our analysis reveals three distinct phases: the empirical period (1900-1950), characterized by trial-and-error approaches; the serendipitous discovery phase (1950-1980), marked by accidental findings leading to major therapeutic breakthroughs; and the rational drug design era (1980-present), driven by advancing neurobiological understanding. This historical perspective demonstrates the critical importance of continued neuroscience research in developing more effective and targeted psychiatric medications, while highlighting persistent challenges in treatment-resistant conditions and the need for personalized therapeutic approaches.

title: Bibliometric analysis of biomedical publications on SSRI during 1980-2000.
abstract: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have emerged as first-line pharmacotherapy for depression and anxiety disorders since the introduction of fluoxetine in the late 1980s. However, a comprehensive analysis of the scientific literature documenting SSRI research development has not been conducted. This bibliometric study examined patterns of biomedical publications on SSRIs from 1980 to 2000 to characterize research trends and identify key areas of investigation. We searched MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases using standardized search terms for fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine, fluvoxamine, and citalopram. Publications were categorized by year, journal, study type, therapeutic indication, and research focus. A total of 4,267 SSRI-related publications were identified, with exponential growth from 12 papers in 1980 to 587 in 2000. The greatest publication volume occurred during 1995-1998, coinciding with widespread clinical adoption. Major depression represented 42% of therapeutic focus, followed by obsessive-compulsive disorder (18%) and panic disorder (14%). Clinical trials comprised 35% of publications, while basic pharmacology and mechanism studies accounted for 28%. The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry, Archives of General Psychiatry, and Biological Psychiatry published the highest number of SSRI studies. International collaboration increased significantly after 1992, with European contributions rising from 15% to 38% by 2000. This analysis demonstrates the rapid evolution of SSRI research parallel to clinical implementation, with sustained focus on efficacy, safety, and mechanism of action studies that established the foundation for evidence-based psychopharmacology.

title: In vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry of central serotonin.
abstract: Serotonin (5-HT) plays crucial roles in mood regulation, sleep-wake cycles, and cognitive function, yet real-time monitoring of central serotonergic neurotransmission in living brain tissue remains technically challenging. Traditional microdialysis approaches suffer from poor temporal resolution and tissue damage, while existing fluorometric methods require exogenous fluorophores that may alter normal physiology. We developed a novel in vivo autofluorescence spectrofluorometry technique to directly monitor endogenous serotonin dynamics in the rat brain without chemical modification. Using a custom-built fiber-optic spectrofluorometer with excitation at 295 nm and emission detection at 340 nm, we implanted 200-μm diameter optical fibers into the dorsal raphe nucleus and hippocampus of anesthetized Sprague-Dawley rats (n=12). The system achieved 1-second temporal resolution with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 15:1. Pharmacological validation demonstrated that autofluorescence signals increased 180±25% following administration of the serotonin reuptake inhibitor fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, i.p.) and decreased 65±12% after depletion with para-chlorophenylalanine (300 mg/kg, i.p., 48h pretreatment). Electrical stimulation of the median raphe nucleus (20 Hz, 1 min) produced rapid, reproducible increases in hippocampal autofluorescence (peak amplitude 45±8% above baseline). Control experiments using 5,7-dihydroxytryptamine lesions confirmed signal specificity for serotonergic transmission. This minimally invasive technique provides unprecedented temporal resolution for monitoring central serotonin dynamics and offers significant advantages for investigating serotonergic mechanisms underlying behavior and psychopathology in real-time experimental paradigms.

title: Building a virtual archive using brain architecture and Web 3D to deliver neuropsychopharmacology content over the Internet.
abstract: The exponential growth of neuropsychopharmacology data necessitates innovative approaches for knowledge dissemination and educational content delivery. Traditional static resources fail to adequately represent the complex three-dimensional relationships between brain structures, neurotransmitter systems, and pharmacological interventions. We developed a novel virtual archive platform that integrates detailed brain architecture models with Web 3D visualization technologies to create an interactive repository for neuropsychopharmacology content accessible through standard Internet browsers. The system was constructed using VRML 2.0 and X3D standards, incorporating high-resolution anatomical models derived from MRI datasets and histological atlases. Interactive modules were designed to visualize drug distribution pathways, receptor binding sites, and neurochemical cascades within accurate neuroanatomical contexts. The platform includes over 200 pharmaceutical agents with their corresponding mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetic profiles, and clinical applications. User evaluation studies involving 45 graduate students and 12 faculty members demonstrated significantly improved learning outcomes compared to traditional textbook-based instruction (p<0.001). Navigation efficiency metrics showed 73% faster information retrieval compared to conventional database searches. The virtual archive successfully renders complex neuropsychopharmacological concepts in an intuitive, spatially-accurate environment while maintaining compatibility across multiple browser platforms and operating systems. This Web 3D approach represents a paradigm shift in scientific information architecture, offering scalable solutions for complex biomedical data visualization and education. The platform's open architecture enables continuous content expansion and collaborative development, establishing a foundation for next-generation neuropsychopharmacology knowledge management systems.
journal: Computer methods and programs in biomedicine

title: Laser-induced autofluorescence measurements on brain tissues.
abstract: It was demonstrated that comparison of the autofluorescence spectra induced with laser radiation in ultraviolet and visible allows the identification of brain tumor tissues and normal tissues as well as the difference between them. The measurements were performed on homogenates to ensure an optimal reproducibility of the results. We conclude that the autofluorescence spectra of the tumor samples are close to those measured for normal tissues, but there are differences between them that allow distinguishing the tumor from the normal tissue. One difference is that for each pair of tumor/normal tissue samples, the peak autofluorescence for the normal tissue is shifted with respect to that for the tumor-typically between 10 and 20 nm; overall autofluorescence intensity is also different for the components of the same pair, the difference being in the range 15%-30%. A parameter that can also be used is the variation of the ratio of some fluorescence intensity peaks between normal and tumor tissue samples. Measurements of this parameter yielded variations ranging between 10% and 40%. Another conclusion of the study is that in vitro experiments show that it is mandatory to use pairs of samples (normal/tumor tissue) taken from the same patient. The results show that, after further experimental in vitro tests, the method may be adapted to real-time intraoperative conditions by measuring the autofluorescence of the tumor and of the adjacent normal tissue.

title: Molecular Signaling Pathways Behind the Biological Effects of Salvia Species Diterpenes in Neuropharmacology and Cardiology.
abstract: Salvia species contain bioactive diterpenes that have demonstrated significant therapeutic potential in neurological and cardiovascular disorders, yet their underlying molecular mechanisms remain poorly characterized. This comprehensive review examines the signaling pathways mediating the biological effects of major Salvia diterpenes, including tanshinones, salvianolic acids, and carnosic acid derivatives. We conducted a systematic analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 2000-2015, focusing on mechanistic studies involving primary neuronal cultures, cardiac myocytes, and relevant animal models. Database searches identified 147 relevant studies examining molecular targets and signaling cascades. In neurological applications, Salvia diterpenes primarily modulate neuroprotective pathways through inhibition of NF-κB signaling, activation of Nrf2-mediated antioxidant responses, and regulation of calcium homeostasis via L-type calcium channel blockade. Tanshinone IIA demonstrated particular efficacy in attenuating neuroinflammation through suppression of microglial activation and reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokine release. In cardiovascular systems, these compounds exhibit cardioprotective effects predominantly through PI3K/Akt pathway activation, mitochondrial membrane stabilization, and modulation of apoptotic cascades involving Bcl-2 family proteins. Salvianolic acid B showed remarkable ability to preserve cardiac function during ischemia-reperfusion injury via enhancement of endothelial nitric oxide synthase activity. Cross-system analysis revealed convergent mechanisms involving oxidative stress reduction and inflammatory pathway suppression. These findings provide molecular rationale for traditional uses of Salvia species and identify specific targets for therapeutic development. Further clinical investigation is warranted to translate these mechanistic insights into evidence-based phytotherapeutic interventions for neurodegenerative and cardiovascular diseases.

title: The stanley neuropathology consortium integrative database: a novel, web-based tool for exploring neuropathological markers in psychiatric disorders and the biological processes associated with abnormalities of those markers.
abstract: The Stanley Neuropathology Consortium has generated extensive postmortem brain tissue data from individuals with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, major depression, and matched controls over the past decade. However, the integration and accessibility of these diverse datasets has remained a significant challenge for the research community. We developed a comprehensive, web-based integrative database to facilitate exploration of neuropathological markers across psychiatric disorders and enable systematic investigation of associated biological processes. The database incorporates quantitative neuropathological data from over 400 subjects, including measurements of neuronal density, glial cell populations, synaptic proteins, and inflammatory markers across multiple brain regions. We implemented advanced search algorithms and data visualization tools, allowing users to query specific markers, compare across diagnostic groups, and examine correlations with demographic and clinical variables. The platform integrates pathway analysis tools to identify enriched biological processes associated with observed neuropathological abnormalities. Our analysis revealed significant alterations in oligodendrocyte populations in the prefrontal cortex across all three psychiatric disorders, with the most pronounced deficits observed in schizophrenia. Microglial activation markers showed disorder-specific patterns, with elevated CD68 immunoreactivity in bipolar disorder and increased HLA-DR expression in major depression. Pathway analysis identified disrupted myelination, synaptic transmission, and inflammatory response processes as common themes across disorders. This integrative database represents a valuable resource for the psychiatric research community, enabling data-driven hypothesis generation and facilitating the identification of convergent neuropathological mechanisms underlying major mental illness.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Integrative biological analysis for neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: Neuropsychopharmacological research has traditionally relied on reductionist approaches focusing on single molecular targets, yet psychiatric disorders emerge from complex interactions across multiple biological scales. This study presents a comprehensive integrative framework combining genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and behavioral phenotyping to elucidate drug mechanisms in mood disorders. We employed a systems biology approach using chronic unpredictable mild stress (CUMS) in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) treated with fluoxetine (10 mg/kg, 21 days) or vehicle. RNA sequencing of prefrontal cortex and hippocampus revealed 1,247 differentially expressed genes following CUMS, with fluoxetine normalizing 68% of stress-induced changes. Quantitative proteomics identified 312 altered proteins, including key synaptic and metabolic regulators. Integration with genome-wide association study data from major depressive disorder patients showed significant overlap (p<0.001) between rodent stress-response genes and human risk variants. Network analysis revealed hub proteins in BDNF, mTOR, and inflammatory signaling pathways. Behavioral improvements correlated with restoration of synaptic protein expression (r=0.72, p<0.01) and normalization of microglial activation markers. Machine learning algorithms successfully predicted treatment response using multi-omics signatures with 84% accuracy. This integrative approach identified novel biomarker panels and demonstrated that antidepressant efficacy results from coordinated molecular network restoration rather than single-target modulation. These findings provide a roadmap for precision neuropsychopharmacology and highlight the necessity of multi-scale biological integration in understanding psychiatric drug mechanisms. Future therapeutic development should embrace systems-level complexity to improve treatment outcomes.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Neuropharmacology of Synthetic Cathinones.
abstract: Synthetic cathinones, commonly known as "bath salts," are β-ketone analogues of amphetamines that have emerged as drugs of abuse with significant public health implications. These psychoactive substances exhibit complex pharmacological profiles through interactions with monoamine transporters, yet their precise mechanisms of action and neurotoxic potential remain incompletely characterized. This review synthesizes current knowledge regarding the neuropharmacological properties of prominent synthetic cathinones including mephedrone, methylenedioxypyrovalerone (MDPV), and α-pyrrolidinovalerophenone (α-PVP). We examined transporter selectivity profiles using in vitro uptake and release assays in HEK293 cells expressing human dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin transporters, alongside behavioral studies in rodent models of self-administration and locomotor sensitization. Synthetic cathinones demonstrated varied pharmacological profiles: mephedrone acted as a non-selective monoamine releaser similar to MDMA, while MDPV and α-PVP functioned primarily as potent dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors with minimal serotonergic activity. Pyrrolidine-containing cathinones showed enhanced potency and abuse liability compared to their non-pyrrolidine counterparts. In vivo studies revealed robust locomotor stimulation and reinforcing properties, with some compounds producing hyperthermia and oxidative stress markers in brain tissue. Structure-activity relationships indicated that α-carbon substitution and pyrrolidine ring incorporation significantly influence transporter selectivity and pharmacological potency. These findings highlight the diverse neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying synthetic cathinone toxicity and provide crucial insights for developing targeted therapeutic interventions and informing regulatory policies regarding these emerging substances of abuse.

title: Closed-loop Neuropharmacology for Epilepsy: Distant Dream or Future Reality?
abstract:
Epilepsy affects over 65 million people worldwide, with approximately 30% of patients experiencing drug-resistant seizures despite optimal medical management. Traditional antiepileptic drug therapy relies on continuous systemic administration, often resulting in subtherapeutic or toxic levels and significant side effects. Closed-loop neuropharmacology represents a paradigm shift toward precision medicine, combining real-time seizure detection with automated, localized drug delivery. This review examines current technological advances and clinical feasibility of closed-loop systems for epilepsy management.

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of recent developments in seizure prediction algorithms, implantable drug delivery platforms, and biocompatible reservoir systems. Key studies from 2015-2019 were evaluated, including preclinical models and early-phase clinical trials utilizing responsive neurostimulation data, microfluidic drug delivery devices, and machine learning-based seizure forecasting.

Current closed-loop systems demonstrate 70-85% accuracy in seizure detection within 10-30 seconds of onset, with response latencies of 2-5 seconds for drug delivery. Microinfusion pumps can deliver precise drug volumes (nanoliters to microliters) directly to epileptogenic zones, achieving local concentrations 10-100 times higher than systemic administration while minimizing off-target effects. Battery life and biocompatibility remain technical challenges, with current devices requiring replacement every 3-5 years. Clinical pilot studies show 40-60% seizure reduction in drug-resistant patients with minimal systemic side effects.

Closed-loop neuropharmacology for epilepsy represents a promising therapeutic frontier that addresses limitations of conventional treatment. While technical hurdles remain, rapid advances in biosensors, drug delivery systems, and artificial intelligence suggest clinical implementation within the next decade is increasingly realistic rather than aspirational.

title: [Current trends in clinical psychiatric research].
abstract: The landscape of clinical psychiatric research has undergone significant transformation during the 1990s, driven by advances in neuropharmacology and neuroimaging technologies. This review examines emerging methodological approaches and therapeutic targets in contemporary psychiatric investigation. We analyzed 347 clinical trials published between 1995-1998 across major psychiatric journals, focusing on study design innovations, novel pharmacological agents, and integration of biological markers. Additionally, we surveyed 125 research centers across Europe and North America to assess current research priorities and methodological trends. Our analysis reveals a marked shift toward multi-modal treatment approaches, with 68% of recent studies incorporating both pharmacological and psychosocial interventions. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors remain the predominant focus (42% of trials), though research into atypical antipsychotics has increased substantially (23% vs. 8% in 1990-1994). Notably, 31% of contemporary studies now utilize neuroimaging techniques, compared to 12% in earlier investigations. Genetic polymorphism analysis is emerging as a significant research component, appearing in 19% of recent pharmacogenetic studies. Quality-of-life measures have become standard outcome assessments, present in 76% of trials. The integration of placebo-controlled designs with active comparator arms has become increasingly common, reflecting enhanced methodological rigor. These trends indicate a paradigm shift toward personalized medicine approaches and dimensional rather than categorical diagnostic frameworks. Future psychiatric research will likely emphasize biomarker-guided treatment selection and the development of mechanism-based therapeutic interventions targeting specific neurobiological pathways.

title: Automated analysis of antidepressants' effect in the forced swim test.
abstract: The forced swim test (FST) is a widely used behavioral paradigm for screening antidepressant compounds, traditionally relying on manual scoring of immobility time by trained observers. However, this approach is labor-intensive, subject to inter-rater variability, and may introduce bias in drug efficacy assessments. We developed and validated an automated video-tracking system for quantifying behavioral parameters in the FST using adult male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=96). Animals were treated with vehicle, imipramine (10, 20, 30 mg/kg), fluoxetine (5, 10, 20 mg/kg), or bupropion (10, 20, 40 mg/kg) 1 hour prior to testing. Our system utilized high-resolution digital cameras and custom software algorithms to detect pixel changes corresponding to swimming, climbing, and immobility behaviors during the 6-minute test session. Manual scoring by two independent observers served as the validation standard. The automated system demonstrated excellent correlation with manual scoring for immobility time (r=0.94, p<0.001) and significantly reduced analysis time from 45 minutes to 3 minutes per session. All tested antidepressants showed dose-dependent reductions in immobility time, with bupropion and imipramine additionally increasing climbing behavior, while fluoxetine predominantly enhanced swimming activity. Inter-session reliability was superior for automated analysis (ICC=0.97) compared to manual scoring (ICC=0.83). This automated approach provides a standardized, objective, and efficient method for FST analysis, reducing variability and improving throughput in antidepressant screening protocols while maintaining sensitivity to drug-specific behavioral profiles.

title: An in vivo technique for pharmacological manipulation of Drosophila brain during optical recording.
abstract: The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has emerged as a powerful model system for investigating neural circuits and behavior, yet current methods for simultaneous pharmacological manipulation and optical monitoring of brain activity in vivo remain limited. We developed a novel technique combining targeted drug delivery with real-time calcium imaging to enable pharmacological dissection of neural function in the intact Drosophila brain. Our approach utilizes a custom-designed perfusion chamber that maintains fly viability while allowing precise application of pharmacological agents to exposed brain regions. We incorporated a moveable micropipette system for localized drug delivery coupled with two-photon calcium imaging of genetically encoded indicators (GCaMP) in identified neurons. We validated this system using GABA receptor modulators (picrotoxin, muscimol) and demonstrated selective modulation of neuronal activity in mushroom body Kenyon cells and antennal lobe projection neurons. Picrotoxin application (10-100 μM) increased spontaneous calcium transient frequency by 340 ± 45%, while muscimol (50-200 μM) decreased activity by 78 ± 12% compared to saline controls (n=8-12 flies per condition). The preparation remained stable for over 2 hours, with consistent pharmacological responses and preserved circuit architecture as verified by post-hoc immunohistochemistry. This technique provides temporal resolution of seconds and spatial precision of individual dendritic compartments. Our method significantly advances the toolkit available for Drosophila neuropharmacology research and will facilitate mechanistic studies of neural circuit function, neurotransmitter systems, and drug screening applications in this genetically tractable model organism.

title: Pharmacological treatment in behavioural medicine: the importance of neurochemistry, molecular biology and mechanistic hypotheses.
abstract: The integration of neurochemical and molecular biological principles into veterinary behavioural medicine has become increasingly important for developing rational therapeutic approaches to companion animal behavioural disorders. Traditional empirical treatment methods often lack mechanistic foundations, limiting their efficacy and predictability. This review examines the current understanding of neurotransmitter systems implicated in common behavioural pathologies and evaluates the therapeutic potential of targeted pharmacological interventions based on underlying neurobiological mechanisms. We analysed published literature from 1990-2000 regarding serotonergic, dopaminergic, and GABAergic systems in relation to anxiety, aggression, and compulsive disorders in dogs and cats. Particular attention was given to studies employing selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and anxiolytics with well-defined receptor binding profiles. Evidence indicates that dysfunction in serotonergic neurotransmission underlies many anxiety-related disorders, with SSRIs demonstrating superior efficacy compared to non-selective agents. Dopaminergic abnormalities appear central to stereotypic behaviours, while GABA receptor modulation shows promise for acute anxiety management. Molecular studies reveal species-specific differences in receptor expression and drug metabolism that significantly impact treatment outcomes. The application of mechanistic hypotheses based on neurochemical principles enables more precise therapeutic targeting and improved clinical outcomes. Future research should focus on developing species-specific pharmacokinetic models and identifying biomarkers for treatment response. This mechanistic approach represents a paradigm shift towards evidence-based behavioural pharmacotherapy in veterinary medicine.

title: Utilizing resources of neuropsychopharmacology to address the opioid overdose crisis.
abstract: The ongoing opioid overdose crisis represents a critical public health emergency requiring immediate implementation of evidence-based neuropsychopharmacological interventions. Current approaches remain insufficient to address the scope of overdose mortality, necessitating comprehensive utilization of existing pharmacological tools and development of novel therapeutic strategies. This review synthesizes current neuropsychopharmacological resources and proposes a multi-pronged approach to overdose prevention and treatment. We conducted a systematic analysis of FDA-approved medications, emerging compounds, and harm reduction strategies with demonstrated efficacy in opioid use disorder and overdose prevention. Key interventions include expanded naloxone distribution programs, evidence-based medication-assisted treatment (MAT) utilizing methadone, buprenorphine, and naltrexone, and implementation of novel alpha-2 adrenergic agonists for withdrawal management. Emerging approaches include partial opioid receptor modulators, abuse-deterrent formulations, and combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems. Analysis of epidemiological data demonstrates that regions with comprehensive MAT implementation show 25-40% reductions in overdose mortality. Naloxone distribution programs have prevented an estimated 26,463 deaths between 2013-2017. Novel formulations combining buprenorphine with abuse-deterrent technologies show promise in reducing diversion while maintaining therapeutic efficacy. Integration of neuropsychopharmacological resources through coordinated clinical protocols, expanded access to evidence-based treatments, and continued research into novel therapeutic targets represents the most viable approach to mitigating the opioid crisis. Immediate policy implementation supporting these interventions is essential to prevent continued escalation of overdose deaths.

title: Microneurography in rats: a minimally invasive method to record single C-fiber action potentials from peripheral nerves in vivo.
abstract: Microneurography is a method suitable for recording intraneural single or multiunit action potentials in conscious subjects. Microneurography has rarely been applied to animal experiments, where more invasive methods, like the teased fiber recording technique, are widely used. We have tested the feasibility of microneurographic recordings from the peripheral nerves of rats. Tungsten microelectrodes were inserted into the sciatic nerve at mid-thigh level. Single or multiunit action potentials evoked by regular electrical stimulation were recorded, digitized and displayed as a raster plot of latencies. The method allows unambiguous recording and recognition of single C-fiber action potentials from an in vivo preparation, with minimal disruption of the nerve being recorded. Multiple C-fibers can be recorded simultaneously for several hours, and if the animal is allowed to recover, repeated recording sessions can be obtained from the same nerve at the same level over a period of weeks or months. Also, single C units can be functionally identified by their changes in latency to natural stimuli, and insensitive units can be recognized as 'silent' nociceptors or sympathetic efferents by their distinctive profiles of activity-dependent slowing during repetitive electrical stimulation, or by the effect on spontaneous efferent activity of a proximal anesthetic block. Moreover, information about the biophysical properties of C axons can be obtained from their latency recovery cycles. Finally, we show that this preparation is potentially suitable for the study of C-fiber behavior in models of neuropathies and nerve lesions, both under resting conditions and in response to drug administration.

title: Neuropharmacological studies on Wedelia calendulacea Less stem extract.
abstract: The neuropharmacological activities of the methanolic and aqueous extract of Wedelia calendulacea stem were screened in rats and mice. The extracts effect on pentobarbital-induced sleeping time, pentylenetetrazole- and styrychnine-induced seizure, spontaneous motor activity, exploratory behaviour, and rota-rod performance (motor coordination) were evaluated. The methanolic extract (20 and 50 mg/kg, i.p.) and aqueous extract (200 and 500 mg/kg, i.p.) produced a significant (p<0.001) prolongation of pentobarbital-induced sleeping time, and reduced the SMA and exploratory behaviour. The extract prolonged onset of the phases of seizure activity but did not protect mice against lethality induced by pentylenetetrazole and strychnine. It also failed to affect the motor coordination test. These results suggest that the extract contained an agent with neuropharmacological activity that may be sedative in nature. In addition, from the crude methanolic extract of Wedelia calendulacea stem a HPLC fingerprint profile and liquid chromatography/sequential mass spectrometry (LC/MS) were performed.
journal: Phytomedicine : international journal of phytotherapy and phytopharmacology

title: S32504, a novel naphtoxazine agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors: III. Actions in models of potential antidepressive and anxiolytic activity in comparison with ropinirole.
abstract: The novel naphtoxazine derivative S32504 displays preferential affinity for dopamine D3 vs D2 receptors and shows promise as a potential therapeutic agent. This study evaluated the antidepressant and anxiolytic properties of S32504 in comparison with the D2/D3 agonist ropinirole using established rodent behavioral models. Male Wistar rats and Swiss mice received S32504 (0.16-10.0 mg/kg, s.c.), ropinirole (0.63-10.0 mg/kg, s.c.), or vehicle 30 min prior to testing. In the forced swim test, S32504 dose-dependently reduced immobility time in both rats (ED50 = 2.1 mg/kg) and mice (ED50 = 1.8 mg/kg), with efficacy comparable to imipramine (15 mg/kg, i.p.). Ropinirole showed weaker antidepressant-like activity (ED50 = 6.4 mg/kg in rats). In the elevated plus-maze, S32504 (0.63-2.5 mg/kg) significantly increased open arm entries and time spent in open arms, indicating anxiolytic effects. These actions were blocked by the D3-preferring antagonist S33084 (10 mg/kg, s.c.) but only partially attenuated by the D2-selective antagonist L-741,626 (10 mg/kg, s.c.). Ropinirole produced less pronounced anxiolytic effects that were fully reversed by both antagonists. S32504 did not impair motor coordination at active doses, unlike ropinirole which caused rotational behavior at higher doses. These findings suggest that S32504's preferential D3 receptor activation underlies its robust antidepressant and anxiolytic properties, supporting the therapeutic potential of selective D3 receptor agonists for mood and anxiety disorders.
journal: The Journal of pharmacology and experimental therapeutics

title: CSF biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease: use in early diagnosis and evaluation of drug treatment.
abstract: Early diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease is important in initiating symptomatic treatment with acetylcholine esterase inhibitors, and will be of even greater significance if drugs with a potential to slow down the degenerative process, such as beta-secretase inhibitors and beta-amyloid vaccination, prove to have a clinical effect. During the last decade, research efforts have focused on developing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers for Alzheimer's disease. In this review, the background and principles for, and the diagnostic performance of, the CSF biomarkers total tau, phosphorylated tau and the 42-amino acid form of beta-amyloid, are reviewed. New candidate CSF biomarkers and new strategies, including multiparameter immunoassays and CSF proteomics techniques, in the search of additional CSF biomarkers are also reviewed. Finally, the rationale for the use of CSF biomarkers to identify and monitor the biochemical effect of new drug candidates is reviewed.

title: Principles of motivation revealed by the diverse functions of neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates underlying feeding behavior.
abstract: The neural mechanisms underlying feeding behavior represent a complex integration of homeostatic, hedonic, and cognitive processes that provide fundamental insights into motivational systems. While hypothalamic circuits have long been recognized as primary regulators of energy balance, emerging evidence demonstrates that feeding behavior engages distributed neural networks involving dopaminergic reward pathways, limbic structures, and prefrontal regulatory regions. This review synthesizes current understanding of the neuropharmacological and neuroanatomical substrates controlling food intake, with particular emphasis on how these systems reveal broader principles of motivated behavior. We examine the roles of key neurotransmitter systems, including dopamine, serotonin, GABA, and neuropeptides such as orexin and NPY, in mediating different aspects of feeding motivation. The hypothalamic arcuate nucleus emerges as a critical integration hub, receiving peripheral metabolic signals and coordinating responses through projections to brainstem and forebrain regions. Dopaminergic circuits in the ventral tegmental area and nucleus accumbens process food reward and incentive salience, while prefrontal cortical areas exert top-down control over feeding decisions. Dysregulation of these interconnected systems contributes to obesity and eating disorders, highlighting their clinical relevance. The multifaceted nature of feeding control illuminates general principles governing motivated behavior, including the integration of homeostatic need states with reward processing, the role of learning and memory in shaping behavioral responses, and the hierarchical organization of neural control systems. Understanding these principles advances both basic neuroscience and therapeutic approaches to metabolic disorders.

title: [Atypical antipsychotic drugs].
abstract: The introduction of atypical antipsychotic drugs has fundamentally altered the treatment landscape for schizophrenia and related psychotic disorders. Unlike conventional neuroleptics that primarily block dopamine D2 receptors, atypical agents demonstrate complex pharmacological profiles involving multiple neurotransmitter systems. We conducted a comprehensive review of the pharmacological mechanisms and clinical efficacy of atypical antipsychotics, focusing on clozapine, risperidone, olanzapine, and quetiapine. Clinical data from 24 controlled studies involving 3,847 patients with schizophrenia were analyzed, comparing efficacy and side effect profiles between atypical and conventional antipsychotics. Atypical agents demonstrated superior efficacy in treating negative symptoms, with 40-60% of patients showing significant improvement compared to 20-35% with haloperidol. Importantly, the incidence of extrapyramidal side effects was markedly reduced: tardive dyskinesia occurred in 0.5-5% of patients treated with atypical drugs versus 15-20% with conventional neuroleptics. The unique receptor binding profiles of atypical antipsychotics, particularly their high 5-HT2A/D2 receptor affinity ratios and interaction with cholinergic, histaminergic, and adrenergic systems, appear to underlie their improved tolerability. However, metabolic side effects including weight gain and glucose dysregulation emerged as new concerns, particularly with olanzapine and clozapine. These findings suggest that atypical antipsychotics represent a significant therapeutic advance, offering improved symptom control with reduced neurological complications, though requiring careful monitoring of metabolic parameters. Their introduction has established new standards for antipsychotic therapy in Dutch clinical practice.

title: Pharmaco fMRI: Determining the functional anatomy of the effects of medication.
abstract: Functional MRI studies have helped to elucidate underlying mechanisms in complex neurological and neuropsychiatric disorders. Disease processes often involve complex large-scale network interactions, extending beyond the presumed main disease focus. Given both the complexity of the clinical phenotype and the underlying dysfunctional brain circuits, so called pharmaco-fMRI (ph-MRI) studies probe pharmacological effects on functional neuro-anatomy, and can help to determine early treatment response, mechanisms of drug efficacy and side effects, and potentially advance CNS drug development. In this review, we discuss recent ph-MRI research in three major neuropsychiatric and neurological disorders and associated network alterations, namely selective serotonin and noradrenergic reuptake inhibitors in affective disorders and emotional processing circuits; antiepileptic drugs in epilepsy and cognitive networks; and stimulants in attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and networks of attention control. We conclude that ph-MRI studies show consistent and reproducible changes on disease relevant networks, and prove sensitive to early pharmacological effects on functional anatomy associated with disease. Further CNS drug research and development would benefit greatly from improved disease phenotyping, or biomarkers, using advanced imaging techniques.

title: Name that neurotransmitter: using music to teach psychopharmacology concepts.
abstract:
Background: Teaching psychopharmacology concepts to nursing students presents significant pedagogical challenges, particularly in helping students memorize neurotransmitter functions, pathways, and associated medications. Traditional lecture-based approaches often result in poor retention and difficulty applying concepts to clinical practice. Music-based learning strategies have demonstrated efficacy in various educational contexts but have not been systematically evaluated for psychopharmacology education.

Methods: We implemented a novel educational intervention called "Name that Neurotransmitter" with 84 undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a psychopharmacology course. Students were randomly assigned to either a traditional lecture group (n=42) or a music-enhanced learning group (n=42). The intervention group participated in sessions where neurotransmitter functions, synthesis pathways, and drug mechanisms were taught through original songs set to familiar melodies. Pre- and post-intervention assessments measured knowledge retention using a validated 50-item psychopharmacology examination. Student satisfaction and perceived learning effectiveness were evaluated using Likert-scale surveys.

Results: Students in the music-enhanced group demonstrated significantly higher post-test scores compared to controls (82.3 ± 8.1 vs. 74.6 ± 9.4, p<0.001). Retention testing at 4 weeks showed sustained knowledge improvement in the intervention group (79.1 ± 7.8 vs. 71.2 ± 8.9, p<0.01). Music group participants reported higher engagement levels (4.6 ± 0.5 vs. 3.2 ± 0.7, p<0.001) and increased confidence in applying psychopharmacology concepts clinically.

Conclusions: Music-based teaching strategies significantly enhance learning and retention of complex psychopharmacology concepts among nursing students, suggesting broader applications for neuroscience education in healthcare curricula.

title: Photoinactivation of native AMPA receptors reveals their real-time trafficking.
abstract: AMPA receptors mediate fast excitatory synaptic transmission in the central nervous system and undergo constitutive trafficking between synaptic and extrasynaptic sites, yet the real-time dynamics of this process remain poorly understood. Here we developed a novel photoinactivation approach using the membrane-impermeant crosslinking reagent bis(sulfosuccinimidyl)suberate (BS³) coupled with caged compounds to irreversibly inactivate surface AMPA receptors in cultured hippocampal neurons. Following photoinactivation of existing surface receptors, we monitored the recovery of AMPA receptor-mediated currents using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings and tracked receptor insertion using immunofluorescence microscopy with GluR1 and GluR2 subunit-specific antibodies. Photoinactivation reduced AMPA receptor currents by 85-90% within 2 minutes, with minimal effects on NMDA or GABA receptor function. Recovery of synaptic AMPA currents followed biphasic kinetics, with a rapid phase (τ = 8.2 ± 1.4 min) representing 40% of recovery and a slower phase (τ = 47 ± 8 min) accounting for the remainder. Immunofluorescence revealed that newly inserted receptors initially appeared at extrasynaptic sites before accumulating at synaptic locations. Brefeldin A treatment blocked recovery, confirming dependence on the secretory pathway. Interestingly, synaptic activity enhanced both the rate and extent of recovery, with bicuculline treatment increasing the fast phase contribution to 65%. These results demonstrate that AMPA receptors undergo rapid, activity-dependent trafficking from intracellular pools to synapses, providing direct evidence for constitutive receptor cycling that may underlie homeostatic plasticity mechanisms.

title: Opinion on monoaminergic contributions to traits and temperament.
abstract: Monoaminergic neurotransmitter systems have long been implicated in individual differences in personality traits and temperamental characteristics, yet their precise contributions remain contentious. This perspective synthesizes current evidence regarding dopaminergic, serotonergic, and noradrenergic influences on dimensional models of personality, particularly focusing on extraversion, neuroticism, and conscientiousness within the five-factor framework. We critically evaluate findings from pharmacological challenge studies, neuroimaging investigations of monoamine transporter and receptor densities, and genetic association studies of polymorphisms affecting monoaminergic function. Converging evidence suggests that dopaminergic pathways, particularly within mesolimbic and mesocortical circuits, contribute substantially to reward sensitivity and approach-related behaviors underlying extraversion. Serotonergic function appears most robustly linked to emotional regulation and stress reactivity associated with neuroticism, with 5-HT1A receptor availability and serotonin transporter expression showing consistent associations across methodologies. Noradrenergic contributions appear more complex, with evidence for involvement in both arousal-related aspects of extraversion and anxiety-related components of neuroticism. However, effect sizes remain modest, and replication challenges persist, particularly for candidate gene associations. We propose that monoaminergic systems likely exert their influence on temperament through dynamic interactions rather than simple one-to-one mappings between neurotransmitter function and personality dimensions. Future research should prioritize polygenic approaches, longitudinal designs examining neurotransmitter-environment interactions, and integration of multiple levels of analysis to advance understanding of neurochemical contributions to individual differences in behavior and temperament.
journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences

title: Fast Hadamard transform capillary electrophoresis for on-line, time-resolved chemical monitoring.
abstract: The rapid detection and quantification of neurotransmitters and their metabolites in biological systems remains a critical challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional analytical methods often lack the temporal resolution necessary to monitor dynamic neurochemical processes occurring on millisecond to second timescales. We developed a novel fast Hadamard transform capillary electrophoresis (FHT-CE) method for real-time chemical monitoring with enhanced signal-to-noise ratio and temporal resolution. The system employs multiplexed sample injection patterns based on Hadamard encoding matrices, followed by mathematical deconvolution to reconstruct individual electropherograms. Validation studies were performed using standard solutions of dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and their primary metabolites (DOPAC, 5-HIAA, MHPG) in artificial cerebrospinal fluid. The FHT-CE method demonstrated a 4-fold improvement in signal-to-noise ratio compared to conventional CE, with detection limits of 50 nM for dopamine and 75 nM for serotonin. Temporal resolution was enhanced to 2.3 seconds per analysis cycle, representing a 10-fold improvement over standard methods. Application to microdialysis samples from rat striatum during pharmacological stimulation revealed rapid fluctuations in dopamine levels (15-340 nM) with concurrent changes in DOPAC concentrations. The method successfully monitored neurotransmitter dynamics following acute amphetamine administration, detecting peak dopamine release within 45 seconds of drug delivery. This FHT-CE approach provides a powerful analytical tool for investigating rapid neurochemical processes and evaluating pharmacokinetic profiles of neuroactive compounds in real-time biological systems.

title: Neurophamacology of yawning.
abstract: Yawning is a stereotyped behavioral pattern observed across vertebrate species, yet its underlying neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. While traditionally associated with fatigue or boredom, emerging evidence suggests yawning serves important physiological functions including thermoregulation and arousal state transitions. This study investigated the neurotransmitter systems mediating yawning behavior using pharmacological manipulation in male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-12 per group). Yawning frequency was quantified over 60-minute observation periods following systemic administration of selective receptor agonists and antagonists. Dopaminergic modulation was examined using apomorphine (0.05-0.8 mg/kg), haloperidol (0.1-1.0 mg/kg), and quinpirole (0.01-0.1 mg/kg). Cholinergic effects were assessed with physostigmine (0.1-0.5 mg/kg) and atropine (1-10 mg/kg). Additional experiments examined GABAergic, serotonergic, and opioidergic contributions. Low-dose apomorphine significantly increased yawning frequency (312% of control, p<0.001), an effect blocked by haloperidol pretreatment. Quinpirole produced a biphasic response with maximal yawning at 0.05 mg/kg. Physostigmine dose-dependently enhanced yawning behavior, while atropine produced modest inhibition. GABAA receptor activation with muscimol suppressed spontaneous yawning by 67% (p<0.01). Naloxone pretreatment potentiated apomorphine-induced yawning, suggesting tonic opioidergic inhibition. These findings demonstrate that yawning is regulated by complex interactions between dopaminergic D2/D3 receptors, cholinergic muscarinic pathways, and modulatory GABAergic and opioidergic systems. Understanding yawning neuropharmacology may provide insights into arousal disorders and serve as a behavioral biomarker for dopaminergic function in neurological conditions.

title: A chronically implantable, hybrid cannula-electrode device for assessing the effects of molecules on electrophysiological signals in freely behaving animals.
abstract: We describe a device for assessing the effects of diffusible molecules on electrophysiological recordings from multiple neurons. This device allows for the infusion of reagents through a cannula located among an array of micro-electrodes. The device can easily be customized to target specific neural structures. It is designed to be chronically implanted so that isolated neural units and local field potentials are recorded over the course of several weeks or months. Multivariate statistical and spectral analysis of electrophysiological signals acquired using this system could quantitatively identify electrical "signatures" of therapeutically useful drugs.

title: Bridging autism, science and society: moving toward an ethically informed approach to autism research.
abstract: Recent developments in the science of autism have provoked widespread unease among autism activists. Drawing on the findings of a major international gathering of researchers, ethicists, and activists, this paper presents the first major analysis of the ethical questions arising from this unease. We outline the scientific developments that have provoked the most discomfort, analyze the response to these developments from within and without the autism community, and trace the current state of the ethical debate. Having done so, we contend that these ethical questions are unlikely to be resolved as they depend on fundamentally conflicting assumptions about the nature and desirability of neurocognitive difference. We conclude by arguing for a new range of democratic mechanisms that could enable the scientific community, autistics, and other concerned parties to respond collectively to such entrenched ethical disputes.
journal: Autism research : official journal of the International Society for Autism Research

title: Achieving synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitter using PDMS microfluidics.
abstract: Precise spatiotemporal control of neurotransmitter delivery remains a significant challenge in neuropharmacological research, limiting our ability to study synaptic transmission mechanisms. Traditional drug application methods, including bath perfusion and iontophoresis, lack the temporal resolution necessary to mimic physiological neurotransmitter release kinetics. Here, we describe a novel microfluidic approach using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) devices to generate synaptically relevant pulses of neurotransmitters with millisecond temporal precision and micrometer spatial resolution. We fabricated multi-channel PDMS microfluidic chips with integrated pneumatic valves capable of switching between control and neurotransmitter-containing solutions within 2-5 milliseconds. The system was validated using whole-cell patch-clamp recordings from cultured hippocampal neurons and acute brain slices.
