Glutamate pulses (1 mM, 10-100 ms duration) applied to individual dendritic spines generated AMPA receptor-mediated currents with rise times of 1.8 ± 0.3 ms and decay kinetics comparable to miniature excitatory postsynaptic currents. Similarly, GABA applications (10 mM, 5-50 ms) produced physiologically relevant inhibitory responses. The spatial confinement of neurotransmitter application was confirmed using fluorescent dextran tracers, demonstrating effective targeting within 5 μm of the delivery site. This microfluidic platform enables reproducible delivery of brief neurotransmitter pulses that closely approximate synaptic release, providing a valuable tool for investigating receptor kinetics, synaptic integration, and pharmacological mechanisms under physiologically relevant conditions. The technique offers significant advantages over existing methods for studying fast synaptic transmission and drug action at individual synapses.

title: Academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry.
abstract: The relationship between academic psychiatry and the pharmaceutical industry has become increasingly complex, raising concerns about potential conflicts of interest that may influence research integrity and clinical practice. This study examined the extent and nature of financial relationships between academic psychiatrists and pharmaceutical companies, and their potential impact on research outcomes and clinical recommendations. We conducted a comprehensive survey of 847 academic psychiatrists at 25 major medical institutions across the United States, supplemented by analysis of pharmaceutical company disclosure reports and published research from 2003-2005. Additionally, we performed a systematic review of 312 randomized controlled trials in major psychiatry journals to assess correlations between industry funding and reported outcomes. Our findings revealed that 78% of academic psychiatrists reported some form of financial relationship with pharmaceutical companies, including consulting fees (45%), research grants (52%), speakers' bureau participation (31%), and equity holdings (12%). Industry-funded studies were significantly more likely to report favorable outcomes for the sponsor's drug compared to independently funded research (OR = 3.4, 95% CI: 2.1-5.6, p < 0.001). Furthermore, 23% of department chairs reported that industry relationships influenced faculty hiring and promotion decisions. These results demonstrate widespread financial entanglements between academic psychiatry and pharmaceutical companies that may compromise scientific objectivity and patient care. Academic medical centers must develop more stringent conflict-of-interest policies and transparency requirements to preserve the integrity of psychiatric research and maintain public trust in the profession.

title: Pharmacotherapy of alcohol dependence: a review of the clinical data.
abstract: Alcohol dependence represents a major public health concern affecting millions worldwide, yet therapeutic options remain limited and show variable efficacy. This review examines the current state of pharmacological interventions for alcohol dependence based on available clinical trial data through 2003. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of randomized controlled trials evaluating FDA-approved medications and emerging therapeutic agents for alcohol dependence treatment. Three medications currently hold FDA approval: disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. Disulfiram demonstrates efficacy primarily through supervised administration, with studies showing 60-80% abstinence rates under monitored conditions versus 20-30% with unsupervised use. Naltrexone, an opioid receptor antagonist, consistently reduces heavy drinking days by 15-25% and craving scores by 20-40% across multiple placebo-controlled trials involving over 2,000 patients. Acamprosate shows promise in European studies, with abstinence rates of 35-45% versus 15-25% for placebo at 6-12 months, though US trial results have been mixed. Emerging agents including topiramate, ondansetron, and baclofen show preliminary efficacy in small-scale studies. Combination therapy approaches demonstrate enhanced outcomes compared to monotherapy in several trials. Patient factors including genetic polymorphisms, particularly in ALDH2 and OPRM1 genes, appear to influence treatment response. Current pharmacotherapies represent important advances but show modest effect sizes, highlighting the need for personalized treatment approaches and continued development of novel therapeutic targets including glutamate and GABA systems.

title: The golden years: a tribute to Erminio Costa.
abstract: The field of neuropharmacology has been profoundly shaped by the pioneering contributions of Erminio Costa, whose groundbreaking research spanning five decades fundamentally transformed our understanding of GABAergic neurotransmission and neuropsychiatric disorders. This tribute examines Costa's seminal discoveries and their lasting impact on contemporary neuropharmacology. We conducted a comprehensive review of Costa's published works from 1955-2009, analyzing his major contributions to GABA receptor pharmacology, benzodiazepine mechanisms, and schizophrenia research. Additionally, we surveyed current literature citing his work to assess ongoing influence on modern neuropharmacological research. Costa's laboratory identified the crucial role of GABA as the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter and elucidated the molecular mechanisms underlying benzodiazepine anxiolytic effects through allosteric modulation of GABAA receptors. His team discovered that benzodiazepines enhance GABA-mediated chloride conductance without directly activating the receptor, establishing the conceptual framework for positive allosteric modulation that remains central to modern drug development. Furthermore, Costa's investigations into GABA dysfunction in schizophrenia revealed reduced GAD67 expression and altered parvalbumin-positive interneuron function, providing neurobiological foundations for current theories of psychiatric pathophysiology. Analysis reveals over 15,000 citations of Costa's work in the past decade, with particular influence on GABA receptor subunit research, interneuron development, and therapeutic target identification. Costa's legacy continues to guide contemporary neuropharmacological research, from basic GABA receptor biology to translational studies of anxiety disorders and schizophrenia, cementing his position as one of neuropharmacology's most influential pioneers.

title: Subcellular neuropharmacology: the importance of intracellular targeting.
abstract: Few cell types are more adapted for cell-cell signaling than neurons. Their responsiveness lies in the formation of highly specialized compartments composed of unique repertoires of selectively distributed protein complexes generated, in part, by the local translation of mRNAs and regulated by their RNA-binding proteins. Utilizing the selective distribution of these neuronal proteins and the underlying mechanisms that generate the differential patterns of expression as central facets of drug design promises to enhance the therapeutic ratio of a drug. It is in this context that we discuss the unique arrangement of mRNAs, RNA-binding proteins and the protein macromolecular complexes at the dendrite, which is the postsynaptic site of synaptic transmission. Recent advances in identifying the function of dendritic components of the mechanisms of protein and RNA transport, non-nuclear RNA splicing and localized translation underscore their importance as targets of neuropharmacology.

title: The future of pharmacotherapy for paediatric anxiety.
abstract: Anxiety disorders represent one of the most prevalent psychiatric conditions in children and adolescents, affecting approximately 10-20% of the pediatric population. Current pharmacological interventions for pediatric anxiety remain limited, with most treatments extrapolated from adult studies despite significant developmental differences in neurotransmitter systems and drug metabolism. This review examines emerging pharmacotherapeutic approaches and evaluates their potential clinical utility in pediatric anxiety management. A comprehensive literature search was conducted using MEDLINE and PsycINFO databases (1990-2002) focusing on novel anxiolytic agents, pediatric-specific clinical trials, and developmental pharmacokinetic studies. Particular attention was given to selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), novel serotonergic compounds, and targeted neurotransmitter modulators. Current evidence suggests that while traditional SSRIs show promise in pediatric populations, concerns regarding suicidality and long-term developmental effects necessitate careful risk-benefit analysis. Emerging agents targeting specific anxiety circuits, including selective 5-HT1A receptor agonists and GABA-A receptor modulators with improved safety profiles, demonstrate encouraging preliminary results. Novel approaches incorporating pharmacogenomic principles may enable personalized dosing strategies accounting for developmental differences in cytochrome P450 enzyme activity. The future of pediatric anxiety pharmacotherapy lies in developing age-appropriate formulations, conducting rigorous pediatric clinical trials, and establishing evidence-based treatment algorithms. Integration of pharmacological interventions with cognitive-behavioral approaches remains essential. Regulatory initiatives encouraging pediatric drug development and improved understanding of neurodevelopmental processes will be crucial for advancing safe and effective anxiety treatments in children and adolescents.

title: Demon voltammetry and analysis software: analysis of cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling using multiple kinetic measures.
abstract: Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) has emerged as a powerful technique for real-time measurement of dopamine release and uptake in brain tissue, yet comprehensive analysis of the complex kinetic parameters remains challenging. We developed DEMON (Dopamine Evaluation and Monitoring with Optimized Neurochemistry), a novel analysis software package designed to extract multiple kinetic measures from FSCV data with enhanced precision and automation. Using this software, we investigated cocaine-induced alterations in dopamine signaling in nucleus accumbens core of urethane-anesthetized rats (n=8). Electrical stimulation of the ventral tegmental area (single pulse, 350 μA, 4 ms) evoked dopamine release, which was measured before and after systemic cocaine administration (10 mg/kg, i.p.). DEMON software automatically calculated peak dopamine concentration, rise time (10-90%), decay time constant (τ), and area under the curve while minimizing user bias through standardized peak detection algorithms and baseline correction procedures. Cocaine treatment significantly increased peak dopamine concentration by 312 ± 45% compared to baseline, prolonged the decay time constant from 1.24 ± 0.18 s to 4.67 ± 0.74 s, and increased total dopamine signal (AUC) by 586 ± 89%. Rise time parameters remained unchanged, consistent with selective inhibition of the dopamine transporter rather than altered vesicular release mechanisms. The DEMON software package provides researchers with a standardized, comprehensive tool for FSCV data analysis that reveals multiple dimensions of dopamine kinetics, facilitating more nuanced understanding of dopaminergic neurotransmission in health and disease states.

title: Anxiolytics antagonize yohimbine-induced central noradrenergic activity: a concomitant in vivo voltammetry-electrophysiology model of anxiety.
abstract: Anxiety disorders involve complex interactions between noradrenergic and GABAergic neurotransmitter systems, yet the real-time neurochemical mechanisms underlying anxiolytic drug action remain poorly characterized. We developed a novel dual-modality approach combining in vivo voltammetry with simultaneous electrophysiology to examine how clinically relevant anxiolytics modulate yohimbine-induced noradrenergic activation in the locus coeruleus (LC) and its projection targets. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) were implanted with carbon fiber electrodes for voltammetric detection of norepinephrine in the LC, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex, alongside tungsten microelectrodes for single-unit recordings of LC neuronal activity. Yohimbine (2 mg/kg, i.p.) significantly increased extracellular norepinephrine levels by 340±45% in the LC, 280±38% in hippocampus, and 195±32% in prefrontal cortex, concurrent with increased LC firing rates (baseline: 1.2±0.3 Hz; post-yohimbine: 4.8±0.7 Hz). These neurochemical and electrophysiological changes correlated with elevated anxiety-like behaviors in the elevated plus maze. Pretreatment with diazepam (1 mg/kg) or buspirone (5 mg/kg) significantly attenuated yohimbine-induced norepinephrine release by 65-75% across all brain regions and normalized LC firing patterns. The temporal correlation between neurochemical and electrophysiological measurements (r=0.87, p<0.001) validated our methodological approach. These findings demonstrate that anxiolytics act, in part, by restraining excessive noradrenergic activity and provide a robust experimental model for real-time assessment of anxiety-related neurochemical processes. This dual-modality technique offers enhanced temporal and spatial resolution for investigating anxiolytic mechanisms and may facilitate development of novel anti-anxiety therapeutics.

title: Psychopharmacology of tic disorders in children and adolescents.
abstract: Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, affect 0.5-3% of children and adolescents, causing significant functional impairment and psychosocial distress. Current pharmacological interventions demonstrate variable efficacy and tolerability profiles, necessitating evidence-based treatment guidelines. This comprehensive review synthesizes data from randomized controlled trials, open-label studies, and case series published between 1990-2010 examining psychopharmacological treatments for pediatric tic disorders. We systematically analyzed 47 studies encompassing 2,847 patients aged 6-18 years with chronic tic disorders or Tourette syndrome. Traditional antipsychotics (haloperidol, pimozide) demonstrated robust tic reduction (40-60% improvement) but showed high rates of extrapyramidal side effects (28-45%) and cognitive dulling. Atypical antipsychotics, particularly risperidone and aripiprazole, exhibited comparable efficacy (35-55% tic reduction) with improved tolerability profiles, though metabolic side effects remained concerning. Alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine) provided moderate tic improvement (25-40%) with favorable side effect profiles, particularly beneficial for patients with comorbid ADHD. Emerging treatments including topiramate and botulinum toxin injections showed promise in preliminary studies but require further investigation. Treatment selection should consider tic severity, functional impairment, comorbid conditions, and individual risk-benefit profiles. Non-pharmacological interventions including behavioral therapy warrant consideration as first-line treatments for mild-moderate tics. Future research should focus on personalized treatment approaches, long-term safety data, and novel therapeutic targets to optimize outcomes while minimizing adverse effects in this vulnerable population.

title: Drug development in neuropsychopharmacology.
abstract: The development of neuropsychopharmacological agents has traditionally relied on serendipitous discoveries and phenotypic screening approaches, resulting in limited therapeutic efficacy and significant side effect profiles for many psychiatric disorders. Recent advances in neurobiology, genomics, and drug discovery methodologies offer new opportunities for rational drug design targeting specific neural pathways implicated in mental illness. This review examines current trends and challenges in neuropsychopharmacological drug development, analyzing 156 compounds in clinical development between 2003-2007 across major pharmaceutical databases and regulatory filings. We assessed target selectivity, mechanism of action, and clinical trial outcomes for agents targeting depression, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, and anxiety disorders. Our analysis reveals a shift toward multi-target approaches, with 34% of compounds demonstrating polypharmacological profiles compared to 12% in the previous decade. Novel targets beyond traditional monoaminergic systems now represent 42% of the development pipeline, including glutamatergic modulators (18%), neuropeptide receptor antagonists (14%), and neurotrophic factor enhancers (10%). However, translation from preclinical models remains challenging, with only 23% of Phase II compounds advancing to Phase III trials. Attrition rates were highest for cognitive enhancement agents (89%) and lowest for anxiety disorders (34%). These findings highlight the need for improved translational models, better patient stratification strategies, and continued investment in understanding the neurobiological basis of psychiatric disorders. The evolution toward mechanism-based drug discovery, while promising, requires sustained commitment to basic neuroscience research and innovative clinical trial designs to address the significant unmet medical needs in psychiatry.
journal: European archives of psychiatry and clinical neuroscience

title: Tobacco smoking and nicotine neuropsychopharmacology: some future research directions.
abstract: Although nicotine is acknowledged as the major pharmacologically active chemical in tobacco that accounts for its continued use, there is a need for much further research. It is necessary to systematically compare the complex pharmacological actions of pure nicotine with those of tobacco, using different routes of administration and, therefore, rates of absorption. Tobacco smoking produces several important behavioral and central nervous system effects. More research is needed to determine the role of nicotine versus the many other substances present in tobacco smoke. Although nicotine is the primary pharmacological agent in tobacco that maintains its use, other chemicals and their biological mechanisms involved in tobacco smoking need to be studied further.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: The "systems approach" to treating the brain: opportunities in developmental psychopharmacology .
abstract: The significance of early life for the long-term programming of mental health is increasingly being recognized. However, most psychotropic medications are currently intended for adult patients, and early psychopharmacological approaches aimed at reverting aberrant neurodevelopmental trajectories are missing. Psychopharmacologic intervention at an early age faces the challenge of operating in a highly plastic system and requires a comprehensive knowledge of neurodevelopmental mechanisms. Recently the systems biology approach has contributed to the understanding of neuroplasticity mechanisms from a new perspective that interprets them as the result of complex and dynamic networks of signals from different systems. This approach is creating opportunities for developmental psychopharmacology, suggesting novel targets that can modulate the course of development by interfering with neuroplasticity at an early age. We will discuss two interconnected systems-the immune and gut microbiota-that regulate neurodevelopment and that have been implicated in preclinical research as new targets in the prevention of aberrant brain development. . Cada vez se reconoce más la importancia de la vida temprana para la programación a largo plazo de la salud mental. Sin embargo, la mayoría de los medicamentos psicotrópicos está destinada actualmente a pacientes adultos, y faltan enfoques psicofarmacológicos en niños, orientados a revertir trayectorias del neurodesarrollo aberrantes. La intervención psicofarmacológica a una edad temprana enfrenta el desafío de operar en un sistema altamente plástico y requiere de un conocimiento completo de los mecanismos del neurodesarrollo. Recientemente, el enfoque de la biología de sistemas ha contribuido a la comprensión de los mecanismos de neuroplasticidad desde una nueva perspectiva que los interpreta como el resultado de redes complejas y dinámicas de señales de diferentes sistemas. Este enfoque está creando oportunidades para la psicofarmacología del desarrollo, sugiriendo nuevos objetivos que pueden modular el curso del desarrollo al interferir con la neuroplasticidad a una edad temprana. Discutiremos dos sistemas interconectados, el inmunológico y la microbiota intestinal, que regulan el neurodesarrollo y que han sido implicados en la investigación preclínica como nuevos objetivos en la prevención del desarrollo cerebral aberrante. L’importance des premières années pour la programmation à long terme de la santé mentale est de plus en plus reconnue. Cependant, la plupart des médicaments psychotropes sont à destination des patients adultes, et des approches psychopharmacologiques précoces ayant pour but de corriger des neurodévelopements aberrants font défaut. Une intervention psychopharmacologique à un âge précoce doit faire face au défi d’agir dans un système hautement plastique et requiert une connaissance exhaustive des mécanismes du neurodévelopement. Récemment l’approche par la biologie des systèmes a participé à la compréhension des mécanismes de la neuroplasticité dans une nouvelle perspective qui les interprète comme le résultat des signaux de réseaux complexes et dynamiques provenant de différents systèmes. Cette approche crée des opportunités pour la psychopharmacologie pédiatrique, suggérant de nouvelles cibles qui pourraient moduler le cours du développement en interférant avec la neuroplasticité à un âge précoce. Nous discuterons deux systèmes interconnectés – le système immunitaire et le microbiote intestinal – qui régulent le neurodévelopement et qui ont été étudiés dans des recherches précliniques en tant que nouvelles cibles dans la prévention du développement aberrant du cerveau.

title: Measurement of kinetically resolved vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux using rotating disk electrode voltammetry.
abstract: The measurement of vesicular dopamine dynamics has been limited by the temporal resolution of existing techniques, hampering our understanding of presynaptic mechanisms underlying dopaminergic neurotransmission. We developed a novel approach using rotating disk electrode voltammetry (RDEV) to achieve kinetically resolved measurements of vesicular dopamine uptake and efflux in isolated synaptic vesicle preparations from rat striatum. Carbon fiber microelectrodes were incorporated into a rotating disk configuration and operated at 2000 rpm to enhance mass transport, enabling detection of rapid concentration changes with sub-second temporal resolution. Vesicular preparations were incubated with varying dopamine concentrations (1-100 μM) in the presence and absence of reserpine (10 μM) or bafilomycin A1 (100 nM) to pharmacologically characterize vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) activity. RDEV measurements revealed biphasic dopamine uptake kinetics with a rapid initial phase (t1/2 = 2.3 ± 0.4 s) followed by a slower equilibration phase (t1/2 = 18.7 ± 3.2 s), consistent with VMAT2-mediated transport. Reserpine completely abolished uptake, while bafilomycin A1 reduced uptake by 89 ± 7%. Efflux measurements using weak bases (NH4Cl, methylamine) demonstrated rapid dopamine release with kinetics matching the collapse of vesicular pH gradients. The technique achieved detection limits of 50 nM dopamine with signal-to-noise ratios exceeding 20:1. This RDEV approach provides unprecedented temporal resolution for studying vesicular dopamine handling and offers a valuable tool for investigating presynaptic dysfunction in neurological disorders.

title: Neuropharmacological profile of aqueous extract of Anaphe venata larva (Notondotidae) in rats.
abstract: **Background:** Anaphe venata larva, commonly consumed as food in West African communities, has been associated with seasonal ataxic syndrome in humans, suggesting significant neurological activity. Despite its widespread consumption and reported neurotoxic effects, the comprehensive neuropharmacological profile of this edible caterpillar remains poorly characterized. This study investigated the neurobehavioral effects of aqueous extract of A. venata larva in experimental rats. **Methods:** Adult Wistar rats (n=48) were randomly assigned to four groups receiving oral administration of A. venata aqueous extract at doses of 100, 200, and 400 mg/kg body weight, or distilled water (control) for 14 days. Neurobehavioral assessments included open field test, rotarod performance, beam walking test, and elevated plus maze. Motor coordination, anxiety-like behavior, locomotor activity, and cognitive function were evaluated. Histopathological examination of brain tissues was performed using hematoxylin and eosin staining. **Results:** A. venata extract produced dose-dependent neurobehavioral alterations. Significant impairment in motor coordination was observed at 200 and 400 mg/kg doses (p<0.05), characterized by reduced rotarod retention time and increased beam walking errors. Locomotor activity was markedly decreased at the highest dose (p<0.01). Anxiety-like behavior increased significantly in treated groups. Histopathological analysis revealed cerebellar Purkinje cell degeneration and neuronal vacuolation in the hippocampus, particularly at higher doses. **Conclusions:** Aqueous extract of A. venata larva exhibits significant neurotoxic properties in rats, producing motor dysfunction and cerebellar damage consistent with human seasonal ataxic syndrome. These findings provide experimental evidence supporting the neurotoxic potential of A. venata consumption.
journal: African journal of traditional, complementary, and alternative medicines : AJTCAM

title: NMDA antagonists: antiepileptic-neuroprotective drugs with diversified neuropharmacological profiles.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonists have emerged as promising therapeutic agents for epilepsy and neuroprotection, though their clinical utility has been limited by psychotomimetic side effects. This study investigated the neuropharmacological profiles of five structurally distinct NMDA antagonists: MK-801 (dizocilpine), CPP, APV, ketamine, and memantine. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=8-10 per group) were subjected to pentylenetetrazol-induced seizures and focal cerebral ischemia models. Anticonvulsant efficacy was assessed using electroencephalographic monitoring and behavioral seizure scoring. Neuroprotective effects were evaluated through histological analysis of hippocampal CA1 pyramidal cell survival 72 hours post-ischemia. Cognitive and motor function were assessed using Morris water maze and rotarod tests. All NMDA antagonists demonstrated significant anticonvulsant activity, with MK-801 and ketamine showing the greatest potency (ED50: 0.12 and 0.8 mg/kg, respectively). Neuroprotective efficacy varied considerably, with competitive antagonists (CPP, APV) providing 60-75% neuronal preservation compared to 40-50% for channel blockers. However, the degree of behavioral impairment correlated inversely with therapeutic index. Memantine exhibited the most favorable profile, showing moderate anticonvulsant and neuroprotective effects (ED50: 5.2 mg/kg) with minimal cognitive disruption. These findings demonstrate that NMDA receptor antagonists possess diversified neuropharmacological profiles that may be exploited therapeutically. The differential effects observed suggest that receptor subtype selectivity and binding kinetics significantly influence both therapeutic efficacy and adverse effect profiles, supporting continued development of subtype-selective NMDA antagonists for clinical applications.

title: 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline antagonizes a rise in brain dopamine metabolism, glutamate release in frontal cortex and locomotor hyperactivity produced by MK-801 but not the disruptions of prepulse inhibition, and impairment of working memory in rat.
abstract: N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor hypofunction has been implicated in the pathophysiology of schizophrenia, with NMDA receptor antagonists like MK-801 producing behavioral and neurochemical alterations resembling aspects of this disorder. 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (1MeTIQ), an endogenous compound with neuroprotective properties, has shown potential modulatory effects on dopaminergic neurotransmission. This study investigated whether 1MeTIQ could attenuate MK-801-induced neurochemical and behavioral changes in male Wistar rats. Animals received pretreatment with 1MeTIQ (25 or 50 mg/kg, i.p.) 30 minutes before MK-801 administration (0.3 mg/kg, i.p.). Dopamine metabolism was assessed by measuring DOPAC and HVA levels in striatum and frontal cortex using HPLC. Glutamate release in frontal cortex was monitored by in vivo microdialysis. Behavioral assessments included locomotor activity, prepulse inhibition (PPI) of acoustic startle, and spatial working memory using the Y-maze test. MK-801 significantly increased locomotor activity, dopamine turnover, and cortical glutamate efflux while disrupting PPI and impairing working memory performance. Pretreatment with 1MeTIQ dose-dependently prevented MK-801-induced hyperlocomotion, normalized dopamine metabolism, and blocked excessive glutamate release in frontal cortex. However, 1MeTIQ failed to reverse MK-801-induced PPI deficits or working memory impairment. These findings suggest that 1MeTIQ selectively modulates certain aspects of NMDA receptor hypofunction, particularly those involving dopaminergic hyperactivity and glutamatergic dysfunction, while cognitive and sensorimotor gating abnormalities remain unaffected. The partial protective profile of 1MeTIQ indicates distinct neurobiological mechanisms underlying different schizophrenia-like phenotypes induced by NMDA receptor

title: The psychological and neurochemical mechanisms of drug memory reconsolidation: implications for the treatment of addiction.
abstract: Memory reconsolidation is the process by which memories, destabilised at retrieval, require restabilisation to persist in the brain. It has been demonstrated that even old, well-established memories require reconsolidation following retrieval; therefore, memory reconsolidation could potentially be exploited to disrupt, or even erase, aberrant memories that underlie psychiatric disorders, thereby providing a novel therapeutic target. Drug addiction is one such disorder; it is both chronic and relapsing, and one prominent risk factor for a relapse episode is the presentation of environmental cues that have previously been associated with drugs of abuse. This 'cue-induced relapse' can be accounted for in psychological terms by reinforcing memories of the pavlovian association between the cue and the drug, which can thus influence behaviour through at least three psychologically and neurobiologically dissociable mechanisms: conditioned reinforcement, conditioned approach and conditioned motivation. As each of these psychological processes could contribute to the resumption of drug-seeking following abstinence, it is important to develop treatments that can reduce drug-seeking re-established via influences on each or all of these pavlovian processes, in order to minimise the risk of a subsequent relapse. Investigation of the memory reconsolidation mechanisms of the memories underlying conditioned reinforcement, conditioned approach and conditioned motivation indicate that they depend upon different neurochemical systems, including the glutamatergic and adrenergic systems within limbic corticostriatal circuitry. We also discuss here the subsequent translation to the clinic of this preclinical work.

title: Cannabinoids: Emerging developments in neuropsychopharmacology and biological psychiatry.
abstract: The endocannabinoid system has emerged as a critical modulator of neuropsychiatric function, with mounting evidence supporting therapeutic applications of cannabinoid compounds in mood disorders, anxiety, and neurodegenerative diseases. This review synthesizes recent advances in cannabinoid neuropsychopharmacology, examining both preclinical mechanisms and clinical therapeutic potential. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed literature from 2015-2021, focusing on cannabidiol (CBD) and Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) effects on neurotransmitter systems, neuroplasticity, and psychiatric symptomatology. Preclinical studies demonstrate that CBD modulates serotonergic and GABAergic signaling through CB1-independent mechanisms, while exhibiting neuroprotective properties via anti-inflammatory and antioxidant pathways. Clinical trials reveal promising anxiolytic effects of CBD in social anxiety disorder (effect size d=0.68, p<0.001) and post-traumatic stress disorder, with optimal dosing ranges of 300-600mg daily. THC shows biphasic dose-response patterns, with low doses (2.5-5mg) reducing anxiety while higher doses may exacerbate symptoms. Emerging evidence indicates cannabinoid efficacy in treatment-resistant depression, with 58% of patients showing clinically significant improvement in randomized controlled trials. Novel synthetic cannabinoids targeting CB2 receptors demonstrate anti-neuroinflammatory effects relevant to Alzheimer's disease and multiple sclerosis. However, individual variability in cannabinoid metabolism and receptor expression necessitates personalized treatment approaches. These findings support continued investigation of cannabinoid therapeutics as adjunctive treatments for neuropsychiatric disorders, while emphasizing the need for standardized dosing protocols and long-term safety studies to optimize clinical implementation.

title: Alexander George Karczmar (1917-2017).
abstract: Alexander George Karczmar (1917-2017) was a pioneering figure in neuropharmacology whose six-decade career fundamentally shaped our understanding of cholinergic neurotransmission and neurotoxicology. Born in Poland and educated at the University of Chicago, Karczmar established himself as a leading authority on acetylcholine systems, contributing over 300 publications and mentoring generations of neuroscientists. His early work in the 1950s helped elucidate the mechanisms of acetylcholinesterase inhibition and established foundational principles for understanding anticholinesterase toxicity, particularly relevant to organophosphate poisoning. Karczmar's research group made seminal contributions to characterizing nicotinic and muscarinic receptor subtypes, developing novel cholinergic agonists and antagonists, and mapping cholinergic pathways in the central nervous system. His investigations into the role of acetylcholine in learning and memory preceded and informed modern research on cholinergic dysfunction in Alzheimer's disease. Beyond his scientific contributions, Karczmar served as Editor-in-Chief of Progress in Brain Research and was instrumental in founding the International Society for Neurochemistry. His work on chemical warfare agents and neuroprotective strategies had significant implications for both military and civilian medicine. Karczmar's interdisciplinary approach, combining behavioral pharmacology, biochemistry, and electrophysiology, exemplified the integrative methodology that became standard in modern neuropharmacology. His legacy endures through his students, many of whom became prominent neuroscientists, and through the continued relevance of his fundamental discoveries in cholinergic neurobiology to contemporary therapeutic development.

title: Examining the 'psychopharmacology revolution' (1950-1980) through the advertising of psychoactive drugs in the British Medical Journal.
abstract: Many modern pharmaceutical products were launched during 1950-1980, as reflected in advertisements in the British Medical Journal ( BMJ). One of the first therapeutic areas to benefit from novel effective medications was psychiatry. We examined BMJ advertising material between 1950 and 1980, including every other issue over six-month periods (October-March) in 1950/1951, 1955/1956, 1957/1958, 1960/1961, 1962/1963, 1965/1966, 1967/1968, 1970/1971, 1972/1973, 1975/1976, 1980/1981. We recorded numbers of adverts for all pharmaceutical products and for psychiatric drugs; we also recorded trade names, generic names and marketing company. Advertising in BMJ peaked in the 1960s and declined markedly in the 1970s. Adverts for psychiatric drugs as a percentage of total pharmaceutical product advertising was broadly similar during 1955-1980, but with peaks in 1960/1961, 1970/1971 and 1975/1976, reflecting the entry of several novel compounds into the market. The peak marketing of antipsychotic drugs, sedatives and anxiolytic drugs was in 1960 and of antidepressants 1970. The time course of the rise of tricyclics and the switch from barbiturates to benzodiazepines can be seen. Drugs for psychiatry rose from ninth (1955/1956) to fourth (1975/1976) in terms of the number of products in the top 10 therapeutic areas. There is no evidence that they were advertised more aggressively (number of adverts/number of products). The birth of modern psychopharmacology is reflected in th e advertising of psychiatric drugs in BMJ. Many drugs currently used, or their closely related successors, were launched in the early to mid-1960s. This rise in modern pharmaceuticals preceded several other major therapeutic areas.

title: A short historical review of SCNP with special reference to the UKU.
abstract: The development of systematic approaches to assess side effects of psychotropic medications has been crucial for advancing neuropsychopharmacological research and clinical practice. The Scandinavian Committee on Neuropsychopharmacology (SCNP), established in the 1970s, played a pivotal role in standardizing adverse event reporting through the development of the UKU Side Effect Rating Scale (Udvalg for Kliniske Undersøgelser). This historical review examines the evolution of the SCNP and its contribution to systematic side effect assessment in psychiatric treatment. We conducted a comprehensive literature review spanning 1970-2014, analyzing key publications, meeting proceedings, and collaborative research initiatives involving SCNP members. Historical documents and personal communications with founding members were also examined to trace the development of the UKU scale. The SCNP emerged from the need for standardized clinical trial methodologies in Nordic countries, with early focus on antipsychotic and antidepressant efficacy studies. The UKU scale, developed between 1983-1987, represented a breakthrough in systematic side effect documentation, incorporating 48 items across four domains: psychological, neurological, autonomic, and other symptoms. The scale demonstrated excellent inter-rater reliability (κ=0.74-0.89) and became widely adopted internationally, translated into over 20 languages. Validation studies confirmed its utility across diverse psychiatric populations and treatment modalities. The SCNP's collaborative model influenced subsequent international research networks, while the UKU scale remains a gold standard for comprehensive side effect assessment. This historical perspective highlights how Nordic cooperation in neuropsychopharmacology contributed significantly to evidence-based psychiatric treatment and patient safety monitoring worldwide.

title: [Use of the antisense oligonucleotide technique in neuropsychopharmacology research].
abstract: The antisense oligonucleotide technique represents a powerful molecular approach for investigating gene function in neuropsychopharmacological research by selectively inhibiting target mRNA translation. This study evaluated the efficacy and specificity of phosphorothioate-modified antisense oligodeoxynucleotides targeting dopamine D2 receptor mRNA in rat striatal neurons. Male Wistar rats (n=32) received bilateral intrastriatal injections of either D2 receptor antisense oligonucleotides (20 μg/2 μL), corresponding sense oligonucleotides, or vehicle control. Animals were assessed for locomotor activity, rotational behavior following apomorphine challenge (0.5 mg/kg s.c.), and D2 receptor binding density using [³H]spiperone autoradiography at 24, 48, and 72 hours post-injection. Northern blot analysis confirmed selective reduction of D2 receptor mRNA levels. Antisense treatment produced significant decreases in spontaneous locomotor activity (62% reduction at 48h, p<0.01) and attenuated apomorphine-induced rotation compared to controls. [³H]spiperone binding was reduced by 45-55% in the injected striatum at 48-72 hours, with maximal effect observed at 48 hours. Sense oligonucleotides and vehicle produced no significant behavioral or biochemical changes. No effects were observed on D1 receptor binding or tyrosine hydroxylase immunoreactivity, confirming target specificity. These findings demonstrate that antisense oligonucleotides provide a valuable tool for temporally controlled, reversible knockdown of specific neurotransmitter receptors, offering distinct advantages over traditional pharmacological approaches for dissecting molecular mechanisms underlying neuropsychiatric disorders and drug action in the central nervous system.
journal: Nihon shinkei seishin yakurigaku zasshi = Japanese journal of psychopharmacology

title: Determination of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity in the same population of cultured cells.
abstract: Cholinergic neurotransmission relies on the coordinated function of high-affinity choline uptake (HACU) and choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) activity, yet technical limitations have prevented simultaneous measurement of these parameters in individual cell populations. We developed a novel dual-assay protocol to assess both HACU and ChAT activity sequentially in the same cultured cholinergic cells, enabling direct correlation of these functionally related processes. Primary septal cholinergic neurons were cultured from embryonic day 18 rat fetuses and maintained for 14-21 days in vitro. HACU was measured using [³H]choline uptake assays with hemicholinium-3 inhibition to confirm specificity, followed by cell harvesting and ChAT activity determination using [¹⁴C]acetyl-CoA as substrate. Muscarinic receptor modulation was examined using carbachol and atropine treatments. The dual-assay protocol successfully quantified both parameters without methodological interference. Baseline HACU rates averaged 45.2 ± 3.8 pmol/min/mg protein, while ChAT activity measured 12.7 ± 1.9 nmol/h/mg protein. Carbachol treatment (100 μM) significantly increased both HACU (168% of control) and ChAT activity (142% of control), effects blocked by atropine pretreatment. Strong positive correlation was observed between HACU and ChAT activity across individual culture wells (r = 0.78, p < 0.001). This methodology provides a valuable tool for investigating cholinergic function at the cellular level and demonstrates coordinated regulation of choline uptake and acetylcholine synthesis mechanisms. The approach will facilitate studies of cholinergic dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases and therapeutic drug development.

title: The Domino Effect: Ed Domino's early studies of Psychoactive Drugs.
abstract: Edward F. Domino's pioneering research in the 1950s and 1960s established foundational principles in psychoactive drug pharmacology that continue to influence contemporary neuropharmacology. This historical review examines Domino's early investigations into dissociative anesthetics, particularly his work with phencyclidine (PCP) and ketamine, alongside his studies of nicotinic acetylcholine receptor function and psychedelic compounds. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of Domino's publications from 1955-1975, laboratory notebooks, and previously unpublished correspondence to trace the evolution of his research methodology and theoretical framework. Our analysis reveals that Domino's systematic approach to characterizing drug-receptor interactions and his innovative use of electroencephalographic monitoring during pharmacological studies provided critical insights into consciousness, anesthesia, and addiction mechanisms. His work demonstrated that ketamine's unique anesthetic properties resulted from NMDA receptor antagonism rather than GABAergic enhancement, fundamentally shifting understanding of anesthetic mechanisms. Additionally, his investigations into nicotine's cognitive-enhancing properties presaged modern research into cholinergic treatments for dementia. Domino's integration of behavioral pharmacology, electrophysiology, and clinical observation established methodological standards that influenced subsequent generations of researchers. His emphasis on translational research bridging basic science and clinical application created a template for modern psychopharmacological investigation. These early studies not only advanced understanding of specific drug classes but also established theoretical frameworks for consciousness research and addiction medicine that remain relevant to contemporary neuropharmacology. Domino's legacy demonstrates the enduring impact of rigorous, mechanistically-focused psychoactive drug research.

title: Treatment of attention deficits in neurological disorders.
abstract: Attention deficits are prevalent across numerous neurological conditions, including traumatic brain injury, stroke, dementia, and neurodegenerative disorders, significantly impacting patients' functional outcomes and quality of life. While stimulant medications have shown efficacy in attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, their utility in neurological populations remains less established. This review examines current pharmacological approaches for treating attention deficits in neurological disorders, analyzing evidence from clinical trials and case series published between 1990-2005. We systematically reviewed literature on cholinesterase inhibitors, dopaminergic agents, noradrenergic medications, and novel compounds in patients with acquired attention deficits. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly donepezil and rivastigmine, demonstrated modest improvements in sustained attention tasks in patients with mild cognitive impairment and early dementia, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.5. Methylphenidate showed promise in traumatic brain injury populations, improving processing speed and divided attention in 60-70% of patients in small controlled trials. Modafinil emerged as a well-tolerated alternative, particularly effective for vigilance deficits in multiple sclerosis and post-stroke populations. Atomoxetine demonstrated benefits in executive attention domains across various neurological conditions, though with variable response rates. However, most studies suffered from small sample sizes, heterogeneous patient populations, and limited follow-up periods. Current evidence suggests that pharmacological interventions can provide meaningful improvements in attention deficits associated with neurological disorders, but treatment selection should be individualized based on specific attention domains affected, underlying pathophysiology, and patient tolerance. Further large-scale, randomized controlled trials are needed to establish definitive treatment guidelines.

title: Neuropharmacological modulation of cognitive deficits after brain damage.
abstract: Cognitive impairments following traumatic brain injury, stroke, and neurodegenerative diseases represent a major clinical challenge with limited therapeutic options. Recent advances in understanding the neurochemical basis of cognitive dysfunction have identified potential pharmacological targets for intervention. This review examines current evidence for neuropharmacological approaches to treating post-injury cognitive deficits, focusing on cholinergic, dopaminergic, and glutamatergic systems. We analyzed data from randomized controlled trials and case studies published between 1995-2004 involving patients with acquired brain injury. Cholinesterase inhibitors, particularly donepezil and rivastigmine, demonstrated modest but significant improvements in attention and memory function in 60-70% of patients with traumatic brain injury-related cognitive impairment. Dopaminergic agents including bromocriptine and methylphenidate showed efficacy in enhancing executive function and processing speed, with effect sizes ranging from 0.3-0.6. Modafinil emerged as a promising treatment for post-injury fatigue and attention deficits, with response rates approaching 65% in preliminary studies. AMPA receptor potentiators and NMDA receptor modulators showed encouraging results in animal models but remain in early-phase human trials. Combination therapies targeting multiple neurotransmitter systems demonstrated superior outcomes compared to monotherapy in limited studies. While current pharmacological interventions provide meaningful but moderate cognitive benefits, optimal treatment protocols remain undefined. Future research should focus on biomarker-guided personalized medicine approaches and novel neuroprotective strategies to maximize therapeutic potential in this vulnerable population.

title: Anxiety in the older patient: differential diagnosis and treatment.
abstract: Anxiety in older patients is more often a symptom of an underlying mental or physical disorder than a primary diagnosis. Its various forms include situational anxiety, adjustment disorder, and phobias. A search for underlying organic causes is important, as age-related diseases, drug side effects, and substance abuse/withdrawal may have etiologic significance. Anxiety may also mask primary psychiatric disorders such as depression or delirium. Therapy requires treating any underlying psychiatric illness and seeking psychosocial intervention for functional causes. If medical therapy is indicated, short-acting benzodiazepines are the first choice, but patients should be followed closely for possible side effects.

title: Inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs.
abstract: Traditional neuropharmacological approaches to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have focused on extracellular drug binding sites, yet emerging evidence suggests that intracellular drug interactions may play crucial roles in receptor modulation. We investigated the inside-out neuropharmacology of nicotinic drugs using patch-clamp electrophysiology on inside-out membrane patches from rat hippocampal neurons and heterologous expression systems containing α4β2 and α7 nAChR subtypes. Bath application of nicotine (1-100 μM) to the intracellular membrane surface produced concentration-dependent potentiation of acetylcholine-evoked currents in α4β2 receptors (EC50 = 12.4 ± 2.1 μM) but had minimal effects on α7 receptors. Conversely, intracellular mecamylamine (10-300 μM) inhibited both receptor subtypes with greater potency than extracellular application (IC50 values: 45.2 ± 8.7 μM vs 127.3 ± 15.4 μM for α4β2). Site-directed mutagenesis of putative intracellular binding residues in the M3-M4 cytoplasmic loop significantly reduced intracellular drug sensitivity. Molecular docking simulations identified distinct intracellular binding pockets with nanomolar to micromolar affinities for tested compounds. Functional assays demonstrated that intracellular drug effects were independent of extracellular competitive antagonism and persisted in the presence of saturating extracellular nicotine concentrations. These findings reveal a previously uncharacterized dimension of nicotinic receptor pharmacology, suggesting that drug penetration across cellular membranes and subsequent intracellular receptor interactions contribute significantly to overall pharmacological profiles. Understanding inside-out nicotinic pharmacology may inform the development of more selective therapeutic agents for neurological disorders involving cholinergic dysfunction.

title: [Atypical antipsychotic drugs].
abstract: In recent years several new antipsychotics have come to market in the Netherlands (i.e. risperidone, olanzapine and quetiapine). These compounds are called atypical for their lack of the extrapyramidal side effects typical of older antipsychotics. Clozapine, which was developed earlier, is also an atypical antipsychotic drug. The new antipsychotics have proven to be more effective than the old ones in reducing the negative symptoms and in improving the cognitive deficits of schizophrenia. Moreover, they indeed induce less extrapyramidal side effects than the older antipsychotics. Head-to-head comparisons of the atypical antipsychotics are sparse. Studies comparing low-dose regimens of the typical antipsychotics with the atypical drugs as well as relapse prevention studies are needed before it can be decided whether the atypical drugs can replace the older compounds.

title: Tobacco smoking and nicotine neuropsychopharmacology: some future research directions.
abstract: Tobacco smoking remains one of the most prevalent forms of drug dependence worldwide, yet fundamental questions regarding nicotine's neuropsychopharmacological mechanisms continue to limit therapeutic interventions. While nicotinic acetylcholine receptors have been established as primary targets for nicotine's reinforcing effects, the complex interactions between receptor subtypes, regional brain distribution, and behavioral outcomes require systematic investigation. This review synthesizes current knowledge of nicotine neuropharmacology and proposes critical research directions for the next decade. Key areas requiring investigation include: (1) characterization of neuronal nicotinic receptor subunit diversity and their differential roles in dependence processes, (2) examination of nicotine's effects on dopaminergic, GABAergic, and glutamatergic neurotransmission in reward circuits, (3) development of animal models that better recapitulate human smoking patterns and individual vulnerability factors, and (4) identification of genetic polymorphisms influencing nicotine metabolism and receptor function. Emerging molecular techniques, including receptor subtype-selective ligands, transgenic mouse models, and in vivo microdialysis, offer unprecedented opportunities to dissect nicotine's neurobiological effects. Additionally, neuroimaging approaches in human subjects may bridge findings from animal studies to clinical populations. Future research should prioritize understanding how chronic nicotine exposure produces long-lasting neuroadaptations that maintain tobacco dependence. Such investigations will be essential for developing more effective pharmacotherapies and identifying individuals at highest risk for tobacco addiction, ultimately reducing the substantial public health burden of smoking-related diseases.
journal: Neuropsychopharmacology : official publication of the American College of Neuropsychopharmacology

title: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor redux: Discovery of accessories opens therapeutic vistas.
abstract: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) have long been considered promising therapeutic targets for neurological and psychiatric disorders, yet clinical translation has been hampered by limited selectivity and off-target effects. Recent structural and functional studies have revealed that nAChRs exist in dynamic complexes with auxiliary proteins that modulate their pharmacology, trafficking, and signaling properties. Here, we systematically characterized the composition and functional impact of nAChR-associated protein complexes in human brain tissue and heterologous systems using proximity labeling proteomics, electrophysiology, and pharmacological profiling. We identified 47 high-confidence nAChR-interacting proteins, including previously uncharacterized chaperones, scaffolding proteins, and regulatory enzymes. Notably, the auxiliary protein NACHO (neuronal acetylcholine receptor-associated protein) enhanced α7 nAChR surface expression 3.2-fold and shifted sensitivity to the positive allosteric modulator PNU-120596 by 15-fold in hippocampal neurons. Co-expression of the scaffolding protein PSD-95 with α4β2 nAChRs increased receptor clustering and altered desensitization kinetics, reducing IC50 values for novel selective antagonists by 8-fold. Viral-mediated knockdown of key auxiliary proteins in mouse prefrontal cortex significantly impaired working memory performance and altered nicotine reward behaviors. These findings demonstrate that nAChR auxiliary proteins represent unexplored pharmacological targets that could enable development of more selective therapeutic interventions. Our results suggest that targeting nAChR-accessory protein interactions, rather than the receptors themselves, may overcome historical limitations in nAChR-based drug discovery and open new therapeutic avenues for addiction, cognitive disorders, and neuropsychiatric diseases.

title: Pharmacological regulation of gene expression.
abstract: The pharmacological regulation of gene expression represents a critical mechanism underlying therapeutic drug action and adverse effects in the central nervous system. While traditional approaches have focused on receptor binding and enzymatic inhibition, emerging evidence suggests that many psychoactive compounds exert their effects through direct modulation of transcriptional machinery. We investigated the capacity of classical neuropharmacological agents to regulate gene expression in primary cortical neurons and assessed the temporal dynamics of these responses. Primary rat cortical neurons were treated with clinically relevant concentrations of fluoxetine (10 μM), haloperidol (1 μM), diazepam (5 μM), or vehicle control for 6, 12, and 24 hours. Gene expression profiles were analyzed using quantitative RT-PCR arrays targeting 84 genes involved in synaptic plasticity, neurotransmitter synthesis, and cellular stress responses. Chromatin immunoprecipitation assays examined drug-induced changes in histone modifications at target gene promoters. Fluoxetine treatment resulted in significant upregulation of BDNF (2.3-fold, p<0.01) and CREB1 (1.8-fold, p<0.05) within 6 hours, accompanied by increased H3K4me3 marks at their promoter regions. Haloperidol induced delayed but sustained changes in dopamine receptor gene expression and immediate early genes. Diazepam primarily affected GABAergic signaling genes with minimal transcriptional effects. Time-course analysis revealed distinct temporal patterns for each compound class. These findings demonstrate that neuropharmacological agents possess intrinsic capacity to reshape neuronal gene expression programs through epigenetic mechanisms, providing novel insights into their therapeutic mechanisms and suggesting new targets for drug development in psychiatric and neurological disorders.

title: In vivo neurochemical correlates of cognitive processes: methodological and conceptual challenges.
abstract: The investigation of neurochemical substrates underlying cognitive processes in living organisms presents formidable methodological and theoretical obstacles that significantly impact data interpretation and translational relevance. Traditional approaches including microdialysis, voltammetry, and pharmacological manipulations have provided valuable insights into neurotransmitter dynamics during learning, memory, and attention tasks, yet each technique carries inherent limitations that constrain temporal and spatial resolution. We critically examine current methodological approaches used to assess in vivo neurochemical changes during cognitive performance, with particular emphasis on technical constraints affecting data quality and reproducibility. Microdialysis studies demonstrate significant variability in neurotransmitter sampling efficiency across brain regions, with recovery rates ranging from 15-40% depending on probe membrane characteristics and tissue properties. Fast-scan cyclic voltammetry offers superior temporal resolution but remains limited to select neurotransmitters and restricted anatomical locations. Pharmacological interventions, while providing mechanistic insights, often produce systemic effects that complicate attribution of cognitive changes to specific neural circuits. Furthermore, substantial inter-individual differences in baseline neurochemical activity and drug metabolism introduce considerable variance in experimental outcomes. Conceptual challenges include establishing causal relationships between neurochemical fluctuations and behavioral performance, accounting for compensatory mechanisms, and integrating data across multiple neurotransmitter systems. We propose that future advances require integration of complementary techniques, development of more selective pharmacological tools, and adoption of standardized protocols to enhance reproducibility. These methodological refinements are essential for advancing our understanding of the complex neurochemical basis of cognition and facilitating translation to clinical applications.

title: Developmental exposure to a serotonin agonist produces subsequent behavioral and neurochemical changes in the adult male prairie vole.
abstract: Prairie voles (Microtus ochrogaster) exhibit complex social behaviors mediated by multiple neurotransmitter systems, including serotonin, which plays a critical role in social attachment and anxiety-related behaviors. Early developmental exposure to pharmacological agents can produce lasting alterations in adult behavior and neurochemistry. The present study examined whether neonatal exposure to the serotonin 1A receptor agonist 8-OH-DPAT affects adult behavioral phenotypes and serotonergic function in male prairie voles. Male pups received daily subcutaneous injections of 8-OH-DPAT (0.5 mg/kg) or saline vehicle from postnatal days 1-14. At 8-10 weeks of age, subjects underwent behavioral testing including elevated plus maze, partner preference, and allogrooming assays. Subsequently, brains were analyzed for serotonin and 5-HIAA concentrations in the prefrontal cortex, nucleus accumbens, and hypothalamus using HPLC. 8-OH-DPAT-treated males exhibited significantly increased anxiety-like behavior in the elevated plus maze, spending less time in open arms compared to controls (P < 0.01). These animals also demonstrated impaired partner preference formation and reduced allogrooming duration toward familiar partners. Neurochemical analysis revealed decreased serotonin concentrations in the prefrontal cortex (32% reduction, P < 0.05) and nucleus accumbens (28% reduction, P < 0.05) of treated animals, while 5-HIAA/serotonin ratios were elevated in all brain regions examined. These findings demonstrate that developmental serotonergic manipulation produces persistent alterations in adult social behavior and anxiety, accompanied by region-specific changes in serotonergic neurotransmission. Such developmental programming may contribute to individual variation in social behavioral phenotypes.
